No. F.2(6)/82-LC?&#x27;\n\nK_ K_ MATHEW Shastri Bhavan,\n\\_&#x27; New Delhi-110001.\n\nFebruary 28, 1983.\n\nMy dear Minister,\n\nI send herewith the Eighty-ninth Report of the _Law Com-\nmission recommending certain amendments to the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_10\">Limitation Act,\n1963</a>.\n\n2. Revision of the Limitation Act has been taken up by the Law\nCommission suo mom in view of the importance of the subject as\nbranch of adjective law. The present Act was enacted about 20\nyears ago. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_11\">The Act</a> is an enactment of general application and\nimportance. It is, therefore, proper that such an important enact-\nment is reviewed from time to time.\n\n3. During the last 20 years extensive developments have taken\nplace both in law and in society. It is proper that those develop-\nments should be taken note of ma comprehensive manner.\n\n&#x27; 4. The Commission wishes to express its appreciation to\n\nShri P. M. Bakshi, Part-time Member of the Commission, for the\n\ufb01nalisation of the Report. We also acknowledge the assistance\nrendered by Shri V. V. Vaze, ex-Member-Secretary in preparing the\ninitial draft of the Report.\n\nWith regards.\n\nYours sincerely,\nSd/-\n(K. K. MATHEW)\n\nShri Jagannatli Kaushal,\nMinister of Law. Justice &amp; Company Alfairs.\nShastri Bhavan,\n\nNew Delhi.\nA (V)\n\n\n\nLAW COMMISSION OF INDIA EIGHTY-NINTH\nREPORT ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\nCHAPTER 1\nINTRODUCTORY\n\n1.1. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_12\">Revision of the Limitation Act, 1963</a> has been taken up by the Law l:eViIsi0n_t0_f\nCommission of India suo mom, in view of the importance of the subject as ahgn \nbranch of adjective law. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_13\">The Act</a> is an enactment of general application and\nimportance; its provisions come up before the course daily for interpretation.\n\nAn unjust or unsatisfactory rule of limitation that bars the institution of legal\nproceedings means that the remedy contemplated by the law for the enforce-\nment of a legal right becomes futile, or--if the injustice or unsatisfactory charac-\nter of the rule consists in its allowing an unduly long time for the pursuit of a\nlegal remedy~then the interests of justice are defeated, because stale demands\nwould thereby be encouraged. It is therefore proper that such an important\nenactment is reviewed from time to time. The present Act was enacted about\ntwenty years ago. During this period, extensive developments have taken place,\nboth in law and in society. It is proper that those developments should be\n\ntaken note of in a comprehensive manner.\n\nThe present Act was passed after the Law Commission reported on the\nearlier Act of 1908 and largely implements the recommendations made by the\nCommission} It is therefore particularly appropriate that a review of the law\nmay be undertaken by the Commission again.\n\n1.2. The statutory law of limitation has a long and interesting history in History.\nIndia, which takes us at least to 1859. Before that year, there was no uniform\nlaw of limitation. Various Regulations, applicable to the Company&#x27;s Courts\ndealing with certain topics falling within the law of limitation, were in force\nin the Mofussil of the three Presidencies. For the Courts established by the\nRoyal Charter in the Presidency towns, the English law was taken as applicable.\nTo introduce uniformity in law. the limitation Act (14 of 1859) was passed.\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_14\">The Act</a> came into force in 1862, but covered only suits, and, moreover, did not\ncontain any provision relating to prescription. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_15\">The Limitation Act</a>. 1871 (9 of\n1871), which replaced the Act of 1859, added certain provisions in the nature\nof law of prescription also, and introduced. for the \ufb01rst time. the arrangement\nwhich one \ufb01nds in the present Act--namely, the general principles relating to\ncomputation and exclusion of time \ufb01nd a place in the body of the Act, while\nthe actual time--limits for various kinds of proceedings are placed in a tabular\nstatement. forming a <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_16\">Schedule to the Act</a>. It may also be mentioned that the\nAct of 1871 introduced a period of sixty years applicable to a suit of any kind\nbrought by the Government. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_17\">This Act</a> was replaced by the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_18\">Limitation Act</a>.\n1877 (15 of 1877). Besides making certain minor changes in regard to the\nclasses of suits expressly covered and the time limit or the starting point of\nlimitation for various suits, the Act of 1877 extended the operation of the law\nof prescription. by making two additions to it. In the first place, while the\nAct of 187] had provided that the right to land or to a hereditary office could\nbe extinguished by lapse of time, the Act of 1877 extended this&#x27;principle to\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India. 3rd Report (Limitation Act. 1908) (July, 1956).\n2-14 M of LI &amp; CA[ND[83\n\n\n\nPrir_1c&#x27;_iples for\nrevision.\n\nNeed for\nhaving ano-\nther look.\n\n&#x27;be given its\n\n89TH REPORT or 1. AW comm. or INDIA ON THF. Ll?\\llTATlO.&#x27;~l ACT, 1963\n\n(C/mp/er] \u00abInlr0a&#x27;u&lt;&#x27;l0r_1&#x27;)\n\nany property, whether movable or immovable. Secondly. while the Act of 1871\nprovided for the acquisition of easements absolutely by open and uninterrupted\nenjoyment for twenty years. the Act of l877 extended this provision to what\nare known as pro\ufb01ts a prcndrc.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_19\">The Limitation Act</a> i908 (9 of 1908) made certain changes in matters of\ndetail, without radically altering the substance or the arrangement of the pro-\nvisions. In the meantime, the lndian <a href=\"/doc/82950642/\" id=\"a_20\">Easements Act</a> dealing with easements,\nhad been passed. which too provided for the acquisition and extinction of ease-\nments by prescription. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_21\">The Limitation Act</a> of 1908 therefore expressly pro-\nvided that its provisions concerning easements shall not apply to cases arising\nin the territories to which the <a href=\"/doc/82950642/\" id=\"a_22\">Indian Easements Act</a>. 1882 may. for the time\nbeing. extend. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_23\">The Limitation Act</a> of l963 (the present Act) has made a few\nchanges of substance in the Act---particularly. in certain time limits and also in\nregard to the scheme relating to applications for execution. At the same time,\nit has not disturbed the basic structure of the Act of l908. As already men-\ntioned,&#x27; the present Act largely implemented the recommendations made by the\nLaw Commission in its Report on that Act.\n\n1.3. It would be convenient to mention at this stage some of the general\nprinciples adopted by us in revising the Act. in the ll!&#x27;St place. we have\nexamined the case law on the Act in some detail. addressing ourselves especially\nto conflict of decisions on important points. In a law of day-to-day use. this\naspect is of practical importance. Secondly, some of the provisions of the Act\nappeared to require review in the light of a few juristic trends that have emerged\nin recent times. By way of example. we may mention the more liberal attitude\nof the law in regard to mistake of law as a basis for granting relief against strict\napplication of rules of limitation. Thirdly, certain commercial and other trans-\nactions have gained popularity in recent times and it is proper that the law\nshould take note of them. We may cite. by way of example. the practice of\nplacing money in \ufb01xed deposits. For the recovery of money so placed. the Act\nhas no speci\ufb01c article at present----- a matter certainlv requiring attention.\nFourthly. apart from conflict of views. judicial decisions have brought out a few\nlacunae in the Act--~-for example. the di\ufb02icultv caused by the draftsmanship of\n<a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_24\">section 29</a> in regard to stilts for dower. Fifthly. some of the provisions of the\nAct are unduly I&#x27;L&#x27;Sil&#x27;lCl.C\\&#x27;l in their scope. and it has been considered worth\nexamination whether the nrint-iplc on which they are based should not\nfull scope by making the provision more ample than\nat present. <a href=\"/doc/206324/\" id=\"a_25\">Section 1</a>] furnishes one example. That section (dealing with cer-\ntain aspects of con\ufb01ict of laws) is unnecessarily restricted to certain kinds\nof causes of action. These have been some of the important aspects that have\nbeen kept in view in reviewing the Act.\n\n1.4. A query may perhaps he raised as to the need for liaving another look\nat the Act within twenty years or so_ It is enough to answer it by quoting\nwhat the Chairman of the law Commission for Fnglmnl and Wales said about\nthe process of law reform:&quot;\n\n&quot;In every societv, law reform in changing times is a process which is\nas endlessly necessary as cleaning the streets. maintaining buildings.\npruning trees and disposing of refuse. It has to he done: either systemati-\ncally and continuously. or drastically from time to time.&quot;\n\n&#x27;Para l.l, supra.\n2M1&#x27;. Iutice Kerr, &quot;Law Reform in Canging Times&quot;, 06 Law Quarterly Review 515.\n\n\n\n89m Rt.-&#x27;PORT or 1..--m cumin. or INDIA on &#x27;lllL LlMl&#x27;l&#x27;;\\T&#x27;0N ACT, 1963\n\n;\\&#x27; C/zap tgr 17------I11troduczoryj\n\n1.5. The policies underlying the law old limitation are ultimately based on Poljcics\n\n. . &#x27; . r V\u00bb  , = \u00ab .  .. , -3 dcrlying\njustice and convenience. mi mdmdual snoutd not hwc under the threat of. a\ufb01ilc law of\npossible action for an indeterminate period, since it would be unjust. All that has been said on the subject can be sum-\n\nmarised by stating that the law of Limitation rests upon three main f0LlI1ClEltl0l]S--\n\njustice, convenience and the need to encourage diligence.\n\n1.6. However, it is obvious that while these considerations are laudable in Hardship\nthemselves, their translation into practical legislation is not a matter of ease. An Occasiionatty\nover-einphasis on these propositions at the risk of disregarding some other muse&#x27;\nweighty counter-balancing consideration might cause serious injustice. Reference is rnadc by the same writer in\nthis context.\n\n \n\nThus, the law oi&#x27; limitation has to strike a balance between the policy\nconsiderations incntioncd abote ten the one hand) and tile risk of injustice (on\nthe other hand).\n\n1.7. So much as tegttntls llh&#x27; iiiipoitaiire of having a law of limitation that Scheme of\n\nis reasonably fair in its substance and reasonably certain in its form. The the Actzi\nscheme of the present Act JHEI} be brie\ufb02y described at this stage. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_26\">The Act</a> (like The &quot;along&quot;\nits predecessors} is dixided into sections and articles. the operative provisions\nand the principles as to computation of the period of limitation, as also provi-\nsions in the nature of prescription, are to be found in the sections of the Act.\nThe periods of llmlt2tf_h_tt1 applicable to various classes of suits. appeals and\napplications. and the time lroin which the period begins to run in each case,\nare matters dealt with in a tabular form in the articles placed in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_27\">Schedule to\nthe Act</a>.\n\n1.8. One of the principal objects of the law of limitation is the discourage-\nment of stale demands&#x27;. particularly tor the reason that where the raising of\ncontroversy is unduly delayed. evidence tends to disappear and opportunity may\narise for the filling of claims which are either dishonest or mistaken in point.\nof facts. Since this dil&#x27;ncult_\\&#x27; would not survive where the party against whom\nthe claim is made has hintselt furnished reliable evidence of the debt or&#x27;-liability,\nthe Act in sections I8 to 20. provides for counting a fresh starting point of\nlimitation where the person sought to be charged in respect of any property or\nright has made a written acknowlcclgcment of liability. as also where payment\non account of a debt or an interest on a legacy has been made by such person\n(coupled with a written record), Of course, such acknowledgement or payment\nmust have been niatle before the claim has already become tinte--barrcd.\n\n&#x27;LI/J V. Pea.s-Icy, twso) I All 13.12. 623.\n\n7BereIt Morgan. &quot;Fault in the Syxzem&quot; (30 Oct. 1980) 131) New L.J. |t)t)2.\n\n&#x27;-&#x27;A Milner, Winnie The Pooh. (ihztpter 6.\n&#x27;cf. para 1.5 supra.\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT OF LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE i.1M1rArioi~i ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 1~Inlroduct0ry)\n1.9. The case of substitution or addition of parties during the course of\nlitigation is dealt with in <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_28\">section 21</a>.\n\nI\n\nContinuing breaches of contracts and torts are taken care of by <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_29\">section 22</a>,\nwhile. in respect of suits for compensation for acts which are not actionable\nwithout special damage, <a href=\"/doc/11639/\" id=\"a_30\">section 23</a> makes a suitable provision. <a href=\"/doc/769768/\" id=\"a_31\">Section 24</a>\nprovides that for the purposes of the Act all instruments shall be deemed to be\nmade with reference to the Gregorian calendar.\n\n1.10. Next follow provisions in the nature of prescription concerned with\nthe acquisition of easements by prescription (<a href=\"/doc/1663327/\" id=\"a_32\">sections 25</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1055013/\" id=\"a_33\">26</a>) and the\nextinguishment of right to property at the determination of the period ol&#x27; limi-\ntation for instituting a suit for possession of any property (<a href=\"/doc/1223371/\" id=\"a_34\">section 27</a>). The\nbody of the Act ends with miscellaneous provisions concerned with the amend-\nment of certain other Acts, savings, transitional provisions and repeal (<a href=\"/doc/1154330/\" id=\"a_35\">sections\n\n28</a> to <a href=\"/doc/614641/\" id=\"a_36\">32</a>).\n\nComing to the body ol&#x27; the Act, sections I and 2 deal with preliminary\nmatters, including de\ufb01nitions. The most important provisions of the Act\nrelating to the bar of limitation occurs immediately thereafter, in <a href=\"/doc/880040/\" id=\"a_37\">section 3</a>.\nDismissal of a suit, appeal or application tiled after the prescribed period is\nmandatory. <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_38\">Section 4</a> deals with the situation of expiry of the prescribed\nperiod at a time when the court is closed. Under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_39\">section 5</a>, in the ease of\nappeals and certain applications, the court has power, for sutlicicnt cause, to\nextend the prescribed period. Here one has an example ol&#x27;-legislative anxiety\nto maintain a balance between ensuring vigilance and avoiding hardship. As\nregards persons under legal disability, appropriate provisions are tnade in sec-\ntions () to 8. Subject to these special provisions, the general principle laid\ndown in <a href=\"/doc/268914/\" id=\"a_40\">section 9</a> is that once time has begun to run, no subsequent disability\nor inability to institute a suit or make an application stops it. By way of an\nexceptional provision, <a href=\"/doc/541605/\" id=\"a_41\">section 10</a> provides that certain suits against trustees and\ntheir representatives are not barred by &quot;any length of time&quot;. The section takes\nwithin its fold Hindu, Muslim and Buddhist religions and charitable endow-\nments as well~a provision which constitutes a tirst rate example of the legis-\nlature stepping in to correct an anomaly brought to light by judicial decisions.\n<a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_42\">Section 11</a> deals with a topic belonging to contlict of laws, namely, suits on\nContracts entered into outside the territories to which the Act extends. Such\nsuits are governed by the Indian law of limitation, whatever be the locus of\n\nthe cause of action.\n\nElaborate provisions as to the computationof the period of limitation next\nfollow. Thus, time taken in certain preliminaries that are requisite for pur-\nsuing legal proceedings of special categories (appeals and certain applications)\nis the subject matter of section l2,--~provision based on necessity. That sec-\ntion also enacts the general rule that the day from which the period of limita-\ntion for any suit, appeal or application is to be reckoned must be excluded in\nComputing the period of limitation. Sections I3 and 14 provide for the ex-\nclusion of time actually taken in certain infruetuous legal proceedings -----in regard\nto the special case of an application for permission to sue as a pauper (if\nultimately the application is not granted), and in regard to the more general\ncase of legal proceedings which become infruetuous because the court, from\ndefect of jurisdiction or other cause of like nature, is unable to entertain them.\nCertain cases where the institution of legal proceedings has to be postponed by\nreasons of necessity, judicial orders (such as, injunctions or stay orders) or\nstatutory provisions as to the giving of a notice as a condition precedent to\n\n\n\n89rH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT\u00bb 1903\n\n(Chapter /---[mii&#x27;0dirctory)\n\nthe institution of a legal proceeding, and the like, are dealt with in <a href=\"/doc/1720337/\" id=\"a_43\">section 15</a>.\nThe absence of the defendant from India is, in computing the period of limi-\ntation for a suit, excluded under section l5(5).\n\nA cause of action arising on death or a cause of action whose accrual does\nnot take place until after the death of the person concerned needs special treat-\nment, and <a href=\"/doc/1019641/\" id=\"a_44\">section 16</a> attends to such causes of action in the context of limita-\n\ntion.\n\nWhile these provisions of the Act are mostly concerned with di\ufb01icultics\narising from certain circumstances which are not dependent on the conduct of\nan individual, justice requires that provision should also be made to relax the\nlaw of limitation in case of fraud or mistakc~thesc two being situations in\nwhich a person is prevented from instituting legal proceedingswithin time,\neither because of the misconduct of the opposite party or because of his own\nhaving laboured under a misconception. These two situations are taken care\nof by <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_45\">section 17</a>.\n\n1.11. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_46\">The Schedule to the Act</a> contains I37 articles, laying down periods Scheme Of\nof limitation for various kinds of Proceedings. 01&#x27; these, <a href=\"/doc/1406924/\" id=\"a_47\">articles 1</a> to 113 relate the Act;\nto suits; articles M4 to 117 relate to appeals and <a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_48\">articles 118</a> to 137 relate to \u00a7:,&#x27;\u00b0t,&#x27;?e&quot;&#x27;C1\u00b0s\napplications. The general scheme adopted in the Schedule is that, in eachSCh\u20acdl1lc\u00ab\ncategory, spccilic types of proceedings are covered by specilic articles dealing\nwith a particular cause of action or head of relief. This is followed in each\ncategory by a rcsiduary article, which is intended to take care of a suit, an\nappeal or an application {as the case may be), not covered by a spccilic article.\n\nThe perioxls of limitation prescribed for various categories of suits, appeals and\napplications vary.\n\n1.12. Coming more particularly to suits, it may be stated that the periods\nprescribed by the relevant articles vary from one year to thirty years; for all\nsuits instituted by the Government, the uniform period (under <a href=\"/doc/280240/\" id=\"a_49\">article 112</a>) is\nthirty years, except a suit before the Supreme Court in the exercise of its origi-\nnal jurisdiction. In general, it can be stated that most suits for money or\nmonetary claims (excepting claims in tort) enjoy a period of three years, and\nmost suits relating to tort are governed by periods of one year, two years or\nthree years. Suits for the recovery of immovable property (and on allied\ncauses of action) enjoy a period of twelve years, with certain exceptions; the\nmost important exception in this context is the period of thirty years, allowed\nfor a suit for redemption or foreclosure of a mortgage under articles 6l(a) and\n<a href=\"/doc/694525/\" id=\"a_50\">63(a)</a>, respectively. The residuary article for suits (article ll3) allows a period\nof three years for suits not otherwise speci\ufb01cally provided for.\n\n1.13. The starting point of limitation (subject to certain exceptions) as\nregards suits, is in general, the date of accrual of the cause of action. How-\never, this concept has not been expressed in any general terms\u00ab--such as &quot;accrual\nof the cause of action&quot;: rather. it has been translated into a date linked up\nwith some specific and concrete act or event which is appropriate for the\nparticular type of cause of action to which the suit relates. In some exceptio-\nnal cases, the date selected as the starting point is not one corresponding to the\naccrual of the cause of action. but some other date which is more appropriate\nfor the particular type of claim. For example, in a suit relating to accounts.\nbeing a claim for the balance due on what is described as a &quot;mutual, open and\ncurrent account&quot;. where there have been reciprocal demands between the parties.\nthe starting point under article l is the close of the year when the last item ad-\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/206324/\" id=\"a_51\">Section 1-</a>\nShort title,\nextent and\n\ncommencement.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/103447/\" id=\"a_52\">Section 2</a> ~--\nDe\ufb01nitions.\n\n89TH Rl-&#x27;l&#x27;(&gt;l{T t)l&#x27; LAW LUMMN. ()l5 INDIA ON THE LlMII&#x27;AT&#x27;lQlj ACT, \n\n(Chapter /~--I/1/i&#x27;u(2&#x27;i(t&#x27;/013&#x27;, C/rapier 2---Seclfolzs /and 2; Pre//&#x27;n11&#x27;n(1ry)\n\nmitted or proved is entered in the account. Similarly, for a suit for the wages\nof a seaman. the starting point under article () is the end of the voyage during\nwhich the wages are earned. Again, under <a href=\"/doc/883495/\" id=\"a_53\">articles 73</a> and <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_54\">74</a>, which are con-\ncerned with compensation [or false imprisonment and compensation for mali-\ncious prosccution respectively. the starting point is termination of the false im-\n\n9 prisonment or of the malicious prosecution, as the case may be. Then. under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1501881/\" id=\"a_55\">article 81</a>, a suit by executors etc. under the <a href=\"/doc/1057059/\" id=\"a_56\">Legal Representatives Suits Act,\n1855</a> can be tiled within one year of the date of the death of the person wronged.\nFinally, in several cases. the knowledge of the plznntill&#x27; as to the accrual of the\ncause of action is material for determining the starting point. Examples of\nsuch an approach are furnished by <a href=\"/doc/1015123/\" id=\"a_57\">articles 4</a><a href=\"/doc/1408921/\" id=\"a_58\">, 56</a><a href=\"/doc/950729/\" id=\"a_59\">, 57</a><a href=\"/doc/858095/\" id=\"a_60\">, 59</a>, bltb), 68, 71. 84, 92 to\n95 and 102.\n\n1.l4. We shall advert to this point at the appropriate place?&#x27;\n\nCHAPTER 5\n\nSECTION 5: EXTENSION OF THE PRESCRIBED PERIOD FOR\nSUFFICIENT CAUSE\n\n5.]. <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_79\">Section 5</a> empowers the court, for &quot;suf\ufb01cient cause&quot;, to entertain an\nappeal or application (except an application for execution) \ufb01led after expiry of\nthe prescribed period. A number of points require to be considered with\nreference to this section. The section does not apply to suits.\n\nI. Suits\n\n5.2. <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_80\">Section 5</a>. as stated above. does not apply to suits. We have consi-\ndered the question whether the section should be amended so as to include\nwithin its ambit suits \ufb01led after the prescribed period. Delving into the old\nrecords relating to the drafting of the Indian Limitation Bill. 1908. we dis-\ncovered that this question was considered at that distance of time also. When\nthe draft of the Indian Limitation Bill 1908 was circulated for comments. Dr.\nHari Singh Gaur commented&#x27; as under:\n\n&quot;I am not sure if a suit may not be also included in the clause: if\nthe intending plaintifl is wrongfully con\ufb01ned b_v the defendant. why he\nshould lose his suit&#x27;? <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_81\">Section 18</a> &#x27;fraud&#x27; does not cover such a case.&quot;\n\nHowever. the Divisional Judge, Nagpur.&#x27; was apprehensive that if the ambit\nof the clause was enlarged. it would lead to unsavoury practices: false grounds\nwere often invented when the litigant found that he was out of time. In most\ncases they were. accordingly to him. based on purely personal incidents or\nconditions which his adversary was not e\\pected to refute. &#x27;\n\n5.3. Though there is some merit in Dr. Hari Singh Gour&#x27;s suggestion. we\n\n-think that enlarging the scope of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_82\">section 5</a> to cover suits would do more harm\n\nthan good to the administration of justice. The rapport between the lawyer\nand his rural client is generally so well established that a visit to the family\nlawyer on the weekly market day is always on the agenda of a villager. Such\nbeing the style of functioning of village folks. it is improbable that the munshi\nto the lawyer would allow his client&#x27;s case to go by default by asking him\nto wait till the last day of limitation. If. as contemplated by Dr. Hari Singh\nGour. a plaintiff may be prevented from reaching his lawyers on the last day\nof limitation by scheming defenda&#x27;nts. he could as well as prevented by other\n\n&#x27;CF. Applications\n\n5.4. Regarding the applicability of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_85\">section 5</a> to applications. the law as -&#x27;\\PDliC3li0&quot;5--\nrevised in 1963 is wide enough. Erroneous legal advice\n\n5.5. In <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_88\">Section 5</a>. the e\\&#x27;pre.~:s&#x27;ion &quot;sullicient cause&quot; for not preferring an Erroneous\nappeal or making an application within the prescribed period has been ititer-lfdg\ufb01lw\npreted by the Supreme Court.&#x27; to include lawyers&#x27; ignorance about the law\nregarding calculation of the period of limitation. In that case. the High Court\nhad refused to condone the delay and to admit the Company&#x27;s appeal. but the\n\nSupreme Court&quot;. reversing the judgment of the High Court. observed as under:\n\n&quot;A Company relies on its Legal Adviser and the Manager&#x27;s expertise\nis in company management. and not in law. There is no particular reason\nwhy. when a company or other person retains a lawyer to advise it or\nhim on legal affairs. reliance should not be placed on such counsel.&quot;\n\n5.6. The principle. is in fact. well established. At the same time. courts :{a\u20acr\u20ac:50&quot;\ufb02bl\u00a2\nhave been circumspect in scrutinising the claim of a party that he was misled cxpiacjed\nby a wrong legal advice. For example. a plea was made before the Madhya*&quot;&quot; C*&quot;&quot;&quot;\u00b0l-\nPradesli High Court&#x27; by a counsel that he had relied on a Full Bench ruling oi&#x27;\nthat High Court under the old Act. The Court refused to countenance such an\nargument. when. in point of time. the new Act had come into force a year\nbefore the event. Similarly. the Delhi High Court&quot; rejected the plea for con-\ndonation of delay. when it was argued that the appellant acted upon the advice\nof an eminent counsel who lnul. in computing limitation. relied on an overruled\njudgment of that Court.\n\n5.7. The fact that the appellant was misguided by the wrong advice given\nby his legal adviser. has not. alone and by itself. been held to be tantamount to\n&quot;sutlicient&quot; cause within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_89\">section 5</a>. The Courts have inquired that\nthe lawyer who gave the opinion exercised reasonable care. In other words.\nthe advice should have been the result of a bona \ufb01de mistake not attributable to\nnegligence or want of skill and the view taken by the lawyer was such as would\nhave been entertained by a competent person exercising reasonable skill&quot;. The scope of the expression &quot;su\ufb02icient cause&quot; in relation to lapse\non the part of a counsel came up for consideration before the Supreme Court1\nagain recently, A Senior Sub-Judge of Narnaul had been authorised by the\nHigh Court to hear appeals, but as the counsel was&#x27; ignorant of this fact,\nhe had \ufb01led the appeal in the court of the Additional District Judge in 1960.\nln 1961, when the appeal came up for hearing, the objection regarding want of\njurisdiction was sustained and the memorandum of appeal was returned to the\nappellants. The appellants liled the memorandum before the Senior Sub-Judge,\nthe proper appellate Court and prayed for condonation of the delay of 185\ndays, being the period of pendency of the appeal in the court of the Additional\nDistrict Judge. Apart from the aspect of etfect of negligence. there is another aspect Limits of\nto be considered. The &#x27;su\ufb02icicnt cause&#x27; must have arisen before expiry of the?,lT\u00b0f,f\u00b0if&#x27;!&#x27;\u00b0*=Pt 01\nperiod of limitation. E23563-l_\u00b0nt\n\nA peculiar situation arose in a case tfroni Gujarat&#x27;. In which an appeal was\npreferred under the Supreme Court (linlargement of Criminal Jurisdiction) Act,\n1970 against the judgment and order of the High Court of Gujarat, setting aside\nthe acquittal of the appellant and convicting and sentencing him for serious\noffences under section 302, read with section 34. <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_101\">Indian Penal Code</a> and under\nsection 326, read with section 34 of that Code. The only fact of relevance\nto the present inquiry is that the appeal to the High Court was \ufb01led three months\nafter the period of limitation had expired and this delay was condoned by the\nHigh Court. The Supreme Court, however, did not countenance this action\nof the High Court ol condoning the delay of three months under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_102\">section 5</a> of the\n\nLimitation Act and observed:\n\n&quot;It appears that initially the State Government took a decision not to \ufb01le\nappeal and it allowed the period of limitation to liilpsc&#x27;. Subsequently, on\ncertain observations made by the High Court while considering a revision\npetition by Bhulabhai that it was a lit case where the State Government\nshould \ufb01le an appeal and on notice being issued by the High Court to the\nState Government in the matter. the appeal was tiled. It was /ilcd three\nmonths after liIIIlI(lli()Il hail t&#x27;,\\&#x27;[)iI&#x27;(&#x27;(l. A faint attempt was maa&#x27;e 10 show\nthat when the initial dcoisimz was taken not to file an appeal all the papers\nhad not been considcrul by the department (&#x27;()IIC(&#x27;I&#x27;IlL&#x27;(/, but we are not im-\npressed by that allegatimz. The truth appears to 13 that the appeal was not\n\ufb01led at \ufb01rst because the State Government saw no case on the merits for\nan appeal, and it was \ufb01led only because the High Court had observed----\nand that was long after limitation had expired that the case was \ufb01t for\nappeal by the State Government. Now. it is true that a party is entitled to\nwait until the last day of limitation for \ufb01ling an appeal. But when it allows\nlimitation to expire and pleads sut\ufb01cient cause for not \ufb01ling the appeal ear-\nlier, the sufficient cause must establish that because of some event or cir-\ncumstances arising before limitation expired it was not possible to \ufb01le the\nappeal within time. No event or circumstance arising after the expiry of\nlimitation can constitute such sullicient cause. There may be events or cir-\ncumstances subsequent to the expiry of limitation which may further delay\nthe \ufb01ling of the appeal. But. that the limitation has been allowed to expire\n\n&#x27;Badlu V. Shiv Clmrutt. (1930) 4 S.(&#x27;.(i. 401-\n3/ljit sing]: Thu/cur .S&#x27;in_e/I v. State of (lujurnt. /\\.1.R&#x27;\n\n495, 497,\n\n1081 S.C. 733, (1981) 1. In the present case. there was no such cause, and\nthe High Court erred in condoning the delay.&quot;\n\nThe Supreme Court. has in the observations quoted above. underscored an\nimportant aspect of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_103\">section 5</a>. by stating that the &quot;su\ufb02ieient cause&quot; must be\ntraced to a cause arising within the period of limitation.\n\n5.12. We have, on an examination oi&#x27; all aspects ol the matter, come to the\nconclusion that the position on the subject of legal advice given erroneously\nneeds to be codi\ufb01ed. However. it would make for a better appreciation ol evidence\nand merits of a claim under this section it&#x27; the court has before it, in black\nand white, the advice given by counsel.\n\nWhile stressing the importance of a written legal advice, we are not oblivious\nof the fact that the regulation of the legal profession has been entrusted by the\n<a href=\"/doc/262262/\" id=\"a_104\">Advocates Act, 1961</a>, to the Bar Council of India. Hence. we recommend&#x27; that\nthe Central Government should request the Bar (&#x27;ouncil of India to frame rules\nmaking it obligatory upon an advocate to tender a written legal advice in\nmatters concerning limitation. We hope that the Bar Council of India would\nappreciate the necessity of such a rule when the fate of an otherwise good\nclaim hangs on the technical plea of limitation.\n\nWe are recommending a suitable Iixplanation to be inserted below <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_105\">section 5</a>\non the subject of erroneous legal advice&#x27;. The draft that we propose will show,\nin a concrete form. the salient features.\n\nIV. Peculiar disabilities of married women\n\n5.l3. We now turn to a slightly dillcrcnt topic namely. the position of\nmarried women. Section XII of Limitation Act No. I4 of 185&#x27;) (legal tlisability)\nprovided as under : --\n\n&quot;XII. In England.\nthe <a href=\"/doc/21156329/\" id=\"a_106\">Married Women&#x27;s Property Act</a> abolished the common law rule and made\nmarried women&#x27;s wages and earnings her separate property and gave her a\npower to recover them in her own name.\n\nAs far as Hindu or Muslim women in India were concerned. they never\nsu\ufb01ered from the disability ol&#x27; prohibition lrom acquiring _propcrt_v*, For others\nappropriate provision has been enacted.\n\n5.14. No doubt. there are certain procedural di\ufb02iculties of a married woman\nin seeking legal redress today. The prevalence of the dowrv svstem and certain\nother anomalies have brought to surface some ol the lrantlicaps of the married\nwomen. While we fully appreciate the dif\ufb01eulties faced by married women\n(especially from the rural parts of India). in obtaining legal advice. we have\n\n&#x27;For implementation by the (&#x27;entral Government.\n2See para 5.l4. infra.\n\n3See Law Commission of India, 66th Report (Married Womcn&#x27;s Property Act I874)\n\n\n\n89lI*I Rl;l&#x27;()R&#x27;l 01&#x27; Li\\\\\\&#x27; C().\\l.\\l.&#x27;-V. Limitation Act. 1939 (so far as is material)\nreads as under:--\n\n&quot;If on the date when any right of action accrued for which a period\nof limitation is prescribed. b_v this Act. the person to whom it accrued was\nunder a disability. the action ma_v be brought at any time before the ex-\npiration of six years. or in the case of actions to which the last foregoing\nsection applies, one year from the date when the person ceased to be under\na disability or died. whichever event \ufb01rst occurred. notwithstanding that the\nperiod of limitation has expired.&quot;\n\nWhile commenting on the special provision regarding limitation for infants.\nHalsbury has observed&#x27; that the provision is a saving clause which does not. of\nitself impose a disability and the plainti\ufb02&quot;. while under disability. may bring\nhis action in the same way as if the Act had not been passed. and may also do\nso within the statutory period after determination of the disability.\n\n7.7 ln regard to the American Law (&#x27;or/)1t.v ./nris Sam/Id:/nz states the\nposition thus: &#x27;\n\n&quot;The saving of the position of limitation by reason of disabilities depends\non the statute as it existed at the time the cause of action accrued. In the\nabsence of such a saving clause the statute runs against all persons whether\nor not they are under disability&quot;.\n\nand this statement of the law has been quoted in various cases.\nHowever. it is observed :\n\n&quot;The statute begins to run against persons under disability as soon as the\ndisability is removed. but. unless a statute prescribed the maximum period\nthat limitations may be tolled by disabilities. limitations do not begin to\nrun until the disability is removed&quot;.\n\nA reading of the above shows that even in American jurisdictions the ex&#x27;-\npression &quot;limitations do not begin to run&quot; has been understood to mean suspen-\nsion of the running of the time. even though that running of time started earlier.\neven against the person under disability and the expression has not been under-\nstood to mean that the starting point of limitation it.sell&#x27; is postponed.\n\n7.8. EXCEPTIONS TO THE PROVISIONS CONCERN-\n&#x27; ING DISABILITY\n\n8.1. <a href=\"/doc/996339/\" id=\"a_137\">Section 8</a> provides that the provisions concerning disability (sections Gspecm\n\nand 7) do not apply to suits to enforce rights of pre-emption. nor shall be %&#x27;1&#x27;:\u00b0\u00a5&#x27;:i(;&quot;f;5.3:\ndeemed to extend, for more than three years from the cessation of the dis-concgrnin\u00e9l S\nability of the death of the person affected thereby, the period of limitation of disability-\nany suit or application. The section thus (i) totally excludes from the bene\ufb01cial\n\nprovisions suits to enforce right of pre--emption. and (ii) puts an arithmetical\n\nlimit on the extent to which the bene\ufb01cial provisions relating to disability can\n\noperate. The \ufb01rst exclusion is limited to one category of suits, while the\n\nsecond applies to all suits and applications.\n\nThe exclusion of suits for pre-emption appears to be based on a legislative\npolicy of not giving any relaxation to such suits. presumably in order that tran-\nsactions concerning immovable property may not be kept in suspense for a\nlong time&#x27;. This is fact is the rationale underlying the requirement generally\nmet with in rules of the substantive law concerning pre-emption. to the effect\nthat the &quot;demand&quot; for pre-emption should be made immediately after the sale.\n\nThe second restriction imposed by <a href=\"/doc/996339/\" id=\"a_138\">section 8</a> obviously shows legislative\nanxiety to maintain a balance between the consideration that stale demands should\nbe discouraged and the counter-balancing consideration that the law of limitation\nought not to operate in such a way as to cause serious hardship in individual\ncases.\n\n8.2. The section has not caused any serious&quot; difficulties or controversies. and Nodcclginge\n. I&#x27;!\ndoes not seem to require change. 56\n\nCHAPTER 9 I\nSECTION 9 : CONTINUOUS RUNNING OF TIME\n\n9.]. The general principle that where time has begun once to run, no sub- <a href=\"/doc/268914/\" id=\"a_139\">Section 9-</a>---\nsequent disability or inability to institute a suit or make an application stops it.rCu&#x27;:$\u00a7:;&#x27;\u00b0:;\nis incorporated in <a href=\"/doc/268914/\" id=\"a_140\">section 9</a>. There is a proviso to the section. very limited in its time.\noperation. to. the effect that where letters of administration to the estate of a\ncreditor have been granted to his debtor. the running of the period of limitation\nfor a suit to recover the debt is suspended while the administration continues.\n\nThe proviso becomes necessary since the same person cannot be both the plain-\n\ntiff and the defendant in the same suit.\n\n&#x27; 9.2. The section does not seem to need any change. :6: d&#x27;\u00a31a&quot;3\u00b0\n\n&#x27;cf. <a href=\"/doc/1019641/\" id=\"a_141\">section 16</a>( 3).\n4----l4 Mof LJ&amp;CA]ND!83\n\n&#x27;I\n\n\n\n18\n\nSection l0---\nSuits against\ntrustees and\n\ntheir representi-\n\ntives.\n\nHistory of\nthe main\nparagraph.\n\nHistory\n\n-- of the\n\nExplanation.\n\n89TH REPORT or I.\\w co\\mN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n((&#x27;/mpI(&#x27;r In S&#x27;w&#x27;liu1I I()----~SuiI.v Agrzimt Trllsmar)\n\nCHAPTER 10\nSECTION 10 : SUITS AGAINST TRUSTEES\n\n10.1. <a href=\"/doc/541605/\" id=\"a_142\">Section 10</a> is one of those very rare statutory provisions which grant\na total exemption from limitation for particular suits. In brief, it provides that\nCertain &#x27;suits against a person in whom property has become vested in trust\nfor any speci\ufb01c purpose are not barred by &quot;any length of time&quot;. The same prin-\nciple applies to suits against the legal representatives of the trustee or against\nassigns from the trustee. not being: assigns for valuable consideration. The\nsuits to which the section applies are suits for the purpose of following. in\\ the\nhands of the trustee. or his legal representatives or assigns. such property or&#x27; the\nproceeds thereof. or for an account of such property or proceeds. There is_a\nVery important Explanation to the section. which has a long historical back-\nground.&#x27; it reads as under:\n\n&quot;Explanatirm.~--For the purposes of this section. any property compri-\nsed in a Hindu. l\\/liuslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment shall\nbe deemed to be property vested in trust for a speci\ufb01c purpose and the\nmanager of the property shall be deemed to be the trustee thereof.&quot;\n\n10.2. The main paragraph of the section is based on early decision and\ncertain stattitory provisions.&quot; But it is more elaborately worded than the earlier\nEnglish provision on the subiect. The current English statutory provision on the\nsubject is. of course, fairly elaborate?-&#x27;\n\n10.3. The Explanation. of course. does not occur in the English statutory\nprovisions. It was, in fact. not contained even in Indian Law before 1929. It\nwas inserted as the second paragraph of <a href=\"/doc/541605/\" id=\"a_143\">section 10</a> of the Act of 1908 by the\nIndian Limitation (Amendment) Act. 1929 (1 of l929i to override certain judi-\n\ncial pronouncements. These were decisions of the Privy Council relating tot\nHindu religious endowments as well as Muslim religious endowments. &quot;7 The&#x27;\n\nPrivy Council held that property dedicated to God (in the Case of Hindu en-\ndowments) did not vest in the shebait. who was merely a Manager though. by\ncustom. he might have certain bene\ufb01cial interest.\n\nIn law. the property vested in the idol or institution. as the case may be.\nSimilarly. under Muslim law. the moment of waqf was created. the property\nvested in God Almighty and the mutawalli or Sajjadenashin had no right in the\nproperty. He was not a &#x27;trustee&#x27; in the technical sense.&#x27; Accordingly. the bene\ufb01t\nof the provision in <a href=\"/doc/541605/\" id=\"a_144\">section 10</a> of the Act of 1908 was not available against such\npersons. It was to override these pronouncements that the relevant paragraph\nwas added in <a href=\"/doc/541605/\" id=\"a_145\">section 10</a> of the Act of l908~---a provision which has been carried\nover in the present Act.\n\n&#x27;cf. The section is based on the principle well recognised in private inter-\nnational law in the Anglo-American Legal Systemsmthat rules of limitation are Principle-\npart of the [ex fort&#x27;. Whether an obligation is to be enforced or not depends ex-\nclusively upon the law of limitation of the forum in which the suit is brought.\n\nAs observed by Story very long ago.&#x27;\n\n&quot;it has accordingly become a formularly in international jurisprudence\nthat all suits must be brought within the period prescribed by the local\nlaw of the country where the suit is brought (Icx fori). otherwise the suit\nwill be dismissed.&quot; A\n\nIn contrast. the foreign law of /1n&#x27;.\\&#x27;r&#x27;rip(1&#x27;()rl would be applied in a country.\nbecause when a law extinguishes the right by reason of lapse of time. the right\nitself does not survive, and there is nothing to enforce.\n\n11.3. It may be mentioned that in proceedings brought in national courts P()51&#x27;\ufb010]!\nto enforce rights created by the Treaty of Rome (which established the Euro-:&#x27;l:u;;&#x27;r \npean Economic Community), the relevant limitation period is that fixed by them&#x27; Rome,\n\nState in which the action is brought?\n\n11.4. The traditional English rule is well settled. The rule has also been incorporated as Rule 004 of the Restatement of\n\nLaw of the American Law lnstitute thus.&quot;\n\nRule 604--F0reign Statute of Limitations\n\n&quot;ll action is not barred by the statute ol limitatioiis of the forum. an\naction can be iilaiiitaiiiedtliough action is barred in the State where the\ncause of action arose.&quot; &#x27;\n\n11.6. Cheshire and North have observed&#x27;, criticising the English approach\n\nas under ;--\n\n&quot;English law in unfortunately committed to the view that statutes of limita-\ntion, if they merely specify a certain time after which rights cannot be en-\nforced by action. affect procedure, not substance&#x27; .........  .... ..In the result,\ntherefore, any relevant statute of limitation that obtains in the lexfori may be\npleaded while a statute of same foreign. law, even though it belongs to the\nproper law of the transaction, must be disregarded.&#x27; The prevailing view on\nthe continent is to be opposite cjjcct.\n\n&quot;This is another cxanipte where English law. through its failure to in-\nterpret a foreign rule in its context has gone astray&quot;.\n\n11.7. The U.K. Law Reform Committee in its 2|st Report&#x27; teok notice of\n\nthis criticism, but did not make any recommendation in this regard:\n\n&quot;As we have explained. English law treats limitation as part of the law\nof procedure. An English court will, therefore, always apply English law\nIex fort&#x27;) &#x27;to an issue of limitation, notwithstanding that the substantive rights\nin question are governed by foreign law.&#x27; In determining the existence of\nthose substantive rights a distinction has to be drawn between a foreign rule\nof limitation and a foreign rule of prescription: if the &#x27;proper law&#x27; of the\ntransaction (Iex causae) extinguishes the right through lapse of time, the\nEnglish court will give effect to that extinction if. on the other hand, under\nthe relevant lex causae lapse of time merely bars the remedy (as it does in\nmost cases in English law). the English courts will ignore the foreign limita-\ntion period and apply English law alone.\n\nlDon v. Nevertheless\nwe received from Dr. F. A. Manna memorandum arguing persuasively in\nfavour of making a change in the English rule (which has been heavily criti-\ncised by academic writers)&#x27; and we think it right to mention the matter so\nthat it may be considered as a possiblesubject for reform.&quot;\n\n11.8. Besides Cheshire another team of distinguished authors?\n\nhave also (.1-nit i~m--\n\nSykes and\n\nattacked this principle of English law thus:\n\n&quot;Statutes of limitation in the familiar sense usually prescribe that no court\nproceeding shall be brought to enforce a right after the lapse of a certain\nperiod of time. Sometimes however they provide that the right or title is ex-\ntinguished.&quot; It is only statutes of the latter type that the English classi\ufb01ca-\ntory technique regards as pertaining to substance. The other type of statute\nof limitations which merely extinguishes the remedy, is regarded as proce-\ndural.&#x27;\n\n&quot;This means that a plaintiff can sue in an Australian court on a cause\nof action which, though barred by the proper law of the transaction. is not\nbarred by Australian lawzi it also means that an action cannot be brought\nin Australia if barred by an Australian statute of limitations. though action\nwould still be competent by the proper law&quot;.\n\n&quot;It is suggested that such applications rest on no intelligible principle.\nnor is the distinction between extinction of right and extinction of remedy\nmeaningful in this context. A statute can hardly be regarded as prescribing\na mode of regulating the course of litigation if it simply says that no litiga~\ntion can take place.&quot;\n\nHowever, they add-\n\n&quot;Nevertheless. the tide of English decisions has probably set so \ufb01rmly\nin the direction of the dichotomy between extinction of right and extinction\nof remedy hat no movement is now possible.&quot;\n\n11.9. We have devoted considerable thought to this aspect. With great res:\nneet. the criticism made by Cheshire of the traditional English Rule (which has\nbeen &#x27;substantially followed in section ll), appears to be based on one faulty con-\ncept of reasoning. The law of limitation may not be a part of the law of &quot;proce-\ndure&quot; in the narrower sense. but it is certainly a part of adjective law in the widest\nsense. The criticism does not do justice to the juristic aspect mentioned above.\nProcedure may be a narrow \ufb01eld. but adjective law is a wider one. covering the\nentire process of litigation. including the time and manner of pursuing a remedy\nin the Courts of a country. It is but fair that the courts operating within a coun-\ntry should have regard only to the law of that country. so far as the pursuit of\n\n&#x27;E2. .l.D. Falconbridge. Limitation is certainly concerned with the\nactual process of litigation; the need&#x27;for invoking a rule of limitation arises only\nif and when the plaintiff invokes the aid of the judicial process. In this sense.\n\nlimitation is very much concerned with the enforcement of the remedy within&#x27;\n\nthe portale of the court and is therefore rightly regarded as belonging to adjec-\ntive law.\n\nWe may quote the views of an American author&#x27; who has beautifully des-\ncribed the distinction between remedy and right:----\n\n&quot;A broken promise is not repaired by the passage of a period. False\nWords that stain a name are not made true because twelve months go by.\nPain may recede in memory, but that does not mean it was not sufferred\nwhen the victim was assaulted. If these things are recognised as deserving\nreparation, they deserve it no less the day after the term is reached than\nthey&#x27;did the day before. Time does not heal all wounds; homily is fatuous\n\n. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . - . ..\n\n&quot;Thus, limitation appears to be not a matter of substantive right, but a\npractical device. It is difficult to deal with an event long after it has hap-\npened. Memories fade. witnesses die. documents vanish. proof one way or\nthe other is hard to find. Moreover. even with all the evidence at hand. the\noutcome of a law suit will not necessarily coincide with truth. Much de-\npends on the hazards of litigation. on the wisdom and honesty of judges\nand jurors, the luck of testimony. Hence it is only reasonable that the alle-\nged wrong doer (in most instances. we will not be certain he did wrong)\nshould one day be entitled to repose. Finally---most important and scarcely\never mentioned~if all disputes were kept alive forever. then there would\nbe an in\ufb01nity of litigation. In the management of a polity there needs to be\nan end of things.&quot; &#x27;\n\n11.10. It has been stated by the critics of the traditional rule that &quot;a statute\ncan hardly be regarded as describing a mode of regulating the course of litiga-\ntion. if it simply says that no litigation can take place.&quot; However. this criticism\nis itself open to criticism. The procedural law contains so many rules which bar\nthe very entertaining of a suit on procedural grounds and nobody has ever said\nthat such rules are not regarded as falling appropriately within the domain of\nprocedure. Our own <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_152\">Code of Civil Procedure</a> contains examples of such rules in\n<a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_153\">section 11</a>, section 47. section 66. section 80, section 86. Order 2. Rule 2. Order\n7. Rule ll, Order 9. Rule 8. Order 23. Rule l and so on.\n\n11.11. Apart from these theoretical aspects. it should also be mentioned that\nthere is an important practical aspect which cannot be overlooked. When a court\nembarks upon an inquiry into the period of limitation. such an inquiry covers\nnot merely the time limits proper (the arithmetical periods). but also various\ndetailed rules for the computation of those periods. lf we were to expect courts\nto have regard to foreign law of limitation. that would mean calling upon the\n\ncourt to &#x27;study. understand and apply the foreign law in all its complexity and\n\nprofusion. This would be an extremely di\ufb01cult task.\n\n11.12. For all these reasons. we have been unable to persuade ourselves to\naccept the criticism made by Cheshire and others? of the traditional English\nRule~a rule which has been adopted in the American Restatement also.&quot;\n\n&#x27;Charles Rembar. The Law of the Land (1980) page 81.\n5&#x27;Para 11.6 and 11.8. .mpra_\n&quot;Para 1l_5, supra.\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMVN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATIONIACT, 1963 25\n((Vmp!er Il~~S&#x27;c(-rim: I/I --Sm&#x27;1.s on Foreign causes of Action.)\n\n11.13} We may add that on grounds of policy also, a court ought not to be p_o1i\u00a2y_\u00a2on-\nbound by the rules of limitation applicable under a foreign [ex causae. The time &quot;d\u00b0&quot;at1&#x27;!&quot;3-\nlimit may be either unreasonably short or unreasonably long:\n\n11.14. It is not always an easy task to ascertain foreign law. This could D_if\ufb01culty&gt;\nprove an onerous burden on the courts. The burden would be all the more one- \u00a7fga\u00a7\u00a7:g;&#x27;n&quot;&#x27;\nrous for the Indian courts to ascertain the foreign law of limitation of I50 coun- rule.\ntries by paying for the fees and costs of foreign experts which would unneces-\nsarily delay the disposal of cases. It should be remembered that, as mentioned\nabove, if the foreign law is to be applied, the Indian court will have to study\nnot only the time limits, but also the principles for the computation of periods\nof limitation. This would be a stupendous undertaking.\n\n11.15. Therefpre, we see no reason to disturb the principle underlying sec- Suits on\ntion 11. Some matters of detail concerning the section may now be dealt wi\ufb021.\u00a7\u00a7&#x27;if)\u00b0If gtfhcr\nThough sub-section (2) of section fl is con\ufb01ned to contracts. the position would than contracts.\nnot, in substance, be different for other causes of action that arise outside the\n\n_ territories to which the Act extends.\n\nThe section has been held not to be exhaustive.&#x27; Thus, for example, the\nsame principle applies to execution? Again, if a suit is for conversion, the same\nprinciple would apply.&#x27;\n\n11.16. In this context, the history of the section is interesting. Section 12 of History of\n\nthe Act of 1871 provided as under:------  gem\nthe point of\n&quot;12. No foreign rule of limitation shall be a defence to a suit in British View Of\n\nIndia on a contract entered into a foreign country, unless the rule has ex-s\u00b0\u00b0p\u00b0&#x27;\ntinguished the contract. and the parties were domiciled in such country\nduring the period prescribed by such rule.&quot;\n\nIn 1877. a positive assertion about the applicability of the Indian Act to\nsuits instituted in British India was made. Section II of that Act read as under-:--\u00ab\n\n_ &quot;ll. Suits instituted in British India on contracts entered into a foreign\ncountry are subject to the rules prescribed by this Act.\n\nNo foreign rule of limitation shall be_a defence to a suit instituted in &#x27;\nBritish India on a contract entered into a foreign country, unless the rule has\nextinguisl1ed~the contract, and the parties were domiciled in such country\nduring the period prescribed by such rule.&quot;\n\n11.17. OF INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n(Chapter, /1 --Se&lt;&#x27;/irm /1\u00bb ~----Suits on Foreign causes 0]&#x27; Action.)\n\naction in respect of which arises in foreign country, are subject to these\nprovisions.&quot; A\n\nThis is an interesting comment raising as it does a point which we our-\nselves have apart from the comment pursued.\n\n11.18. To revert to the present section and current needs, we wish to point\nout that the principle of private international law upon which the section is\nbased, makes no distinction between suits based on contracts and other suits.\nPractical considerations also justify a widening of the section so as to make it\napplicable to all causes of action. There is no reason for adopting a di\ufb01erent\napproach as to non-contractual causes of action. We think that the section should\nbe widened.&#x27; &#x27;\n\n11.19. There is another point in regard to which also the ambit of the sec-\ntion can be conveniently widened. The section, as it stands at present, is con-\n\ufb01ned to suits; we are of the view that it should be extended to all proceedings.\nAlthough proceedings other than suits wheteinthe question of competition bet-\nween&#x27; Indian and foreign laws of limitation may be in issue are not many, there\n\nis no reason why the legislature should not recognise the principle underlying\n\n<a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_154\">section 11</a> and give it the widest amplitude. Irrespective of the character of the\nproceeding, if the remedy sought is not barred by the law of limitation of the\nforum, then in that forum the law should allow the proceeding to be maintained,\neven though it may be barred by the law of limitation of .the country where the\ncause of action for the particular proceeding arose, The principle on which\n<a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_155\">section 11</a> is based----- a matter which has been already elaborated in the preceding\npa1&#x27;agrap&#x27;nsi--5should be regarded as equally applicable, whether the litigation is\nin the shape of a suit or in the shape of any other proceeding. Accordingly, we\npropose a widening of the section on this point also?\n\n11.20. One more change needed in <a href=\"/doc/1267250/\" id=\"a_182\">Section 12</a> concerns tribunals. We are\n\nof the opinion&#x27; that the section should, by an express provision, be extended\n\nto quasi-judicial bodies, since there is no reason why the time taken in the\nvarious steps contemplated by .the section should not be available in regard tp\nappeals before such tribunals. It is desirable that the matter should be governed\nby a speci\ufb01c provision applicable to all such tribunals rather than be left to be\ndealt with on a case--to-case basis.\n\n12.12. There is yet another point (though a minor one) on which Section\nThe marginal note to <a href=\"/doc/1267250/\" id=\"a_183\">section 12</a> speaks of &quot;Exclu-\nsion of time in legal proceedings&quot;, and the expression &quot;legal proceedings&quot;\nshould be given awide meaning so as to include proceedings before tribunals.\nThis would mean amending the section. If <a href=\"/doc/1267250/\" id=\"a_184\">section 12</a> is to be amended so as\n\nto put it beyond doubt that the section is applicable to proceedings before tri-_\n\nbunals,&#x27; it would be better to amend the marginal note also. This is so because\nwhere the marginal note has not been amended despite the addition of matter\nin the section, sometimes controversies arisef Such controversies are worth\navoiding wherever possible.\n\n12.13. In the light of the above discussion, we recommend the following\namendments to section 121--\n\n(i) The marginal note to the section should be revised as under:---- *\n\n&quot;Exclusion of time in legal proceedings, including proceedings before\nquasi-judicial tribunal.&#x27; V\n\n(ii) The present Explanation should be revised, and certain further Expla-\nnations should be added. The Explanations to appear before <a href=\"/doc/1267250/\" id=\"a_185\">section 12</a> will\nnow read as under : --\n\n&quot;Expla&#x27;uatioh I. --~----Any time taken by the court to prepare the decree\nor order before an application for a copy thereof is made shall not be re-\ngarded as time requisite for obtaining the copy within the meaning of this\nsection?&#x27; &#x27;\n\nExplanati0n ll.~-~--Whcre there is a legal impcditltc/It to the prepara-\ntion of a decree or order on account of a direction in the judgment or non-\ncompliance with the direction or for any other legally permissible reason,\nany time during which the decree or order cannot be prepared on account\nof such legal impediment shall be regarded as time requisite f0r obtaining\nthe copy within the meaning of this section.&#x27;\n\n&quot;Explanation III .--F or the purposes of this section, &#x27;decree&#x27; includes the\nlast paragraphrof the judgment, where it is proposed to use a copy thereof for\nthe purpose permitted by rule 6A of Order XX in the First Schedule to\n<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_186\">Code of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908.&quot;\n\n&quot;Explanation I V.~\u00abFor the removal of. doubts, it is hereby declared\nthat the provisions of this section apply to proceedings before quasi-judicial\ntribunals, as they apply to proceedings before courts.&quot;&#x27;\n\n&#x27;Paragraph 12.11. supra.\n\n2Tara Prasad Singh etc. v. Union of India &amp; others (1981) l S.C.J. 53, 67.\n\n-&#x27;Paragraph 12.12 supra.\n\n&quot;Paragraph 12.9 supra.\n\n~&quot;Paragraph 12.8 supra.\n\n&quot;Paragraph 12.10 supra.\n\n&quot;Paragraph 12.11 supra.\nI\n\n\n\n:\n89TH REPORT or IAW comm. or INDIA on rnr LIMITATION ACT. 1963\n(Chapter l3----\u00bb--Sn-lion l3---- Ext Imiun of IfII&#x27;It&quot; whvre Imw In .\\&#x27;lI\u00a2&#x27; or nppzwl\nus pzmpcr is applied for\n\nCHAPTER 13\n\n\\\n\nSECTION 13: EXCLUSION OF TIME WHERE\nLEAVE T0 SUE OR APPEAL AS PAUPER IS\nAPPLIED FOR\n\n13.] Section l3 deals with at tel} special case; provision is made for ex-\ncluding time taken in prosecuting an application for leave to sue or appeal as\na &quot;pauper&quot;. where ultimately such leave is refused, and the &quot;pauper&quot;, has then\nto file a suit or appeal as an ordinary person. The section was introduced _in\n\n1963. It was not contained in the original Bill and was inserted&#x27; by the Joint\n\n1 Committee on the Bill for the following reasons:--\n\n&quot;The Committee feel that in a case where an application for leave to\nsue or appeal as a pauper has been made and rejected, the time taken in\nprosecuting in good faith such application should be excluded in comput-\ning the period of limitation prescribed.&quot;\n\n4&#x27;\n\n13.2. The Section really consists of two parts. and its content may be re-\n\nstated in a simplified form thus: ~ I\n\n(l)&quot; Where an application for leave to sue or appeal as a pauper has been\nmade and rejected, then. in computing the period of limitation pres-\nribed for any suit or appeal in such case, the time during which the\napplicant has been prosecuting in good faith his application for such\nleave shall be excluded. &#x27; i\n\nt (2) The Court may, on payment of the court fee prescribed for such suit\nor appeal, treat the suit or appeal as having the same force and effect\nas if the court fee had been paid in the \ufb01rst instance.\n\n_ 13.3. The purport and signi\ufb01cance of section l3 will be better understood\nif one bears in mind tie s;chcinc of the provisions&quot; in the Code of Civil Pro-\ncedure&#x27;, 1908 as to suits and appeals by &quot;indigent persons&quot; (who were. before\nthe amendment of the Code in 1976, described as &quot;paupers&quot;). A person who\ndoes not have su\ufb01icient means to pay the court fee prescribed for the plaint can\nseek leave of the court to sue as an indigent person. To determine whether\n-such leave should be granted, the court is required to hold a preliminary in-\nquiry, at the end of which either the leave prayed for is granted or refused.\n\nIf the leave is granted, then the application is converted into a plainta<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_187\">the\nCode of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908 so provides in express terms. In harmony with\n\nthis procedural provision, the Lirmtatioiv Act&quot; enacts that for the purposes of\n\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_188\">Limitation Act</a>,&#x27;\n&quot;(a) a suit is instituted .... ..\n\n(ii) in the case of a pauper when his application for leave to sue as\na pauper is made.&quot; &#x27;\n\nWhat happens, however, where the application is refused and the applicant\nhas to \ufb01le a suit in the ordinary manner after payment of court fees? <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_189\">The Indian\nLimitation Act</a>. 1908 contained no provision on the subject. As regards the\n\n&#x27;Joint Committee Report on the Liinitation Bill. 20 November, 1962.\n2Orders 33 and 44, Code Civil Procedure, 1908.\n3Section_ 3(2)(d)(ii), <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_190\">Limitation Act</a>, 196_3.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_191\">Section 13</a>.\n\nAnalysis.\n\nProcedural\nprovisions\n\nas to indigent\n-persons.\n\n31\n\n\n\n32\n\n&quot;\\\n, Ver\ufb01l change\nrecommended.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_192\">Section 14-</a>-\nExclusion of\ntime taken by\n&#x27;infructuous\nlegal procee-\ndings.\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter l3--SecIimz l3&quot;~E/\\&#x27;(&#x27;llISI&#x27;()ll of lime where leave to sue or appeal\nas pauper is applied for\n(Chapter 14--Section l4---TI&#x27;me talcen in infructuous legal proceedings.)\n\n<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_193\">Code of Civil Procedure</a>, section 149 of the Code empowers the Court to give\ntime to a person for the payment of court fees generally. However, this section\ndoes not provide for the starting point of Limitation. Moreover, there is a\ncon\ufb02ict of decisions on the precise elfect of an order under the section.&#x27; So\nthere was no &quot;relation&quot; back of the suit \ufb01led within the extended time.&quot;\n\nThe provision in <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_194\">section 13</a> of the present <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_195\">Limitation Act</a> now gives a de\ufb01-\nnite answer; the time taken in the (infructuous) application is excluded when\ncomputing the period of limitation prescribed for the suit \ufb01led (on payment of\ncourt fees). As regards appeals, the Courts, under the earlier <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_196\">Limitation Act</a>,\ngenerally used to allow the bene\ufb01t of the relaxing power under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_197\">section 5</a> of the\nLimitation Act, treating the fact that the infructuous proceedings had been\nprosecuted in good&#x27; faith as a &quot;suflicient cause&quot;,within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_198\">section\n5</a> for condoning the delay. Under the present <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_199\">section 13</a>, resort to <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_200\">section 5</a> is\nno longer&#x27; required, since <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_201\">section 13</a> makes an appropriate for appeals also.\n\n13.4. While no changes of substance are needed in the section, it is neces-\nsary to pdlnt out that the words &quot;as a pauper&quot; occurring in the section are out\nof tune with the phraseology of the Code _of Civil Procedure, 1908 as amended\nin 1976 on the recommendation3 of the Law Commission. The current phraseo-\nlogy is &quot;indigent person&quot; in the amended Code.\n\nAccordingly, we recommend that in <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_202\">section 13</a>, for the words &quot;as a pauper&quot;,\nthe words &quot;as an indigent person&quot; should be substituted.\n\nCHAPTER 14\n\nSECTION 14 : TIME TAKEN IN INFRUCTUOUS\nLEGAL PROCEEDINGS\n\n14.1 It often happens that a litigant pursues civil proceedings bona fide and\nwith due diligence. but the proceedings fail because the Court is unable to enter-\ntain them by reason of defect of jurisdiction or other cause of a like nature.\nThe litigant is therefore, compelled to institute fresh proceedings in a competent\ncourt in the proper manner. It is but fair that the time taken in the earlier civil\nproceedings should be given credit, when computing&#x27; the period of limitation for\nthe later proceedings. Though the English law recognises no such rule, the Indian\nlegislature has, for a long time, given recognition to this principle, in regard to\ncertain situations. <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_203\">Section 14</a> codi\ufb01es the principle. Sub-section (1) covers suits\nrelating to the same matter in issue. Sub-section (2) covers applications for the\nsame relief. Sub-section.(3), which was inserted for the \ufb01rst time in 1963, is\nmeant for a case ,where the \ufb01rst suit was withdrawn unde-r 0.23, R. 2 <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_204\">of the Code\nof Civil Procedure</a>, with permission to \ufb01le a fresh suit. In all the three sub-\nsections, it is necessary that the earlier proceeding must have failed by reason\nof a &quot;defect in jurisdiction or other cause of a like nature&quot;.&#x27; A\n\nAn Explanation to the section----\n\n(a) provides that the day of institution and the day of termination of the\nearlier civil proceeding are both to be counted;\n\n&#x27;Mulla, Code of Civil&#x27;Procedure. Abridged edition (1982), Dage 323.\n\n&quot;Naraim&#x27; v. Makhan, (1885), I.L.R. 17 All 526. _ _\n\n3Law _Commission of India, 54th Report (<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_205\">Code of Civil Procedure</a>. 1908), Chapters\n33 and 44.\n\n&#x27;For various cdnditions of the section, see Peerappa V. These suggestions were accept-\ned, and that is how the Explanation made its appearance on the statute book.\n\n14.2. An important question that has arisen in the context of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_206\">section 14</a> <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_207\">section 4</a> and\nmay be considered at the outset, since it concerns <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_208\">section 4</a> as well. Put in5&#x27;3&quot;i\u00b0&quot; &quot;-\nsimple terms, the question is this--~Can a plaintiff avail himself of the combined\nbene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_209\">section 4</a> and /<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_210\">section 14</a>. A Supreme Court case3 illustrates the pro-\nblem. A suit for damages was \ufb01led in a Kamal Court on 2nd March, 1959.\nLimitation had expired on 1st March, 1959, but that was a holiday. The pre-\nsentation being to a wrong court, the Karma] court returned the plaint on October\n28, 1959, and it was presented the next day, i.e.,. on ,29th October, 1959 to the\n-Ambala Court, which had jurisdiction to try the same. The plaintiff claimed (1)\nexclusion of the delay of one day in presenting the plaint in the Karnal Court\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_211\">section 4</a> and (ii) exclusion of the succeeding period spent in the Karnal\nCourt under <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_212\">section 14</a>. The Supreme Court, relying on an earlier decision of\nthe Privy Council&#x27;, held that <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_213\">section 4</a> is inapplicable Where the plaint is pre-\n\nsented to a wrong court, and observed as under:\n\n&quot;If the plaintiff had \ufb01led the suit in the trial court on March 2, 1959,\nthen certainly the suit would have been within time under <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_214\">section 4</a>, as that\nwas the propec Court in which the suit should have been \ufb01led. As the\nKarma] Court had no jurisdiction to entertain the plaint, it was not the\nproper Court. The fact that the plaintiff would be entitled to take advantage\nof the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_215\">section 14</a> of the Act would not, in any way, affect the\nquestion whether the suit was \ufb01led within the time as provided in <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_216\">section\n4</a> in the Kamal -Court. <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_217\">Section 14</a> of the Act only provided for the exclusion\nof the time during which the plaintiff has been prosecuting with due dili-\ngence another civil proceeding against the defendant, where the proceeding\nis founded upon the sums CZIUSS of action and is prosecuted in good faith\nin a court which, from defect of ju11&#x27;sdiotion&#x27;or other cause of a like nature\nis unable to entertain it. Even if the plaintiff was entitled to get an exclusion\nof the -time during which he was prosecuting the suit in the Kama] and\nPanipat Courts, the suit would not be within time, as the \ufb01ling of the suit\n\n, in the Kama] Court was beyond the period of limitation&quot;.\n\n/ 14.3. A Nagpur case&quot; has followed the majority trend in holding that <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_222\">section\n4</a> of the Limitation Act cannot refer to other Courts in which an unsuccessful.\nattempt at institution of a suit was made during the summer vacation when the\nCourt was closed. &#x27; However, in a later case&#x27;, this ruling was distinguished and\nit was held that <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_223\">section 4</a>, which by itself excludes no time, can be tacked on to\n<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_224\">section 14</a> of the &quot;<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_225\">Limitation Act</a>.\n\n14.5. The inapplicability of <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_226\">section 4</a> to such cases has been the view in\nLahore5 also.\n\n14.6. In a judgment of the Bombay High Court&quot; we have the following obser-\nvations: &#x27;\n\n&quot;Clearly the plaintiff is entitled to take advantage of those days during\nwhich the \ufb01rst Court was closed for the vacation, and the computation should\nbe made in the same way as if the second Court had been closed for the\nvacation.&quot;\n\n14.7. Having considered that matter at some length we are of the view that\nthe situation as in the Supreme Court\u00bb case7 requires to be remedied, We take the\n\nview that where a plaintilf,&#x27;\ufb01ling a suit in a_Court without jurisdiction on a day_\n\nnext following the day when the limitation period expired because the earlier\n.day was dies non, \ufb01nds himself denied the bene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_227\">section 4</a>, hardship is bound\nto result. If the principle of <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_228\">section 4</a> is that a plaintiff or applicant should not\n&#x27;be penalised merely because the day on which he should have _\ufb01led the plaint\nor application in court was a court holiday, there is no reason why the principle\nshould not be extended to the situation when the plaintitf bona fide \ufb01les the\nplaint in a Court which later on discovers that it has no jurisdiction&#x27; to try the\nsuit.\n\n14.8. We therefore recommend that a suitable Explanation should be added\nto <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_229\">section 4</a>, on the point discussed above.&#x27;\n\n14.9. However, several decisions hold that even though section &#x27;14 has no\napplication to appeals which are governed by section .5. the principles of section\n- 14 will apply even to proceedings in appeal.&#x27; &#x27;\n\nIn a Saurashtra case,&quot; the High Court relied on three Privy Council rulings\non the subject&#x27;&#x27;&#x27;-&#x27;-5 and summarised the result of these cases thus.\n\n1\n\n&quot;These three Privy Council rulings seeni to lay down thatalthough\n<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_234\">section 14</a> of the Limitation Act is not directly applicable lo appeals, the\nprinciples underlying it on grounds of equity should be applied to appeals in\nconsidering an application under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_235\">section 5</a> of the Limitation Act. The exis-\ntence of circumstances contemplated by <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_236\">section 14</a> has consequently been\nregarded as a ground for excusing the delay caused by the wrong proceed-\n_ ings. and a sufficient cause for the delay within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_237\">section 5</a>\nof the Limitation Act, it being assumed that the party would be deemed\nto have acted with reasonable diligence where such circumstances existed.&quot;\n\n14.1l.[A. few other cases on the subjects also take the same view.&#x27;&#x27;-3\n\n14.12. No doubt, even though <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_238\">section 14</a> does not apply to appeals, invoking Need to\nthe provisions of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_239\">section 5</a> may in. practice solve the hardship that would other-g\ufb01gfgn 14_\nwise result&quot; We, are, however, of the view that a right should be given to an to appeals.\nappellant to exclude the time taken in proceedings borta \ufb01de in a court without &#x27;\nin/risdiction, rather than leaving the matter to the discretion of the court under\n\n2 <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_240\">section 5</a> to be exercised for &quot;sufficient cause&quot;. While &#x27; terpreting the expression\n\n&quot;su\ufb02icient cause,&quot; the courts may take con\ufb02icting-v_&#x27; s which appear to be\nworth avoiding on such matters.\n\nA&#x27;We, therefore, recommend the extension of section&#x27; 14 to appeals by express\nphraseologym. &#x27;\n\n&#x27; 14.13. When the plaint is returned and the plainti\ufb01 is required to undertake Time taken\nsome journey to present the plaint to the proper court, the question may arise in l\u00b0&quot;&quot;&#x27;\u00b0V&#x27;\nwhether the time taken, in such a journey should also count for the purposes of\n<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_241\">section 14</a>. It has been held in an Allahabad case,&quot; that reasonable \ufb01me spent for\ngoing to place of proper court can be excluded but if the plaintiif falls ill.after the\nplaint was returned, the period of his illness can, in no case, be excused to condone\nthe delay. We are of the view that in a country as diverse as ours, with means of\ncommunications not always satisfactory, a plaintilf or applicant is bound to take\nsome time in performing the physical act of carrying the plaint from the court\nwhich pronounces want of jurisdiction, to the proper court. The period spent in\nperforming this physical process would depend upon the distance between these\nA [two courts, but it&#x27; cannot be gainsaid that some time is bound to be spent in this\n1*: ioumey.; -Consequently, we recommend that the Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_242\">section 14</a> should\n\nbe amended suitably for the purpose.&quot;\n\n&#x27;Subedar Baman v.
