At this stage, it is proper to&quot;~refer to a point concerning the appli-/&#x27;\ncability of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_245\">section 14</a> to arbitration proceedings. The question is whether the\nbene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_246\">section 14</a> can be claimed where the earlier infructuous proceedings took\nplacelbefore arbitrators, and not before courts. In this context, one has to make&quot;\na distinction between (i) arbitrations governed by the <a href=\"/doc/1052228/\" id=\"a_247\">Arbitration Act, 1940</a> and\n(ii)l}arbit&#x27;rations not governed by that Act. As regards the \ufb01rst category of arbi-\ntrations, <a href=\"/doc/237684/\" id=\"a_248\">section 37(5)</a> of the Arbitration Act. 1940 has made a suitable provi--e\nsion for the exclusion of time taken in an arbitration. As regards the second\ncategory of arbitrations, though the <a href=\"/doc/1052228/\" id=\"a_249\">Arbitration Act, 1940</a> will naturally be in-\napplicable, it seems from a fairly recent judgment of the Allahabad High Court&#x27;\nthat the bene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_250\">section 14</a> can be availed of in such arbitrations. It also appears\nthat before the incorporation of the relevant provisions in the &#x27;Arbitration law,&quot;\nthere were other rulings which regarded <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_251\">section 14</a> as applicable to the arbitration.&#x27;-&#x27;\nWe do not, of course, consider it necessary to suggest any express amendment\non the above point.\n\n14.15. In connection withsection 14 it is of interest to refer to one suggestion\nwhich was made by a District Judge&#x27; in 1907. He suggested the addition of the\nfollowing further clause to <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_252\">section 14</a>:---- &#x27;\n\n&quot;(3) The time requisite for&#x27; obtaining a copy of the decree or order in\nthe cases mentioned in clauses (1) and (2) and&#x27; the time which the plaintiff\nor the applicant prosecuting the suits or application with due diligence\nrequires in going to the proper court. shall be excluded.&quot;\n\nThe suggestion falls&#x27; in-two parts: (i) exclusion of the time spent in obtaining\ncopies of the \ufb01nal order of the court, where the plaintiff or the applicant was\nwrongly prosecuting his claim; (ii) &#x27;exclusionof the time required by the plaintilf\nor the applicant in physically performing the journey from the wrong court to the\nproper court. .\n\nThe second part deals with .1 point that has been already attended to.&#x27; The\n\ufb01rst point is dealt with below.\n\n14.16. &#x27;An examination of the case law discloses that there is some con\ufb02ict\non the subject. For example. an Andhra Pradesh case&#x27; takes the view that the\ntime occupied in obtaining certi\ufb01ed copies of the judgment, and in taking other\nindispensable and necessary steps preparatory to an initiation of the proceedings,\nin&#x27; a court which ultimately proved to be &#x27;fruitless, should also be regarded as\nthe time during which the plaintiff has been prosecuting the civil proceedings.\n\n. 14.17. The Patna High Court has, however, taken a contrary view on the\nsubject.&#x27; V . -\n\n&quot;There is nothing in <a href=\"/doc/39597/\" id=\"a_253\">section 14(1)</a> which can justif the View that the\ntaken by a party in taking steps for invoking the aid of the Court should also be\nexcluded while computing the period of limitation. Under <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_280\">section 15(5)</a>. the time during which the defendant has been\nabsent from India and from the territories outside India under the administration\nof the Central Government. is excluded in computing the period of limitation\nfor any suit against such a defendant. As long back as in 1851, an action was\n\n&quot;brought in the Supreme Court of Judicature?&#x27; at Bombay for conversion of cer-\n\n, tain chests of opium. Upon a defence being raised that the suit was barred\n\nby limitation, as having. been brought beyond six years of the accrual of cause\n\nof action, the plaintiff alleged that the defendant was residing outside the juris-.\n\ndiction of the Supreme Court of Judicature at Bombay and beyond the \\territory\nsubject to the Government of the East India Company and, therefore, the suit\nwas not barred. The Supreme Court upheld the plaintiff&#x27;s contention on the\nbasis of the&quot;British statutes then in force in the country&quot;. i\n\n15.5. The word &quot;defendant&quot; in <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_281\">section 15(5)</a> has given rise to certain pro&#x27;-\nblems, in as much as the section does not indicate in so many words as to\nwhat a plaintiff should do when he is to \ufb01lea suit against several defendants some\nof whom are absent from India. But this is an injustice&quot; which the legistlatute. if they\n\nso pleased, could easily remedy. and which has been. in fact. remedied in &#x27;\n\nEngland by the Statute of 19 and 20 Vict.. c. 97.&quot;\n\nHis brother on theBench Markby J.. pointed o1It how the rule had been\nmodi\ufb01ed in England: &#x27; s\n\n&quot;The rule laid down by Parke.&#x27;B., in King ~v. But it must be borne in mind that this rule was abolished\nin the Roman law 1300 years ago, and has been since repudiated in America\nand everywhere in Europe, except in England. Even in England, until the deci-\nsion of King V. Hoare&#x27; it was very doubtful whether the rule prevailed or not in&#x27;\njoint contract; whilst since that time one learned Judge (Sir James Knight Bruce)\nhas spoken of the rule in strong terms of disapprobation (27 L.J. Bank, 29). Lord\n&#x27; Mans\ufb01eld also expressed the opinion in Rice v. Shute that all contracts with\npartners were joint and several, and the rule in King v. Hoare has been since\nmodi\ufb01ed by Statute in England. The 19 and 20 Vict., C. 97, <a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_283\">Section 11</a> directs\nthat the period of limitation as to joint debtors shall run notwithstanding that\nsome are beyond seas but expressly provides that the creditor shall not be\nbarred as against those out of the jurisdiction by judgment recovered against\nthose who remain within it. If the rule laid down in King v. Home be combined\nwith the law of limitation here. which is very strict, it is by no means clear\n.that a creditor might not very often be left to the choice between a remedy\nagainst an insolvent debtor and having his debt barred.&quot;\n\n15.6. When the draft  of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_284\">Indian Limitation Act</a>. 1908 was circulated comment,\nfor comments, the injustice arising out of such an interpretation formed the sub-\nject matter of the comments of one Mr. M. Gupta:----\n\n&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_285\">Section 13</a> [present <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_286\">section 15(5)</a>] of the Act might be with advantage amend-\ned so as to provide for the case where some of the defendants have been absent\nfrom British India. If on the grammatical construction of the section it is held_\nto be applicable to a case where only someof the defendants have been absent\nfrom British India, then it has been observed that if some of the joint debtors\nwho are within British territories are insolvent and the plaintiff waits for the\n\nreturn for the absent defendants. he will often find himself altogether barred by\nlimitation&quot;?\n\n15.7. Dr. Whitley Stoekes in his Anglo-Indian Codes: has also made similar Comment.\nobservations.  stokes.\n\n15.8.&#x27; In England, <a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_287\">section 11</a> of the 19 and 20 Vict., c. 97, had removed\nthe anomaly pointed out by the Calcutta High Court. The section reads:\n\n&quot;l1. Where such cause of action or suit with respect to which the\nperiod of limitation is \ufb01xed by the enactments aforesaid or any of them\nlies against two or more joint debtors, the person or persons who shall be\nentitled to the same shall not be entitled to any time within which to com-\nmence and sue any such action or suit against any one or more of such joint\ndebtors who&#x27;shall not be beyond .the seas, at the time such cause of action\n\n&#x27; or suit accrued, by reason only that some other one or more of such joint\ndebtors was or were at the time such cause of action accrued beyond&#x27; the\nseas, and such person or persons so entitled as aforesaid shall not be barred\nfrom commencing and suing any action or suit against the joint debtor or\njoint debtors who was or were beyond seas at the time the cause of action\n\nr\n\n1King v. Hoare (1844) 14 LI. Et. 29 2 67 RR. 694, 702, 703, 704.\n2M. Gupta, Barrister-at-Law, Hoshangabad. Annexure II to the ietter No. 2063, dated\n19th December, 1901 from the Chief Secretary to the Chief Commissioner, Central Pro-\nEinces to the Secretary to the Govt. of India, Legislative Department, National Archievol\nle\n\n&#x27; 3Stokes Anglo-Indian Codes (1888) Vol.2 page 950.\n7-14 Mof IJ&amp;cA1ND/83 &#x27;\n\nDr. Whitley &#x27;\n\n\n\n42,\n\nLaw Com-\nmission\nReport on\nContract Act.\n\n89rH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter l5--SccIimI l5--Exclusi0n of Time in certain other cases.)\n\nor suit accrued after his or their return from beyond seas, by reason only\nthat judgment was already recovered against any one or more of such joint\ndebtors who was not or were not beyond seas at the time aforesaid.&quot;\n\n15.9. The difficulties raised by the Calcutta judgement, as well as by cases of\nother&#x27;High Courts, were considered by the Law Commission in its <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_288\">Report on\nthe Contract Act, 1872</a>&#x27; and we reproduce. below their observations from that\n\nReport : --\n\\\n\n&quot;70. <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_289\">The Contract Act</a>/treats all contracts as joint and several. The\nnecessary consequence is that it is not open to one promisor who is sued to\ncompel the promisee to sue others. There has, however, been considerable\ndivergence of opinion on the effect of a judgment obtained by the promisee\nagainst one out of a number of promisors. In the words of the Federal\nCourti, unlike English Law, the Indian Law makes a general liability joint\nand several, in the absence of an agreement to the contrary. It&#x27;is. therefore,\nopen to the promisee to sue any one or some of the joint promisors and it is\nno defence to such a suit that all the promisors should have been made\nparties. We think that Strachey, C..I., correctly stated the law in Muhammad\nAskari V. Radhe Ram&quot; when he said:. The doctrine now rests not so much\non King V. Hoarv&#x27; as on the judgment of the Law Lords in Kendal] v. Hamil-\nton&#x27;. As explained in these judgments, the doctrine that there is in the\ncase of a joint contractia single cause of action which can only be once\nsued on, is essentially based on the right of joint debtors in England to have\nall their contractors joined as defendants in any suit to enforce the joint\n\nobligation.\n\nThe right was in England enforceable before the Judicature Act by means\nof a plea in abatement, and since the Judicature Acts by an application for\njoinder which is determined on the same principles as those on which the plea\nin abatement would formerly have been dealt with. In India that right of joint\n\ndebtors has been expressly excluded by <a href=\"/doc/243421/\" id=\"a_290\">section 43</a> of the Contract Act, and,&#x27;\n\ntherefore, the basis of the doctrine being. absent, the doctrine itself is inapplicable.\nCessanite ralione legis, cesswt ipsa lex.&quot; The result is that a decree obtained\nagainst some of_ several joint promisors remaining unsatis\ufb01ed ought not to be held\nas a bar to a subsequent action against the other promisors. We recommend that\n\n7&quot; -\n\nthis result may be incorporated in the Act by.inserting a new section.\n\nAfter making these observations, the Law Commission recommended&#x27; the\ninsertion of.a new section 44 A as follows in the <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_291\">Contract Act</a>:---\n\n&quot;44 A. Effect of decree obtained against one promisor.----A rdecree\n\nagainst any one or more of a number of joint promisors does not, if it has\n\n&#x27; remained unsatis\ufb01ed. and in the absence of express agreement to the contrary,\nbar a subsequent suit against any one or more of_ the other promisors.&quot;\n\nWe reiterate the recommendation for amending the Contract&quot; Act, quoted\nabove. Such an amendment would resolve the difficulty that at present arises\nin the law of limitation. when only some of the defendants (joint promisors) have\nbeen absent from India.\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 13th Report (Contract Act, l872),&#x27;pages 32-33.\n\nZlainarain V.&#x27; Suraimull, I949) 12 Fed, L]. 216, 225.\n\n322 All. 307.\n\n&#x27;King V. Hoare (1844) 153 BR. 206. 67 RR 694.\n\n5(1879) 4 AC. 504. - .\n\n622 All. 307 (311, 312). _\n\n&quot;Vide section 44A, Appendix I (of the 13th Report).\n\n&quot;Law Commission of India, &#x27;13th <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_292\">Report (Contract Act, 1872</a>), App. 1. section 44A.\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW CUMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 15-Section lS--E.tclusion of Time in certain other cases.)\n\n15.10. Another point relating to <a href=\"/doc/1720337/\" id=\"a_293\">section 15</a> is concerned gith cases where Ag.-rm in\nthe defendant had an agent in India. A Full Bench of the Calcutta High Courtlot\ndecided that section 13 of the Limitation Act, 1877 which excluded the time\nduring which 3. defendant had been absent from British India incomputing the\nperiod of limitation for any suit applied even where, to the knowledge of the\nplaintiff, the defendants, (partners in a \ufb01rm), were during the period of their\nabsence, carrying on business in British India through an authorised agent who was\nauthorised to bring suits in India. The Full Bench overruled an earlier ease of\n\nthe same High&#x27; Court,&quot; and o&quot;nserved:----\n\n&quot;In support of this contention the case of Harrington v. Gonesh Roy\nis strongly relied on by the defendants. I doubt, however, whether, having\nregard to the clear and precise language of <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_294\">section 13</a>, that decision is well\nfounded, for it seems to me that, whatever may be the common sense of the\ndecision, it can only he arrived at by interpolating into the section words\nthat are not there, words to the effect that the time of absence is not to be\nexcluded if the defendants are, during the period of personal absence, repre-\nsented by a duly constituted agent in British India. Although we have been\nreferred to the case of Hawkins V. Gathercolef as to the manner in which\nstatutes are to be construed, I do not see my way to put the construction\nupon the section for which the defendantscontend; if we did so; I think we\nshould be rather legislating than adjudicating upon the section as it stands.\nIt may well be that it would be expedient, not to allow the time of absence\nfrom British India to be excluded, if the defendants be carrying &quot;on business\nin British India, and be represented by a duly authorised agent during such\nabsence; but if this change is to bemade, it must be made by theLegislature.\nReading the language of <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_295\">section 13-</a>-a section be it remembered in a Limita-\ntion Act, the provisions of which must be construed strictly, and which, when\nset up as a defence, must not be extended to cases which are not strictly\nwithin the enactment, whilst exceptions of an exemption from its operation\nare to be construed leberally (see per Lord Cranworth in Boddan v. Morely,)&#x27;--\nreading, I say, that section according to the ordinary signi\ufb01cance of the words\nused, I think we are not warranted in holding that the section does not apply\nto cases where the defendants are duringithexperiod of absence, carrying on\nbusiness in Biitish India through an aiithorised agent. In other words, I\ndo not see my way to getting over the clear and precise language of the\nsection, feeling as I do that the words of the section are too strong against\nthe view contended for by the defendants, and that we could only support\nthat view by the interpolation of words to the effect I have stated above.&quot;\n\n15.11. The Full Bench ruling&quot; based itself decision on the strict interpretation\n&#x27;of the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_296\">section 13</a> of the Limitation Act of 1877 (corresponding to\n<a href=\"/doc/1720337/\" id=\"a_297\">section 15</a> of the present Act). In Harrington&#x27;s case,&#x27; the division bench had\nheld that <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_298\">section 13</a> of the 1877 Act did not apply to a case when to the know-\n(ledge of the plaintiff, the defendant, though not residing in British India, is re-\npresented by a duly constituted agent and moolchtar. Powerful arguments were\nadvanced to reverse the \ufb01nding of the District Judge, who had held otherwise\nand the court said:\n\n&quot;The Judge relies on <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_299\">section 13</a> of the Limitation Act, which provides\nthat &#x27;in computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time\n1Paorno Chunder Chose v. He goes on to say &#x27;admittedly Mr. Harrington,&#x27; the defendant in\nthis case, &#x27;has been so absent from the date of dispossession till now&#x27;. It\nseems, however, that Mr. Harrington is &#x27;represented in this country by Mr.\nCrowdy, who, in the first instance, was made a defendant in the case as\nManager and Mookhtar of the Bhugwanpur Factory.\n\nIf the Judges interpretation of <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_300\">section 13</a> were correct, there would be\nno limitation at all as against a proprietor residing in England, although\nsuits might be conducted for and against him through his agent in this coun-\ntry. It is impossible to believe that this was the intention of the law.&quot;\n\n15.12. A reading of the judgements referred to above shows that the judges\nin both the benches were in unison that it would be expedient not to allow the\ntime of absence from British India to be excluded under <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_301\">section 15(5)</a>, if the\ndefendant is represented by a duly authorised agent during such absence. The\ndifference between the approaches of the two benches was that while the Full\nBench in the later ruling felt hamstrung by the language of the section and left\nit to the legislature to amend it, the Division Bench in the earlier ruling sought\nto put an interpretation on that section which in their opinion accords with the\ntrue intention of the legislature&quot;.\n\n15.13. Whatever way one looks at it, seems to us most incogruent--consi-\ndering the fact that more and more of non-residents are cafrying on business\nin India through their authorised agents--that the non-residence in India of such\na defendant himself should ensure to-the advantage of a plaintiff, who knows\nthat the defendant could be served through a duly authorised agent in India.&#x27;\n\n15.14. It could as well happen that a judgment against the defendant, ope-\nrating through an agent, would be inconformity with the tenets of private inter-\n\n&#x27; national law &#x27;also. By appointing an agent to use or defend, the absentee defen-\n\ndant has, in a sense, submitted to the jurisdiction of the court where the agent\nis working for gain. However, we need not express any \ufb01rm view on _this point.\nThis.aspect in any case is not directly material to the present issue.\n\nIn View of the above discussion it is desirable that a suitable Explanatio\ufb01&#x27;\nshould be added to <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_302\">section 15(5)</a> on the above point.&#x27;\n\n15.15. We now deal with another situation to which <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_303\">section 15(5)</a> applies.\nConsidering the number of Indians going abroad for trade, commerce or seek-.\ning livelihood, it could as well happen that the &quot;plaintiff and the defendant were\nboth residing outside India during the relevant period and the plaintiff could\nhave obtained redress without much di\ufb02iculty, in the court of that country where\nthey were so residing. In such a case, it would be unjust to allow such a plain-\ntilf the bene\ufb01t of exclusion of the period during which the defendant was resid-\ning outside India, when the plaintiff himself was also during the same period,\noutside India and could have sued the defendant in the foreign country.\n\n15.16. A situation of the nature contemplated above is not merely hypothe-\nticai; such a case&#x27; did occur more than/&#x27;a century ago in the then North West\n\nProvinces and was decided under the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_304\">Limitation Act</a> of 1859.\n\n&#x27;Cf. Andappa, A.I.R. 1955\nMad. 96 (F.B.). _ _ ; .\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT OF LAW COMMN. OF INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n(Chapter l5---Section l5--Exclusiort of Time in certain other cases.)\n\nThis was a suit brought in by a wife against her husband for the recovery\nof the amount due to her on account of dower payable under a written agreement\nand for maintenance and other reliefs. The parties were married in England in\n1863, according to Mohammadan law. The defendant--husband left England\nand eventually returned to India in December, 1865, only to be followed by\nhis wife in June, 1869, who, immediately, upon arrival, instituted the suit in\nIndia.\n\nIt was argued by the wife-plaintiff; that she was entitled to rely on the pro-\nvisions of section 13 of the Limitation Act, 1859 [corresponding to present sec-\ntion 15(5)] and to exclude, from the computation of the periods of limitation .\napplicable to her several claims, the time during which the defendant husband\nwas absent from British India. This contention was countered by the husband-\ndefendant, according to whom, when both the parties were living in England, to\nput a construction on <a href=\"/doc/1602186/\" id=\"a_307\">section 13</a> and to_ allow one of the parties to take advan-\ntage of the absence of the other party from British India would lead to extremely\ninconvenient results. Repelling the defendant&#x27;s argument, the High Court said:\n\n&quot;It is true that such a construction may lead to very inconvenient results.&#x27;\nA person may resides out of India for years, and according to the law of\nlimitation of the country in which he resides, the remedy against him in\nrespect of a cause of action of the nature of a personal action may be lost,\nyet, on his coming to India, it will revive. We can hardly conceive that\nthis was the intention of the legislature. It, would seem that in the Act no\npravision has been made for cases in which the cause of action arises in a\nforeign country. or in which, at the time the cause of action accrues, both\nparties are residing in one and the same foreign country possessing tribu-\nnals to which they might have recourses; at the same time, it is to be --\n, remembered that the law of limitation is a law which bars the remedy and\ndoes not destroy the right, and therefore, if by any of its sections we \ufb01nd\nindulgence, shown to suitors, we are bound to give full e\ufb01ect to the lan-\nguage in which that indulgence\\/is conceded.&quot; &#x27;\n\n15.17. The judgment referred to above raises two important issues: \ufb01rst,Qucs\ufb01om\nwhether the Indian courts should entertain a suit by a person based on a cause-raised by\nof action arising in a foreign country when his remedy to obtain redress in the &quot;R l&quot;d3&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot;&#x27;-\nforeign court would have been barred on account of the law governing actions\nin that foreign country? Secondly, whether the exclusion of time allowable under\n<a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_308\">section 15(5)</a> should also be granted even if the plaintiff was residing in the same\nforeign country where the defendant was residing during the relevant period?\n\nAs regards the \ufb01rst point, it falls within the ambit&#x27; of <a href=\"/doc/438099/\" id=\"a_309\">section 11</a>. However,\n\n7 the second limb of the suggestion in the judgment, namely, that the bene\ufb01t of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_310\">section 15(5)</a> should not be available to the plaintiff who, during the relevant\ntime, was also residing in the same foreign country where the defendant also\nresided, deserves acceptance in our opinion. A plaintiff who could, but did\nnot choose to, resort to the court of competent jurisdiction in the foreign coun-\ntry where he was then residing should not in an Indian court be allowed the\nadvantage of an extension of limitation period. Accordingly, we recommend\nthat an exception should be engrafted on <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_311\">section 15(5)</a> on the subject?\n\n15.18. The draftsman of <a href=\"/doc/1427618/\" id=\"a_312\">section 15(5)</a> may not have focussed his attention on Fr t\nthe possibility of a defendant \ufb02itting across the globe more than once during the mp\u00b0\u00a7&quot;\u00a7,&quot;\n\nperiod of limitation. In the light of the above discussion, we recommend the &#x27;following\namendments to the law on the topics dealt with in section l5:-- &#x27;\n\n(i) An exception should be inserted below section l5(_5) of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_317\">Limitation\nAct</a> as under:---- &#x27; &#x27;\n\n&quot;Exccpti0n.------N0thing in sub-section (5) shall apply to a period subse-\nquent to the date on which the cause of action arises, being a period during\nwhich----\n\n(a) both the parties were residing in one and the same foreign country\nand the plaintiff was aware of the residence of the defendant; and\n\n(b) there were, in the foreign country, properly constituted courts or\ntribunals to which the parties had, or could have had, recourse for\nenforcing the cause of action.&quot;&quot;&#x27;\n\n(ii) The following Explanations should be added to <a href=\"/doc/1720337/\" id=\"a_318\">section 15</a> of the Limi-\ntation Act:\n\n&quot;Explanation I.-\u00abThe attachment of a decree under rule 53 of Order\nX X] in the First Schedule to <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_319\">the Code of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908&#x27; docs not\nA amount to a stay of execution of the decree within the meaning of sub--scc-\n, tion (1)&#x27;. &#x27;\n\n. &quot;Explanation Il.----~For the purposes of this section, a defendant shall\nn0t be deemed to have been absent from India during any period during\nwhich he had, to the knowledge of the plainti\ufb02, a duly constitute&#x27;d&#x27; agent&#x27; in\nIndia, authorised to institute and defend legal proceedings on his behalf in\nIndia.&quot;&quot;&#x27;\n\n(iii) <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_320\">Amendment of the Indian Contract Act, 1872</a> by the insertion of new\nsection 44A in that Act, may also be carried out, for resolving the controversy\nrelating to decree against one of several joint promisors.&#x27;\n\nCHAPTER 16 _\nSECTION 16 : EFFECT OF DEATH ON LIMITATION &#x27;\n\n16.1. The general principle is that when a right to sue accrues to a person\nduring his lifetime, his death thereafter does not suspend the running of limita-\ntion. The reason is, that time commences to run at once (in general) and,&#x27; as the\nAct puts it, once time has commenced to run, no subsequent inability to sue stops\nor suspends its running. &#x27;\n\n&#x27;Mohammad Sulaiman Rowther V. N. K. A&quot;. Mohammad Ibrahim. (1967) 2 M.L.J. 483.\n\n&#x27;-&#x27;Ismailji Haji Ha/imbhai v. Ismail Abdul Kadar, A.I.R. 1921 Born. 460, 461 : I.L.R, 45 .\n\nBorn. 1228.\n\n&quot;See para 15.17, supra.\n\n&#x27;See para 15.3, supra.\n\n-&#x27;See para 15.14, supra.\n\n&quot;See para 15.9, supra.\n\n&quot;<a href=\"/doc/268914/\" id=\"a_321\">Section 9</a>, Limitation Act, 1963.\n\n\n\nthe section to such cases would tend to create insecuritv of title.&#x27;\n\n. with the operation of this rule.\n\n89m REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 16~SecIion l6--E[7&#x27;ect of Death on Limitation.)\n\nHowever, where a right to sue accrues for the \ufb01rst time after the death of a\nperson- i.e. if a person dies before the right to _sue accrues, there must be some\nperson capable of representing his estate-when the right to sue accrues. Where\nthe right to sue accrues on the death of a person (i.e. where the death and accrual\nof the cause of&#x27; action are simultaneous), the same is the position. Dealing with\nthese two situations, <a href=\"/doc/929849/\" id=\"a_322\">section 16(1)</a> of the Act provides that the period of limita-\ntion shall be computed from the time when there is a lega_l representative of the\ndeceased capable of instituting the suit or application in question..\n\nWhile <a href=\"/doc/929849/\" id=\"a_323\">section 16(1)</a> deals with the death of the would-be plaintiff, <a href=\"/doc/160416/\" id=\"a_324\">section 16(2)</a>\napplies the same principle to the case of the death of a person liable to be sued----\nthe would-be defendant. Where his death takes place before, or simultaneously\nwith, the accrual of the cause of action, the period of limitation is to be comput-\ned from the time when there is a legal representative of -the deceased against whom\nthe plaintiff may institute :1 suit or make an application for enforcing the cause\nof action in question.\n\nHowever, <a href=\"/doc/1623596/\" id=\"a_325\">section 16(3)</a> provides that nothing in sub-section (1) or sub-sec-\ntion (2) applies-- 1 &quot;\n\n(a) to suit to/ enforce rights of pre-emption, or\n\n(b) to suits for the possession of an immovable property or of a hereditary\noffice.\n\nThe exception takes out these suits, presumably because the application of\nMoreover,\nthe period of limitation for suits for immovable property is generally twelve\nyears, and no hardship would be caused by the non--existencc of a legal repre-\nsentative for some period. In the case of suits &#x27;for pre-emption, though the\nperiod is not so long, there is the general rule that the demand for pre-emption\nmust be made quickly and the legislature has not considered it proper to interfere\nIt may be mentioned that the same approach\nhas been shown while dealing with the case of legal disability. The special pro-\nvisions? enacted to give extra time to persons under legal disability do not apply\nto a suit to enforce a right of pre-emption3.\n\n16.2. The Law Commission,&#x27; relying on the Privy Council case of Magyyappa\nV. Subrama_nya,&quot;&#x27; recommended extension of the old <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_326\">section 17</a> (now <a href=\"/doc/1019641/\" id=\"a_327\">section 16</a>)\nof the 1908 Act&#x27;even to rights of action accruing on death and this recommenda-\ntion has since been accepted. The gist of the Privy Council decision is that time\ndoes not run or commence to run until there is a person in existence who is_capable\nof being sued. This proposition has been sought to be clari\ufb01ed in present sec-\ntion 16, which takes the place of <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_328\">section 17</a> of the Act of .1908. The crux of\nthe amendment in the law achieved by <a href=\"/doc/1720337/\" id=\"a_329\">section 15</a> of the present Act is the addi-\ntion, in sub-section (1), of the words &quot;where a right to institute a suit&#x27;or make\nan application accrues only on the death of a person&quot; and the addition of cor-\nresponding words in sub-section (2). The change was made in the light of cer-\n\ntain decisions that had applied <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_330\">section 17</a> of the Act of 1908 even to cases where &#x27;\n\nthe right to sue had accrued on death.&quot;\n\n16.3. No further change is needed in the Section.\n\n1Cf- Kesho Prasad V. Madho Prasad, (1924) I.L.R. 3 Pat 880.\n&quot;Sections 6-7. .\n\n3Section 8.\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of.India, 3rd Report, Page 21. Para 47.\n5Mayyappa v. Subraman_v(I, 30 C.W.N. 833 (RC).\n&quot;Meyappa v. Subramanya, (1916) 20. C.W.N. 833 (P.C.).\n\n47\n\nNo change\n\nneeded.\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_331\">Section 17-</a>\nElfect of\n\n&#x27; fraud or\n\nmistake on\nlimitation.\n\nSources of\nthe contents\n\nof the section.\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n(Chapter l7--~<a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_332\">Section 17-</a>-Fraud and Mistake)\nCHAPTER 17.\nFRAUD AND MISTAKE\n\n17.1 In general, a peron&#x27;s ignorance of his right tb sue does not suspend\nthe running of limitation. However, it has been recognised for a long time that\n&quot;the right of a party defrauded is not alfected by lapse of time .... ..so long as\nhe remains, without any fault of his own, in ignorance of the fraud which has\nbeen committed&quot;? Acting on this principle, <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_333\">section 17</a> provides that in the case\nof certain types of fraud, the period of limitation shall not start running until the\nfraud has been discovered. This, of course, is only a broad statement of the\nprinciple, several matters of detail _which are dealt with in the section will be\nmentioned. in due course.\n\nMore or _less the same principle is applied by the Act where relief from the\nconsequences of a mistake is the very basis of the cause of action, the period of\nlimitation shall not begin to run until the plaintiff or applicant has discovered,\nthe mistake.\n\nIn both these cases, the relaxation is given by the Act itself. In addition, I\n\nthere is a third case where the Act gives a power to the court to extend the period\nof limitation on the ground of fraud. Where a judgment debtor has, by fraud\nor force, prevented the execution of a decree or order within the period of limi-\ntation, on the ground of fraud. Where a judgment debtor has, by fraud or\nforce, prevented the execution of a decree or order within the period of limita-\ntion, the court may, on the judgment creditor&#x27;s application made after the expiry\nof the period prescribed for execution, extend the period, if the application is\nmade within one year from the date ofdiscovery of fraud or the cessation of\nthe force, as the case may be. Incidentally, this provision takes in not only\nfraud--a circumstance that affects knowledge-but also force----a circumstance\nthat affects the exercise of the right, and not its knowledge.\n\n17.2. The section ,may appear to be rather long; this is due to several fac-\ntors, mainly historical. The main subject of the section--fraud~was dealt in\nthe Act of 1908 in <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_334\">section 19</a>. &#x27;\n\n. However, that section did not speci\ufb01cally \\deal with a cause of action which\nitself was founded on fraud: it dealt only with fraud that prevented knowledge\nof a right already accrued. The Law Commission, in its Report2 on the earlier\nAct, considered it proper that that case should be included--as in the <a href=\"/doc/1003813/\" id=\"a_335\">English\nAct</a>&#x27; Secondly, as recommended by the Law Commission, in the same Report.\nthe case of mistake has also been covered in the present Act again, on the\nlines of the <a href=\"/doc/1003813/\" id=\"a_336\">English Act</a>. In India, the earlier <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_337\">Limitation Act</a> of 1908 did not\ncontain a provision for mistake in the body of the Act. <a href=\"/doc/1018551/\" id=\"a_338\">Article 96</a> of that Act,\nhowever, laid down a period of three years for a suit &quot;For relief on the ground\nof mistake&quot; and provided that the starting point shall be &quot;When the mistake\nbecomes known to the plainti\ufb02\u00b0.&quot; .\n\nThus, fraud&#x27; and mistake are the matters dealt with in <a href=\"/doc/1991893/\" id=\"a_339\">section 17(1)</a>. Sub-\nsection (2) of the section, relating to fraud or force preventing the execution of a\ndecree, isiderived from section 48(2) proviso <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_340\">of the Code of Civil Procedure</a>,\n1908. -\n\n&#x27;Rolfe v. Gregory, (1864) 4 De G.J. &amp; S. 576. 579 (Lord Westbury).\n&#x27;Law Commission of India. 3rd Report (Limitation Act, 1908) page 21, para 48-49.\n3Section 26, Limitation Act, 1939 (Eng.). p\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 17--\u00bbSecti0n l7----Fraud and Mistake)\n\nSection 48 was repealed (as recommended by the Law Commission in its\nreport on the Limitation Act, 1908) but it was necessary to retain, on the statute\nbook, this part of section 48 and the Law Commission recommended that it\nshould \ufb01nd a place in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_341\">Limitation Act</a>, in the section dealing with fraud.&#x27;\n\n17.3. (1) Subject to sub-section (3) of this section, where in the case of any\naction for&#x27;which a period of limitation is prescribed by this Act, either---_\n\n(a) the action is based upon the fraud of the defendant; or\n\n(b) any fact relevant to the plaintiffs right of action has been deliberately\nconcealed from him by the defendant; or ( &#x27;\n\n(c) the action is for relief from the consequences of a mistakes;\n\nthe period of limitation shall not begin to run(until the plaintiff has disco-\nvered the fraud&#x27;, concealment or mistake (as the case may be) or could with\nreasonable diligence have discovered it.\n\n. _ (2) For the purposes of the last foregoing sub-&quot;section, deliberate commission\nof a breach of duty in circumstances in which it is unlikely to be discovered for\nsome time amounts to deliberate concealment of the facts involved in that breach\nof duty.\n\n&#x27; (3_) Nothing in this section shall enable any action----\n(a) to recover, or recover the value of, any property; or\n\n(b) to enforce any charge against, or set aside any transaction affecting, any .\nproperty; - A\n\nI\nto be brought against the purchaser of the property or any person claiming\nthrough him in any case where the property has been purchased for valuable\nconsideration by an innocent third party since the fraud or concealment or (as\nthe case may be) the transaction in which the mistake was made, took place.\n\n(4) A purchaser is an innocent third party fortthe purposes of this section----\n\n(a)&#x27; in the case of fraud or concealment of any fact relevant to the plaintiffs\nright of action, if he was not a. party to the fraud or (as the case may\n\n. &#x27; be) to the concealment of {hat fact and did not at the time of the pur-\nchase know or have rear:-r. to believe that the fraud or concealment\nhad taken place; and &#x27;\n\n(b) in the case of mistake if he did not at the time of the purchase know or\nhave reason to believe that the mistake had been made.\n\n_ Case law that has accumulated on the above provision in England though&#x27;\n. &#x27; (Eng.)., &#x27;\n3<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_344\">Limitation Amendment Act</a> 1980 c. 24 (Eng).\n\ns&#x27;--14 Mot&#x27; _m;cA/ND/as I\n\n\n\n50\n\nf\n\nWide meaning\nto the word\n\n&quot;fra.ud&#x27;1n\n\nEngland. W\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or lNDlA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n&#x27; (Chapter l7----<a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_345\">Section 17</a>%-Fraud and Mistake)\n\n17.4. The English authorities have been interpreting the word &#x27;fraud&#x27; very\nwidely and have been regarding dishonesty as fraud for the purposes of the Limi-\ntation Act, even&#x27; if the conduct in question, would not constitute fraud at com-\nmon law.&#x27;-&quot; &#x27;\n\n&#x27;\\ &quot;The contention on behalf of the appellants that the statute is a bar unless\nthe wrong &#x27;doer is proved to have taken active measures in order to prevent deten-\ntion is opposed to common sense as well as to the principles of equity. Two men,\nacting independently, steal a neighbour&#x27;s .coa1. One is so clumsily in his opera-\ntions, or so incautious, that he has to do something more in order to conceal his\nfraud. The other chooses his opportunity so wisely, and acts so warily, that he\ncan safely calculate on not being found out for many _,a long day. Why is the\none to get sclot-free at the end of a limited period rather than the other? It would\nbe something of a mockery for courts of equity to denounce fraud as &quot;a secret\n\nthing&quot;, and to profess to punish it sooner or later, and then to hold out a reward&#x27;\n\nfor the cunning that makes detection di\ufb01icult or remote.&quot;\n\nWe do not, of course, propose any change in the article in our Act on this\npoint. I\n&#x27; I\n\n0\n\n17.5. In relation to <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_346\">section 17</a>, we \ufb01rst deal with a few cases relating to fraud\nsubsequent to accrual of the cause of action. In a Calcutta case3, the father of a\nminor obtained a decree, butdied before its execution. The judgment debtor got\nhimself appointed as guardian of the minofs property, but did&#x27; not disclose to the\nCourt his indebtedness&#x27; to the minor under the decree. It was held that the judg-\nment debtor, once he became a guardian, was under an obligation to make a\nfull disclosure to the court of his indebtedness.\na fraud, both on the court and on the minor.\n\nThe Madras Law Journal, commenting on thiscase (while recognising that\nsubstantial justice was done in the case),;doubted whether an act of fraud com-\nmitted after accrual of the cause of action is within the section.&#x27;\n\nThe view of the Madras High CouIt5-iisalso to the effect that section 18 of\nthe Limitation Act, 1908 cannot apply to a case..where there was no fraud at the\ndate when the cause &#x27;of action arose, but a subsequent act of fraud was relied on\n\n. to save limitation.\n\nThe Patna High Court&#x27;, while commenting upon <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_347\">section 18</a> (of the Act or\n\nl908) in connection with an application under Order 21. rule 90 of the Civil Pro-p\n\ncedure&#x27;Code, 1908, observed :-\n\n&quot;It is clear from the language ofthis section that the petitioner,&#x27; &#x27;in order _\n\nto get the bene\ufb01t of it, must satisfy the Court that he had been kept from\nthe knowledge of his right to \ufb01le an application to set aside the sale by the\nopposite. party. His right to set aside the sale clearly accrues after the sale.\nTherefore, the fraud perpetrated by the opposite party must be a fraud com-\nmitted after the sale and not fraud committed in bringing about the sale: and\nthe fraud must be one by which the petitioner has been kept from the know-\nledge of his right to \ufb01le the application} to set aside the sale.&#x27;-&#x27;\n\nIn the absence of later case law continuing the controversy, no change is\nneeded.\n\n1Beaman v. Unlike the English law (where there is no general statutory de\ufb01nition Th, 1,3,,&quot; ~y\nof &quot;fraud&quot;), the <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_348\">Indian Contract Act, 1872</a> de\ufb01nes &quot;fraud&quot;. Case law that has l3W- -\n\n, developed in India around this statutory de\ufb01nition governs the concept of fraud.\n\nso far as that Act is concerned. Limiting ourselves to the law as contained in sec-\n\ntion 17 of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_349\">Limitation Act</a>, we \ufb01nd that it woul not be inconsistent with the\n\nscheme of things if we give a wider meaning and content to the word &quot;fraud&quot; for ,\nthe purpose of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_350\">Limitation Act</a>, leaving the de\ufb01nition of &quot;fraud&quot; in the <a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_351\">Contract\nAct</a> unaltered. That is to say, on proof, of the existence of certain circumstances,\na plaintiff would be able to cross the \ufb01rst hurdle of limitation and to seek post-\nponement of the point from which time shall begin to run against him under the\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_352\">Limitation Act</a>. This would be regardless of the question whether the matter is\ntried on merits, the same set of circumstances is to be regarded as suf\ufb01eient to\nenable him to obtain a judgment against the defendant on the ground of &quot;fraud&quot;\nas a matter of substantive rights. \u00bb\n\n~ r\n\n17.7. The&#x27; facts and circumstances\nof a particular case may be such as to persuade the High Court to exercise\nits discretion in favour of the party claiming refund even beyond the period\nof three years from the date of his knowledge of the mistake. Then there is a Madras case also on the subject. In a suit \ufb01led by\nParry and Company against the State of Tamil Nadu on the original Side of -\n. the Madras High Court&#x27;, the question of applicability of section l7(l)(c) came up\nfor consideration. Parry and Company, a dealer in engineering equipments (for\ngoods imported under the Import Trade Control Permit) were taxed by the sales\ntax authorities on certain transactions as local sales for the assessment year upto\n1966-67-. &#x27;In view of the decision of the Supreme Court in Kh0sla&#x27;s case&#x27; the\nplaintiffs became entitled to refund of the sum of Rs. 43,659/- paid by way of\nsales tax for the recovery of which the suit was \ufb01led. The Supreme Court judg-\nment was delivered on 18th January 1966 and reported on 1st May 1966. The\ncourt found, as a question of fact, that the plaintiff had knowledge about the\njudgment on 14-3-68. Another case&quot; relating to the refund of sales tax recovered under mis-\ntake of law--this time from Madhya Pradesh brings out how the omission from\nthe Act of 1963 of <a href=\"/doc/1018551/\" id=\"a_358\">article 96 (which occurred in the Act of 1968)</a> makes no sub-\nstantial difference to the position. The following observations are pertinent:\n\n&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_359\">Under the Indian Limitation Act</a>,&#x27; 1908, a claim for relief on the\nground of mistake was governed by <a href=\"/doc/1018551/\" id=\"a_360\">art. 96</a> and &#x27;-time commenced to run\nfrom the date when theqmistake becomes known to the plaintiff. How-\n,ever&#x27;, section l7(1)(c) of this&#x27; new Act provides that in the case of a suit\n\nfor relief from the consequences of a mistake, the period of limitation shall&#x27;\n\nnot begin to run until the plaintiff has discovered the mistake or could with\nreasonablevdiligence have discovered it.&quot;\n\n17.16. The upshort of the above discussion is&#x27; that even though Shiv Shankar\nDal Mills case expounds a theory that there is no law of limitation for returning\nwhat was wrongly recovered, this observation was intended to indicate only\nthe width of the writ jurisdiction. It should not be taken to mean that the courts\nshould ignore the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_366\">section 17</a> read with <a href=\"/doc/10691/\" id=\"a_367\">article 113</a> of the Limitation\nActand decree a time--barred suit.\n\n17.17. In the result, the only change needed inscetion 17 is the addition of&#x27;\n\nan Explanation to deal with conduct which is unconscionable, having regard\nto the special relationship between the parties--a point already dealt with earlier\nin this Chapter.&#x27; For this purpose, we recommend the insertion of the following\n\n&#x27; Explanation below section l7:----\n\n&quot;Explanation.--~F0r the purposes of (this section, &#x27;fraud&#x27; includes con-\nduct on the part of the defendant or the opposite party, as the case may be,\nwhich, having regard to some special relatiohship between the parties, was\nunconscionable.&quot; 3\n\nCHAPTER &#x27;l8\n\nSECTION 18: ACKNOWLEDGMENT &#x27; &#x27;\n\n18.1. Where a suit or application is in respect of any property or right, an\nacknowledgement of liability in respect &quot;of such property or right, made in writ-\n\n&#x27; ing signed by the party against whom the property or right is now claimed (or\n\nby his predecessor-in-interest), gives a fresh period of limitation, to be computed\nfrom the time when the acknowledgement v\ufb01ts so signed. This is the gist of\n<a href=\"/doc/1529784/\" id=\"a_368\">section 18(1)</a>. <a href=\"/doc/1464198/\" id=\"a_369\">Section 18(2)</a> deals with the cases in which oral evidence of the\n\n-contents of the acknowledgment may be given.\n\nThe Explanation to the section, in clause (a), lays down that an acknowledg-\nment may be su\ufb02icient though it omits to specify the exact nature of the pro-\nperty or right, or avers that the time at payment etc. has not yet arrived, or is\n\naccompanied by a refusal to pay etc., or is coupled with a claim to set off, or is\n\naddressed -to a person entitled to the property or right. 7\n\nUnder Explanation (b) to the section the expression &quot;signed&quot; means signed\n\n&quot; either personally or by an agent duly authorised in this behalf\u00bb\n\nExplanation (c) provides that an application for&#x27;theI execution of&#x27; a decree\nor order shall not be deemed to be an application in respect&#x27; of property or right&quot;_.\n\n18.2. It was as a result of the recommendations of the Law Commission&quot;\nthat Clause (c) of the Explanation modifying the earlier rules provides that an\napplication for the execution of a decree or order does not make it equivalent to\nan application in respect of any property &#x27;or right (<a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_370\">Section 18</a>). It has similarly\n\n&#x27;See paragraph _17.8. supra: ._ _~ _. _\n2Law Commission of India. 3rd Reports.&#x27; (Lnnitation Act 1908) page 23, para 52\n\n1\n\nand \n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or IIVDIAI ON THE LIMITATION Acr, 1963 .\n\n(C hapicr 18--- -Section 18-Acknowledgement.)\n\nbeen provided that &quot;debt&quot; does not include money payable under a decree or\norder of a court (<a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_371\">section 19</a>)&#x27;. Dr. Whitley Stokes in his Anglo-Indian Codes&#x27; has made the following Person\nsuggestion relevance to the <a href=\"/doc/206324/\" id=\"a_372\">Section 1</a> ---- &quot;5&#x27;d\u00b0&#x27;.&#x27;. So far as could be ascertained, no &#x27;serious controversy exists at present\non_- the form or substance of <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_391\">section 18</a> and we do not therefore see any need to\nrecommend a change in the section. 1 . -\n\nM CHAPTER 19\n\nSECI&#x27; ION 19: PJXRT PAYMENT\n\n19.1. Analagous. to the acknowledgment of liability, dealt with in <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_392\">section 18</a>\nis payment of a part of the amount due, made by the person liable. Such payment\nmay be regarded as an implied acknowledgement, or at least analogous thereto.\nIn any case it preserves evidence, and the demand is no longer a &#x27;stale&#x27; one. The\nsubject is dealt with in the next section--<a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_393\">section 19</a>. The main paragraph of the\nsection provides for computing a fresh period of limitation when the person lia-\nble to pay a debt or legacy (or his agent) makes, on account of the debt or of\ninterest on the legacy, payment before the expiry of the prescribed period. The\n\nroviso to the section enacts that (save in the case of a payment of interest made\nEzof\u00e9re the 1st day of January 1928), an &quot;-acknowledgment of the payment must\na\n\nar in the handwriting of the person making payment, or in a writingsigned\nby that person.&quot; s\n\nThe Explanation to the section consists of two clauses de\ufb01ning in certain\nrespects the ambit of &quot;payment&quot; the ambit of &quot;debt&quot;. In clause (a), it provides\nthat where mortgaged land is in the possession of the mortgagee, the receipt of\nthe rent or produce of such land shall be deemed to be &quot;payment&quot;. Under clause&#x27;\n(b) of the Explanation, &quot;debt&quot; does not&#x27; include money payable under a decree\nor order of a court.\n\n19.2: In the past, the section (as occurring in the Act of 1908 gave rise to\nsome debate as to its application to a suit for redemption.&#x27; It is now well settled&quot;\n\n* that the section applies only to shitspfor the recovery of a debt (and not to a suit\n\nfor the recovery of property). To put the matter beyond doubt, we recommend\n\nthat the <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_394\">section 19</a> should be amended so as to indicate expressly that the&#x27; section -\n\nis meant for a suit of recovery of a debt or legacy.&#x27;\n\nI\n\n19.3.&#x27;The Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_395\">section 19</a> provides that receipt by the mortgagee\nof rent or produce of the land mortgaged &quot;shall be deemed to be a payment&quot;\nHowever,-&#x27;the Explanation does not link itself up with the proviso preceding it,\n\n. which requires that the &quot;payment&quot; should appear, in the handwriting of, orin a\n\nwriting by, the person making the payment. The non-mention of this requirement\nin the Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_396\">section 19</a> has been interpreted to mean that the proviso\ndoes not apply to cases coveredby the Expianation.&#x27; This&quot; construction can be\nsaid to be supportable on the fact that the physical possession of the property be-\ning with the mortgagee, it is not possible for the mortgagor to make the &quot;payment&quot;\nof produce whichthc mortgagee might appropriate to the debt. To put the mat-\n\nter beyond doubt, we had contemplated the addition _of a clause somewhat as\n\nunder: *\n\n&quot;Notwithstanding anything containedin the proviso to this section, it\n\n&#x27; . shall not be necessary to have the acknowledgment required by the proviso\nin respect of such payment&quot;, at the end&#x27; of clause (a) of the Explanation.\nBut the judicial &quot;interpretation being in conformity with the philosophy of\n\n&#x27; &#x27;Mohammad Akbar Khan V. Motczi, A.I.R. 1948 EC. 36. /38.\n&#x27;Mohammad Akbar Khan v. Motai. AJR. 1948 PC. 36; 39.\n&#x27;See para 19.12, infra. for a draft of the revised&#x27; section.\n&#x27;India V. Vanad, A.I.R. 1966 Gui. 59, 60.\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter l9----~SecIion l9--Part Payment.)\n\n&#x27;the section, we are refraining from making any suggestion in this regard.&#x27;\n\nIt is apparent that in the case of land in the possession of the mortgagee, an\nacknowledgment of the payment would not appear in the handwriting of, or in\nthe writing signed by, the, person making the payment for the simple reason that\n-the payer and the payee are one and the same person. As stated above, we are\nnot recommending any clari\ufb01cation in this behalf to the proviso.\n\n19.4. One speci\ufb01c point requires attention in connection with <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_397\">section 19</a>, Section 1_9, _\nI, Explanation (a). Where mortgaged land is in the possession of the mortgagee, E&#x27;:&#x27;\u00a7Il1\u00b0\ufb01&#x27;l3f__&#x27;t&quot;&#x27;a&#x27;:&#x27; (&#x27;*1\nthe receipt of the rent or produce of such land is deemed to be a payment for to &quot;land&quot;.\nthe purposes of that section, thus enabling the computation of a fresh period of\nlimitation from the time the payment is made. Now, the expression &quot;land&quot; has&#x27;\nbeen dilferently interpreted. The Madras High Court,&#x27; construing the word &#x27;rent&#x27;\nas ejusdem generis with &#x27;produce&#x27;, holds that the word &#x27;land&#x27; in the section re-\nfers on1y&quot;to cultivable land and would not include a house--or even the site on\nwhich a house might have been built.\n\n19.5. On the contrary, the Madhya Pradesh High Court} dissenting. from Con\ufb02ict.\n\nthe Madras view} and relying on the meaning as given in judicial dictionaries,\nholds that &#x27;there is no warrant for reading the word &#x27;rent&#x27; as ejusdem generis\nwith &#x27;produce&#x27;. According to it, the section applies not only to the rent received\nfrom the cultivable land, but also to the rent received from a house or house-\nsite.\n\nTo the same effect are rulings of the High Courts of Bombay&#x27;, and Gujarat&#x27;.\n\n19.6. In our opinion, there is no rationale in limiting the. provisions of the Need for\n&#x27;section to cultivable land, simply because the section um the&#x27; word &#x27;produce&#x27;. clari\ufb01cation.\nIn theory, one could enlarge the applicability of the principle to cases involving\nmoveable property, because it is conceivable that a person may hand over his\nproperty to a creditor whogin his turn, can appropriate&#x27; the income derived from\nthe use-thereof towards payment of the debt (e.g. a taxi driver taking a loan from\na bank and hypothecating his taxi to the bank). However, to avoid any further\ncontroversy on the question whether the word &#x27;land&#x27; includes house property\nor not, we recommend that the Explanation to <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_398\">section 19</a> should be suitably\namended by using the expression &#x27;immovable property&#x27; for the word &#x27;1and&#x27;.&#x27;\n\n19.7. Another point concerning <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_399\">section 19</a> Explanation (a) concerns the <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_400\">Section 19</a>\n\nword &#x27;mortgage&#x27;, as occurring in this explanation. There is no reason why Explanation (a)-\nclause (a) of the Explanation should not include a &#x27;charge&#x27;. But the Chief Court \u00e9&#x27;h1:gl\u00e9::t&#x27;\u00b0&quot; &#x27;\u00b0\n&#x27;of Oudh&#x27; has held otherwise, and, to avoid controversy on this point, it is desi-\n\n~ rable that the section should be suitably amended to cover a case of &#x27;charge&#x27;\n\n&quot;also.&quot;-9\n\n&#x27;Gudur Ramalgrishna Reddi v. Muniratnammal, A.I.R. 1954 Madras 890. 893; I.L.R.\n(1954) Mad. 1226: (1954) l M.L.J. 546.\n\n2Radha Krishna V. Chennamalai, (1970) 1 M.L.J. 341, 343.\n\n&#x27;See para 19.10, infra for a draft.\n\n9-14 M of LJ&amp;CAINDI83\n\n\n\n58\n\nPayment by\ncheque.\n\n,Need for\nclari\ufb01cation\n\n&#x27;Agreement for\ndeferring\npresentation\nof the\n\ncheque. _\n\n89rH -=&quot;&lt;F.POR,T or I.-xw co\\IMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter l9--Section l9----Part Payment.)\n\n19.8. &#x27;We. now turn to the question of payment by cheque. With the pro-\ngress of institutionalised banking, payment by cheque has become the normal\n\npractice, especially in urban areas. If the cheque, accepted by the creditor, is\n\nultimately&#x27; honoured, no problems present themselves. The &quot;payment&quot; consti-\ntutes part payment.&#x27; In fact, Halsburyi&#x27; has gone further to say that &quot;the accep-\ntance by&#x27;the debtor of a bill drawn upon him by a creditor, or the delivery to\nthe creditor of a bill drawn by the debtor on a third person, on account of part\nof the debt, is also su\ufb02icient part payment, whether the bill is paid at maturity\nor not; it seems that in that case the part payment is deemed to be made at\nthe time of the delivery of the bill, and not when it is paid.&quot; The effect, it is\nstated, would be the same if a cheque is given on the day, with an agreement\nthat the same should not be presented to the bank until a later day and, acting\n\n_ upon the agreement. the creditor presents the cheque on the agreed day and\n\nreceives payment?\n\nHowever, opinion in India on the subject is not uniform. The High Courts\nof Bombay&#x27;, Patna5, and Punjab&#x27; have held that the accpetance of a cheque\nwhich is subsequently dishonoured does not constitute &quot;payment&quot; within the\nmeaning of <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_401\">section 19</a>. The Madhya Pradesh High Court has held to the con-\ntrary.&#x27; According to the Andhra High Court. whether payment by cheque\namounts to &quot;payment&quot; depends on whetherthe payment was accepted as such\nor not.&#x27;\n\nI 19.9. There appears to be a substantial case for clari\ufb01cation on the sub&#x27;-\nject. We see no reason in principle why the bene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_402\">section 19</a> should not\nbe extended to such cases, I&#x27;.e., to cases where a cheque is accepted in payment\nirrespective of whether it is honoured or not. Such a wide View would not be\ndoing violence to the rationale&quot; on which payment is regarded as giving a\n\nfresh lease of life.rEven in the substantive law, so far as a dishonoured cheque ,\n\nis concerned, a new cause of action accrues upon delivery of the cheque to the\ncreditor&quot;.\n\n19.10. We are not oblivious to the fact that whilg giving and accepting a\n. . . O\ncheque, the debtor and the creditor may enter Into a private agreement that\n\nthe creditor will defer &#x27;the actual presentation of the cheque to some agreed&#x27;\n\nfuture date, by which date the debtor is expected to arrange to ensure adequate\nfunds to his credit with the drawee. Even in such a contingency, there appears\nto be no reason why the bene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_403\">section 19</a> should not be extended to the\ncreditor. If, in the hypothetical situation mentioned above, the debtor manages\nto have adequate funds in his account by the agreed date, none is worse off. If\nthe debtor fails so to do and litigation becomes necessary, a fresh period of\n\na limitation would accrue in favour of the creditor. By not allowing the bene\ufb01t\n\nof <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_404\">section 19</a> to cases of dishonoured cheques. the law may precipitate matters\nand drive&#x27;the creditor to court. by denying the parties a chance to settle their\naccounts amicably. &#x27;\n\n1Marreco v. RiI&#x27;-&#x27;Im&#x27;dsorI. M908) 2 KB. 584 (C.A.).\n\n31-lalsbuigv, 4th Ed. Vol. 28. page 404, para 900 and f.n. ll.\n\n3Marreco V. Riclimdsolr (1908) 2 K.B. 584; (1908-12) All E.R. Rep. 55 (C.A.).\n&#x27;Chimtaman v. .Sndguru Narain Maharai. A.I.R. 1956 Born. 553, 554. _\n\n5Ariun Lal v. Daya Ram. A.I.R. 1971 Pat. 278, 279, 280.\n\n&quot;Northern India Finance Corpn. V. R. L. Soni, A.I.R. 1973 Punjab 35, 37, para 5.\n&quot;Geri Lal v. Ramjee, A.l.R. 1961 MP. 346. 347, para 5. ,\n3Subrahaman_vam V. Venknraratlznmn, A.I_R_ 1956 Andhra &#x27;108.\n\n9Cf. para 19 1, supra.\n\n&quot;Halbury, 4th Ed., Vol. 28, page 300, para .668.\n\n\n\n_89&#x27;1&#x27;1~i REPORT or LAW cumin. or INDIA on THE L1MrrA&#x27;1&#x27;ioN ACT, 1963 59\n\n(Chapter l9-~Section l9----Part Payment.)\n(Chapter 20------<a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_405\">Section 20</a>~~A L&#x27;l&lt;tI(}Wl:&#x27;dg(&#x27;l)Ic&#x27;I!I or Payment By Another Person.)\n\n19.11. For these reasons, we consider it desirable that payment by cheque\nshould be expressly covered, on lines to be indicated in the amendment that\nwe are suggesting.&#x27;\n\n19.12. In the light 01&quot; the above discussion. we recominentl that section [9 Recommendation.&#x27;\nshould be revised as indicated below :--\n\nRevised <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_406\">Section 19</a>\n\nWhere payment on account of a debt or of interest on a legacy is made\nbefore the expiration of the prescribed period for a suit or application for the\nrecovery of such debt or legacy by the person liable to pay the debt or legacy\nor by his agent duly authorised in this behalf, a fresh period of limitation shall\nbe computed from the time when the payment was made. in respect of any\nsuit or application for the recovery of such debt or legacy, as the case may be.\n\nProvided that, save in the case of payment of interest made before &#x27;the\n\ufb01rst day of January, 1928, an acknowledgement of the payment appears in the\nhandwriting of, or in a writing signed by. the person making the payment.\n\n_ Explanation 1.--For the purposes of this section. &#x27;\n\n(a)\u00ab Where immovable property which is mortgaged or charged is in the\npossession of the mortgagee or charge-holder, the receipt of the in-\ncome of such property shall be deemed to be a payment.\n\n(b) &quot;debt&quot; does not include money payable under a decree or order of a\ncourt.\n\nExplanation II.--Where payment on account of a debt or of interest on a\nlegacy is sought to be made by a negotiable instrument, the tender of the nego-\ntiable instrument, if accepted by the payee in such payment, amounts to pay-\nment of the amount for the purposes of this section whether or not the nego-\ntiable instrument is subsequently honoured.\n\nCHAPTER 20\n\nSECTION 20: ACKNOWLEDGMENT OR PAYMENT BY ANOTHER\nPERSON &#x27;\n\n1. Introductory\n\n20.1. The two sections discussed in the preceding chaptersmsections l8 <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_407\">section 20</a>,\n(acknowledgment) and 19 (payment) -operate in situations of considerable &#x27;\nvariety. Thus, in business and commerce. where debts and other liabilities are\ndealt with through agentsmthe more so when&quot; the debtor is a corporate body-\nthere might, be cases where more persons, than one share the burden of a lia-\nbility, but the correspondence is carried on by only one of them. Hindu law\nalso presents certain peculiar situations. There may be limited owners of pro-\nperty, on the expiry of whose limited interest others will succeed to the property.\n\nOr,&#x27; which is the more common case now~--a liability may have been incurred\nby or on behalf of a Hindu undivided family as such, but its affairs are (as is\nthe usual practice) managed by the manager, who transacts dealings on its be-\nhalf. In all these cases, the question may arise -whether, and if so, how far, a\n\n&#x27;Para 19.12, infra.\n\n\n\noint\nobligecs.\n\nEnglish [\nActs.\n\n8911! REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON &#x27;THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n(Chapter 20----Section 20-A cknowledgemenz or Payment By Another Person.)\n\nperson is bound by an acknowledgment signed by another person e.g. (i) agent,\n(ii) another joint contractor etc. (iii) or limited owner in Hindu law or manager\nof a Hindu undivided family. To put it differently, to what extent is a person\nvicariously bound by an acknowledgment or payment made by another person,\nin the context of the statutory provisions under which the acknowledgment or\npayment has the effect of giving a fresh period of limitation.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_408\">Section 20</a> seeks to answer these questions, in three sub-sections--Sub-sec-\ntion (1) gives, to the express &quot;agent yduly authorised in this half&quot;----an expres-\nsion that occurs in <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_409\">section 18 (acknowledgment)</a> as well as -in <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_410\">section 19</a> (pay-\nment),--an extended meaning, whereunder, in the case of a person under disabi-\nlity, it includes his lawful guardian, committee or manager or an agent of such\nguardian, committee or manager. --\n\nSub-section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_411\">section 20</a> reads as under:--\n\n-&quot;(/2) Nothing in the said section [i.e., sections 18-19] renders one of several\njoint contractors, partners, executors or mortgagees chargeable by reason\nonly of a written acknowledgement signed by, or of a payment made by, or\nby the agent of, any other or others of them.&quot;\n\nSub-section (3) deals with the special situation arising under Hindu\nlaw, mentioned above. &#x27;Brie\ufb02y;-(a) an acknowledgment or .payment in\nrespect of liability by a limited owner of property governed by Hindu law\nis valid as against a reversioner succeeding to such liability and, (b) an ac-\nknowledgement or payment in respect of a liability (incurred by or on be-\nhalf of a Hindu undivided family), made by the manager of the family for\nthe time being is deemed to have been made on behalf of the whole family.\n\nThis is a statement of the signi\ufb01cant features of the section. We now pro-\nceed to consider some of the important points.\n\n11. Joint. Obli-gees\n\n20.2. A point worth some discussion arises from <a href=\"/doc/654160/\" id=\"a_412\">section 20(2)</a>. <a href=\"/doc/654160/\" id=\"a_413\">Section\n20(2)</a> provides that nothing in <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_414\">sections 18</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_415\">19</a> (dealing with the effect of\n\nacknowledgment in writing or effect of payment on account of debt) renders, 3\n\none of several execution on mortgageesvchargeable by reason only of a written\nacknowledgment signed by, or of a payment made by or by the agent of, any\nother or others of them. The controversies that require discussion can be better\nappreciated if a look is had at the English Law on the subject and its execu-\ntion.-. &#x27;\n\n20.3. In England ruling&quot; by Lord Mans\ufb01eld&#x27; that any acknowledgment or\npayment &#x27;by one co-debtor was su\ufb02icient to take the case out of the statute of\nlimitation, even as regards other co-debtors, raised a number of protests which\nresulted in curbing the ambit of the rule, as regards acknowledgments, by Lord\nTenderten&#x27;s Act? and, as regards payments, by the Mercantile Law Amend-\n\n&#x27; _ ment Act? The language of Lord T enderten&#x27;s Act was as under:--\n\n&quot;No such joint contractors, executors or administrator shall lose the bene- &#x27;\n\n\ufb01t of the said enactmentsl(i.e., enactments as to limitation) so as to be\n&#x27; chargeable in respect or by reason only of any written acknowledgment or\npromise made and signed by any other or others of them.&quot; .\n\n&#x27;Withcomb v. A recent ruling&#x27; of\nAndhra Pradesh High Court deals with a case of joint contractors who have\nsigned a prornisory note. it was held that even though one of the promisors\nwas the son of the other two promisors, that cannot make him liable for the\nendorsement made by the other two promisors on the promisory Note.\n\nThe word &quot;only&quot; was again noticed in a Bombay casef&#x27; which ruled that\nthe meaning to be given to the word &#x27;only&#x27; in <a href=\"/doc/86701/\" id=\"a_426\">section 21(2)</a> is that it may, also\nbe shown that the partners signing the a_cknowledg1ncnt had authority, express\nor implied, to do so and that, in a going mercantile concern, such agency is to\nbe presumed as an ordinary rule.\n\n20.10. Similar is the approach adopted when the question arises&#x27; as to the\nelfect of an acknowledgment by the principal&quot; on the obligation of the surety\nand the repercussions of the acknowledgment by the surety&#x27; upon the principal.\nMost of the High Courts are agreed that the liability of a surety cannot be\nequated to that of a co-debtor or joint--contractor within the &#x27;meaning of sec-\ntion 20(2) of the Act5 and that the debt of the surely is distinguished from the\ndebt of the principal debtor.&quot;\n\nThe Bombay High Court in one case&#x27; observed:\n\n&quot;The payment of interest by the debtor within limitation does not give\nfresh starting point for limitation against the surety under <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_427\">section 20</a> of\nthe Limitation Act (XV of 1877) even in the absence of a prohibition by\nsurety against the payment of interest by the debtor on his account. Let\nus apply the words of <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_428\">section 20</a> to the case: the principal is not the per-\nson liable to pay the debt of the surety, so that even if the payment of\ninterest could be regarded as a payment of interest on the debt of the\nsurety, still it was not made by a person liable to pay the surety&#x27;s debt.\nCan it then be said that there was a payment of interest on the surety&#x27;s\ndebt by an agent duly authorised in this behalf? Apart from the di\ufb02iculty\noftreating the interest as due on the surety&#x27;s debt&#x27;. we think this must be\nanswered in the negative, the question propounded in the reference ex-\ncludes an express authority, and (in our opinion) the relation of principal\nand surety does not give rise to any implied authority.&quot;\n\nIn this context it may be proper to emphasis that in a going mercantile\n\nconcern a partner has an implied authority I0 bind l.i1C Oiil\ufb02   \nacknowledgment of liability 5 -&quot; _-\n\n20.11. But the exposition of this sub-section\nby the Courts (as discussed above) shows that the Courts are examining the facts\nand circumstances of each case and ascertaining whether, an agency (express or\nimplied) is made out therefrom. In this position, we do not propose any amend-\nment inthis regard.\n\n20.12. As regards joint contractors and mortgagees, certain questions-arise persona: jaw.&quot;\nwhen Mohamedan co-heirs succeed to the estate of the deceased in severalty. The d\u00b0e&#x27;:i3&quot;3e\npart-payment by one of the Mohamedan co-heirs cannot avail to save limitation &#x27;\nagainst the other heirs.&#x27; This also is a matter of the personal law of succession\nand falls outside the scope of the present enquiry. Different considerations apply\nto the case of an acknowledgment of the liability by a Hindu father&quot; but. as a\ngeneral rule, it can be stated that where a part-payment by one of the joint deb-\ntors is made, the mere presence of the other debtors at the place of payment will\nnot save limitation. unless it is proved that they also made the payment or that the\nperson who actually paid it did so, being authorised by the others?\n\nConsequently. we do not recommend any change in the text of <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_431\">section 29(2)</a>\non this count.\n\n20.13. We have given anxious thought to the question whether we should No change\nrecommend deletion of the words &#x27;joint contractors, partners. executors or mort- gggf\ufb01&#x27; &#x27;goal\ngagees&quot;and bring the language of sub-section (2) back to the stage it was in sec- &#x27;\ntion 4 of the Limitation Act, 1859. thereby doing away with the embellishments\n\nand illustrations added to the section in the course of time. However, we do not\n\n&#x27; think that this would amount to a practical improvement and are not inclined to\n&#x27; recommend any such change.\n\n20.14. The position is that in applying <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_432\">section 20</a> the court will have to bear Substantive\nin mind not merely the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_433\">Limitation Act</a>, but also the general law relating to them&quot;-\nparticular \ufb01eld. such. as agency, joint promises, suretiship, guarantee, partner-\nship and succession. Such an inquiry cannot be avoided. Courts have taken a\ncommonsense view of the matter. &#x27;\n\n20.15. In the result. no change is needed in <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_434\">section 20</a>. Iigdihlange\n/&#x27; I&#x27; .\n\nCHAPTER 21\nSECI&#x27; ION 21 : ADDITION QR SUBSTITUTION OF PARTIES .\n\n1. Introductory\n\n21.1. The sections of the Act that have been so far considered were about <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_435\">Section 21-</a>\ncomputing the period of limitationwsomething concerning the period prior to\u00a7&#x27;\u00a7\u00a7f:io\u00b0If or\nthe institution of proceedings. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_436\">The Act</a> now, (in <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_437\">section 21</a>) devotes some atlen-substitution\ntion to determination of the date when the proceedings are deemed to have beenggl 11&#x27;.1*&#x27;r;&#x27;i&#x27;t&#x27;:\ufb01 om\ninstituted. The general rule, of course. is that a suit commences when the plaint -\n\nis presented.&#x27; This rule creates no problems when all the parties are before the\n\n&#x27; Court from the very beginning. However. often it so happens that in the course\n\n&#x27;Pathumma Boovi v. Rlrziakrislma Memm, A.l.R. 1975 Ker. 91.\n2Deen Narain v. Bhagauzi Din, A.I.R. 1952 All. 116.\n\nMnnoda Charon V-. Jhatu Charan, A.l,R. 1935 Cal. 648,\n&#x27;<a href=\"/doc/1550355/\" id=\"a_438\">Section 3(2)(a)</a>.\n\n\n\n&#x27;64\n\n\\\n\\\n\nNewly added\nproviso to\n<a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_439\">section 21(1)</a>,\nand its\nexposition by\nthe Supreme\nCourt.\n\nlaw before\nthe addition\nof the\nproviso.\n\n89TH REPORT or Lxw COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\nChapter 2I--~--Section --2I~Addit(ion or Substitutioir of Parties\n\nof litigation, a new plaintiff or defendant is substituted or added. Insuch a case.\nthe general rule, if taken literally, cannot apply, and one needs a speci\ufb01c provi-\nsion in that regard. That is to be found in <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_440\">section 21</a>.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_441\">Section 21(1)</a> enacts (in the main paragraph) that as regards a new plainti\ufb01 or,\n\ndefendant substituted or added after the institution of a suit, the suit is deemed\n\n- to have been instituted &quot;when he was made a party&quot;--a date which would neces-\n\nsarily&#x27;be later than the date of presentation of the plaint.\n\nThere is, however, a proviso to sub-section (1}--inserted at the instance of\nthe Law Commission1----under which, where the court is satis\ufb01ed that the omis-\nsion to include a new plaintiff or defendant was due to a mistake made in good\nfaith, it may direct that the suit shall, as regards such plaintiff or defendant. be\ndeemed to have been instituted on any earlier date. &#x27;\n\nSub-section (2)- of <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_442\">section 21</a> makes it clear that sub-section (1) does not\napply to. the following two cases :--\n\n(i) where the addition or substitution is due to assignment or divolution\nof any interest during the pendency of a suit. or.\n\n(ii) where the parties are transposed.\n\nThe reason for making this clari\ufb01cation_is obvious. In the first case. the\npredecessor-in-interest of the added party was already on the record. and the\nsuit is only sought to be continued against the successor. In the second case. the\nparty transposed was himself on record, though arrayed on the opposite side.\n\n21.2. As stated above acting upon the recommendations of the Law Com-\nmission,&#x27; a proviso has been added to sub-section (1) of <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_443\">section 21</a> to enable the\ncourt to direct that a suit shall as regards a newly added plaintiff or defendant.\nbe deemed to have been instituted on an earlier date, if certain conditions are\n\nsatis\ufb01ed.\n\nThe Supreme Court also regretted that this newly added proviso had no\napplication to the facts of the case before it, with the result that it had no power\nto direct that the suit should be deemed to have been instituted on a date ear-\nlier than&#x27;November 4, 1958. &#x27; I\n\n21.3. Before the addition of the proviso in <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_444\">section 21(1)</a>, there was consi-&#x27;\nderable difference of opinion as regards the power of the court to add new par-\nties on a date when the relevant limitation period had already expired. In a\nCalcutta case} an eminent judge, Ameer Ali J. refused to permit the additioniof\none of the partners after the expiry of the limitation period and observed:--\n\n-&quot;What was the reason for leaving him (i.e., one of the partners) out I do not\nknow. These people have innumerable combinations and for all kinds of\nreason, \ufb01rms with different names but t11e_same partners, \ufb01rms with the\nsame names and different partners and so forth. They take undue advan-\n\ntage of the facilities provided by the law and they must not be surprised if V\n\nthe law sometimes takes advantage of them&quot;.\n\nSame was the view of the High Courts of Madras.&#x27; Rajasthan-&#x27; and Jammu\nand Kashmir.&quot;\n\n&#x27;Cf.
