On the other hand, in a Madras case, a foreign \ufb01rm instituted a\nsuit in its own name which it could not do in view of Order 30, Rule 1, <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_448\">Code of\nCivil Procedure</a>s, 1908. Upon an objection being raised regarding the maintain-,\nability of the suit, the \ufb01rm sought an amendment of the plaint by the substitu-\ntion of the names of the partners. It was held thaHhere was no addition of new\nplaintiffs, but merely a classi\ufb01cation of the individuals already on record.&#x27; It is\nunnecessary to multiply the authorities. \u00bb 5\n\n21.6. The Calcutta? case was criticised, and dissented from by the Madhya\nPradesh High Court&#x27; in which a comparison has been drawn between the draft-\ning of Order 30, Rule 2 of Civil Procedure Code and Order I, Rule 10 thereof.\nWhereas the latter speci\ufb01cally makes a mention of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_449\">Limitation Act</a>, the for-\nmer does not do so and the court for this reason concluded that in the absence\nof any reference to <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_450\">section 22</a> of the Limitation Act in Order 30, Rule 2, <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_451\">Code\nof Civil Procedure</a>, 1908, it would not be proper to apply it to the disclosure of\nnames. The omission to sign the plaint by one partner was allowed to be cured\nin a case from Himachal Pradesh,&#x27; on the ground that this was not a case of\naddition of new &#x27;plaintiff.\n\n21.7. A peculiar situation arose in a Patna casef where the names of only\ntwo out of \ufb01ve partners appeared in the certi\ufb01cate of registration andthe remain-\ning partners were sought to be added as proforma defendants after the expiry\nof the period of limitation. By harmonious construction of <a href=\"/doc/797638/\" id=\"a_452\">section 69</a> of the\nPartnership Act, Order 30, Rule 1, <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_453\">of the Code of Civil Procedure</a> and <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_454\">section\n22</a> of the Limitation Act of 1908 (corresponding to present <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_455\">section 21</a>), the court\n\n. allowed the application on the ground that the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_456\">section 22</a> of the\nLimitation Act 1908 were not attracted in such a case.\n\n21.8. The enabling powers of this proviso have been utilised for the bene\ufb01t Newly added.\nof the party who, on account of a bona \ufb01de mistake, had failed to add a neces- Ef\ufb01fs\ufb01a&#x27; by\nsary party to a suit. Subsequent rulings are con-\nveniently collected in a Madras case? The Supreme Court has ruled&#x27; that failure\n\nto describe oneself as a &#x27;manager&#x27; in the plaint is not decisive of the question\n\nwhether the suit was instituted by him in his capacity as a manager, this will\ndepend upon the circumstances &#x27;of each case. It could well be that the suit\nis instituted by the Manager in his personal capacity or it could be that he\nrepresented the family. The Supreme Court summarised the law as follows:----\n\n&quot;In a suit by the manager of a joint Hindu family for enforcement of a\nmortgage, an adult member who is interested in the mortgage security, is\nnot a necessary party though he can be joined as a proper party; and failure\nto join a person who is a proper party not necessary party, does not affect\nthe maintainability of the suit nor does it invite the application of <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_464\">section\n22</a>, Limitation Act.&quot;\n\n21.17. In the above position we recommend that suitable words may be\nadded in <a href=\"/doc/86701/\" id=\"a_465\">section 21 (2)</a> on the above point. The amendment that is being re-\ncommended&#x27; is merely intended to codify the well understood position as to\nsuits_concerning the alfairs of the Hindu Undivided Family to which the managing\nmember of the family is a party.\n\n21.18. It may be incidentally mentioned that under Order 30, Rule 10, as\namended in 1976, a Hindu undivided Family carrying on business has been equa-\nted with a \ufb01rm for the purposes of the rules contained in that order. But even\nbefore that amendment. the Bombay High&#x27;Court5 had allowed the substitution of\nthe names&#x27;ofp the co-parceners in regard to what was admittedly a joint family\nbusiness, on the ground that it is no more than a correction of mis-description,\nand not an addition of a party&#x27;. V -\n\nIII. E\ufb01ective Date\n\n21.19. We now come to one point of detail. In <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_466\">section 21(1)</a>, it has been\nprovided that the suit shall, as regards the added party, be deemed to have been\ninstituted when he was so made a party. The expression &quot;when he wasjso\nmade a party&quot; has given rise to three different interpretations.\n\n21.20. The first view is to the effect that a person becomes a party only\nwhen he is actually brought on the record as party. The Madras judgment&#x27; on\nthe subject observes as under:----\n\n&quot;It cannot be said that a suit is instituted, if the plaintiff, who wants to\n\ufb01le it in court, puts it into his drawer or keeps it in vakils&#x27; chambers. In\nthe same manner, when a person is sought to be added as party, the person\nbecomes a party only when he is actually brought on record as a party.&quot;\n\nllmdad Ahmed v. Pabitra Partap Narain (1910) I.L.R. 32 All 241 (P.C.).\n\n&#x27;~&#x27;Venkatanarayana v. Somragu. A.I.R. 1937 Mad. 610.\n\n3Devidas V. Shrishailappa, A.I.R. 1961 S.C. 1937.\n\n&#x27;See para 21.34 irifra. +\n\n5Ram Prasad v. Shrinavas, A.I.R. 1925, Bombay 525, 527.\n\n&quot;See further Law Comnlissirm of India, 54th Report, (<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_467\">Code of Civil Procedure</a> 1908}\n\nChapter 30.\n&#x27;Ammayya v. Narayana, A.I.R. 1925 Mad. \n\nv\n\n\n\n89m. REPORT or LAW C()MMN. or mum ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 21--<a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_468\">Section 21-</a>-Additi0n or Substitution of Parties.)\n\nThe reasoning in the Madras case is, with respect, obscure because an appli-\ncant who wishes to add other persons as parties has done all that is in his\u00b0power\nby \ufb01ling an application in court. The analogy of keeping the plaint in the\nvakils&#x27; drawer does not appear to be exactly applicable.\n\n21.21. The second view on the subject is that a diligent applicant who had\napplied withinithe period prescribed should not suffer merely because the court,\nfor some reason or other, cannot, or does not, give him the relief within the\nperiod. On this principle it has been held by the same High Court&#x27; that a new\nperson is made a party not on the date of the order of the court directing to\nimplead him, but on the date of the application to implead.\n\n21.22. A third view has been propounded in a Patna casei, The ratio of\nthe judgment seems to be that the limitation in relation to claim as against the\nnewly added defendant commences from the date of service of the summons on\nhim. Reliance has been placed on Order 1, Rule 10(5), <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_469\">of the Code of Civil\nProcedure</a> 1908 which speaks of the service of summons as the date when pro-\nceedings are deemed to have started. But the judgment also&#x27;contains a sentence\nto the effect that the suit shall be deemed to have been instituted against the\nnewly added defendant &quot;when he was made a praty to the suit&quot;.\n\n21.23. In view of the relative uncertainty of the position and to avoid any Naed for &#x27;/\ncontroversy on this point, it is desirable that a suitable explanationi&#x27; should be3m\u00b0ndm\u00b01&quot;5-\n\ni added to <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_470\">section 21(1)</a> to make it clear that the date of the application for im-\n\npleading a party will be deemed to be the date from which <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_471\">section 21(1)</a> becomes\neffective.\n\nIV. Misdescription\n\n21.24. We now turn to cases of misdescription of parties. The Railway R31-\ufb02ways and\nAdministration being .the largest net-work of public utility, a number of cases are lb? i55&#x27;-&#x27;&quot;1 \u00b0.f\n\ufb01led against the Railways for tort or breach of contract, arising out of their misdescription&#x27;\nfunction as carriers of goods and passengers. Owing to inadvertence of the\ncounsel, the suits against Railways often contain a misdescription of parties and\nprayers for amendment to correct the mis-description have been agitated right,\nunto the higher courts.\n\n21.25. In a&#x27; Calcutta&#x27; case&#x27;, the suit was against the Railways for non~delivery case jaw,\nof certain goods. In the plaint, the name of the defendant was given as &quot;Agent\nof the Bengal Nagpur Railway Saheb Bahadur&quot;. An objection was taken that\nthe frame of the suit was bad, being in contravention of the provision of Order\n29, rule. 1 and Appendix A to <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_472\">the Code of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908. The plaintiff\napplied for leave to amend the plaint. Reliance was placed on a decision&#x27; in\nwhich the Bombay High Court had (under similar circumstances) allowed the\ncorrection of the clerical error. However, the Calcutta High Court refused to\nallow an amendment and -distinguished the Bombay case by observing that even\nthough, in the Bombay case, there was a mis-description in the title of the suit,\nthe plainti\ufb02&#x27; therein had made su\ufb02icient averments to make it known to the\n\ndefendant that he was seeking relief&#x27;against the Railway administration, and\nnot against the Agent. &quot;\n\n&#x27;Trustees of Port of Madras V. Good Year India. A.l.R. 1973 Madras 316.\n&#x27;~&#x27;Indu Blmslmn v. Hare Ram. A.l.R. I972 Patna 229.\n\n3For a draft see para 21.34 infra.\n\n&#x27;Agent B. N. Rly, v. Behuri Lal Dun. A.l.R. 1925 Cal. 716.\n\n5Saraspur Mfg. Co. v. B. B. &amp; CI.Rly., A.I.R. 1923 Born 452.\n\n\n\n70\n\nDeity.\n\nSummary of\nthe position-\nno change\n\nrecommended\n\nas to. rr_1is-\ndescription,\n\nAppeals and 4\n\nthe addition\nof parties.\n\n2 of course, outside <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_473\">section 21</a>, Limitation Act.\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON rue LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n(Chapter 2l----<a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_474\">Section 21-</a>-Addition or Substitution of Parties.)\n\n21.26. Same fate was met by the plaintiff in a Patna case&#x27;. But the Madras\nHigh Court&quot; &#x27;held that a notice claiming damages for loss of goods in railway\n\ntransit which was sent to the Member-in-charge of the Railway Board was good_\n\nand the mis-description in the plaint was allowed to be corrected. The court\nquoted from the Report of the Civil Justice Committee regarding the proper\nfunction of procedure.\n\n&quot;Procedure is the machinery of the law after all------the channel and means&#x27;\nwhereby law is administered and justice reached. lt strangely departs from\nthe proper otlice if in place of facilitating it is permitted to obstruct and\n\neven to extinguish the legal rights and is thus made to govern where it&#x27;\n\nought to subserve.&quot;\n\n21.27. Apart from cases involving Railways, a niis-description can also take\nplace while \ufb01ling a suit against a deity. In a Calcutta case,&quot; where a suit for\npossession was \ufb01led against the shebait and there was nothing in the plaint to\nindicate that the plaintill was proceeding against the deity and not against the\nshebait, it was held that <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_475\">section 22</a> of the old <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_476\">Limitation Act</a> of 1908 applies and\nso far as the deity was concerned the suit was barred&#x27;.\n\n7:\n\n21.28. A review of the above case law shows that the courts, in their anxiety\nto see that a plaintiff does not get non-suited merely because of bad &#x27;drafting of the\nplaint, have been scanning the entire plaint to see whether the averments therein\n\nmanifest, at some place or the other, the person who has been really the plainti\ufb02. ,\n\nHere again, this exercise may prove to be futile in the case of a plaintiff who (no\ndoubt, erroneously) believes that suing the Manager or Agent of the Railway is\ntantamount to a suit against the Railway administration, or that \ufb01ling a suit\n\n&#x27;against the shebait or pujari of the temple is equal to \ufb01ling a suit against the\n\nreligious institution (i.e. the institution of which the natural person is shebait or\nthe pujari).\n<a href=\"/doc/780756/\" id=\"a_477\">section 20(1)</a> so as to include cases discussed above. Ultimacly, it will have to\nbe left to the good sense and judgment of the courts as to when the -proviso\nshould be pressed into service.\n\nV. Appeals and applications.\n21.29. Under 0.41, r. 20 <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_478\">C.P.C</a>. an appellant must implead all the parties\n\n&quot;within limitation. However 0. 41, r. 20(2), <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_479\">C.P.C</a>. gives power to the court to\n\nallow the impleading of a party after the expiry of limitation. The case is,\n\n.7\nThe position may be considered in some detail. A suit is instituted (in an\nordinary case) when the plaint is presented to the proper o\ufb02icer under <a href=\"/doc/1782312/\" id=\"a_480\">section 3(2)\n(a)(i)</a>; (Other sub-clause of <a href=\"/doc/551950/\" id=\"a_481\">section 3(2)</a> deal with a suit by a pauper and a\nsuit against a company). Now, what <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_482\">section 21(1)</a> provides is that, if, after the\n&quot;institution of a suit&quot; which is de\ufb01ned in <a href=\"/doc/1550355/\" id=\"a_483\">section 3(2)(a)</a>, &quot;a new party is added,\netc.&quot;, then, as regards that new party, the suit is deemed to have been instituted\nwhen he was so made a party (subject to the proviso). In this manner, <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_484\">section\n21(1)</a> modi\ufb01es <a href=\"/doc/1550355/\" id=\"a_485\">section 3(2)(a)</a> and makes it necessary for the plaintiff to show\nthat even on the date of impleading the new party, the suit is within time.\n\n21.30. It would be of interest to mention, in this context, that in one of\nthe decisions of the Patna High Court, the following observations occur regarding\n<a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_486\">section 22</a> of the Act of 1908 (corresponding to present <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_487\">section 21</a>):\n\nQ &#x27;East India Rly Ca V. Ram Lakhan, A.I.R. 1925 Pat. 37. &#x27;\n&quot;G.C.-in-Council v. Sankurappa, A.l.R. 1953 Mad. 838. \\-\n&quot;Pravakar Roy v. Bibuti Bhushan, A.I.R. 1957.011. 177.\n\nHowever, it would be an arduous task to re-draft the proviso to\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n/\n\n(Chapter 2l---S(&#x27;l(&#x27;ll&#x27;()l1 21- --/tdr/[lion or Snbstitmiorz of Parties.)\n\n&quot;If it were not for <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_488\">section 22</a> of the Limitation Act (1908). it Could\nnot-be urged that &#x27;a suit. as regards the added party should be regarded as\nhaving been instituted when he was made a party&#x27;.&quot;\n\n21.31. Coming concretcly to the position relating to appeals, Order 41, rule\n20 <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_489\">of the Code of Civil Procedure</a>. 1908 (as amended in 1976) reads as under:----&#x27;\n\n&quot;20. Power to adjourn hearing and direct persons appearing interested to be\nmade respondents. &#x27;\n\n(1) Where it appears to the Court at the hearing that any person who\nwas a party to the suit in the Court from whose decree the appeal is pre-\nferred, but who has not been made.a party to the appeal, is interested in\nthe result of the appeal. the Court may adjourn the hearing to a future day\nto be \ufb01xed by the Court and direct that such person be made a respondent.\n\ng (2) No respondent shall be added under this rule, after the expiry of the\nperiod of limitation for appeal, unless the Court, for reasons to be recorded,\nallows that to be done. on such terms as to costs as it thinks \ufb01t.&quot;\n\n21.32. I would thus appear that as regards the addition of parties to an\nappeal, where the case falls within Order 41, rule 20, that rule takes care of the\nsituation and we do not consider it necessary to suggest any provision in <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_490\">Section\n21</a>.\n\n21.33. As regards applications, would seem that although section 3 of the Applications\n\nLimitation Act speci\ufb01cally deals only with applications made in the High Court\nby notice of motion, the principle would be the same regarding applications in\ngeneral, namely, it &#x27;is enough if the application is made within time as on date\nof presentation to the proper officer of the court. The case law at present does\nnot regard <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_491\">section 21</a> as applicable to applications. It has generally been re-\ncognised that if <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_492\">section 21</a> is regarded as applicable to applications, it would\nbene\ufb01t the opposite party and not the applicant. The most important case is\napplication for execution. to which also <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_493\">section 21</a> does not, at present, apply.\n\n4&#x27;\n21.34. It is well-established that <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_494\">section 22</a> of&#x27; the Act of 1908 (correspond-\n-, ing to present <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_495\">section 21</a>) did not apply to applications, so that the addition of a\nparty during the pendency of the application does&#x27; not necessarily mean that as\nregards&#x27; him, the application will be taken as \ufb01led only on the date of the implead-\ning of &#x27;that party. This principle has been laid down by decisions of the High\nCourt of Calcutta. Judicial Commissioner of Nagpur and High Court of?-&quot;\nPatna.\n\n21.35. In a Kerala case&#x27;, present <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_496\">section 21</a> has been held to be inapplicable\n&#x27; to a petition for revision. &#x27;~\n\n\\/ _, \\\n21.36. We think that <a href=\"/doc/1114571/\" id=\"a_497\">section 21(1)</a> should be amended so as to bring appli-tAplI;1iC\u00a7ti0nsh\ncations within its scope by an express provision. This will settle the position. &quot;ifithfn mug t\n<a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_498\">section 21</a>.\n\n&#x27;Chandrika R0)&#x27; v. Recommendation\n\n21.37. In the light of the above discussion. we recommend that <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_501\">section 21</a>\n\nshould be revised as under:\n\nRevised&#x27; <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_502\">section 21</a>\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_503\">21</a>. (1) Where, after the institution of a suit, or the making of an\napplication, a new plaintiff or applicant, or a new defendant or respondent, as\nthe case may be, is substituted or added, the suit or application shall, as\nregards him, be deemed to have been instituted, or made, as the case may be,\nwhen he was made a party ;\n\nProvided, that where the court is satis\ufb01ed that the omission to include\na new plaintiff or application or a new defendant or respondent, as the case\nmay be, was due to a mistake made in good faith, it may direct that the\nsuit, or application as regards such plaintiff or applicant or defendant or\nrespondent shall be deemed to be have been instituted, or made, as the case\nmay be, on any earlier date. &quot;Explan.-ation.--For the purposes of this\nsection, and subject to the provisions&#x27; of the proviso to this sub-section, a\nperson is deemed to have been made a party was the application for making\nhis a party is made to the court.\n\n(2) Nothing in sub-section (1) shall apply----\n\n(a) to a case where a party is added or substituted owing to assign-\nment or devolution of anytinterest during the pendency of a\nsuit or application, or,\n\n(b) where a plaintiff is made a defendant or an applicant is made a\nrespondent, or\n\n(C) where a defendant is made a plaintiff or a respondent is made\nan applicant, or T. &#x27;\n\n(d) to a suit or application by or against the members of a joint\nHindu family to enforce a right or liability relating to the\naffairs of the joint Hindu family, where one or more of the\nmembers of the family, not being originally impleaded, is in\n\n\\ the course of the suit or application, brought on the record at\nthe instance of the other party.&quot;\n\nCHAPTER 22 ,\nSECTION 2.2: CONTINUING WRONGS\n\n22.1. <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_504\">Section 22</a> provides that in thecase of a continuing breach of contract\n\nor in the case of a continuing tort&#x27;, a fresh period of limitation begins to run at\nevery moment of the time during which the breach or the tort, as the case may\nhe, continues. The object of the section is to enable the injured party to wait\nfor so long as the breach or the wrong is persisted in and then to bring a suit\nto recover all the damage he may &quot;have suffered, instead of being compelled to\nbring innumerableseparate suits, one after&#x27; the other&quot;.\n\n22.2. <a href=\"/doc/11639/\" id=\"a_505\">Section 23</a> of the Act of 1877, which dealt with the subject, ran as\n\nunder : --\n\n&#x27;Compare <a href=\"/doc/561143/\" id=\"a_506\">section 2(m)</a>----;de\ufb01nition of tort. &#x27;\n&#x27;Secy. of State v, Venkayya, (1977) I.L,R. 40 Mad. 910, 920, 921.\n\n\n\nI &#x27; 115-14 M&#x27;or Ll &amp; CA/NDl83\n\n\\\n\n89rH REPORT OF LAW COMMN. From the above discussion it. transpires that the doubts expressed \n\nabout the applicability of the section to suits based on contracts have been; re-\nsolved by the courts mostly in favour of its applicability. However, in order to\nobviate, further controversy, on the point, it is desirable to extend the section\nexpressly to suits on contracts.&#x27; . &#x27; i\n\nx\n\n23.12. In fact, while&#x27; making it clear that <a href=\"/doc/11639/\" id=\"a_557\">section 23</a> is comprehensively and 4\n\nis not con\ufb01rmed to tort, etc.  can also be takenof clarifying that the\nsection applies to all wrongs (for example breach of statute or breach of trust).&#x27;\n\n23.13. Another point on which the section seems to require verbal impt&#x27;oye-\nment relates to the \ufb01rds &quot;speciik injury&quot; occurring in the section. The word\n&#x27;injury&#x27; is now-a-days &quot;used in the ,,context of civil liability for the wrong itself\n(which is a legal concept), whilefthe word &quot;damage&quot; is used to indicatetthe\nharmful physical consequence of the  The section has obviously in mind\n\n&#x27; the consequence and from this angle &quot;speci\ufb01c damage&quot;, is better than the present\nwording &quot;speci\ufb01c injury&quot;. Insfact, the Limitation Act itself (in the articles relat- ,\n\nRecommendation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_20\">section _24-\n\nin . instrument ts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_21\">ing to libel and slander); uses the expression &quot;speci\ufb01c damage&quot;. These words\n\nwere also used&#x27; in illustration (b) to section-2,4 of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_558\">Limitation Act</a> of 1877&#x27;, The *\n\nwords &quot;speci\ufb01c injury&quot;, therefore, should be replaced by &#x27;fspeci\ufb01c damage&quot;. A\n23.14. In the light. of the above discussion, we recommend that <a href=\"/doc/11639/\" id=\"a_559\">section 23</a>\nshould be revised as under: &#x27; . &#x27; &#x27;\nR\u00a2risqI.5soeIton 23\n&quot;23. Provisions for themccguisition  easements by pres-\ncription .on the analogy of the <a href=\"/doc/1003813/\" id=\"a_565\">English Act</a>&quot; were intmdir\u00e9ed for the \ufb01rst time\nin India in 1871 in the Limitation Act, 1871, and inthe Limitation\n\n&#x27; \u00bb Act of 1877. Later, as a part of the plan tor a civil code, the\n<a href=\"/doc/82950642/\" id=\"a_567\">Easements Act, 1882</a>, was passed, b_ut it wasnot app_iied&#x27;throughout India in\n\n_i &#x27;  of_-the reservations expressed by some of the local Governments. Introductory\n\n29.1. It remains now to consider (so far as&#x27; the body til&quot; the Act is con- section 29__\ncerned), the provisions of the Act in the nature of Z savings, transitional provisions General\nand repeal. Of these, the most important is <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_593\">section 29</a>, consisting of four sub- chem&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_31\">sectiqns. Subsection (1) provides that nothing in this Act shall affect <a href=\"/doc/1903729/\" id=\"a_594\">section 25</a>\nof the Indian Contract Act, 1872. It may be recalled that by virtue of <a href=\"/doc/1266802/\" id=\"a_595\">section\n25(3)</a> of the Contract Act, a time barred debt may. by means of &#x27;a fresh promise\nsatisfying the formalities required by that section, be revived even though there\nis~no consideration for the promise. It should, however, be noted that a right\nother than a right to debt does not fall within the purview of that section of the\n\na &#x27;<a href=\"/doc/171398/\" id=\"a_596\">Contract Act</a>. Thus. a right to property or to a legacy or to damages does not\nT fall Within it.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_32\">&#x27;Ganga Gobfnd Manda.&#x27; v. According to the \ufb01rst half, where a special or\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_37\">-local law prescribes a period di\ufb01erent from that prescribed in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_602\">Limitation\nAct</a>, then <a href=\"/doc/880040/\" id=\"a_603\">section 3</a> of the Limitation Act will apply as if that period had been\nset out in&#x27; the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_604\">Schedule to the Limitation Act</a>. In other words, the suit, appeal\nor application&#x27;to&#x27;which the special or local law applies must, under <a href=\"/doc/880040/\" id=\"a_605\">section 3</a>,\nbe dismissed if it is instituted or made after the expiry of that particular period.\nThe second half of <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_606\">section 29(2)</a> provides that the provisions contained in sec-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_38\">tions 4 to 24 apply only in so far as, and to the extent to which, they are not ex- &#x27;\n\npressly_ excluded by such special or local law. Although apparently simple, sub\nsection .(2) has given rise to &#x27;a number of questions of interpretation and appli-\ncation, some of which will be referred to in due course}\n\nSub-section (3) of <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_607\">section 29</a> reads as under:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_39\">&quot;(3) Save as otherwise provided in any law for the time being in force:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_40\">with respect to marriage and divorce, nothing in this Act shall apply to any\nsuit or other proceeding under any such law.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_41\">This -sub-section has also created certain problems in regard to suits for.\ndower, which will be mentioned in due course.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_42\">Finally, A sub-section (4) of section&#x27;29 &#x27;provides that in the provisions of the\nAct relating to easements do not apply_ to cases arising in the territories to\nwhich the <a href=\"/doc/82950642/\" id=\"a_608\">Indian Easements Act</a>. 1882, for the time being extends.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_43\">: \u00ab &#x27; II. Special andllsocal Laws\n\n29.2. With reference to the savings eifected as regards special and local\nlaws, the Law Commission in its Report on the Act of 1908, while dealing with\nsection 29 of the Limitation Act, 190% (corresponding to present section 29), ob-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_38\">served? as under:---- &#x27; .\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_44\">&quot;;I&#x27;he combined operation of sub-clauses (a) and (b) of sub-section (2) A\n\nis that so far as special and local laws are concerned, only <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_609\">sections 4</a>. 9 to\n. 18 and .22 of the Act apply, and thatetoo subject to such modi\ufb01cations as\nmay be prescribed. We consider -that there is no need for this restriction and\n\nthat the principles contained in <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_610\">sections 4</a> &#x27;to 25 should be made applicable 1\nto all special and local laws, leaving it open to the legislature to exclude the ,,\n\napplication of any or all of these. sections. in any given case.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_39\">Implementing this recommendation. <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_611\">section 29(2)</a> as re-enacted in the pre- &quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_45\">\\\n\nsent Act. reads as under :----\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_46\">&quot;(2:) Where an special or local law prescribed for any suit, appeal or .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_47\">application a period of limitation di\ufb01ererit from the period prescribed by the\nSchedule, the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/880040/\" id=\"a_612\">section 3</a> shall apply as if such period were the\nperiod prescribed by the Schedule and for the purpose of determining any\n\nperiod of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application by any spe- &#x27;\n\ncial or local law, the provisions contained in <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_613\">sections 4</a>. to 24 (inclusive), shall\n\napply only in so far as, and to the extent to which, they are not expressly&#x27;\n\nexcluded by such special or local law.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_48\">&quot;Para 29.2, et seq, infra. &#x27; &#x27; ~W\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Repor:t(Li1:nitation Act 1908), page 24, para 59 and\npages 82-83. <a href=\"/doc/1234316/\" id=\"a_614\">section 25(2)</a>. &#x27; V .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_49\">. 5.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_50\">, C I\n89rH~Iu=.poRT or Lxw COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT. 1963\n\n(Chapter 29~--SecIions 29 to 32----Savings, Transitional Provisions and Repeal.) A\n\n29.3. Several decisions of the Supreme Court 1-2-7&#x27; have made it clear that &quot;Spe\u00a2ial&quot; and i\n\na &quot;special law&quot; is a law relating to a particular area or territory.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_51\">29.4. The Supreme Court lIas also noticed&#x27; the.important departure made\nby <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_615\">Limitation Act, 1963</a> (from the Act of 1908) in so far as the provisions con-\ntained in <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_616\">section 29</a>, sub-section (2) are concerned. The Court concluded that\nsince, under the new <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_617\">Limitation Act</a>, <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_618\">section 5</a> is&#x27; speci\ufb01cally made applicable\nby <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_619\">section 29(2)</a>, it can be availed of for the purpose of extending the period of\nlimitation prescribed by a special or local law, if the applicant can show that he\nhad su\ufb02icient cause for not presenting the application within the period of limi-\ntation. The court took notice of the fact that a time&quot; limit of 60 days was laid\ndown by section 417(4) of the Code of. Criminal Procedure 1898, which&#x27;was a\n\ni - &quot;special&quot; law of limitation. But the mere fact that the special law has, by peremp-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_52\">-tory and imperative language, prescribed a period of limitation in section 417(4)\nwas held &quot;as not a su\ufb02icient ground to displace the applicability of <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_620\">section 5</a>.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_53\">29.5. In View of the above authoritative pronouncement, there appears to\n\nbe need to de\ufb01ne the expression &quot;special law&quot; and &#x27;local law&#x27; as occurring in\n\no\n, the [<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_621\">Limitation Act</a>. &#x27;\n\n29.6. The expression &quot;expressly excluded&quot; by\u00bb such special or local law, as\n\n&#x27; occurring in &#x27;<a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_622\">section 29(2)</a> also, came up for consideration before the Supreme\n\nCourt&quot; in a case dealing with section 81 of the Representation ofthe <a href=\"/doc/55081742/\" id=\"a_623\">Peoples\nAct</a> 1951. The Supreme Court overruled an earlier view about the interpretation\nof this expression as occurring in section 29(2)(a) of the Limitation Act, 1908-\n\n&quot;lqeal&quot; law.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_54\">De\ufb01nition ol\nspecial or\nlocal lawye-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_55\">not needed.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_56\">&quot;Expressly *\nexcluded&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_40\">a view to the effect that the exclusion must be by express words i.e., by express&#x27;\n\nreference to the &#x27;sections of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_624\">Limitation Act</a>. The Supreme Court held that\nwhat had to, be seen was whetherthe scheme _of the special law and the nature\nof the remedy provided. therein &quot;are such that the Legislature intended it to be\na complete code by itself which alone should govern the several matters provided\nby it.&quot; &quot;If, on an examination of the relevant provisions, it is clearpthat the pro-\nvisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_625\">Limitation Act</a> are necessarily excluded, then the bene\ufb01ts conferred\ntherein cannot be called in\u00bb aid to supple t the provisions of the Act.&quot; The\nSupreme Court further held that even in a case where the special law does not\nexclude the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_626\">sections 4</a> to <a href=\"/doc/769768/\" id=\"a_627\">24</a> of &quot;ihe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_628\">Limitation Act</a> by aI1 express\nreference, &quot;it would nonetheless be open to the Court to examine whether and to\nwhat extent the nature of those provisions or the &#x27;nature of the subject-matter and\nthe scheme of the special law exclude their operation.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_41\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10459\" id=\"span_13\">29.7. In view of the pronuncement of the Supreme Court mentioned above,\nCourts enter uponia comparative study of the scheme of the special or local law\n\nv and the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_629\">Limitation Act, 1963</a>, with a view to finding out whether an exclusion is\n\nsuggested, evenithough the exclusion does not appear in so many words in the text\nof the special or local law. ~\n\n29.8. For example, a Full Bench of the Madhya Pradesh High Court&quot; has\napplied <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_630\">section 5</a> of the Limitation Act to an objection petition \ufb01led under sec-\ntion 11(3) of the <a href=\"/doc/186240693/\" id=\"a_631\">Madhya Pradesh Ceiling on Agricultural Holdings Act</a>. l960,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/10459/\" id=\"a_632\">Ikdushalya Rani v. Gopal Singh</a>.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1895282\" id=\"span_14\">A.I.R. 1964 S.C. _4s0_\n<a href=\"/doc/1895282/\" id=\"a_633\">3Mangu Ram v. Municipal Corporation of Delhi, A.I.R.</a> 1976 S.C. 105-\n3].</span> A. Dr&#x27;-Piedade V. A. V. Consequently, it can-\nnot be doubted that to an objection application as provided for in sub-sec-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_61\">tion (3) of section 11 of the Ceiling Act, <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_639\">section 5</a> of the Limitation Act will\u00bb\n\nbe applicable.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_47\">29.9. The Calcutta High Court has held&#x27; that the appellate Cooperative\nTribunal, established by the Bengal Cooperative Societies Act, 1940, would be\n\ngoverned by the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_640\">Limitation Act</a> which are applicable&#x27; to &quot;courts&quot; &#x27;\n\nand accordingly <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_641\">section 29(2)</a> of the Act would also be applicable. A certi\ufb01ed\n\ncopy is not required to be \ufb01lled along with therappeal, but even then the Court\n\ncame to the conclusion that the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/23013/\" id=\"a_642\">section 12(2)</a> of the Limitation\nAct, 1963 [regarding excluding the period for obtaining copy of the award], would\n\nbe applicable, because an aggrieved person cannot make up his mind whether to&#x27;\n\n\ufb01le an appeal or not unless he reads the reasons given by the lower court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_48\">29.10. From the above discussion, it appears that the expression &quot;expressly\nexcluded&quot; occurring insub-section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_643\">section 29</a> has been construed liberally\nin determining the question whether <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_644\">sections 4</a> to <a href=\"/doc/769768/\" id=\"a_645\">24</a> are available to proceedings\nunder special or local laws. It may be dil\ufb01cult to devise a verbal formula that\nwill cover the variety of situations, and we do not, in the present state&#x27; of the\ncase~law, propose to make any verbal change. I\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_49\">111. Local laws--The problem of Portuguese and French laws.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_50\">29.11. As to the savings etfected for &quot;local laws&quot;, two cases decided by the\nCourt of the Judicial Commissioner and the Madras High Court regarding Goa\n\nand Pondicherry respectively, have brought to surface the anomalous situation\nprevailing in these two former foreign enclaves.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_51\">29.12. In at Goa case&#x27;, it was held that articles\\529 and 535 of the Portu--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_52\">guese Civil Code are &quot;special or local law&quot; within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_646\">section 29(2)</a> A &#x27;\nof the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_647\">Limitation Act</a>.&#x27;l963, and &#x27;g not. on the coming into force of the Limita- &#x27;\n\ntion Act 1963, in the Union Ter P ry of Diu, Daman Goa. stand repealed by\nthe corresponding <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_648\">articles 74</a><a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_649\">, 31</a> i\u00e9fnd 65 of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_650\">Schedule to the Limitation Act,\n1963</a>. A reading of the judgment shows that the articles of the Portuguese Civil\nCode in question are more liberal to the plaintiff than the corresponding provi-\nsions of the Act of 1963. So vast was the disparity between these two provisions\nas regards the prescribed limitation periods that the Counsel for the defendant\nwent to the extent of arguing that articles 529 and 535 of the Portuguese Civil\nCode (which prescribed a period of 30 years) were demonstrably discriminatory\nand irrational in nature. The argument wasidismissed. but the ruling accentuates\nthe fact that a system substantially different from the Indian Act has been re-\ngarded as continuing in force in the Union Territory of Goa.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_53\">29.13. The Madras High Court (which has jurisdiction over the former\nFrench Possession of Pondicherry} has also held in the same refrain&quot; that arti-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_54\">cles of Limitation laid down in the French Civil Code answered the description 7\n\nof &quot;local law&quot; in <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_651\">section 29(2)</a> of the Limitation Act, 1963. The French Civil\nCode was a &quot;local&quot; law because it applied, and was inforce, in the former French\nestablishments of Pondicherry. It. was not in force anywhere else in India. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1144253\" id=\"span_19\"><a href=\"/doc/1144253/\" id=\"a_652\">After\n\n&#x27;Nirma-I Kumar v. It may be mentioned in this context that the question&#x27; whether\nthe provisions of the Portuguese Civil Procedure Lode as to innitauon still\nsurvive in the Union Territory of Goa, Daman and Din came up before the\nbupreme &#x27;Court, where the point at issue was whether the appeal in &#x27;question\nwas governed by the Portuguese Code by article lie of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_664\">Limitation Act,\n1903</a>. H it was the former, the appeal was out of time. it it was the latter,\nthe appeal was in time. The court of Judicial Commissioner. Goa had held\n(in the case under appeal} that the Portuguese Civil Procedure Code was still\napplicable and that <a href=\"/doc/614641/\" id=\"a_665\">section 32</a> of the Limitation Act, 1963 had repealed only\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_666\">Indian Limitation Act</a> of 1908 and not the Portuguese Code. Moreover,\n<a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_667\">section 29(2)</a> of the Act of 1963 saved a&quot;&#x27;local&quot; law andlhence the provisions\ncontained in the Protuguese Civil Procedure Code still applied, so that the\nappeal was out of time. However, on appeal to the Supreme Court, the Supreme\nCourt, after posing the question, considered it unnecessary to decide it, since\nit held that in the circumstances of the case, the delay (ii any) should be\ncondoned. It is su\ufb02icient to quote the following passage lroin the last para-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_55\">graph of the judgment of the Supreme Court: &#x27;\n\n&quot;However, the other preliminary objection upheld by the &quot;learned\n\\,Additional Judicial&#x27; Commissioner is more important and the question that\nreally arises for our consideration is, what was the law of limitation appli-\ncable in the Union Territories of Goa, Daman and Diu to proceedings\nlaunched therein prior to and pending at the date of liberation&#x27;?\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_77\">&quot;In other words, whether the period of limitation for -the appeal in\nquestion was the one prescribed by the Portuguese Code or by <a href=\"/doc/436447/\" id=\"a_668\">Article 116</a>\nof the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_669\">Limitation Act</a> 1963? It was not disputed that if it was the former,\nthe appeal was out of time and if it was the latter, the appeal was well\nwithin time. lt is really unnecessary for us to decide this question in view\nof the application for condonation of delay for \ufb01ling the said appeal in\nthe Judicial Commissioner&#x27;s Court at Goa, that has been made by the\nappellants before us which we are inclined to grant. It cannot be gdinsaid\nthat the aforesaid question of limitation is a complex one and not free\nfrom&#x27;d0ubt,&quot;&#x27; and if in such a situation the appellants, bona fide believ-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_78\">ing that the appeal could be \ufb01led within 90 days as provided by <a href=\"/doc/436447/\" id=\"a_670\">article 116</a>\nof the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_671\">Limitation Act</a> 1963, \ufb01led their appeal within that period, it would\nbe a clear case of su\ufb02icient cause which could be said to have prevented\nthem from \ufb01ling the appeal within the\u00bb time prescribed by &#x27;the Portuguese\nCode. Where two views are equally possible on this complex question\nand where a party, being guided by one of such views, adopts a course\nconsistent with that view, it would equally be a case of &#x27;just impediment&#x27;\nwithin the. meaning of <a href=\"/doc/1537130/\" id=\"a_672\">article 145</a> of the Portuguese Code, which could\nbe said to have prevented the party from \ufb01ling the appeal within limita-\ntion prescribed by the Portuguese Code.&quot; &#x27;\n\nThe aboy passage indicates that the position as regards the limitation law\nin force in the area in question is still uncertain.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_79\">IV. Two judgments of the Madras High Court, however, appear to have\ncreated certain dif\ufb01culties in the matter of computation of limitation in situa-\ntions envisaged&#x27; by section 20(1) of the Travancore Act, which relates to pum-\nvaippu or purakkadam deeds (deeds of further charge). A provision similar to\nthis was not found in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_676\">Indian Limitation Act</a> 1908, and does not appear in\nthe Act of 1963. Brie\ufb02y, this section provides that if, before the expiration of\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_58\">-the period prescribed for redemption, the mortgagee acceptsa deed of further\ncharge-, then a fresh period starts running.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_59\">&#x27; 29.21. The first judgment? on the subject (delivered by Mohan J .) held that\n\n, a mortgagor ought to consolidate both the mortgages and a piecemeal redemp-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_60\">tion was impossible. Mohan J. held that in spite of the repeal of Travancore\n\nLimitation Act. the suits are not barred by limitation and the period had to\nbe counted from the purakkadam deed (deed of further charge). &#x27;\n\n29.22. A1 somewhat contrary view3 was expressed by the same High Court\n(in a judgment by Varadarajan J .1 holding that pizravaippu or purakkadam\ncannot be used to get fresh period of limitation after the extension of the Indian\nLimitation Act, 1908 to Travancore-Cochin and that the doctrine of consolida-\ntion cannot be applied at all. The earlier ,Madras ruling does not appear to\nhave been cited before him.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_61\">29.23. All the same, we have made a brief note of this matter and trust\nthat the State Governments of Tamil Nadu and Kerala would examine the\n\nA pointsraised in the judgment. with a View to ensuring that no hardship is caused\nby such an extended period of limitation.&#x27; 1&#x27; .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_63\">&#x27;Section 20, Travancore Limitation Act (IV of 1100xM.E.}.\n3Thom\u00a2i.r v.&quot; Victor, AJR. 1976 Madras 273: (1976) 2 M.L.J. 5.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_64\">3Parameswm-an Thampv v. At this stage, it is necessary to refer to the part of <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_679\">section 29</a>( .\nwhich excludes the applicability of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_680\">Limitation Act</a> in regard to &quot;a suit 0\nproceeding under any law for&#x27; the time being in force relating to marriage and\ndivorce.&quot; In view of an Allahabad ruling&#x27; on this sub-section, an amendment\nof <a href=\"/doc/1088177/\" id=\"a_681\">section 29(3)</a> seems to be needed. According to the Allahabad ruling, the\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_682\">Limitation Act, 1963</a>, does not apply to a-claim for dower made by a Muslim\nwoman under the Muslim law of marriage and &#x27;divorce, and is, by <a href=\"/doc/1088177/\" id=\"a_683\">section 29(3)</a>.\nexcluded from the purview of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_684\">Limitation Act</a>.&#x27; The reasoning underlying\nthe judgment, is as under. In the Indian Limitation Act, 1908, the exception\n\nwas only in respect of proceedings under the <a href=\"/doc/806295/\" id=\"a_685\">Indian Divorce Act</a>. and there.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_72\">were speci\ufb01c articles 103-104 which prescribed the limitation period for a suit\nfor dower. In the present Act, there are (the High Court has observed) n0\ncorresponding articies prescribing the period of limitation for a suit for dower.\nAccording to the Allahabad ruling, in View of the express provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1088177/\" id=\"a_686\">section\n29(3)</a>, nothing in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_687\">Limitation Act</a> can_ bar the suit for dower.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_73\">29.25.&#x27;This ruling raises an important point that needs clarification. So\nfar as ,we could ascertain, it was not the intention of the Legislature that the\nLimitation&#x27;. Act 1963, should not apply. to;claims for dower.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_74\">purview of the Act. Its object is to exclude matrimonial litigation proper com-\nmenced under some statute, i.e. statutory proceedings for divorce, judicial sepa-\nration and the like, which themselves terminate or suspend the legal bond of\nmarriage. The reason for not making the Li-mitation Act applicable to matri-\nmonial causes was presumably that these statutes themselves have their own\nin-builtdoctrines such as condonation, laches, and the like, which can be taken\n\ninto account by the court. Subject to these doctrines. the court has a discre- &#x27;\n\ntion as to the grant of matrimonial relief, in the exercise of which it can take\ninto account the conduct of the parties, the interests of the children and other\nrelevant factors. These considerations have no application, however. to a\nMuslim Wife&#x27;s claim for dower, which is a purely monetary claim that can be\ndecided on fairly precise rules. It, therefore, stands on a different footing from.\nsay, a petition for divorce or judicial separation is regard to which (as stated\nabove) special considerations of laches, discretion and the like apply.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_75\">29.26. We need not, pause to discuss.if-&quot;the Allahabad ruling mentioned\nabove is&#x27;correct, on the present wording &#x27;of <a href=\"/doc/1088177/\" id=\"a_688\">section 29(3)</a>. But obviously the\nposition resulting therefrom needs to be set right. The point being one of re-\ncurring frequency, some step needs to be taken to ensure that a claim for dower\ndoes not remain totally outside the Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_76\">29.27. A&#x27;few words appear&#x27; to be needed about the changed wording in\nthe present Act (in contrast with the earlier Act); The difficulty seems to have\nbeen created because the Act goes beyond what the Law Commission recom-\nmended. <a href=\"/doc/1088177/\" id=\"a_689\">Section 29(3)</a> of the Act of 1908 made the Act inapplicable to suits\nunder the <a href=\"/doc/806295/\" id=\"a_690\">Indian Divorce Act, 1869</a>. Commenting on this. the Law Commis-\nsion, in its earlier Report. observed as under2:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_85\">&quot;There are other Acts like the <a href=\"/doc/122564/\" id=\"a_691\">Parsi Marriage Act</a> and the Special Marriage\n\nActdealing with marriage and divorce. The reason for excluding proceed-\nV ings under the <a href=\"/doc/806295/\" id=\"a_692\">Divorce Act</a>. 1869, are equally applicable to proceedings\n\n&#x27;Ahmadi Bibi v. Muliamed Maboob,jA,I.1V\u00a7._19&#x27;I_9 A&quot;. 374-(Deoki Nandan, 1.).\nV Raw, Commission of India, 3rd Report -:(Lunitatxon~ Act, 1908). Page 24. Dara 50-\n\nThe exclusion T\nclause in <a href=\"/doc/1088177/\" id=\"a_693\">section 29(3)</a> is not really intended to exclude such claims from the .\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_86\">is-Q14 M ofu .&amp; CA/ND/83\n\n8911;&#x27; REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON Tina LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 29-- <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_694\">Sections 29</a> to <a href=\"/doc/614641/\" id=\"a_695\">32-</a>-Savings, Transitional Provisions and Repeal.)\n\nunder these other Acts. We recommend &#x27;that the _sub-section may be am-\npli\ufb01ed to include all Acts relating to matrimonial causes. The Acts to be\nincluded may be speci\ufb01ed when drafting the amenriment.tol&#x27;1he section.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_77\">29.28: The. draft provision suggested in the Report of the Law Commission\n\n&#x27;to give shape to its recommendation as regards the relevant part of <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_696\">section 29</a>\n\nof the Act of 1908 was as under&#x27; :4-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_87\">&quot;25. Positionyin India\nS\ufb01op\u00e9hof &#x27; 30.1. *It is proposed in this Chapter to deal with the subject of amendment\nt e awe&quot; of pleadings &#x27;in so far as it is relevant to the question of limitation.\nI &#x27;i ,\n0.6. R. 17. I 30.2. The most important provision on the subject is in the Code of Civil\nC&#x27;P&#x27;C&#x27; Procedure, 0.6, R. 17. That rule, being intended primarily to confer a general\npower on the court to allow amendment of the pleadings (in the inte/rests of\njustice), naturally does not deal in detail with the rami\ufb01cation of the exercise\nof that power in the varieties of situations that occur in life. It does not, for\nthat reason, deal with the question in what circumstances the power may be\nexercised. V .\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_86\">Amendment by adding a party is primarily taken care of by a speci\ufb01c pro-\nvision in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_711\">Limitation Act</a>? &#x27;\nC133&#x27; C\u00b0&quot;n\u00b0i1 30.3. The leading case in India on the subject of amendment of pleadings\n\nis a Privy Council one.&#x27; The plaintiif&#x27;s object in that suit was/to exercise his\nright of pre-emption, but, while drafting the&#x27; plaint the plaintiff who had asked\nfor declaration of pre-emption rights) forgot to include a &#x27;prayer for possession\nof the property. Later, he sought to amend the plaint to includegthe prayer\n&#x27;for possession. Allowing .the amendment, and dealing with the aspect of limi-\ntation, the Privy Council observed :---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_97\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10416883\" id=\"span_27\">&quot;That there was full power to make the amendment cannot be dis- ,\nputed, and though such a power should &#x27;-not as a rule be exercised where\nits eifect is to take away from a defendant a legal right which has accrued\nto him by lapse of time, yetthere are cases (see for example, <a href=\"/doc/10416883/\" id=\"a_712\">Mohummed\nZahoor Ali Khan v. Mussumwt Thakooranee Rutta&quot;K0er</a>, (1867) 11 -M.I.A.\n468 (P.C.), where such considerations are outweighed by the special cir-\ncumstances of the case.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_98\">&quot;If this be so, all that happened-was that the plaintiffs, through some\nclumsy blundering, attempted to assert rights that they undoubtedly pos-\nsessed under the statute in a form which the statute did not permit. But if -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_99\">1Para.29.l7, supra.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_100\">&#x27;Para 29.23, supra.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_101\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_713\">Section 21</a>, Limitation Act, 1963; Chapter 2], supra.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_102\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1209054\" id=\"span_28\">&#x27;Charan. <a href=\"/doc/1209054/\" id=\"a_714\">Das V. The earlier.case&#x27; cited by the Privy Council arose out of a suit to\nrecover a stated sum due on a&#x27; bond alleged to have been executed by Mussumat\nThakooranee Rutta Koer. After the institution of the suit some more defendants\nwere added on the allegation that they had combined with the obligor and had\n&quot;colorably&quot;2 procured her estate to be transferred to them in order to deprive\nthe plaintilf of his remedy against the property.\non the date of the execution of the bond in question, the property of Mussumat\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_88\">-Thakooranee Rutta Koer was in charge of the Court of wards and hence the\n\n_&#x27;I&#x27;hakooranee was not competent to incur any debt.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_89\">The Privy Council found that as the bond was a simple money bond to\nwhich the other defendants were not parties and as the bond did not purport\nto be a mortgage, no case was made out against the other defendants. But, as\nregards the obligor, i.e. Mussumat Thakooranee Rutta Koer, the Privy Couiicil\ndid not agree with the \ufb01ndings that she was incompetent to incur a debt. How-\never, they were averse to pronouncing in favour of the appellant without an\namendment of the pleadings and a full trial. The Privy Council observed as\nunder:--- .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_90\">I\n&quot;Though this Committee is always disposed to give a liberal construc-\ntion to pleadings &#x27;in the Indian courts, so as to allow every question fairly\narising on the case made by the pleadings-to be raised and discussed in\nthe suit, yet this&#x27; liberality of construction. must have some limit. A plain-\ntiff &#x27;cannot be entitled to relief upon facts or documents not stated or\n\nThe main defence was that i\n\n, referred to by him in his&#x27; pleadings .......... ..They have, however, felt some A\n\ndoubt as to the Order which it will be their duty to recommend Her Maj-\nesty to make&#x27; on this appeal. They have already intimated that the appeal\n&#x27; must be dismissed against all the Respondents except Rutta Koer; and\nthey have felt some &#x27;doubt whether, inasmuch as the suit was wholly mis-\nconceived, the proper course was not to dismiss this appeal altogether.\nwithout prejudice to the rights of the appellant to bring a new suit against\nRutta Koer upon this Bond, treating it as a mere money Bond. Considering,\nhowever, that such a suit would probably be met by a plea of the Act of\nLimitation; that in the circumstances of this case such a defence would be\ninequitable; and that, the Respondent not having appeared, their Lord-\nships are not in a condition to &#x27;put her on terms as to her defence to a\nfresh suit; they have come to the conclusion that thefairer course is to do\nwhat the judge of the Court of First Instance might, under the Code of\nProcedure,_ have done at an earlier stage of the course,--namely, allow the\nappellant to amend-&#x27; his plaint so as to make it a plaint against Rutta Koer\nalone for the recovcryiof money due on a Bond.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_91\">o\n\n30.5.&#x27; Even in the later case3 the Privy Council cautioned that such a power Powcr tn\n\nshould not, as a rule, &#x27;be exercised where its effect is to take away from a defen-\ndant a legal right which had accrued to him by lapse of time.\nobservations have also been repeated by the Supreme Court&#x27;:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_104\">&#x27;4 &#x27;Mohammad Zahoar Ali Khan, v. Subsequently, the. plaintiff sought to amend the\n\nplaint by adding an extra relief for a decree for the contract-money. The amend-</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_96\">ment was allowed, on the ground that the amendment sought to introduce a\nclaim based on the same cause of action. However, the Supreme Court obs-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_97\">_ erved:-- -\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_121\">&quot;No amendment will be allowed to introduce a new set of ideas to\nthe prejudice of any right acquired by any party by lapse of time.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_98\">30.10A. The dictum of the Supreme Court that the power can be appropria- Summary of\ntely exercised in the interests of justice, the law of limitation notwithstanding?}g\u00b0h1;&#x27;\u00a7f:f1\u00b0&#x27;?\nshows that the power has been regarded as a wide one. All the same, one can &#x27;\ndiscern from the judgments certain guiding lights that indicate the proper app-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_99\">roach to  adopted in exercising the judicial discretion in the matter.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_100\">I V\ufb01 review of -the judicial pronouncements in Indiaon the subject of amend-\nment of pleadings brings to surface the following broad principles:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_122\">(I) The courts have a plenary power to permit amendment of pleadings at\nany stage of the proceedings. But the power can be exercised appro-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_101\">priately only in the interests of justice and the discretion should be\nexercised with due care and circumspection. &#x27;\n\n(2) When all the allegations which are necessarily for sustaining a claim\n\n&#x27; already exist in the plaint, the court should exercise its discretion to\nallow a technical amendment, evenpthough the claim would be barred\nby limitation on the date of the application.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_102\">(3) A suitor should not suffer on account of merely technicality or clumsly\nblunderingf&#x27;\n\n(4) A &#x27;suitor/should not suffer becausehe failed to amend his plaint at any\nearly stage of the proceedings? -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_103\">(5) When all the allegations which arenecessary for sustaining a claim were\nalready in the plaint and what was lacking is only a prayer for an\n. alternative claim, the amendment should be allowed.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_104\">(6) Amendment should be allowed if no prejudice is caused to any party\nand thepamendment is based entirely on facts stated in the original\nplaint.&quot; A &#x27;\n\n(7) Except in special circumstances, an amendment. should not be allowed\nto introduce a set of ideas to the prejudice of any right acquired by any\nparty by lapse--of time.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_105\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1002427\" id=\"span_38\"><a href=\"/doc/1002427/\" id=\"a_722\">1Govinda Chem&#x27; v. Position in England\n\n30.11. Developments in certain other countries are also of interest. IniEn&#x27;g-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_130\">land, as long back as 1887, Lord Esher refused&#x27; to a plainti\ufb02? leave to amend to\nadd to her action for slander (after the expiry of the period limitation), claims .\u00bb\nfor assault and false imprinsonment. But, while doing so, he made certain gene-\nral observations which were, until recently considered almost an immutable guide-\nline in practice:-- 6\n\n&quot;We must act on the settled rule of practice, which is that amendments are\nnot admissible&#x27; when they prejudice the rights of the opposite party &quot;as exist-\ning at the date of such amendments. If an amendment were allowed setting&#x27;\nup a cause of action, which, if the writ were issued,in respect thereof at the\ndate of the amendment, would be barred by the statute of Limitations. it\n\nwould be allowing the plaintiff to take advantage of her former writ to 1\n\ndefeat the statute and taking away an existing right from the defendant, a\nproceeding which, as a general rule, would be, in my opinion, improper and\nunjust. Under very peculiar circumstances the court might perhaps have\npower to allow such an amendment, but certainly as a general rule it will\n\n_ not do so.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_106\">Even though Lord Esher .(in the last sentence of the above passage) had\n\n&quot;made reservations for &quot;every peculiar circumstances&quot;, it has been generally as-\nsumed that whenever an amendment sought would change the cause of action\nafter the expiration of the period of limitation, allowing the same could cause\ninjustice to the defendant which cannot be compensated for by cost. &#x27; &#x27;\n\n30.12. This position practically, held the \ufb01eld in the England till the coming\n\nin force of the Rules of the Supreme Court, 1966. Order 20, Rule 4-of these\nRules provide as under:----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_131\">&quot;S (1) Subject to Order .18, rules 6, 7 and 8 and the following provi-\nsions of this rule, the court may at any stage of the proceedings allow the\nplaintiff to amend his wiit, or any party to amend his pleadings, on such\nterms as to costs or otherwise as) may be just and in such manner (if any),\nas it may direct. &#x27; &#x27;\n\n(2) Where an application to the court for leave to make the amendment\nmentioned in paragraph (3), (4) or (5) is made after any relevant period of\nlimitation current at the date of issue of the writ has expired, the court may\nnevertheless grant suchleave in the circumstances mentioned in that para!\u00bb\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_132\">-graph if it thinks it just to do so. &quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_107\">(3) An amendment to correct the name of a party may be allowed under\nparagraph (2) notwithstaning that it is alleged that &#x27;the &quot;effect of the amend-\nment will be to substitute --a new party if the court is satis\ufb01ed that the mis-\ntake sought to be corrected was a genuine mistake and was &#x27;not misleading\nor such as to cause any reasonable doubt as to the identity of the person\nintending to sue or, as the case may be, intended to be sued.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_108\">(4) A amendment to alter the capacity in which a party sues (whether .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_109\">as plaintiff or as defendant by counter-claim) may be allowed under para-\ngraph (2) it the capacity in which, if the amendment is made, the party will\nsue is one in which at the date of -issue of the writ or. the making of the\ncounter claim, as the case may&quot; be, &#x27;he might have sued.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_110\">&#x27;Weldon v. Neal (1887) 19 Q.B.D. &#x27;394,[395;- 56 LJ. Q.B. 621 (CA)\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON, THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 30------LI&#x27;mitation and the Amendment of Pleadings.)\n\n(5) -An amendment may be allowed under paragraph (2) notwithstand-\ning that the effect of the amendment will be to add or substitute a new cause\nof action if the new cause of action arises out of the same facts, or subs-\ntantially the same facts as a cause of action in respect of which relief has\nalready been claimed in the action by the party applying for leave to make\nthe amendment.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_111\">30.13. Doubts were expressed by- a writer&#x27; that such a sweeping provision,&#x27; Doulits ex-to\nwhich in a sense, quali\ufb01es the limitation Acts might be argued to be ultra vires  as\nthe statutory authority for making the rules of the Supreme Court. In Canada, the Saskatchewan, &#x27;jurisdiction has gone a step further.\nThe Queen&#x27;s Bench Act&quot; provides as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_134\">&quot;Where an action is brought to enforce any right, legal or equitable, the\ncourt may permit the amendment of any pleading or other proceeding therein\nupon such terms as to costs or otherwise as it deems just, notwithstanding\n\nthat, between the time of the issue of the writ and the application for amend- \nment, the right of &#x27;action would, by reason of action brought, have been\n\nbarred by the provision of any statute; provided that such amendment does\nnot involve a change of parties other -than a change caused by the death of\none of the parties.&quot; A\n\nUnfettered discretion given by the Saskatchewan law to the courts to permit\namendments has been severally criticised&#x27; as going too far, because it is pointed\n\nout that the primary consideration underlying the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_729\">Limitation Act</a> is that [the &#x27;\n\ndefendants ought not to be called on to resist&#x27; a claim when evidence has been\nlost, memories have faded and witnesses have disappeared.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_135\">30.20. If the Saskatchewan law grants blanket power of amendment to the\ncourt the Alberta Limitation of Actions Act, (R.S.A. 1970 Chapter 209) in its\nsection 61, gives a long list of various &#x27;types&#x27; of situations which have given\nrise to dif\ufb01culties in the past such as misnomers, cases involving dead persons,\netc., in which the courts may allow amendments to the pleadings notwithstanding\nthe expiration of the period of limitation. ~\n\n30.21. Position in U.S.A. \u00ab\n\n30.22. The U.S. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure read as under:---- U.S. Federal\n\nRules.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_138\">&quot;(c) Relation Back of AmendmentS.----Whenever the claim or defence as-\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_128\">. serted in the amended pleading arose out of the conduct, transaction, or occur-\nrence set forth or attempted to be set forth in the original pleading, the amend-\nment relates back to the date of the original pleading. An amendment chan-\nging the party against whom a claim is asserted relates back if the forego-\ning provision is satis\ufb01ed and, within the period provided by law for com-\nmencing the action against him, the party to be brought in by amendment\n(1) has received such notice of the institution of the action that he will not\nbe prejudiced in maintaining his defence on the merits, and (2) knew or\nshould have known that, but/for a mistake concerning the identity of the\nproper party, the action would have been brought against him.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_129\">The delivery or mailing of process to the United States Attorney, or his\n\n. designee, or the Attorney General of the United States, or any agency or\no\ufb02icer who would have been a proper defendant if named, satis\ufb01es the re-\nquirement of clauses (1) and (2) hereof with respect to the United States or\nany agency or o\ufb02icer thereof to be brought into the action as a defendant.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_130\">VI. Summary of developments elsewhere\n\n30.23. The developments elsewhere outlined above show a certain amount Diverse\n\nof diversity of approach. The Saskatchewan Act,&quot; which is at the one end of the &quot;PP&#x27;\u00b0&quot;\u00b0h\u00b0s&#x27;\nspectrum, confers a very wide power onithe court----an approach also favoured\n\nby the Ontario Law Reform Commission. However, this approach has not es-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_131\">caped criticism. At the other end of the spectrum is the Alberta Limitation of\n\nActions Act, 1970 which de\ufb01nes certain types of situations in which an amend-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_132\">ment could be allowed. The English Rules of the Supreme Court, 1960 stand\n\nbetween the two. According to Lord Deming, &quot;the Court can allow an amend-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_133\">ment whenever it is just so to do.&quot; But the position in English law should be\n\nregarded still as \ufb02uid.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_134\">CHAPTER 31\n<a href=\"/doc/1406924/\" id=\"a_730\">ARTICLES 1</a> to 5 : surrs RELATING TO ACCOUNTS\n\n31.1. When <a href=\"/doc/1924932/\" id=\"a_751\">article 89</a>\nof the Act of 1877 was pressed during the arguments, the court observed:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_142\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1924932/\" id=\"a_752\">Article 89</a>, which is suggested as the article applicable to this case, has clear-\nly no application, because the suit is not against the agent, but against the\nlegal representative of the agent. It has been held by the Punjab Chief Court,\nin a case undistinguishable from the present one, that under such circumstances\n<a href=\"/doc/1226884/\" id=\"a_753\">article 120</a> applies, and that the terminus a quo is the time when the right\nto sue accrues. The right of the plaintiff to sue thepresent defendant could\nnot have accrued until he (the defendant) had received the money from his\nfather on his father&#x27;s decease.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_159\">Similar observations appear in another Allahabad case,&#x27; which was a suit\nagainst ,the heirs of an agent and, on the facts of the case, instead of article 89.\nthe Court held that <a href=\"/doc/436447/\" id=\"a_754\">article 116</a> of the Act of 1908 applied.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_160\">31.6. With al1_respect I differ from this view. In my\nopinion, whether an account was demanded and refused or not,must de-\npend upon the circumstances of each case, and I see no reason why a refu-\nsal may not be inferred or implied from the \u00abfacts of the case.&quot; }\n\n31.9. As the judicial view now seems to have been fairly well settled, we\ndo not think it necessary to add any Explanation to indicate that the &quot;refusal&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_165\">\u00bb could be an implied one.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_166\">31.11. The third point that arises under <a href=\"/doc/1659104/\" id=\"a_761\">article 3</a> is: what should be taken\nas the point of time when the agency terminates&#x27;, within the meaning of the\n\n&#x27;. third column of the schedule?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_167\">This was also a doubt lingering in the mind of Sir Richard Garth,&#x27; when he\ncommented on the draft Bill that led to the Act of 1877.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_154\">&quot;I now proceedto clause 90, by which the period of limitation \ufb01xed for a\nprincipal to bring his action against his agent for moveable property receiv-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_155\">ed, and not accounted for, is three years from the time when the account is &#x27;\n\ndemanded and refused.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_168\">-&quot;This clause would virtually give the principal&#x27; an almost inde\ufb01nite time\nfor bringing his action. He may wait for ten, twenty or thirty years after the\nagent has left his service, when the latter may have lost every means of explain-\ning or refuting any demands made upon him, and then at the end of that time\nthe principal &#x27;may demand money or any other property he thinks proper, and\nplace the agent in the unfair position of having to discharge himself at that dis-\ntance of time from the claim.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_169\">&#x27;\\\n\nUpon this, Hon&#x27;ble Arthur Hobhouse Q.C., commented:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_156\">&quot;Surely if the relation of principal and agent comes to an end, there\nmust be a time at which it is reasonable to presume that all accounts have\nbeen settled, and it is those reasonable presumptions which we are trans-\nlating into de\ufb01nite rules&#x27;in framing a statute of limitations. I think the time\nshould run from the demand, or the close of the relation, whichever \ufb01rst\nhappens. But then <a href=\"/doc/1453009/\" id=\"a_762\">section 20</a> ought to extend to acknowledgements given in\n\n&#x27;answer to such demands, whereas it &#x27;appears to be con\ufb01ned to debts and\nlegacies.&quot; a /\n\n\u00ab 31.12. The Madras High Court? held that the agency terminated with the sell-\ning of goods, and did not continue till the accounts were settled and money re-\nmitted. A contrary view was expressed by the Calcutta, High Court in &#x27;an&#x27; early\ncase,&quot; its decision&#x27;being to the effect that the agents continue to be liable to the\nplaintiff till they accounted to him.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_157\">31.13. The Sind&#x27; and Allahabad&#x27; -view is that the \\question of termination\n\ndepends on the circumstances of each case.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_158\">/\n\n_ 31.14. It appears that the pharaseology used in the article will have to be&#x27;\napplied and interpreted in the facts of each case. A change in the pharaseology\nwould be no improvement in practice. In the result, <a href=\"/doc/1659104/\" id=\"a_763\">article 3</a> needs no change.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_159\">&#x27;Hon&#x27;ble Sir Richard Garth D.O. letter to,the Hon&#x27;ble Arthur Hobhouse, Q.C., dated\n8th March, 1876--Nationa1 Archives File pertaining to papers of 1877.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_160\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"489558\" id=\"span_53\">3Na1,-uvva and <a href=\"/doc/489558/\" id=\"a_764\">another V.
