For wages in the case of any\nother person.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_170\">When the wages accrue &quot;due&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_181\">The subject was covered in the Act of 1908 by two articles.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_182\">There was no parallel provision in the Acts of 1871 and 1877. The two\narticles on the subject occurring in the 1908 Act have been combined, on the\nrecommendation of the Law Commission in its Report on the Act of 1908.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_183\">32.3. Some questions need to be discussed in connection with the article.\nThe \ufb01rst is--Does the expression &#x27;wages&#x27; include &#x27;salary&#x27;? Arguments have been\naddressed before the Courts time and again that the salary of a Goveminenti\nservant cannot be called &quot;wages&quot;. In a case. before the Supreme Court, where\nthe appellant was a clerk in the Accounts Department of the Railways, the\nSupreme Court observed&#x27; 1--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_171\">&quot; .......... .. a good deal can be said of the contention that a claim for arrears\nof salary is distinguishable from a claim for wages. But, our dil\ufb01culty is\nthat the question appears to us to be no longer open for consideration afresh\nby us, or, at any rate, it is not advisable to review the authorities of this/\nCourt, after such a lapse of time when, despite the view taken by this Court\nthat <a href=\"/doc/390434/\" id=\"a_782\">Article 102</a> of the Limitation&#x27;Act\u00bb of 1908 was applicable to such cases,\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_783\">Limitation Act</a> of 1963 had been passed repeating the law, contained\nin <a href=\"/doc/390434/\" id=\"a_784\">Articles 102</a> and i20 of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_785\">Limitation Act</a> of -11908, in identical terms with-\nout any modi\ufb01cation. The Legislature must be presumed to be cognizant of\nthe view of this Court that a claim of the nature before us, for arrears of salary\nfalls within the purview of <a href=\"/doc/390434/\" id=\"a_786\">Article 102</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_787\">Limitation Act</a> of 190 .&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_184\">In a later case,&quot; the Supreme Court, relying on a judgment of the Federal\nCourt&#x27;, held that the term &quot;wages&quot; appearing in <a href=\"/doc/390434/\" id=\"a_788\">article 102</a> of the 1908 Act\nincludes salary and a suit for the recovery of pay is covered by this article. It\nhas also been held to include pension.&#x27;\n\nIt is proper that the judicial interpretation should be codi\ufb01ed by amending\nthe. article? The clari\ufb01cation can apply to <a href=\"/doc/19636/\" id=\"a_789\">article 6</a> as well. &#x27;\n\n32.4. The second question in connection with <a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_790\">article 7</a> relates to the situa-\ntion where the dismissal of a Government servant is set aside and, on re-instate-\nment, he sues for arrears of salary. The Supreme Court has dealt with the matter\nthus&quot;. &#x27; <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10416883\" id=\"span_55\">. &#x27;\n\n&quot;When the order of dismissal or removal is set aside by the Court on the\nground of failure to afford the constitutional protection, the order is dec-\nlared invalid ab initio, i.e., as if it, in law, never existed, and the public ser-\nvant concerned was unlawfully prevented from rendering service. If that&quot;be\nthe correct view, salary due to the public servant concerned must be deem-\ned to have accrued month after month because he had been wrongfully pre-\nvented from rendering service. The period of limitation under <a href=\"/doc/10416883/\" id=\"a_791\"><a href=\"/doc/390434/\" id=\"a_792\">Article 102</a>\n\n&#x27;S. D. S. Srivastava&#x27; V. Union of India, A.I.R.</a> 1974 S.C. 338, 341; (1974) 2 SCR 485.</span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"681259\" id=\"span_56\"><a href=\"/doc/681259/\" id=\"a_793\">&#x27;-&#x27;Maimoona Khatzm v. State of U.P.</a> (1980) 3 S.C.C. 578.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_185\">3Pmrjab Province V. Tarachand, A.l.R. 1947&#x27; F.C. 23, para 28, 33 (1947) F.C. S9.\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"984484\" id=\"span_57\"><a href=\"/doc/984484/\" id=\"a_794\">&#x27;Anand Swarup V. Punjab A.I.R.</a> 1972 SC. 2638, .</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_186\">-&quot;See paragraph 32.8, infra.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_172\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1154882\" id=\"span_58\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1154882/\" id=\"a_795\">&#x27;Jai Chand Sawhney V. Union of India</a>, (1970) 2 S.C.I. 288, 289.</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_173\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 32----Artieles 6 to 55: Suits Relating to Contracts.)\n\ndo run when the wages accrue due&#x27;, and wages accrue due when in law\nthe servant becomes entitled to wages.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_187\">32.5. These observations gave rise to a view that a period of three years\n\nfor \ufb01ling the suit for arrears of salary would be computed from month to month.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_188\">irrespective of the date when the dismissalsis held to be invalid.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_189\">However, considering the fact that courts may take a longer time than three\nyears to pronounce upon the validity or otherwise of the dismissal of a Govem-\nment servant, some High Courts have been referring to the service conditions\nto enable such a Government servant to compute the period of limitation from\nthe date when his dismissal was set aside by the court. The Bombay&#x27; High Court\nreferred to the effect of Fundamental Rules 52 and 53 upon the Government\nservant&#x27;s salary, which ceases upon his suspension when he becomes entitled\nonly to get subsistence allowance. and ceases, altogether upon his dismissal. As\na result of the Fundamental Rules, the Governinent servant cannot even osten-\nsibly put a claim to salary so long as the orders of suspension and dismissal\n\nstand. On this basis, the Bombay High Court held that the right of a suspended\n\nor dismissed Government servant to claim arrears of salary can arise only when\nthe order of suspension or dismissal is quashed and set aside, either by the de-\npartment or by the order of a civil court. On this reasoning, the Court held the\ndate of quashing of the order to be the starting point of limitation for a suit by\nsuch a Government servant for salary.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_190\">The Gujarat High Court&#x27;? like the Bombay High Court--has made the\nstatutory rules as the plank upon which to base&#x27; its judgment in this regard.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_191\">32.6. The Cuttack High Court&quot;, on the facts of a case before it, held that\n\ni the Government servant was illegally- prevented from performing his duties,\n\nalthough he was ready and willing to perform those duties, and hence the cause\nof action arose only on the day when the court quashed the impugned order of\ntransfer. &#x27;\n\nThe Madras High Court&#x27; had, before it, a case of an employee who was\ninformed that the period during which he was off from service would be treated\nas if he was on duty. The Court observed:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_174\">&quot;In the light of our observations as above, the third column in <a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_796\">article 7</a>,\n&#x27;when the wages accrue due&#x27;, in a case like the onevwith which we are faced,\nhas to be interpreted, as we said, liberally and equitably. When an employee\nwhose services have been illegally terminated has been reinstated and when\nhe is informed that the period during which he was off from service would\nbe treated as if he was on duty, then a fresh cause of action would arise on\nthe date when he was reinstated and on the date when a communication to\nthat effect was issued to him.&quot; &#x27; &quot; &#x27;\n\nThe Calcutta High Court&#x27; also held that a claim for arrears of salary of a\npublic servantrwho had been wrongfully prevented from attending to his duties\nfollowed from the declaration that the relevant order was void and a nullity.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_175\">&#x27;State of Bombay v. Sarioo Pmsad Gumasta, I.L.R. (1968) Born. 1024.\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"195113509\" id=\"span_59\"><a href=\"/doc/195113509/\" id=\"a_797\">&#x27;Laxmiben v. State of Gujarat</a>, (1970) &#x27;I Gujarat Law Reporter 51.</span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"125110072\" id=\"span_60\">&#x27;Bai_kunth\u00a2math <a href=\"/doc/125110072/\" id=\"a_798\">Pratihari V. State of West Bengal</a>, (1972-73) 77 C.W.N. 899.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_63\">103</span>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_64\">104</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_177\">Later Judg-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_178\">ment of the\nSupreme Court.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_179\">[ /\n\nRecommendation\nas to\n<a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_801\">article 7.</a>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_180\"><a href=\"/doc/691208/\" id=\"a_802\">Article 8.</a>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_181\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 32---Articles 6 to 55: Suits Relating to Contracts.)\n\n32.7. It is now no longer necessary to cull out niceties from the facts pecu-\nliar to each case, because the Supreme Court has itself, in a recent judgment&#x27;,\ndistinguished its earlier case&quot;. The Supreme Court has observed: 7\n\n&quot;We are clearly of the opinion that in cases where an employee is dismiss-\ned or removed from service and is reinstated either by the appointing autho-\nrity-or by virtue of the order of dismissal or removal being set aside by a\ncivil court, the starting point of limitation would be not the date of the\norder of dismissal or removal, but the date when the right actually accrues,\nthat is to say, the date of the reinstatement, by tl_1e&quot;appointing authority\nwhere no suit is \ufb01led or the date of the decree wl1&#x27;e&#x27;\u00a3re&quot;a suit is \ufb01led and\ndecree.&quot; &#x27; &quot; &#x27;\n\nThe earlier casei having not been overruled, the obsenva\ufb01ons made therein\nthat even if the dismissal or removal is declared ab initiaintralid, the salary due to\nthe public servant must be deemed to have accrued month-\u00abafter month, may be\nconstrued as still holding the \ufb01eld. The result is, that except in cases where the\nGovernment servant has been suspended and thereafter reinstated, the ratio of the\nearlier case may disentitle a Government servant from claiming arrears of\nsalary for a period three years prior to the institution of the suit for such arrears.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_182\">32.8. In this position, we think that the matter should be put beyond doubt\nby amending the third column of article-7 as follows :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_183\">&quot;When the wages accrue due or, where the suit is for relief consequential\non the setting aside of an order of dismissal or removal, when the order of\ndismissal or removal is aside.&quot; &#x27;\n\nFurther, as already recommended&#x27;, an Explanation should be inserted be-\nlow <a href=\"/doc/735354/\" id=\"a_803\">article 7</a>, as under :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_184\">&quot;Explanation.-----In this&#x27;article and in <a href=\"/doc/19636/\" id=\"a_804\">article 6</a>, the expression &quot;wages&quot; in-\ncludes salary and pension.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_192\">32.9. OF INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 32-~-Artie/es 6 m&#x27;55:&#x27; Suits Relating to Contrltcts.)\n\n32.13. A single Judge of the Madras High Court&#x27; has held that <a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_830\">article 31</a> of\nthe Act of 1908 (now <a href=\"/doc/1678224/\" id=\"a_831\">article 11</a>) applies to a suit against a carrier for compensa-\ntion for misdelivery of goods, and not <a href=\"/doc/1983234/\" id=\"a_832\">article 30</a> of the Act of 1908 (now article\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_203\">10). The High Court placed reliance on Supreme Court case&quot; which, though not\nexactly on the point, contains observations interpreting the expression &quot;goods ought\nto be delivered&quot;. The Delhi High Court3 also relying on a Supreme Court case&#x27;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_209\">has ruled otherwise. In the case relied upon by the Delhi High Court, however.\nthe Supreme Court had not decided the question at all, as would appear from\nthe following excerpt from the judgment&#x27; of the Supreme Court: A\n\n&quot;The learned Counsel for the appellant argued that <a href=\"/doc/1983234/\" id=\"a_833\">article 30</a> would apply\nto the suit claim, whereas the learned Counsel for the respondent contended\nthat <a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_834\">Article 31</a> would be more appropriate to the suit claim. We shall assume&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_204\">that <a href=\"/doc/1983234/\" id=\"a_835\">Article 30</a> governed the suit claim and proceed to consider the,question on\nthat basis.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_210\">On an examination of the matter, we see considerable force in the reason-\ning ofthe Madras High Court that misdelivery should be included in non-deli-\nvery (<a href=\"/doc/1678224/\" id=\"a_836\">article 11</a>), because, in such a case, the carrier has neither lost nor injured\nthe goods (<a href=\"/doc/492977/\" id=\"a_837\">article 10</a>). To put the matter beyond doubt, we recommend that the\n\nI\n\n\ufb01rst columnof article ll should be revised so as to cover misdelivery.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_211\">32.14. The third column of <a href=\"/doc/492977/\" id=\"a_838\">article 10</a>, which prescribes as the starting point\nthe time &quot;when the loss or injury occurs&quot;, has created di\ufb02iculties in the minds\nof consignors. They argue that if the goods have been lost by the Railways some-\nwhere in transit, it is impossible for them to know the date of such loss. Dealing\nwith the subject, the Madras High Court&#x27; has held that the period runs from\n\nA the date of knowledge, and not the date of quanti\ufb01cation of the damage, and\n\nhas observed that the article ought to be grammatically interpreted while apply-\ning _&#x27;&#x27;the commonsense rule&quot; that the period begins to run from the knowledge\nof damage or injury. However, observations in another Madras case&quot; indicate\nthat the starting point would be the date of actual loss. The Allahabad High\nCourt&#x27; has observed :~--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_205\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1825113\" id=\"span_70\">&quot;The period of limitation runs from the actual date of loss and not from\n&quot;thedate when it comes to the notice of the plaintilf; the date when he comes\nto know of&#x27; the loss is utterly irrelevant, ,not being an ingredient of the cause\nof action. It is axiomatic that the date&#x27;, of the happening of an event is\ndifferent from the date of knowledge of the happening. A consignee may\nnot know of loss of the consignmentas soon as it takes place and if the\nlawrof limitation containedin &#x27;article.30 operates harshly upon the consignee,\n&#x27;the remedy is with the Legislature and not in distorting the language used\nin <a href=\"/doc/1983234/\" id=\"a_839\">article 30.&quot;</a> &#x27; i i &#x27;\n\n32.15. Whenever open delivery of goods is taken, the courts have been\nholding that limitation would begin to&quot;&quot;1&quot;un from the date of open delivery which\n\n\\\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1825113/\" id=\"a_840\">&#x27;Union of India v. Ramchand Kishanchand &#x27;&amp; Co., A.I.R.</a> 1974 Mad. 335, 337.</span>\n2Botlamai V. Union&#x27; of India, A.I.R. 1962 S.C. l7l6.\n30972) 74 Punjab Law Reporter 101.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_212\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"118581261\" id=\"span_71\"><a href=\"/doc/118581261/\" id=\"a_841\">&#x27;Union of India V. There was previously some con\ufb02ict of opinion on the question whe-Sh9rt--~\nther short delivery amounted to &quot;loss&quot; within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/492977/\" id=\"a_845\">article 10</a> or whe- d\u00b0&quot;V\u00b0&#x27;y C\nther itamounted to &quot;non-delivery&quot; within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/1678224/\" id=\"a_846\">article 11.</a> But the\ncon\ufb02ict has now been set at rest by the Supreme Court,&quot; which has held that\nwhere a part of the consignment has been delivered, that should be taken to be\nthe date when the goods ought to have been delivered as a whole within the\nmeaning of thesewords in <a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_847\">article 31</a> of the Act of&#x27;1908 (now <a href=\"/doc/1678224/\" id=\"a_848\">article 11</a>). A question that needs to be considered is what should be&quot; the start-\ning point for limitation under article 14. when the goods are delivered at di\ufb01erent\ntimes? The view of the Madras High Court&#x27; is that when goods are supplied\nfrom time to time and the payment is also made from time to time, <a href=\"/doc/1205651/\" id=\"a_864\">article 52</a> of\nthe Limitation Act, 1908 [corresponding to <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_865\">article 14</a> of the present Act) would\napply after the payments are appropriated under <a href=\"/doc/1426985/\" id=\"a_866\">sections 60</a> and <a href=\"/doc/421859/\" id=\"a_867\">61</a> of the Indian\nContract Act, 1877. The result would be, that if the date corresponding to the\n\ndelivery that is unadjusted falls beyond three years from the date of institution &#x27;\n\nof the suit, the claim would be time-barred.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_232\">The Rajasthan High Court&#x27; has, on the other hand, regarded a payment in\nsuch case of goods sold at different dates as &quot;payment&quot; within the meaning of\nsection 20 of the Limitation Act, 1908 (<a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_868\">section 19</a> of the present Act), so as to\ngive a fresh period of limitation to the items which are not harried by limitation\non the date of that payment. The Karate, High Court&#x27; has held that even though\nsome payments by cheque were made by the defendant towards the price of\ngoods supplied from time to time on account, such a payment cannot be cons-\ntrued as amounting to an acknowledgement of the debt under <a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_869\">section 19</a> of the\nLimitation Act, 1963.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_233\">32.22. The manner in which payments are to be applied by the creditor has\nbeen codi\ufb01ed in <a href=\"/doc/1169374/\" id=\"a_870\">sections 59</a> to <a href=\"/doc/421859/\" id=\"a_871\">61</a> of the Contract Act. The applicability of arti-\ncle I4 to such a situation would primarily depend upon the facts of each case,\nand on the mode of agreement between the purchaser and the seller.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_234\">Consequently, no amendment of the article is called for, to deal speci\ufb01cally\nwith cases of &#x27;delivery of goods from time to time.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_235\">32.23. (an) <a href=\"/doc/609295/\" id=\"a_872\">Article 15</a> reads as under :~--- -\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_216\">&quot;For the price of goods sold and\n\ndelivered to be id for after the\nexpiry of a \ufb01xe\ufb01ilveriod of credit.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_217\">Three When the period of credit expires.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_236\">years. &#x27;\n\nIt is identical with <a href=\"/doc/1597349/\" id=\"a_873\">article 53</a> of the Acts of 1908 and 1877. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_874\">In the Act</a> of\n1871, <a href=\"/doc/1205651/\" id=\"a_875\">article 52</a> was in the same terms but the starting point was---&quot;Thc expiry\nof -the period of credit&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_237\">and page 83, Article 1. .\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_218\">&quot;Md. The Patna High Court&quot; has held that when a contractor executes aArticle 10 and\nwork for the Government under a contract which is \ufb01nalised after the mvitationgitgggfnt\nof tenders, it cannot be said that the work was done at the &quot;request of the Gov-\nernment&quot; (<a href=\"/doc/1408921/\" id=\"a_887\">article 56</a> of the Act of 1908).&#x27; Hence, the case is outside <a href=\"/doc/1408921/\" id=\"a_888\">article 56</a>,\n\nand is governed by the residuary article,(<a href=\"/doc/1226884/\" id=\"a_889\">article 120</a> of the Act of 1908, now\n<a href=\"/doc/10691/\" id=\"a_890\">article 113</a>). &#x27; &#x27;\n\nA contrary view has been taken by the Andhra High Court} which has held\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_251\">-that such a suit is one for claiming price for work done and not &quot;for compensa-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_252\">tion for breach of any contract&quot;. Three. When.the demand is made.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_275\">agreement that it shallbepayable years. .\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_276\">on demand.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_277\">There was no corresponding provision in the Act of 1871.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_278\">32.31. Controversies often _arise as to period of limitation applicable\nwhere a transaction is on the border-line between deposit and loan. The\nSupreme Court has observed&#x27; as under, regarding such border-line cases :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_226\">&quot;Further, as this was a deposit, limitation would run at the earliest\n&#x27;from the refusal and not from the date of demand. It was contended that\nthe plaintilf had made a demand as far back as 1948 and therefore the\nperiod of limitation would run from that date, and hence the suit was\nhopelessly barred by limitation, because six years expired some time in the\nyear 1954.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_279\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1315480\" id=\"span_81\">&#x27;A bdul <a href=\"/doc/1315480/\" id=\"a_913\">Karim v. Dy. Custodian General, =A.</a>(1.n. 1964 so 1256, 1259.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_280\">\\\nl\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA&#x27; ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 32~AArticles 6 to 55: Suits Relating to Contracts.)\n\nEarlier also. the Court&#x27; had made general observations indicating the\nguidelines to be followed for determining whether a transaction is one of deposit\nor a loan:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_227\">I\n\n&quot;Even though the transaction was a transaction of deposit as above\nstated, the deposit could be coupled with an agkement that it would be\npayable on demand.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_228\">&quot;Such an agreement could be expressed or implied and if an express\nagreement in that behalf was recorded inthe document in the terms above,\nthe transaction of deposit could not be thereby converted into a transaction\nof loan and the words &#x27;we shall pay thesaid sum&#x27; could not convert the\ndocument into a promissory note.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_229\">&quot;The promise to pay would be involved in a promissory note as well\nas in a deposit within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/1102405/\" id=\"a_914\">Article 60.</a> <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_915\">Limitation Act</a> and the\ncourt would have regard to the intention of the parties and the circumstances\nof thecase in order to arrive at the conclusion whether document was a pr&#x27;o-\nmissory note.&quot; i I\n32.32. In this position, it does not seem necessary to suggest any amendment\n\nin. the article. The question of \ufb01xed deposits (deposits for a speci\ufb01ed period) will\nbe dealt with separately.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_281\">32.33. Although the Limitation Act contains fairly elaborate provisions re-\ngarding suits for the recovery of money, it does not deal speci\ufb01cally with claims\nfor the recovery of money which is placed in \ufb01xed deposits with banks or other\ncompanies. As a result, one cannot immediately locate the article that is applica-\nble to suits which are instituted for getting back money deposited for a stipulated\n\nperiod. It appears to us that the law of limitation should contain a speci\ufb01c provi-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_282\">sion on the subject, since the placing of money in \ufb01xed deposits with companies\nand banks is now a very common practice. The articles of the Act which might\n&#x27;possibly become applicable to such transactions----artiele 27 or <a href=\"/doc/1164414/\" id=\"a_916\">article 55</a> are not\nspeci\ufb01cally meant for \ufb01xed deposits. If the law is to be easy of understanding.\nit is &#x27;necessary that a speci\ufb01c article should be inserted in the Act to cover such\nsuits-. This will help in removing the obscurity of position which has been referred\ntoabove. &#x27;\n\n According to a Supreme Court judgment, the period of limitation in the case of\nmoney deposited for a speci\ufb01ed period would start running on the expiry of the\nperiod of deposit and a&#x27; demand. as such. is not a condition precedent to the\nstarting of the period of limitation.&quot; <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"930830\" id=\"span_82\">According to the Allahabad High Court&#x27; and\nthe Nagpur High Court&#x27;, <a href=\"/doc/1102405/\" id=\"a_917\">article 60</a> of the Act of 1908 (present <a href=\"/doc/581566/\" id=\"a_918\">article 22</a>) does\nnot apply to \ufb01xed deposits. ,5 &#x27;\n\nAccording to the High Court of Patna.&#x27; <a href=\"/doc/581566/\" id=\"a_919\">article 22</a> applies where a demand\nafter the termination of the period is necessary. =\n\nAccording to the High Court of Jammu and Kashmir&#x27; and the High Court of\nPunjab and Haryana&quot;, a demand is required before money placed in \ufb01xed deposit\n\n<a href=\"/doc/930830/\" id=\"a_920\">&#x27;Annamalai v, Veerappa, A.I.R.</a> 1956 S.C. 12.\n&#x27;-&#x27;See discussion relating to article 22A. infra.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_283\">3K. The\nPunjab decision&#x27; (referred to above) seeks to distinguish the Supreme Court ruling\nof 1958 on the ground that that was not a case of deposit with a bank. --\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_237\">The di\ufb02iculty seems to have arisen because the distinction between money\n\n_ payable on demand and money payable without demand does not seem to have\n\nbeen given its due prominence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_238\">Where money is deposited with a bank, no doubt, the question whether it is\nto be paid only on demand depends on the intention of the parties and the terms\nof the agreement. Where money is payable only on a demand .being made. limi-\ntation does not commence until the demand is made? However, the case is\ndifferent where the period of deposit is \ufb01xed. It should be noted that banks and\ncompanies accepting deposits do not, in general, pay interest for &quot;the period after\nexpiry of the term of deposit. This shows that on expiry of the term of deposit\nthe money becomes payable.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_239\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"125110072\" id=\"span_85\">From the case law on the subject. it appears that there is considerable obs-\ncurity as to the position concerning the computation of the period of limitation\nfor money placed in \ufb01xed deposits. In the absence of a speci\ufb01c provision on the\nsubject, courts have had to hunt out the article to be applied &quot;and to take resort\nto some provision or other which was not perhaps speci\ufb01cally intended for a\n\ufb01xed deposit (i.e., deposit for a stipulted period). For example, in an <a href=\"/doc/125110072/\" id=\"a_923\">Orissa</a>\ncase} it was held that the deposit of money was covered by <a href=\"/doc/1537130/\" id=\"a_924\">article 145</a> of the\nAct of 1908, corresponding to <a href=\"/doc/1811608/\" id=\"a_925\">article 70</a> of the present Act (Deposit of &quot;movable\nproperty&quot;), and that the limitation for a suit to recover money so deposited start-\ned not on the date of demand. but from the date of the refusal following the\ndemand.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_240\">32.34. By way of a statement of the legalpposition under the earlier&#x27; Act\n(Act of 1908), it may be mentioned that the situation was not unambiguous\nunder the Act also. <a href=\"/doc/858095/\" id=\"a_926\">Article 59</a> of that Act, for example, applied to a suit &quot;for\nmoney lent under an agreement that it shall be payable on demand.&quot; Again,\n<a href=\"/doc/1102405/\" id=\"a_927\">article 60</a> covered certain other suits for money. But none of these articles was\nspeci\ufb01cally applicable to a suit for the recovery of money deposited for a \ufb01xed\n\n\u00bb\n\nterm. Sometimes, the residuary article as _to &quot;compensation for the breach of .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_241\">any contract&#x27; was resorted to&#x27;. it is to be remembered that generally where\nmoney is placed for a de\ufb01nite term, the liability to repay commences as soon as\nthe term expires----which is the reason why articles dealing with money payable\n&quot;on demand&quot; do not take within their fold \ufb01xed deposits. The result of this\nposition is that &#x27;both under the earlier.Act and under the present Act. the courts\nhave to fall back upon the residuary article governing a suit for &quot;compensation\nfor breach of any contract&quot; (<a href=\"/doc/1464822/\" id=\"a_928\">article 115</a> of the Act of 1908, corresponding to\n<a href=\"/doc/1164414/\" id=\"a_929\">article 55</a> of the present Act) or on some article not speci\ufb01cally intended for \ufb01x-\ned deposits.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_242\">&#x27;K. S. Wani V. New Akola Cotton Ginning Press, A.l.R. 1958 SC. 437, 438, 439,\n&quot;Annamalai v. Veerappd A.I.R. 1956 S.C. 12. 15.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_243\">&#x27;Gouri Shankar Misra v. Bannamali Babu, I.L.R. (1979) 2 Cut. 108.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_244\">&#x27;Article 115, 1908 Act, <a href=\"/doc/1164414/\" id=\"a_930\">article 55.</a> 1963 Act.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_245\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"45485388\" id=\"span_86\">51. s. <a href=\"/doc/45485388/\" id=\"a_931\">Seema v. R. K. Bcmerjee, A.I.R. lS</a>!36 Rang. 338.</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_246\">891=n REPORT or L1}.W COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION. ACT, 1-963 . M3\n\n(Chapter 32----<a href=\"/doc/19636/\" id=\"a_932\">Articles 6</a> to 55----Suits Relating to Contracts.)\n\n32.35. This is hardly a satisfactory situation. Once the deposit becomes Recommendation\npayable, Jimitation should start running and there is need for a speci\ufb01c provi-\nsicn on the subject. For these reasons, we recommend that a speci\ufb01c article on\nthe subject, to be numbered as article 22-A should be inserted in the Limitation\n.&#x27; Act, after <a href=\"/doc/581566/\" id=\"a_933\">article 22.</a> It should run somewhat on the following lines :----~\n\n&quot;22A&quot; For money deposited under an Three When &#x27;the st&#x27;ipulat&#x27;ed period\nagreement that it shall be payable Years expires.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_284\">on the expiry of a stipulated period,\nincluding money of a customer in\nthe hands of is banker so payable.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_285\">v\n\n32.36. This takes us to article 23. which reads as under : Articlc 23,\n\n_ &quot;For money payable to the plain- Three When the money is paid.&quot;\n3111&#x27; for money paid for the defen- years.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_286\">ant.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_287\">It is identical with <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_934\">article 61</a> of the Acts of 1908 and 1877, and with article\n.59 of the Act of 1871.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_288\">32.37. A question may arise whether the payment contemplated by the ar- Voluntary\nticle should be voluntary payment or one &#x27;in lieu of the coercive process of law. paymn&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_247\">&#x27; The following observations&#x27; of Anson&#x27; (in the context of the substantive law of\ncontracts) may be of interest in this connection, though they do not deal with\nlimitation as such: .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_248\">&#x27;&quot;Thc payment made by the plainti\ufb01 may be purely voluntary. It need not\nbe the result of any legal&#x27; liability, or compulsion. Nor need it operate so\nas to relieve the defendant of a liab&#x27; &#x27; &#x27; for the claim to reimbursement is\nnot affected by the fact that payment not made in discharge of a debt\n&quot;for which the defendant would himself be liable.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_289\">The basis of the cause of action for the claim; in such cases is a matter of &#x27;\nsubstantive law. There is a Madras judgment&quot; from a very distinguished Judge\nCBha.shyam Ayyangar, J.) to the effect that it is immaterial whether the party\nseeking contribution made the payment voluntarily or involuntarily. In either\n\n, case, he is damnilied and the parties against whom ..he seeks contribution have to\nthat extent been bene\ufb01ted. A Calcutta judgment&quot; bases the right and duty of\n\n&#x27; contribution on the doctrine of equity (and not on contract). When a person&#x27;s\nproperty is sold fora debt where the liability is joint, he does have, even accor-\nding to the Calcutta view, a claim against his joint debtor for the amount paid\nin excess of his half share (though he cannot be said to have &quot;paid&quot; the amount).\nIn -either view of the matter. the person makingepayment is not left without a\nremedy. We would not therefore recommend any amendment in the article under\nconsideration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_290\">32.38. The. Supreme Court has ruled&#x27; that in orderto attract <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_943\">article 62</a> of\nthe Act of 1908 (present <a href=\"/doc/1540780/\" id=\"a_944\">article 24</a>), it is notinecessary that at the moment of\nthe receipt of money the defendant should have actually intended to receive it\nfor the use of the plaintilf. As the same time, the article cannot apply if the\nright to refund does not arise immediately on receipt by the defendant, but arises\n\n&#x27; .only by reason of facts that transpire subsequently. The philosophy of the ar-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_306\">ticle is that the plaintill has a cause of action for instituting the suit at the very\nmoment of the receipt.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_307\">32.42. Since the group or <a href=\"/doc/1734560/\" id=\"a_973\">articles (28</a><a href=\"/doc/1888152/\" id=\"a_974\">, 29</a> and<a href=\"/doc/1983234/\" id=\"a_975\">&#x27; 30)</a> deals with limitation for\nsuits based on a bond, the use of the expression &quot;single bond&quot; occurring in arti-\ncles 28 and 29 needs some comment. The simple de\ufb01nition of &quot;single bon &quot; is\nthat it is a bond merely for the payment of a certain sum of money, without any\ncondition annexed to it.-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_259\">But even Halsbury,&#x27; while discussing &quot;single bond&quot;, to fall back on Shakes-\npeare and quote from &quot;Merchant of Venice&quot; :3\n\n&quot;Go with me to a notary, seal me there Your single bond, and, in a merry\nsport. In such as place, such sum or sums as are Express&#x27;d in the condition,\nlet the forfeit Be .............. .. ......... ..&quot; A\n\nHalsbury comments that such instruments have become rare and the term\n&quot;single hon &quot; acquired various meanings&#x27;, including a bond given by one obligor\nas distinguished from one given by two or more.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_260\">32.51. Application of <a href=\"/doc/1734560/\" id=\"a_976\">articles 28</a><a href=\"/doc/1888152/\" id=\"a_977\">, 29</a> or 30 would depend upon the terms and\nconditions of the bond in question. Such being the case. it is not surprising that\ncertain amount of con\ufb02ict did arise regarding the application of these articles. The\ncon\ufb02ict was, however, predicated more on the interpretation of the term of the\n&quot;bond&quot;, that on any conditions of interpretation of the text of the articles?-&#x27;\n\n<a href=\"/doc/211413/\" id=\"a_978\">Article 27</a> is a general article covering breaches of promise to do anything\nat a speci\ufb01ed time or upon the happening of a speci\ufb01ed contingency. Certain con-\n\ufb01ict has emerged when the article is applied to breaches arising out of the bonds.\nFor example, in a Madras cases, where a bond was executed for subscriptions\npayable to a chit fund in speci\ufb01ed instalments, with the condition that the whole\namount would become exigible on failure to pay any instalment on demand, the\nCourt laid emphasis on the expression &quot;on demand&quot; contained in the bond and\nheld that the claim for the whole amount due could be based only on the bond\nitself, and not on the original cause of action. This ruling did not follow an ear-\nlier one of the same High Court.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_90\">0</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_333\">32.52. Conflicting views expressed by the Bombay&#x27; and Allahabadz High\nCourts are sometimes referred to under this article. But the decisions referred to\nrelate to problems of Hindu law and obligations imposed thereunder upon the\nson for the debts of the deceased father. Recently, this article (along with\n<a href=\"/doc/280240/\" id=\"a_998\">article 112</a>) &#x27;came up for consideration in the Allahabad High Court} which\nheld that when the claim of a private person was barred by time on the date\nof his death, the devolution of his property on the Government by escheat would\nnot enable the Government to take advantage of the extended period of <a href=\"/doc/280240/\" id=\"a_999\">article\n112</a>, so as to bring the cause of action within limitation. Herein also, the text\nof <a href=\"/doc/448465/\" id=\"a_1000\">article 35</a> has not presented any di\ufb01iculty and we recommend no change\ntherein. &#x27; &#x27;\n\n32.60. <a href=\"/doc/784506/\" id=\"a_1001\">Article 36</a> reads as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_279\">Three\nyears\n\n&quot;On a promissory note or bond\n\nThe expiration of the \ufb01rst term\npayable by instalments.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_280\">of payment as-to the part then\npayable; and for the other parts,\nthe expiration of the respective\nterms of payment.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_281\">It is identical with <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1002\">article 74</a> of the Acts of 1908 and 1877 and <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1003\">article 74</a>\n\nof the Act of 1871.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_282\">32.61. With the increased solicitude for thewelfare of the oppressed villagers\nand landless labourers, the Legislature has&quot; stepped in to relieve the borrower\nof the hardship inherent in the unpleasant prospect of facing a decree, when, owing\nto natural calamities or failure of crops, he is unable to discharge his obligations.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_360\">One such Act is the Madras indebted Agriculturist (Repayment of Debts) Act.\n1955, which de\ufb01nes a &quot;debt&#x27;? as including even a decree or order of a court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_361\"><a href=\"/doc/880040/\" id=\"a_1004\">Section 3</a> of the Act provides that no suit for the recoveryof a debt shall be /i A\n\ninstituted, and no application for&#x27; execution of adecree in respect of a debt shall\nbe made, against any agriculturist in any civil or revenue court before the expiry\nof four months from the commencement of the Act. <a href=\"/doc/1393166/\" id=\"a_1005\">Section 4</a> prescribes a mode\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_362\">-of payment of debt in instalments, while <a href=\"/doc/996339/\" id=\"a_1006\">section 8</a> excludes the time during which\nthe institution of the suit was barred from computation of _the period of\nlimitation. &quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_283\">Similar provisions exist in &#x27;other State laws, like the <a href=\"/doc/139344480/\" id=\"a_1007\">Bengal Agricultural\nDebtors Act, 1936</a> and C.P. &amp; Berar Relief of Indebtedness Act, 1939.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_284\">32.62. In spite of the changes effected in the \ufb01rst and third columns of the Con\ufb02jct ,,\narticle as detailed above, certain amount of con\ufb02ict did arise in the application:I&#x27;l&#x27;e1i&quot;:5.&quot;&#x27;;&#x27;\u00b0&#x27;;l\u00abds\nof this article to practical situations. It has been held by the Patna High Cou &#x27;\nthat in the case of an ordinary money instalment bond with a default clause\nwhich provides that if any instalment is not paid the whole sum would be due,\nthe limitation for a claim to recover money due under the bond runs from the\ndate of the \ufb01rst default that is not waived. The creditor cannot stopthe period\nof limitation from running by waiting till the whole of the sum becomes due.\nTherefore, in such cases, it was no longer at the option of the creditor (unless\nhe had waived the default) to stop the period of limitation from running.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_368\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"153096948\" id=\"span_96\">32.66. ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963 ,\n\n(Chapter 32~~ <a href=\"/doc/19636/\" id=\"a_1023\">Articles 6</a> to 55--Suits Relating to Contracts.)\n\npay and which had not become themselves time-barred under <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1024\">article 74</a> of the\nAct of 1908 (now <a href=\"/doc/784506/\" id=\"a_1025\">article 36</a>). The two remedies are co-existent, and both are\nopen to the creditor. The creditor has his choice either to wait and sue for the\ninstalments in default under <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1026\">article 74</a>, or to enforce the default clause in a suit\ncontemplated by <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1027\">article 75 (both of the Act of 1908).</a> A suit \ufb01led under <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1028\">article\n75</a> may be dismissed &#x27;on the debtor proving waiver by the creditor of the default\nthat entitled the creditor to \ufb01le such a suit, in which case the creditor can, of\ncourse, still fall back on his right to -sue for instalments within the time limits\nprescribed_&#x27;by <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1029\">article 74.</a> The creditor has also the other alternative of suing to en-\nforce the default clause for a subsequent default which he has not waived within the\nperiod prescribed by <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1030\">article 75.</a> Reading the two articles together, the contention that\n\nbecause the whole amount becomes payable on default, the creditor must sue on\n\nthe wholebond within three years of. such a default could not be accepted. Thus\nthe plaintiff has the option of enforcing the default clause in <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1031\">article 75</a>, and if he\ndoes not do so, he must be deemed to have waived the bene\ufb01t of the provision,\nand he then can fall back on his ordinary right of suit on the covenant to\npay by instalments under <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1032\">article 74 (present <a href=\"/doc/784506/\" id=\"a_1033\">article 36</a>).</a> -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_291\">32.67. In one case before the KeralaHigh Court,&#x27; an argument was addressed\nthat once a default has taken place in the payment of instalments, time starts\nrunning from the date of default and a suit \ufb01led three years after the first default\nis also barred by time. But the High Court held that default in each instal-\nment creates a separate cause of action and causes of action within three years\nbefore the suit are not barred.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_292\">32.68. It seems that the applicability of <a href=\"/doc/76375/\" id=\"a_1034\">article 37</a> to a particular case de-\npends largely on the terms of the bond and on any statute which may govern or\nmodify the terms of the bond as a matter of substantive law. The words &quot;creditor\nshall have liberty&quot; may, for example. create an option. while more stringent\nwords may rule out an option. Waiver, again, is /a mixed question of law and\nfact. and whether the waiver was express or implied will &#x27;have to be discerned\n\nfrom the facts of each case? This is evident from numerous rulings of High\nCourts, involving different facts situations.&#x27;-5\n\nIn this position, no amendment is recommended in the article.\n32.69. (Chapter 32-\u00ab ---Articles 6 I0 55: Suits Relating to Contracts.)\n\n&quot;But what should be the rule when the party suing for the mesne pro\ufb01ts\nhas been dispossessed of the land in respect of which mesne pro\ufb01ts are claim-\ned, by a decree of court afterwards set aside on appeal.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_437\">5 32.90. From the legislative history&#x27; of article 51. it would be seen that the\nclause pertaining to recovery of possession consequent upon a decree being set\naside an appeal (obtaining in the third column of <a href=\"/doc/729393/\" id=\"a_1094\">article 109</a> of the 1871 Act\nand the 1877 Act) has been omitted in the 1908 Act. Commenting on this\nomission, the Mysore High Court&#x27; after comparing the phrascology of the two\nActs, observed: ~\n\n&quot;In my opinion, this deletion indicates the intention of the legislature that\n<a href=\"/doc/729393/\" id=\"a_1095\">article 109 (<a href=\"/doc/854952/\" id=\"a_1096\">article 51</a> in the Act of 1963)</a> would not be applicable to a\nsuit for restitution.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_438\">,This reasoning has been adopted by theiAl1al1abad3 and Andhra Pradesh&#x27;\nHigh Courts, &#x27;\n\n32.91. Earlier, the High Courts of Lahore&#x27;, Allahabad&quot; and Bombay&#x27; had\nheld otherwise.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_439\">The change inthe phraseology of the third column was noticed by the\nMadras High Court*&#x27;: &#x27;\n\n&quot;The reason for omission was probably the change which section (144,\nclause (2), <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1097\">C.P.C</a>. 1908 introduced, as a separate.&#x27; suit for recovery of such\npro\ufb01ts was barred by that section.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_440\">32.92. The Supreme&quot; Court has since held that an application for restitu-\ntion under <a href=\"/doc/61862423/\" id=\"a_1098\">section 144</a>, C.P.C. is an application for execution of a decree and\nis therefore governed by article .182, Limitation Act, 1908 (present <a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_1099\">article 136</a>)\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_441\">--and not by <a href=\"/doc/1865420/\" id=\"a_1100\">article 181</a> of the Limitation Act, 1908 (present <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_1101\">article 137</a>)----a\nresiduary article of limitation for applications. This position needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_442\">32.93. The Calcutta High Court&#x27;&quot; has held that the true test for determining\nwhen a cause of action has accrued is to ascertain the time when the plaintiff\ncould \ufb01rst have maintained his action to a successful conclusion. Carrying on this\nanalogy of &quot;cause of action&quot; to the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1102\">Limitation Act</a>, the Court held that a plaintiff\nwho could sue for mesne pro\ufb01ts for the period from 1913 to 1918 only on 22nd\n\n&#x27; June, 1918 is not debarred from maintaining the suit which was instituted on\n\n28th April, 1921.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_443\">32.94. However, the general trend is represented by an earlier Allahabad\njudgement&quot; which ruled that the period of limitation cannot be suspended once it\nhas begun to run (unless that suspension is itself provided for in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1103\">Limitation\nAct</a>) and that the plaintilfs are not entitled to get a decree for mesne pro\ufb01ts for\nmore than three years prior to the date of the suit:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_348\">.. _\n&#x27; &#x27;Para&#x27;32.87 snpra.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_349\">2BaIappa V. Mata Prasud</a>, (1927) I.L.R. 49 All. 565, 573.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_107\">1</span>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_354\">89m REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Clmpitr 32 /Irticlrnr 6 to 55: Suits Relating to Comracis.)\n\n_ &quot;It would be dangerous to lay down generaliy that there is some prin-\nciple outside the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1106\">Indian Limitation Act</a> under which Limitation can be sus-\npended. Such&#x27; a conclusion would be quite contrary to the intention of the\nlegislature.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_444\">32.95. &quot;Their Lordships of the Privy Council in the case of Soni Ram v.\nKanihaiya Lalf themselves remarked that there was nothing in the Indian Limi-\ntation Act which would justify the Board in holding that&#x27; once the period of limi-\ntation had begun to run it could be suspended. Their Lordship considered that if\nthey were to hold that by some reason the period of limitation was suspended.\nthey would be deciding contrary to the express enactment of <a href=\"/doc/268914/\" id=\"a_1107\">section 9</a> that &#x27;when\nonce time has begun to run, no subsequent disability &#x27;or inability to sue stops it&#x27;.\nThis remark of their Lordships clearly indicates that the period of limitation can-\nnot be suspended once it has begun to run, unless that suspension is itself pro-\nvided for in the Act. We are, therefore, unable to&#x27; accept any universal principle\nof suspension of limitation outside the Limitation. Act&#x27;). --\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_108\">127</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_445\">32.96. The Calcutta High Court* ruled in 1908 that the words &quot;when the pro- No changa as &#x27;\n\n\ufb01ts are received&quot; mean &quot;when the pro\ufb01ts are actually received&quot;. As the point has &#x27;\u00b0 &quot;&quot;&quot;5&quot;3\n\nnot recurred in recent cases. there appears to be no need to suggest any amend-\nment on that score. &quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_355\">32.97. Most of the suits under this article coming from the-mofussil relate Starting\n\nto agricultural property and the point of time _from which limitation should be\ndeemed to be running (in regard to mesne pro\ufb01ts of agricultural land) became a\nmatter of controversy. Unlike the occupant of a tenement or a building (the rent\nwhereof is payable on a \ufb01xed date of a month), a person in possession of an agri-\ncultural farm would be cutting the crops, drying them and selling them in the\nMandi at various times of the year, and, depending upon the fertility of -the soil,\nmight reap two or three crops a year. When the Draft Bill of the 1877 Act was\ncirculated for comments, two views were expressed. The National Archives \ufb01le\nfor the 1877 Indian Limitation Bill contains a summary of the various opinions:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_356\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"2766853\" id=\"span_109\">&quot;Balu Opendro Nath Mitra, Government Pleader, Dacca is of the opi-\nnion--and.in this opinion the judge of that District by whom he was con-\nsulted, concurs----that the time allowed (three years) should be at least &#x27;six\nyears. if not twelve,~the time allowed for a suit for the recovery of the land\n&quot;from the wrongful holder: and that the cause of action for pro\ufb01ts receivable\nin each year, should be. held to accrue on the \ufb01rst of the following year. In\nany case the judge thinks that time should begin from when the pro\ufb01ts were\n&quot;receivable&quot;, not &quot;received&quot;, whatever the number of years the legislature\nmay allow.&quot; &#x27;\n\n&quot;Mr. W. J. Money. Oliiciating Judge of Maimansingh, said the clause is very\ngenerally worried and it is not clear whether it is applicable to suits for mesne\npro\ufb01ts. Added that it would seem. from the time when-the periodbegins to run\nbeing&#x27; \ufb01xed to be &quot;when the pro\ufb01ts are received&quot;, that the clause is not applicable\nto cases of dispossession and suits for mesne pro\ufb01ts&#x27; thereon; inasmuch as the\nrule for the calculation of the latter is that their determination is not limited to\nthe amount actually received. but to that which, according to the assets, might\nwith due care and diligence, have been realised, and the period of limitation dates\nfrom the time of dispossession.&quot; &#x27;\n\n<a href=\"/doc/2766853/\" id=\"a_1108\">&#x27;Sam&#x27; Ram v. Declaration of forgery of\n\n/89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 32~~ArtI&#x27;cles 6 to 55: Suits relating to Contracts.)\n( Chapter 33 ~Ar-ticles 56 to 58: Suits relating to Declarations.)\n\ntract. This dichotomy was found unnecessary by the Law Commission which&#x27;,\nin its Report on the Act of 1908, recommended as under: I\n\n&quot;If simpli\ufb01cation is desirable, as undoubtedly it is, all the above-men-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_488\">. tioned articles may be omitted and a provision may be made as in the\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1003813/\" id=\"a_1149\">English Act</a>, that in case of suits founded on contract, time runs from the\n\ndate on which the cause of action accrues and a uniform period of three\n\nyears may be prescribed. It is not necessary to retain the period of six\n\nyears in case of registered contracts on the analogy of speciality debts under\nEnglish law.&#x27;&#x27;. &#x27;\n\nThe change has put an end to many of the earlier controversies which had i\n\ncentered on the difference between the two articles. No further comments are\nneeded on <a href=\"/doc/1164414/\" id=\"a_1150\">article 55.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_489\">L CHAPTER 33\n<a href=\"/doc/1408921/\" id=\"a_1151\">ARTICLES 56</a> TO 58: SUITS RELATING TO_ DECLARATIONS\n\n33.1. <a href=\"/doc/1408921/\" id=\"a_1152\">Article 56</a> runs as follows&#x27;.--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_386\">&quot;To declare the forgery of an Three years. The nearer reversionary heir may be an old man\nwho may not care to undergo the worry and expenses of a litigation whose\nfruits he may not live to enjoy. The present article applies only to\u00bb suits for declaration simplicitcr\nand combines <a href=\"/doc/986229/\" id=\"a_1202\">articles 93</a>, I19 and 129 of the Act of 1908. Some comments received at the time of drafting of the I877 Act\nhave been discussed earlier&quot;, but one additional comment deserves notice. . One\nMr. N. H. Thomson&quot; expressed the following view on the subject matter of arti-\ncles 119 and 129 &#x27;of the Act of 1877 :---- (the gist whereof is now combined in\n<a href=\"/doc/1665174/\" id=\"a_1203\">article 58</a>).\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_481\">&quot;No. 124.--Where no maintenance has been received 1 would make the\nperiod begin to run from the time when the right to maintenance accrued.\nWhere the suit is for arrears, from the time they were payable.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_482\">&quot;No. 125.----Therc seems to be a doubt. whether a suit to set aside an\nadoption will lie unless consequential relief be asked.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_557\">33.33. The doubts expressed by Mr. N. I-I..Thomson about the applica-\nbility of the article to suits for a declaration coupled with consequential relief\npersist even now; In a case which went upto the Supreme Court&#x27; (though not\ndirectly concerned with the implications -of this article) it was observed as\nunder :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_483\">&quot;A suit for declaration with Li consequential relief for injunction, is not a\nsuit for declaration simplicitcrz it is a suit for declaration with further\nrelief. Whether the further relief claimed-in a particular case is conse-\nquential upon a declaration is adequate must always depend upon the facts\nand circumstances of each case.,&#x27; ........ .. A suit for a declaration of a right\nand an injunction restraining the defendants. from interfering with the exer-\ncise ofthat right is governed by <a href=\"/doc/1226884/\" id=\"a_1204\">article 120</a> of the&#x27;<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1205\">Limitation Act</a> and in\nsuch a &#x27;suit the right.to sue arises when the cause of action accrues.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_558\">33.34. The Law Commissio? recommendedlin its Report on the Act of 1908\nthat the period of limitation o 60 years allowed for the redemption of a mort-\ngige in <a href=\"/doc/1422498/\" id=\"a_1243\">article 148</a> of the Act of 1908 should&#x27; be cut down to 12 years. on the\n\n&#x27; analogy of the practice available in England. As regards <a href=\"/doc/1251904/\" id=\"a_1244\">article 105</a>, the Com&#x27;-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_595\">mission recommended the retention of the period, and, as regards <a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_1245\">article 134</a>,\n\n&quot;the Commission recommended that it should be split up in so far as it related\n\nto mortgages. However, all these articles were clpbbed into one article and enac-\nted as <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1246\">article 61</a> in the Act of 1963, &quot;with the reduction of the period of limitation\n\n&#x27; for redemption from 60 years to 30 years only.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_596\">35.3. The Bombay High Court has held&quot; that a suit by a puisne mortgagee\nwho had not been impleaded by a prior mortgagee and who sought to enforce his\nmortgage rightragainst his mortgagor was governed not by <a href=\"/doc/783690/\" id=\"a_1247\">article 132</a> of the\nAct of 1908 (now <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1248\">article 62</a>) but by <a href=\"/doc/1422498/\" id=\"a_1249\">article 148.</a> The Calcutta High Court has\nheld&#x27; that Where a prior mortgagee obtained a decree on his mortgage without\n\nimpleading a puisne mortgagee, purchased the property in execution and entered &#x27;\n\ninto possession, a suit by the puisne mortgagee (who also purchased the property\nunder his decree without impleading the mortgagee)_&#x27;for redemption and posses-\nsion against the prior mortgage was governedwby -article 132 of the Act of 1908\n(now <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1250\">article 62</a>) and not by <a href=\"/doc/1422498/\" id=\"a_1251\">article 148 (now article &#x27;61).</a> A puisne mortgagee is\nan assignee of the equity of redemption and is, tlierefore, entitled to redeem a\nprior mortgage in accordance with the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1476197/\" id=\"a_1252\">sections 91</a> and <a href=\"/doc/351384/\" id=\"a_1253\">94</a> of the\nTransfer of Property Act which -incorporate the familiar rule &#x27;redeem up, fore-\nclose down&#x27;. The distinction seems to be this. A suit to redeem&#x27; is governed\nby <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1254\">article 61.</a> A suit to enforce paymentifalls within <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1255\">article 62.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_597\">35.4. The Madras High Court hasheld&#x27; byiinaiority that <a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_1256\">article 134</a> of the\nAct. pf 1908 (now <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1257\">article 61</a>) did not apply\u00bbto a transfer from a trustee or (mort-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_508\"> &quot;&#x27;15vh\u00e9i&#x27;e possession was not taken by ithetrirnsfeiee. Wallis, C.J. and Courts\n\nTmtter, JJ, however, dissented from the aforesaid (inajority) view and held that &#x27;\n\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_1258\">article 134</a> applied to a transfer froma trustee or mortgagee under which\npossession was not taken by the transferee. Wal\ufb01s. CJ. further observed that it\n\n not be held that the intention of the Le\ufb01slature in enacting <a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_1259\">article 134</a>\n\nW35 tomake &#x27;a provision in favour of the cestuti quetrust or mortgagor and give\n\n a furthergperiod than he would otherwise have had. On the contrary, the,\n\nintention was clearly restrictive. The Indian&#x27; Legislature must have been perfectly\nwell aware that in the case of sales and mortgages -alike, possession was rarely\n\ngiven on the date of transfer and if the legislature had intended the date of tanks\n,ing possession under the sale or mortgage to be the starting point, nothing\n\nwoulg have been easier to say so.. It had i1ot&#x27;done&#x27;*so, and it was not open to\nthe judiciary to effect this by taking out of &#x27;thearticle nearly all the cases which\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_509\">- would therefore fall within it.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_510\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"347404\" id=\"span_127\">&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report imitation Act, 1908), paras 48-49, 129-130.\n<a href=\"/doc/347404/\" id=\"a_1260\">&#x27;Nagu Tukaram v. The doctrine of acquision of title by adverse possession is deeply\n_of the&#x27; illccal rooted in our system of jurisprudence. The doctrine is derived from the Roman\n\nucwpmyt Law concept of usucapio and longi tetnporisiliraescriptio, but in Roman~Law,\nthere was an added requirement that the possession must be bona &#x27;ride and for\n\n&quot;justa&#x27;causa&quot;. English law has not insisted on the requirement of justa causa.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_632\">The UK. Law Reform Committee?&#x27; has defended the doctrine by observing that&#x27;\n\n&quot;certainty of title to land is a social need and occupation of land which haslong\n&#x27;been unchallenged should not be disturbed.&quot; Thus, the English version is just\nthe opposite of the Roman concept. a\n\n\\\n\n. &#x27;See para 3S._l7, infra. _ , _ _ _ &#x27;\n. &#x27;Law Commission of _Ind1a, 3rd Report ([.1nntatmn;z_&amp;\u00a2t). D3368 49-52. &#x27;\n&#x27;Law Reform Committee, 14th Report (on aeqn\ufb01moh of easements and pro\ufb01ts by&#x27;\nprescription), emnd. 3100, page 12, para 36. _--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_633\">-, , ,\n\n\n\n&#x27; *f\u00a7(icl_iael I,  n, &quot;Adverse Possession--Morality and \n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA oN-- E &#x27;LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 35--<a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1297\">Articles 61</a> to_ 67: Suits relating to immovable property)\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_634\">-In the registration systems of certain Commonwealth countries, a distinc-\ntion is, made between the acquisition of title_by an adverse possession to regis-\ntered land and unregistered land, with the result that a rank trespasser or a\nsquatter is notable to extinguish the title of a registered proprietor.&#x27;-3\n\n35151 This,ihowever, is not the position in England. A person with evil\n\n_&#x27; ntions can usurp somebody else&#x27;s land, even after forcibly evicting him, and\n\nstill perfect his title by adverse possession. On the other hand. if he enters\ninto possession in a __bona \ufb01de manner and with &quot;justa causa&quot;, he would not be\nable to perfect his &quot;title. When the inequity of the position resulting from the\ninterpretation ofthe word &quot;adverse&quot; was argued in England. before Harman If&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_635\">(is he then was), he had interjected as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_554\">&quot;You are worse off when you enter lawfully&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_636\">To this, the reply from the Bar was~&quot;Yes, often; the case is by no means\nas startling as it sounds, because one has a comparable situation in detinue.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_637\">35.16. In view of the possibility of unjust results \ufb02owing from the applica-\ntion of this doctrine, one writer&#x27; poses two questions:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_555\">(I) Has &quot;squatter&#x27;s title&quot; to land, with its undertones of &quot;land-stealing&quot;, any\nplace in a civilised society, particularly a society committed ultimately to univer-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_638\">sal state registration of title to land? (2) If, &quot;squatter&#x27;s tit &quot; can still perform a\nuseful, indeed a necessary, function, ought it to require that the &quot;squatter&quot;\nsheitgid have a particular intention or motive before allowing him to acquire\ntitle? --However, after an exhaustive discussion of ,_;the merits, the same writer\nproposes the following answers to the questions so posed: &#x27;\n\n(1). Acquisition of title by adverse possession is. not immoral and can have\nf in\ufb02uence on the social policy that security should be given to the long\n\n1 e possessor of&#x27;&#x27;1and.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_639\">&#x27; {2)The motive, intention or belief of the allegedly adverse possessor is im-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_640\">T material. The deliberate evicter and the mistaken encrotcher should both be\n\nable to acquire title. Long-continued possession should ipso facto confer title.\nunless it is proved that that possession began and continued tinder a valid tran-\n with the true owner, i.e. that the possession was purely derivative. ad-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_641\">&#x27;  possession. But if the defendant trespusser is a person who wishes to oust\n\n&#x27;tile plaiiitiif who was himself a prior trespasser or a person who did not come\niiito possession as a trespasser but continued to hold it as such, in order to\nenable &quot;the plaintiff to continue his wrongful possession without disturbance.\n law must undoubtedly step in and give relief to, the plaintiff. As against the\nlilac owner, a. p&#x27;erso_n who is in possession for a length -of time short of the\nstattitory period is not entitled to any protection; but the net result of the deci-\nsions-under <a href=\"/doc/500307/\" id=\"a_1298\">article 142</a> is that the true owner must prove that he had a subsist-\nitigtitle on the date of suit. g i\n\nWe therefore suggest that in order to avoid injustice and inequity to the\n owner and to simplify the law, <a href=\"/doc/500307/\" id=\"a_1299\">article 142</a> should be restricted to suits based\n\n ptissessory title and the owner of the property should not lose his right to\n\nit  the. defendant in possession is able to establish adverse possession.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_642\">&#x27;Michael &#x27;J. &quot;Goodman,&quot; Adverse Possession, Morality and Motive (1910) 33 Modern\nLaw Review 281, 282. - &#x27;\n&quot;CE. I. S Williams, &quot;Title by Limitation in a registered conveyancing system&quot; (1968)\n6A! &quot; Law Rev. 69. I _ .\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_643\">y &quot; yes v. Reverting. to the text of <a href=\"/doc/948485/\" id=\"a_1300\">article 65</a>, the articles relating to possession\nwere examined in great detail by the Law&#x27;Commission&#x27; in its Report on the\nAct of 1908. There was .a preliminary observation that <a href=\"/doc/500307/\" id=\"a_1301\">articles 142</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1799967/\" id=\"a_1302\">144</a>\nhad introduced a good deal of confusion in the law relating to suit for posses-\nsion &#x27;by the owners of property. The law, Commission also discussed the <a href=\"/doc/1381219/\" id=\"a_1303\">Privy\nCouncil</a> case? on the subject, which had settled the proposition that the rule of\n\nprescription should be applied not to cases of want of actual possession by the&#x27;\n\nplainti\ufb01, but to cases where the plainti\ufb01 had been out of possession and another\nperson was in possession for the- prescribed time. The Commission then made\nthe following recommendation on the subject:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_556\">&quot;In our opinion, <a href=\"/doc/500307/\" id=\"a_1304\">article 142</a> must be restricted in its application only to\n\nsuits based on possessory title. The plaintiif in such a suit seeks protections _\n\nof his previous possession which falls short of the statutory period of pres-\ncription, to recover possession from another trespasser. The plaintiff&#x27;:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_557\">&#x27; prior possession no doubt entitles him to protection against a trespasser.\nL though not against the true owner. The true owner&#x27;s entry. would be a&#x27;\nrightful entry and would interrupt.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_558\">35.18. For these reasons, the Law Commission (in that Report) recom-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_647\">mended a re-draft of <a href=\"/doc/500307/\" id=\"a_1305\">article 142</a> as under:-- L\n\n&quot;For possession of immovable property based on possessory title\nwhere the plaintiif while in possession of the property has been disposses-\nsed-----l2 years from the date of dispossession.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_648\">A new article was to govern suits&quot; based on title----the 12 years period to be\n\ncounted from the time when possession becomes adverse. -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_649\">4 35.19. The amended article, though&#x27; phrased somewhat differently has not\ngiven rise to any serious controversy and the Supreme Court. in a recent judg-\nment on the subject, has succintly summarised the law on adverse possession &#x27;or\nhostile title thus? &#x27; T &#x27;\n\n&quot;Adverse possession or hostile title&quot; must be established by&#x27; a consistent\ncourse of conduct and it cannot be shown by a stray or sporadic act \npossession. However, all that the law requires is that the possession must\nbe open and without any attempt at concealment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_650\">of the owner. Such a requirement may\u00bb he insisted on, where an ouster of\ntitle is pleaded but that is not the case here. One of the important facts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_651\">which clearly proves adverse possession, may be that the possessor had let ~\n\nout the land for cultivatory purposes and used it himself from time to time\nwithout any protest from the owner or any serious attempt by the owner\nto evict the possessor. knowing. full well that he was asserting hostile title\nin respect of the land. If a person asserts a hostile title even to a tank\nwhich, as claimed in they present case by the owner, i.e. the municipality,\nbelonged to it and despite&#x27; the hostile assertion of title no steps were taken\nby the owner, to evict the trespasser, his title by prescription&quot; would be\ncomplete-after thirty years.&quot; 7 F\n\n131 to I36. - .\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_652\">&#x27;- &#x27;Agency Company v. Short (1888) 13 A.&#x27;C. 793 CP.C.).\nl&#x27;Ka_.s-hitish Chandra Bose V. Commijgbnerjof Iiglnchi (1981) 2 S.C.Q. 103.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_653\">It is not necessary that\nthe possession must be so effective so as to bring it to the speci\ufb01c knowledge\n\n&#x27;Law Corrimission of India, 3rd&#x27; Report&#x27;(Liri1itat:&#x27;on Act. 1908), pages 49 to 52.  1\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_129\">1</span>\n\n\n\n(Chapter 35--/-Articles\u00bb 61 to 67 : Suits Relating  Immovable Property)\n\n35.20. \\In England, certain developments in the theory of adverse possession -Dedclopngems\nhave taken place as regards proiccting_the real owner of the land (who has.\nreserved the same for development or some speci\ufb01c&#x27; purpose in mind. to be exe-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_654\">&#x27; &#x27; cuted at a future date). Many of the judicial decisions usually discussed under this article. Caseglsw&#x27; : &#x27;\ninvolve matters of substantive law and we need not pause to discuss them. It is \nenough to mention one important aspect which Wasin issue in a Madras case.&#x27;\nDefedants in that case were in possession of a land on-tenancy basis by virtue of a\ndeed executed in l\u00a736. In 1871, the defendants&#x27;. setup a right of permanent\n. tenancy. In 1894, the plaintilf gave notice to the defendants to give up possession\nof the lands and \ufb01led a suit for eviction.\u00bb The defendants contended that the\n&#x27;suit was barred by limitation. The High Court held that the suit was not\n, barred by limitation and that the tenant repudiatinge the title of land became\n&#x27; liable for immediate eviction at the option of-&#x27;the landlord, but until the landlord\n, indicated that he intended to exercise- the option, the tenancy subsisted from year\nto year and <a href=\"/doc/556297/\" id=\"a_1313\">article 139</a> of the&#x27; <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1314\">Limitation Act</a> of 1908&#x27; (present <a href=\"/doc/1944038/\" id=\"a_1315\">article 67</a>) applied.\nBut the same High&#x27;Court, in a later case,&quot; held that the landlord had got a right\n\u00bb &#x27;to recover possession the moment the forfeiture  incurred, and no overt act\nI (i.e. no act or intimation of an election to avoid the lease) was necessary before\nbringing an action for ejectment. -- In such a case, &#x27;article 148 (present <a href=\"/doc/597714/\" id=\"a_1316\">article 66</a>)\n* applied and limitation began to run from the:date of fbrfeiture incurred (i.e. the\n&#x27;date of wrongful alienation of the property), unless the lessor, by a positive act,\n.a\ufb02irmed the tenancy and thereby waived the forfeiture. .fI&#x27;hese and similar rulings&#x27;\nare concerned essentially with the law of landlord and tenant. Depending on the\n&quot;&#x27;view taken on the question of substantive rigits of the parties, there may result\n3 di\ufb01erence as to the precise article &#x27;of the LiIT_n&#x27;\u00a3ati;on Act that would be attracted\nto the particular situation. For that reason, it seems hardly necessary to-pursue\nthe matter further. --  I\n&#x27; I . 2 .1 &#x27;\n&quot; ~ $5.27. Ordinarily, we would have gone -into details of such controversies,\n\nrevealing a disparity of views. However, &quot;the general law of landlord and tenant\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_675\">-has now veryilimited application,&quot; and the article: is how needed&#x27; only for tenancies &#x27;\n\nnot embroiled by special legislation; Its utility.&#x27; being so limited, we would leave&#x27;\nthe matter at that. &#x27; . &#x27; e\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_134\">3</span>\n\n; In the result no change is needed in  article. There is some diversity of views as to the expression &quot;movable pro- Money?-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_731\">. perty&quot; as occurring in the article.&#x27; One&quot; view; is that money is &quot;movable property&#x27;&quot;:a:&#x27;g\u00b0;p&quot;;g;;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_593\">&quot; for the purposes of this article. A contrary view has, however, been taken by Q0n\ufb0210t.= \n\nsome High Courts? In an Allahabad case, the main relief claimed by the plaintilfs &#x27;\n\n&quot;was the recovery of certain sum which repnesented the price of goods deposited,\n\nand interest on &quot;the same. It was held that such a&#x27; suit could not be described to\n\nbe a. suit for the recovery of &quot;speci\ufb01c movable property deposited&#x27; or pawned&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_732\">. within <a href=\"/doc/1537130/\" id=\"a_1353\">article 145</a> of the Act of 1908. \u00bb\n\nThe expression &quot;movable property&quot; has &#x27;been_-de\ufb01ned in various Central Acts.\n&#x27; Thus, <a href=\"/doc/1439341/\" id=\"a_1354\">section 22</a> of the Indian Penal Code restricts the expression &quot;movable\nproperty&quot; to &#x27;corporeal property, that is, property which might be perceived by\nthe senses. <a href=\"/doc/160788397/\" id=\"a_1355\">Section 3(36)</a> of the General Clauses Act, 1897, de\ufb01nes &quot;movable\nproperty&quot; as meaning property of every description, except immovable property:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_594\">. <a href=\"/doc/515323/\" id=\"a_1356\">The Transfer of Property Act</a>,&#x27;1882, does not- de\ufb01ne the term at all. <a href=\"/doc/103447/\" id=\"a_1357\">Section 2(9)</a>\nof the&quot; <a href=\"/doc/1489134/\" id=\"a_1358\">Registration Act, 1908</a> makes a special provision for including certain\nitems aspa species of movable property. Section? 82 of the <a href=\"/doc/1353758/\" id=\"a_1359\">Companies Act, 1956</a>\n3 makes a share or other interest of any member in a company to be a &quot;movable\nproperty&quot;. None&#x27;of these Acts. however.&#x27; speci\ufb01cally provides that money is\nmovable property. . I\n\n36.7. Ordinarily such a con\ufb02ict of views would need to be attended to. But No change &#x27; A\n\nt we note that in the Andhra Pradeshocase, this was only one of the grounds of &#x27;&#x27;\u00b0\u00b0d\u00b0&#x27;&#x27;&#x27;&#x27; &#x27;&#x27;\n* decision, and the decision was based on a number of alternative grounds. We\n\ndo. not therefore consider it necessary to recornmendfany change in the wording\n\nof the article. We may add that the recenttrend &quot;is not to include money in this&#x27;\n\narticle. --\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_733\">&#x27; 36.8. <a href=\"/doc/1185443/\" id=\"a_1360\">Article 71</a> reads as under 2-: &#x27; A - I A.&quot;i\u00b01\u00b0 71-\n\n,&quot;7 1&#x27;.
