The reasons -given were as under:---- &#x27;\n\n&#x27;,&#x27;Arlticle-=2 is for a suit for compensation for doing or for omitting to do an\nact alleged to be in pursuance of any&#x27; enactment in force for the time being\n11] India.&#x27; The period of limitation is 90 days and time beings to run when\nthe act or omission takes&#x27; place. This article is intended to cover the case\nprovided&#x27; for in England; by <a href=\"/doc/996339/\" id=\"a_1371\">section 8</a> of &#x27;the Public Authorities (Protection)\nAct, 1893. The provisions therein&#x27; are somewhat elaborate and the period of\nlimitation is six months. &#x27;The object of-- the Legislature in England and in/\nIndia seems to be to provide a shorter period of limitation in the case of\nactions against public authorities for any &quot;act done in pursuance or execution\nor intended execution of any Act.or of pubiic duty, or authority, or in&#x27;\nrespect of any neglect or default or in the execution of such Act, duty or\nauthority. It protects the public autho &#x27;ties&#x27;by providing&#x27; a shorter period\na of limitation. It has been held that.&#x27; so long  the officer concerned acts\nhonestly and bona\ufb01de. he gets the adiantage of the shorter period of limita-\ntion. If the statute authorises the injury, no action lies. If an o\ufb02icer pur--\nporting to act, in pursuance of statute,-&#x27;does; sonrething which causes an\ninjury or by reason of his omission toido an act an injury results, the person\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report A\u00e9t), &quot;vanes 40-41.\n&#x27; . {T} It\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n~ . (Chapter 37\u00bb--<a href=\"/doc/1008926/\" id=\"a_1372\">Articles 72</a> to 91 .&#x27; Suits Relating to T art)\n\nso injured is entitled to claim compensation for the neglect or default. If\nhe abuses the power, the shorter period of limitation will not apply and the ac-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_751\">tion will be outside the article. The law in England was altered by\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1373\">Limitation Act</a> of 1939 (<a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_1374\">section 21</a>) which &quot;provided a period of\none year instead of_ six months. Time was made to run from the\n\ndate of the accrual of the cause of action instead of from the act or\nneglect or default complained of, as under section 8 of the Public Authorities\n(Protection) Act. Owing to public agitation, the&#x27;<a href=\"/doc/1003813/\" id=\"a_1375\">English Limitation Act</a> was\namended in 1954 and the period of limitation was increased to three years\nfor actions relating to personal injuries. By the amending Act, <a href=\"/doc/1772105/\" id=\"a_1376\">section 21</a>\nof the Act was repealed and a proviso to sub-section (1) of <a href=\"/doc/103447/\" id=\"a_1377\">section 2</a> was\nadded. cutting down&#x27;the period of six years, which applies for actions founded\non torts, to three years in such cases. The period, therefore, under the\nEnglish Law for actions on tort as respects personal injury. whether caused by\na private individual or by a public authority is now three years instead of six\n\nyears as in the case of other actions based on tort. This article would &#x27;\n\ncome under the general provision for all suits on tort for which we propose\n\n to prescribe a period of three years from the date of accrual of the cause\n\nof action. There does not seem to be any justi\ufb01cation for making a distinc-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_752\">&#x27; tion between public authorities and a private citizen except in matters like\nnotice under section 80. <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1378\">C.P.C</a>. Further, if a shorter period for suits against\npublic authorities is prescribed, it will compel parties to rush to a suit with-\nout exhausting the possibility of getting redress by negotiations which neces-\nsarily take time. One of us, Dr. Sen Gupta, is inclined to take a different\nview and has added a separate note to this Report on the subject. After\na full consideration of his views we think that the consideration mentioned\nabove in favour of a uniform period for suits against public authorities and\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_753\">-private citizens should prevail and that no change is needed in the proposals\nsuggested above.&quot; -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_754\">37.3. The dissenting note of Dr. N. C. Sen Gupta,&#x27; to the Report of the Law\n\nCommission on the Act of 1908. was as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_596\">&quot;The reasons for the proposed amendments in respect of <a href=\"/doc/1456610/\" id=\"a_1379\">article 2</a> are \ufb01rstly,\nthat there should be no difference between the State and private parties\nin respect of suit on tort and that a suit for compensation in respect of a\nthing purported to be done by an officer under some enactment in force\nis nothing but a tort for which the Government is liable. To the general\nprinciple of parity between the Government and private persons in respect\nof limitation, I have no serious objection. But there are important differences\nbetween suits for ordinary torts by private persons and suits under this\nArticle. There may be suits of this character which a&#x27;re purely suits for\ndamages for a particular wrong against a particular person. But most of\nthese cases would be cases in which an of\ufb01cer of the Government has been\nacting or purporting to act under authority of an enactment and in most\nof these cases, questions about the validity of the enactment or of the inter-\npretation of it upon which the o\ufb02icer is acting would be in question. In\nsuch cases it is by all means necessary that such suits should be disposed\nof as quickly as possible.~--so that if thepdecision goes against the action of\na particular o\ufb02icer. the Government maytake early steps that further action\nmay not be taken on the erroneous view of law. Further, it must be re-\n&#x27; membered that the Government is made vicariously responsible for the acts\nof: its o\ufb02icers and having regard to the extremelv large area of Government\nactivities and its responsibility for acts of a multitude of officers, it is neces-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_597\">22---14&#x27;Mof\n\nLJ &amp; CA/NDI83\n\n.&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation &#x27;Act, 1908) pages 69-70.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_598\">\\\n\nDr. Sen Gupta&#x27;:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_599\">dissent -to\n\nthe earlier\nReport.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_142\">162</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_600\">Need for\n\namendment\nand recom-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_601\">mcndation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_602\">Time taken\n\nin prelim1- I.\n\n119-1&#x27;? 3993-\n\n89111 REPORT or LAW COMMN. or mom on me LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37----ArticIes 72 to 91: Suits Relating to Tort)\n\nsary that the courts&#x27; decision about the correctness or otherwise of the ,\n\nact of such o\ufb02icers should be made known to the government as soon as\npossible.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_603\">&quot;Public policy requires that acts of government of\ufb01cials purported to\n\nhave been done under the provisions of some enactments in force should be .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_604\">tested, if necessary, as soon as possible in order that public administration\nmay not be affected by an erroneous course of action based on wrong\napplication of the law for a long time; and if there has been an error,\nit should be rectified as soon as possible. &#x27;\n\n&quot;I am afraid that the principle that this makes a discrimination between\nthe government and a private person does not provide a correct approach to\nthe problem. The difference in the provisions lies not in the character of\nthe person against whom the suit is brought, but in the nature of the claim\nwhich justi\ufb01esa short period.&quot; &#x27; A\n\n37.4. The recommendation made by the majority of the Law&#x27; Commission\nin its Report on the Act of 1908 was to increase the period to three years, but\nthe period has been increased (by the Act of 1963) only to one year.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_605\">37.4A. In this -connection. an aspect to which attention must be drawn IS\nthe fact that where a private person Wants to sue the Governmenf&quot;or a public\no\ufb02icer in respect of an act alleged to have been done under an enactment. consi-\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_755\">derable time of that private person is taken up in certain preliminary stcps. These\n\npreliminaries include----\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_756\">(i) collecting the necessary information:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_606\">(ii) getting the necessary copies of documents etc. from the public office\nconcerned;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_607\">(iii) taking legal advice; and\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_608\">(iv) arranging for the payment of court fee and other expenses.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_757\">Furthermore. the scope and importance of suits governed by <a href=\"/doc/1008926/\" id=\"a_1380\">article 72</a> is\ndaily increasing. as almost every official action affecting the rights of a citizen\nnow takes place under the colour of some enactment or the other. To put the\nmatter in different words. the sphere of common law wrongs is diminishing and\nthe sphere of wrongs taking place under the colour of statute is expanding in\nvolume. day by day. It is. therefore, necessary that the increased importance of\nsuch acts should be realised and reflected in the law of limitation. For this\nimportant reason, we recommend that the change mentioned above so as to\nincrease the period of limitation to 3 years should be carried out. In reaching\nthis conclusion. we have not overlooked the points that were raised in the dis-\nsenting note of Dr. Sen Gupta appended to the report of the Law Commission&#x27;\non the Act of 1908. However, on deep reflection. we have come to the conclu-\nsion that the increasing importance of suits falling under <a href=\"/doc/1008926/\" id=\"a_1381\">article 72</a> and the\ndi\ufb02iculties of the litigants (in the shape of time consumed by preliminaries to such\nlitigation) must be given great weight. It. is true that there is need to ensure that\nthe validity of an official act is decided without unreasonable delay-which was\none of the points made by Dr. Sen Gupta. However. three years would not be\n\n&#x27; too long a period or an unreasonable one.&#x27; having regard to the considerations\n\nthat have been mentioned above.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_758\">&#x27;Law Commission of&#x27; lijziort (Limitation&quot;Actr,&quot;r~l9lllll/  3&#x27;5,\nsupra. &#x27;\n\nF\n\n\n\n\\\n\n39TH REPORT or LAW CQDMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963 168\n\n(Chapter 37----Artic/es_72 to 91: Suits_Re/ating to T art)\n\n37.5. For compensation for a malicious\u00bb One\n\nWhen the plaintiff is_ acquitted or\nprosecution. year.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_624\">the prosecution is otherwise ter-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_625\">tminatcd.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_765\">It is identical with <a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1396\">article 23</a> &#x27;of the Acts of 1908 and 1877.\n<a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1397\">Article 23</a> of the Act of 1871 was as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_626\">&quot;23. For a malicious prosecution. One When the plaintiff is acquitted.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_766\">year. 1\n37.9. When the draft Bill of 187.7 was circulated for comments, the earlier\nphraseology (Act of 1871) was changed by adding the words &quot;or the prosecution\n\nis otherwise termiIIated&quot;.1 The following comment was received on the proposed\nchange:---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_627\">&quot;When can a prosecution be said to have terminated? Has it terminated\nif the accused be released on the ground that a conviction was held before\na Court which had no jurisdiction, or upon some other technical ground which\nleaves the accused still liable to be prosecuted? See Chambers v. Robinson.\n2 Str. 691; Wicks V. Pentham 4 T.R. 247; and T ippet V. Neasm, 4 B. &amp;\nAld. 634. An acquittal upon a technicality is a different thing from a convic-\ntion set aside for want of jurisdi&#x27;ction.&quot;&quot;&#x27;\n\nThe other comment seems to be more pertinent and applauded the proposed\nchange as follows :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_628\">&quot;The note in &#x27;Thomson&#x27; as to this is quite correct-- an acquittal is not\nnecessary for the purpose of bringing an action. The termination of the\nprosecution is the time from which limitation should run.&quot;-&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_767\">_ \\ The then Secretary, Legislative Department agreed&#x27; with the latter view and\nthe text of the article was ultimately passed as it was proposed.&#x27;\n\n37.10. The Law Commission in its Report on the Act of 1908 recommended&#x27;\na uniform period of 3 years for suits founded on tort. [It had no speci\ufb01c comments\non the corresponding article of that Act as such].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_768\">However, the recommendation to increase the period to three years was not\n\n&#x27; accepted, and the period of limitation has remained the same as before (one year).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_769\">37.11. A controversy still exists in regard to the starting point of limitation\nperiod in the third column. There are two starting points given in the third\ncolumn. They are--(l) when the plaintiff is acquitted, or (2) when the prosecution\nis &quot;otherwise terminated.&quot;--a test added&#x27; in 1877. According to the Bombay\n\n,High Court&#x27;, these two different points are applicable to two dissimilar circum-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_770\">stances. The order of acquittal or of discharge passed by the trial court remains\n(agsorcling to this view) operative for the purposes of limitation and the \ufb01ling of\n\n&#x27;Statement of Objects and Reasons; National Archives File 1877, page 1.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_629\">&quot;Letter dated 19th April, 1877 from Charles D. Field, National Archives File 1877,\nPaper No. 8, page 8.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_630\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10416883\" id=\"span_146\">3Demi O\ufb02icial letter from the Hon&#x27;ble Sir Richard Garth to the Hon&#x27;ble Arthur Hob-\nhouse dated 24th July, 1876_ National Archives File 1877, Paper 2, page 4.\n&#x27;Note in National Archives File 1877, Paper 1, page 5.\n5Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act, 1908), page 44, para 116.\n&quot;Paragraph 37.9 supra. &#x27; *\n7(<a href=\"/doc/10416883/\" id=\"a_1398\">a)Bhaskar v. Kishanlal, A.l.R.</a> 1968 Born. 21.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_631\">(b) Purshortam Vithaldas V. Rnvji Hart&#x27;, A_I.R. 1922 Bom. 209.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_771\">2 Court observed&#x27; as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_632\">89m REPORT or LAW comm. or man ON me LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37~~Artz&#x27;c/es 72 to 91 .&#x27; Suits Relating to T ort)\n\nan appeal or revision in superior courts does not stay its operation so far as\nlimitation is concemed.- In one of its more recent cases on the subject, that High\n\n&quot;The original acquittal is still operative and on the language of the provi-\nsion, it is the date of acquittal from which time begins to run. The other alter-\nnative is that &#x27;the prosecution is otherwise terminated&#x27;. Now, whenever a prose-\ncution is started, it may not necessarily end_ in an acquittal. &#x27; A prosecution may\nend, either in acquittal or conviction. If it is the first, then, it is governed by\n\np the first part of this provision, and it it is the second, there can be no case for a\n\nsuit. It may also result in an order of discharge, or in a dismissaljof the com-\nplaint if the complainant is absent on the datep\ufb01xcd for the hearing of the com-\nplaint. The latter part of the provision &#x27;the prosecution is otherwise terminated&#x27;\nis intended to meet such cases, and here again. it is the end of that proceeding\nwhich is operative for all intents and purposes and governs the point of time\nwhen the period begins to run.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_633\">The High Court further pointed out that whenever the Legislature intended\nthat the time should commence to run from the \ufb01nal order, it had expressly\nsaid so. The following observations of the Allahabad High Court in its earlier\ncase&quot; on the subject re\ufb02ect the same approach : --\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_634\">&quot;A perusal of <a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1399\">article 23</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1400\">Limitation Act</a> (of 1908) goes to show ,\n\nthat that article deals with two alternative&#x27; cases: one envisages acquittal\nand the other termination of the prosecution. So far as the second alternative\nor the expression &#x27;termination of the presecution&#x27; goes, that is not applicable\nto the present case. That governs such cases as those of discharge. It is the\nfirst alternative which will govern the case of acquittal; and &#x27;acquittal&#x27; would\nmean acquittal from the trial court or if there is conviction from a trial court,\nthen the order of acquittal was passed in appeal or revision. In a case where\nacquittal has been ordered by the trial court and the complainant has \ufb01led\nrevision, the \ufb01ling of revision cannot alfect the order of acquittal already\npassed. It will remain an order of acquittal till the acquittal is converted\ninto an order of sentence. It would thus appear that under <a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1401\">article 23</a> of the\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1402\">Limitation Act</a>, the limitation would run from the date when the plaintiffs\nwere acquitted by the trial court or in appeal if there was conviction from the\ntrial court. Filing of a revision against an order of acquittal cannot suspend\nthe period of limitation, which started running from the date of the order of\nacquittal.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_772\">On this view, the \ufb01ling of a revision against acquittal does not suspend\nthe running of limitation which has once started. *\n\n37.12. The opposite view has, however, been taken in a later decision of the\nAllahabad High Court?&#x27; A full Bench of the Madras High Court took this view\nquite a long time back.&#x27; A Same view has been taken in comparatively more re-\ncent judgments of the Mysore&quot; and Rajasthan&quot; High Courts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_773\">The pivotal objection of the Bombay High Court to the acceptance of the\nMadras view&#x27; is contained in these observations in one of its judgments.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_774\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10416883\" id=\"span_147\"><a href=\"/doc/10416883/\" id=\"a_1403\">&#x27;Bhaskar v. Kisanlal, A.I.R.</a> 1968 Bom. 21. 22, para 3.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_775\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1605010\" id=\"span_148\"><a href=\"/doc/1605010/\" id=\"a_1404\">2Madho La] v. Hari Shankar, &#x27;A.I.R.</a> 1963 All. 547, 548, para 5 (M. Lal J.).</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_776\">&#x27;Madha Lal v. Shyam Sundar Vaish. (1969) All L.J. 587, 589 (See paragraph 37.13, infra).\n&#x27; <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1663506\" id=\"span_149\"><a href=\"/doc/1663506/\" id=\"a_1405\">&#x27;Kulasekara Chetty V. Tholasingam Chetty, A.I.R.</a> 1938 Mad. 349 (FR).</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_777\">5Basappa Sangappa v. Narayanappa Kriyappa, I.L.R. (1973) Mys. 201, 206 (See infra). .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_778\">\u00b0Ramvilax v.&#x27;Gopal Lal, (1973) Ray. Kisan_IaI, A.I.R. 1968 Bom. 21, 24. . _\n\nOpposite\nschool of\nthought.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_780\">1,65\n\n\n\n_AlIahabad\njudgment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_781\">V statesman\n&#x27;\ufb01t&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_782\">edz&#x27;\n\nhit\n\n,4\n89rn neporgr or LAW camw. or lN)IA on me u.\\rr&#x27;r,x&#x27;r1o,N AC1&#x27;, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37- --<a href=\"/doc/1008926/\" id=\"a_1406\">Articles 72</a> to 91 .&#x27; Suits Relating to T art)\n\n&#x27;&#x27;In our view, therefore, there can be no justi\ufb01cation to add the word\n&#x27;\ufb01nal&#x27; before the word &#x27;acquittal&#x27; according to the Madras .High Court,\nand the word &#x27;\ufb01nally&#x27; before the words &#x27;otherwise terminated&#x27;.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_783\">This objection has been met by the Mysore High Court, which has observ-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_635\">&quot;lhe criticism, therefore, that the Madras view illogically imports the\nidea of \ufb01nality or actually adds the -word &#x27;finally&#x27; or &#x27;final&#x27; to the language\nof the third column is, with respect, not justi\ufb01ed. Finality is actually inher-\nent in the choice of the word &#x27;terininati0n&#x27;. Taking, therefore, the two\nthings together, viz., that one of the essential elements in the cause of action\nfor malicious prosecution is termination of proceedings in favour of the\n&#x27;plaintiff and that there can be no termination if proceedings are continued\nuntil one reaches the stage where further continuance is not possible, then\nit follows that if the acquittal entered by the trial court is taken up on\nappeal: the plaintiff is not in a position to say that the proceedings have\nterminated in his favour, and that, therefore, the cause of action itself is\nnot complete so as to start the running of time in favour of the defendant.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_784\">37.13. The Allahabad High Court has also, in a later case held&#x27; as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_636\">&quot;The phrase used in <a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1407\">article 23</a> &#x27;of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1408\">Limitation Act</a> \ufb01xing the point\nof time from which the period of limitation begins to run as &#x27;when the\nplaintiff is acquitted or the prosecution is otherwise terminated&#x27;, must be\nconstrued as equivalent to &#x27;when the rosecution of the plaintiff is termi-\nnated by acquittal or otherwise&#x27;; and determination of the prosecution by\nacquittal shbuld be deemed to occur only when all appeals and revisions\nthat may have been filed&#x27; against the basic order of acquittal have been\n\ufb01nally disposed of. This interpretation obviates the necessity for making an\nillogical distinction between cases of acquittal and cases where the prosecu-\ntion is terminated by discharge, and it has the further merit of avoiding the\npossibility of hardship for the plaintiff by permitting him to wait until his\nacquittal has been placed beyond doubt before he \ufb01les his suit for compen-\nsation for malicious prosecution.&quot; V\n37.14. In the Rajasthan judgment,&quot; the following passage represents a simi.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_637\">view:--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_638\">&quot;The phrase used in <a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1409\">article 23</a> \ufb01xing the time from which the period\nof limitation begins to run as &#x27;when the plaintiff is acquitted or the prose-\ncution is otherwise terminated&#x27;, must be lnterpr-stcd as meaning &#x27;when the\nprosecution of the plainti\ufb01 is terminated by acquittal or otherwise&#x27; and the\ntermination of the prosecution by acquittal should be (&#x27;seemed to occur only\nwhen the appeals and revisions that may have been filed against the order\nof acquittal have been \ufb01nally disposed of. I am further of the opinion\nthat the words used in <a href=\"/doc/1071750/\" id=\"a_1410\">article 23</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1411\">Limitation Act</a> &#x27;when the plairiti\ufb01\nis acquitted&#x27; must not be read independently of the words or the prosecution\nis otherwise terminated&#x27;. The illogical distinction pointed out by the Bom-\nbay High Court between the cases of acquittal and those where the prosecu-\ntion is terminated by discharge would only lead to hardship and one will\nhave to incur expenditure of \ufb01ling the suit even before the order of&#x27;acquitta_l\n\nadded)\n\n&#x27;Basappa Sangappa V. Narayanappa Kariyappa, l.L.R. (1973) Mys. 201, 206 (Lin Jhasis\n&#x27;lVladho Lal. v. Shyam Sunder Vaish, (1969) All. LJ. 587. (D.B.) [The earlier case of\n\n1963 does not appear to have been cited].\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_639\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"123285970\" id=\"span_150\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/123285970/\" id=\"a_1412\">Ram Vilas v. Gopal Lal</a>, (1973) Raj. L.W. 92, 94 (Modi J.).</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_640\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or mm\u00bb. In this context~.ethough this is a matter of substantive law\u00bb--it may American\nbe worth mentioning that the Restatement of the Law &#x27;(Second) on Torts&#x27; has R\u00b0sm\u00b0m&#x27;m&#x27;\nthis to say regarding the termination of proceedings for the -purpose of the tort_\n\nof malicious prosecution:~----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_641\">&quot;658. General Rule : To subject a person to liability for malicious pro-\n,secution, the criminal proceedings must have terminated in favour of the\naccused.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_642\">&quot;659. Manner of Termination: Criminal proceedings are terminated\nin favour of the accused by\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_643\">(a) a discharge by a magistrate at a preliminary hearing, or\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_644\">(b) the refusal of a grand jury to indict. or V\n\n(C) the formal abandonment of the proceedings by&#x27;the public prosc-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_786\">cutor, or\n\n(ti) the quashing of an indictment or information, or\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_787\">(e) an acquittal, or\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_788\">(f) a \ufb01nal order in favour of the accused by a trial or appellate court.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_789\">37.16. Street has dealt&quot;-&#x27; with this ingrediant of malicious prosecution thus: English\n\n&quot;The proceedings must have terminated in favour of the plaintil\ufb01 \u00a7&#x27;\u00a7&#x27;,&#x27;,&quot;,&quot; to\n\nEven though the plainti\ufb02 has been convicted of a lesser offence&#x27;, or has had 05 \nhis conviction quashed on appeal, or has been acquitted on a technicality, ,\u00a7&#x27;,&#x27;{,&#x27;;\u00b0,2d3.,,,\ne.g. ardefect in the indictment, this requirement is satis\ufb01ed. If the conviction\nof the plaintiff stands. then even though there is no right of appeal from\nit and although he can satisfy the court in the instant proceedings that the\nconviction was grossly unjust, there is no cause of action in this tort. The\n&#x27; plaintiff seems to satisfy the present requirement if he proves that the defen-\ndant has dis-continued the proceedings; the plainti\ufb01 cannot sue, it seems,\nwhile the proceedings are still pending.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_790\">37.16A. It may also be added that Charles D. Field hit the nail on the head\nin 1877 when he asked the question: &quot;when can a prosecution be said to have\nterminated&quot;? Even now, the ditference of opinion continues. A review of the&#x27;\nstate of_ the law made above shows that the majority of the courts would be\nreluctant to entertain an action for damages for malicious prosecution unless they\nare satis\ufb01ed that the judgment may not be rendered infructuous by a subsequent\ndecision &#x27;in the criminal proceedings. ,9\n\n_ 37.17. The net result of the above discussion is that there is an obvious con- Recommengi.\n\ufb02ict of views between the--Bombay High Court on the one hand and the other &quot;\u00b0&quot; &#x27;\nHigh Courts on the other hand. There seems to be a need for clarifying the\n\n&#x27; &#x27;American Law Institute Restatement (Second) on Torts (1977) Vol. 3, Articles 658,\n659_ &#x27;\n\n3Harry Street, Law of Torts (1976). page 397.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_791\">-&quot;This requirement is not imposed where, for example, an arrest or search warant is\nprocured. . _\n\n\\ &#x27;Cases cited in suport of the discussion have been omitted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_792\">\\\\ .\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_151\">163</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_793\">Article: 75-76.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_794\">English lapv.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_795\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37-----Articles 72 to 91 : Suits Relating to Tort)\n\nposition.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_796\">of the Allahabad High Court and the rulings of the High Courts of Madras,\nMysore and Rajasthan), we recommend that in <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_1413\">article 74</a>, before the word\n&quot;terminated&quot;, the word &quot;\ufb01nally&quot; should be added. Apart from the fact that\n\ni . such an amendment would be in harmony with the majority view, it will be of\n\nconsiderable practical utility and avoid the ditliculty that might be caused. if\nlimitation is to start running even where the proceedings constituting the cause\nof action have every possibility of being rc-opened in a higher court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_797\">37.18. The next two articles are concerned with defamation, and may\nnow be taken up.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_798\">(a) <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1414\">Article 75</a> reads as under:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_645\">One&quot;  When the libel is published.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_646\">year.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_647\">&quot;75. One _\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_653\">- if the words are not actionable\n\nyear.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_654\">in themselves, when the special &quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_801\">damage complained of results.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_802\">&#x27; <a href=\"/doc/631708/\" id=\"a_1421\">Article 25</a> of the Act of 1871 was as under:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_655\">&quot;For compensation for slander. One When the words are spoken.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_803\">year.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_804\">Though libel and slander fall under the general heading of the tort of defa-\nmation, the Limitation Acts, right from 1871, have been treating&#x27;the causes of\n\naction for these two types of torts as. distinct.--though the period of limitation is&#x27;\n\none year in both the cases.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_805\">I\n\np 3&#x27;7.19. Halsbury states the position in England thus&#x27; :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_656\">&quot;In an action of libel time runs from the publication, but where, for exam-\nple, a block or newspaper is published and offered for sale, a fresh cause of\naction arises on each sale, notwithstanding that more than six years have\nelapsed since the \ufb01rst publication. In an action for slander where the words\nare actionable without proof of special damage, time runs from the utter-\ning of the slander. Where the words are not actionable without special\ndamage, time does not run until the dainage occurs.&quot; A\n\n&#x27;Halsbury, 4th Ed, vol- 28, para 689. -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_657\">In conformity with the majority view (that is to say, the later ruling,\n\n\n\n&#x27;S9111 REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA oN&#x27;-mg LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37---Articles 72 to 91 : &quot;Suits Relating to tort).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_658\">37.20. Before 1871, the period of limitation} in India probably followed L\u00a2_=8iS1|\ufb01V0.\nthe English practice. The period of one year proposed for suits for libel was Hlst\u00b0ry&#x27;~ _\nexplained in the statement of Objects and Reasons annexed to the Bill that lead\nto the Act of 1871, as follows : ~\u00e9\n\n&quot;On the other hand, we have diminished to one year the period \ufb01xed for\nsuits for false imprisonment, for any other injury to the person, for a mali-\ncious prosecution, for libel, for slander, for taking or damaging movables,\nfor loss of service occasioned by seduction, for inducing breach of contract,\nfor illegal distress, and for wrongful seizure of movables under le-gal pro-\n&quot; cess&#x27;.&quot; - &#x27; -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_659\">-37.2l.iWl1en the draft Limitation Bill 1877 was circulated for comments, a\nplea was made for counting the period from the_~time when the libel came to\nthe knowledge of the person libelled. One comment, makingxsuch a suggestion,\n\n_ \u00abmay be quoted:---- &#x27;\n\nI &#x27; &quot;I would suggest an alteration in the period applicable to libel; for\n\nI think the term now \ufb01xed, namely, one yeartfrom publication, may, as I\nknow it has done, work a great hardship on the person libelled. The term\nshould in my opin-ion, run from the date when the libel became known to\nhim. I do not refer to cases of libel published in newspapers, for there a\nman reads at once what has been written of him, and can generally bring\n\nhis action without further delay: but I alude to private communications, or\nthose written under a sense of duty to a publico\ufb02icial, where the person\nlibelled, although he may have sustained loss from what he knows has been\n\nsaid of him, still is unable, through obstacles7put in the way_either by the\nwriter or receiver of the communication,&#x27; to discover what has been said\n\n, against him until the year from the date when it was sent has expired, and\nso, though the statements or charges madefagainst him may &#x27;have been\ntotally unfounded and grossly calumnious, and he&#x27;has suffered the greatest\nloss, still because of the present state of &#x27;the law. his suit would be barred\nunless it was brought within a year of the time when it was sent to the post.\n<a href=\"/doc/1375684/\" id=\"a_1422\">Section 19</a> of the Act would not help him, for he may have been kept from\n\nthe knowledge of his right to sue without any fraud on his libellefs part, but\n\nby simple obstructions which he has put in his way. Or if it be said he knew\n\nof his right to sue before the time of limitation expired, inasmuch as he had\n\n&#x27; been told he had been libelled, still he might not have been able to bring a\nsuit, because. he could not, until it was too ilate,&#x27; see or get a copy of the libel-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_660\">lous document. A case of this kind occurred some &#x27;few years ago in Calcutta\nwhere great injury resulted to a merchant whose conduct in a certain mer-\ncantile transaction had been improperly and falsely &#x27;represented by his Coun-\n\n&#x27; \u00ab. sel to the Minister of Foreign Affairs of&#x27; their country. The High Court\nwas of opinion that the communications were&#x27;-&#x27;highlyjlibellous, and that the\nplaintiff would have been entitled to heavy  had it not found&#x27; that\n\nthe suit had been brought beyond the period alfliywqd from the date of pub-A\nlication, although it was brought at the earliest o\ufb01portunity. The case is\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_661\">-- &#x27; Charriol v. Lombard, and is reported in 1 Ihdian Jurist N.S., page &#x27;209 at\n\n_ ywq.&quot;2 &#x27; .  -(I \\ .\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_662\">&#x27;Another suggestion made by Mr. Field was to combine the two articles deal- .\n with &#x27;libel&#x27; and &#x27;slander&#x27; into one composite&#x27;ar[ticle*for defamation&#x27; :\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_663\">&#x27;Statement of Objects and Reasons&#x27;; National Archives Bile 1871, page 4. -\n&#x27;C. Field, National Archives File 1908, paper 6,\n\n\u00ab*3.-like-ma/sis \n&gt;.;\\~ xx &#x27; ~ = &#x27;*\n\n  \n\n   \n\n\n\nCommt\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_152\">(1907)</span>\n\nI  Com-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_665\">mission&#x27;:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_666\">Report.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_667\">Him&#x27;! 0&#x27; .\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_668\">article relating\nto slander.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_669\">Position in\nIndia as to\n\namendment hf\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1423\">Limitation\nAct</a>,\n\n89rH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LlMlTA&#x27;l&#x27;IO&#x27;N ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 3717-Articles 72 to 91: Suits relating to tort.)\n\n&quot;For &#x27;libel&#x27; and &#x27;slander&#x27; might besubstituted &quot;defamation&quot; which in-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_670\">cludes both, and which is thepeculiar term of Indian Law~--see section 499 -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_671\">of the Penal Code&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_806\">37.22. In 1907, Sir Lawrence Jenkins, Chief Justice of the Bombay High\nCourt also expressed himself thusz&#x27;\n\n&quot;<a href=\"/doc/631708/\" id=\"a_1424\">Article 25</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1540780/\" id=\"a_1425\">24</a> should be only one article providing for defamation.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_807\">37.23. The Law Commission, in its Report on the Act of 1908, did not exa-\nmine the above question, because it preferred one article for all tortious acts\nand observed thus&quot;:-----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_672\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1540780/\" id=\"a_1426\">Article 24</a> relates to an action for libel and the period of limitation is one\nyear from the time when the libel is published. <a href=\"/doc/631708/\" id=\"a_1427\">Article 25</a> relates to slander\nand the period provided for is one year from the date when the words were\nspoken or, if the words are not per se actionable. when the special damage\ncomplained of results. It is settled law that the cause of action for libel\naccrues from the date of the publication of the defamatory statement. &#x27; When\nslander is actionable per se. the cause of action is its publication and time\nruns from that date. If the action is maintainable only on proof of the\nspecial damage, the happening of the damage is the cause of action. (See\nBurry v. Perry&#x27; and also Darley Main Colliaryt Co. v. Thomas Wilfria&#x27;).\nIn respect. therefore. both of libel and slander, the time from _which limita-\ntion runs coincides with the accrual of the cause of action. The suits under\nall these articles are suits founded on tort and a uniform period of three\nyears may be \ufb01xed for the institution of these suits, time running from the\ndate of the accrual of the caus_e of action.&quot; &#x27;\n\n37.24. As regards the article relating to slander in the Act of 1877, in the\nthird column of the schedule, the words &quot;or, if the words are not actionable in\nthemselves, when the special damage complained of results&quot; were added, along-\nwith the pre-existing words &quot;when the words are spoken&quot;. Since then. there has\nbeen no change. The addition of these words was presumably intended tocover\nthe cases which can arise under the rule of English Law that slanderious words\nare not actionable per se and a suit is maintainable only after when special da-\nmage is caused.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_673\">37.25. We have given anxious thought to the question whether <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1428\">articles 75</a>\nand <a href=\"/doc/1985537/\" id=\"a_1429\">76 (libel</a> and slander) should be retained as they are. This necessitates an\nexamination of the substantive law of defamation as a tort. The common law\nposition is that libel is actionable per se, while slander is actionable without proof\nof special damage (unless the case falls within certain exceptional categories).\nThis distinction, however, hardly possesses any realistic importance in India. since\nmost High Courts do not recognise the distinction between libel and slander. It\ncan be asserted that net result of Indian judicial decisions is that both libel and\nslander are actionable as torts, without theneed to prove special damage. We are\naware that there is one solitary ruling&#x27; which holds&#x27; that the distinction is still\nvalid in the town of Calcutta, but the majority of -the High Courts have, as stated\nabove. taken the view that the technical distinction made by English common law\n\n&#x27;Chief Justice L. Jenkins, Bombay High Court Letter No. 2469 dated 18-l2-I901.\nNational &quot;Archives File 1908, paper 6. page 13.\n. &#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act}. page 44., para 116\n\n&#x27;(l725) Raym. I588. &#x27;\n. &#x27;(1886) II A.C. 127. ,\n\n&#x27;Bhooni llloney V. Naobar, (1901) I.L.R, 28 Cai. 432, 452, 462, 463 (Case relating&quot; to\n, Town of Calcutta). .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_674\">.\\{\n\n\n\n&#x27; &quot;75. F orlcompensation for defamation- One &#x27;\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37---Artie-les----72 to 91 ---Suits relating to&#x27; tort.)\n\nhasno validity in India, and is not in harmony with justice, equity and good\nconscience and is out of tune with the notions of Indian society.&#x27;-&#x27;&#x27;-3\n\nIt should also be pointed out that even in the West, the distinction between\nlibel and slander has not been regarded as satisfactory, and has been very strong-\nly criticised both by academic writers and by law reform agencies who have had\noccasion to deal with the subject. Though the distinction still exists in the United\nKingdom, the English Committee of Defamation, popularly known as the Faulks\nCommittee in its Report recommended its abolition and removal from the law\nof England and Wales, so that for the purposes of civil proceedings in England\nand Wales, slander should be assimilated to libel.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_675\">\u00bb In fact, in at least one Commonwealth country, namely. New Zealand. tho\ndistinction has been abolished by statute.&#x27;\n\nIt should also be recorded here that the reform e\ufb01ected in New Zealand\nhas not given rise to any serious problems. Sir Denis Blundell (who later became\nthe Govemor-General of New Zealand), in his evidence given to the Faulks\u00bb Com-\nmittee, stated that in New Zealind the abolition of the distinction between libel\nand slander had not resulted in any spate of petty actions for slander.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_676\">The retention of the two articles in the present form in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1430\">Limitation Act</a>\n\nthus tends to perpetuate a distinction which is unfair in principle, anachronistio\n\nin nature and, in any case. in the Indian context, almost out of tune with reality.\nIt is also not in conformity with the position in substantive law, as generally\nunderstood in India. The Law of limitation should, as far as possible, maintain\nharmony with the substantive law and should not give a \ufb01llip to a distinction that\nis already discarded.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_677\">37.26. In the light of the above considerations, we, are of the opinon, and\nwe recommend, that in place of <a href=\"/doc/1324537/\" id=\"a_1431\">articles 75</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1985537/\" id=\"a_1432\">76</a>, one single article should be\nsubstituted, to deal with compensation for defamation, the period being one year\nand the starting poi-nt being the date of the publication or, where the defama-\ntory statement is not published in a permanent form, the date when the defamatory\nmatter comes to the knowledge of the plaintiff. When the words are not\npublished in a permanent form. it is fair that the starting point should be the\nplaim&#x27;:i\ufb01&#x27;s knowledge, so that the short period of limitation available does not\ncommence to run until he has knowledge. &#x27;\n\nWe, therefore, recommend that articles 75-76 should be replaced by one\nsingle article to read as under i--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_678\">When the defamatory statement is\npublished, or where the defamatory\nstatement is not published in a\npermanent form, when it comes to\nthe knowledge of the plainti\ufb02f&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_808\">year.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_809\">[To be substituted for present ar-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_810\">ticles 75-76.]\n\n171-\n\nRecommenda-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_811\">tion.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_812\">37.27. This disposes of articles 75-765. <a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1433\">Article 77</a> prescribes a time limit\u00bb of <a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1434\">Article 77</a>\n\none year for a suit &quot;for compensation. for loss of service occasioned by the seduc-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_813\">and case-law\n\nnon of the plaintilfs servant or daughter&quot;. The time limit is to be counted from &quot;cm\n\nthe date when the loss occurs. The article is identical with <a href=\"/doc/1858991/\" id=\"a_1435\">article 26</a> of the Acts\nof 1908 and 1877 and <a href=\"/doc/211413/\" id=\"a_1436\">article 27</a> of the Act of 1871.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_814\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"769343\" id=\"span_153\">&#x27;A. <a href=\"/doc/769343/\" id=\"a_1437\">D. Naryana Saha V. Kannamma</a> (1931) I.L.R.\n450, 451, 452. &#x27; -</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_815\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"503984\" id=\"span_154\"><a href=\"/doc/503984/\" id=\"a_1438\">&#x27;Hirabai v. Dinshaw</a> (1926) I.L.R. 51 Bom. 167.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_816\">3Sukhan v. Bipad (1907) I.L.R. 34 Cal 48.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_817\">&#x27;Section 4 of the (New Zealand) Defamation Act, 1954.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_818\">55 Mad 727: A.I.R. 1931 Mad 445,\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_155\">172</span>\n\nComments\n1908.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_819\">Present\nEnglish\nLaw as to\nseduction.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_820\">Need for\nchange in\nthe law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_821\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37--~-Artic/es 72 to 91----Suits relating to tort.)\n\nThe \ufb01rst case on this article is from Allahabad, in which a Hindu father claim-\ned damages for the seduction of his married daughter and consequential loss of ser-\nvices as a result of abduction by the defendant. He also claimed the costs incurred\nby him in prosecuting the defendant for the offence of abduction. The trial court\ngave him a decree for Rs. 200 for the loss of his daughter&#x27;s services and Rs. 300 for\ncosts incurred by him in prosecuting the defendant. In appeal, the High Court dis-\nallowed the sum of Rs. 200 decreed as damages for the loss of service, while main-\ntaining the decree for the costs incurred in prosecution. Stuart, C.J. discussed the\nEnglish theory on which the remedy for seduction is based and expressed himself\nas against encouraging its introduction into the Indian legal system. He pointed out\nthat in Scotland and other European countries, the seduced woman needed no\nhelp from her father or other relation &quot;but may sue directly for the wrong done\nto her.&quot; His colleague on the bench, Old\ufb01eld, J. was of the view that the English\ncause .of action for seduction was founded not upon the wrongful act of the defen-\ndant but upon the loss of service of the daughter in which service the parent is.\nby \ufb01ction, supposed to have a legal right or interest. He observed as under:---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_679\">&quot;It would be very undesirable to introduce a fiction of this kind into\nthe law of this country. The plaintiff cannot be allowed to maintain a suit\non a contract for service which is not seriously asserted, nor indeed found\nto exist in fact, and which is not consonant with Hindu custom. Hindu\nwomen are no doubt dependent to a great extent on their male relatives, and\nthey have certain household duties whichithey are expected to perform, but\n\ntheir position is not one of servitude, from which any contract of service\ncan be implied.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_822\">37.28. In a Nagpur case, Mr. Justice Niyogi, while awarding damages for\nseduction, held that it was more appropriate to look at such a wrong as consti-\ntuting an invasion of the right of the family to the security of domestic relations,\n\n&#x27;rather asian interruption of the service rendered by the daughter to her father.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_823\">However, this reasoning was not approved by the Division Bench that heard the\nmatter on appeal. It held&#x27; that the law of torts was administered in the Central\nProvinces and Berar (as then constituted) under <a href=\"/doc/165391856/\" id=\"a_1439\">section 6</a> of the Central Provinces\nLaws Act as a rule of justice, equity and good conscience. The only law &#x27;of se-\nduction was that found in the English law of torts applicable to India and it was\n\nnot open to Indian courts &quot;to invent new heads of law not covered by the Eng--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_824\">lish law, though the legislature can?&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_680\">37.29. It may be of interest to note that when the draft Bill which led to\nthe Act of 1908 was circulated for comments, the District Judge, Guntur suggest-\ned that the words &quot;or daughter&quot;&#x27; should be omitted from the article.&quot; On the\nother hand, Mr. Justice Knight of the Bombay High Court suggested a widening\nof the article by adding the words &quot;or ward&#x27;&quot;;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_681\">37.30. It may also be mentioned that in England, by statute, actions for sed-I\nuction have been abolished?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_682\">37.31. Taking note of the case law as well as current thinking on the subject,\nwe have come to the conclusion that there are two special aspects that necessitate\na change in <a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1440\">article 77.</a> In the \ufb01rst place, the element of &quot;loss of service&quot; is em-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_683\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"146525594\" id=\"span_156\">phasised in the article, but, in India, as a matter of substantive law there is some &#x27;\n\n<a href=\"/doc/146525594/\" id=\"a_1441\">&#x27;Ram Lal v. Tula Ram</a> (1882) I.L.R. 4 All 97.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_684\">2Babu V. Subanslzi Dlzobi. A.l.R. 1942 Nag. 97.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_685\">3Dewan Bahadur S. Gopalachariar. Avargal Distt. Judge, GIIntur. National Archives\nFile 1908, letter No. 3073. dated 9th December, Paper No. 8, page 10.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_686\">*Letter No. 2469. dated 18-12-1907. National Archives File 1908, Paper No. _6.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_687\">-&quot;<a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_1442\">Section 5</a>, Law Reform (Miscellaneous provisions) Act. 1970 (Eng).\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_688\">89TH REPORT OF LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37~--Art1&#x27;cles 72 to 9l--~Sui(s relating to tort)\n\nuncertainty in this regard. This is apparent from the Allahabad decision men-\ntioned above.&#x27; How far the English common law rule which stresses the element\nof loss of service applies in lndia thus itself becomes doubtful. Secondly, in\n<a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1443\">article 77</a>, the laying of stress on loss of service amounts to lending prominence\nto an aspect that is totally out of tune with the views of society. No right think-\ning member of society seeking conipensation for seduction would attribute any\nrelevance to the economic factor represented by &quot;loss of service&quot; of the female&#x27;.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_689\">37.32, 111 this situation. the present wording of <a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1444\">article 77</a> needs change. as\nit accentuates an unjust rule of the common law whi:;l1. even it&#x27; it is applicable\nat all, should not be given prominence in the law of limitation. We would. there-\nfore, re-word the article as one applicable to a suit in!&#x27; compensation for seduc-\ntion, thereby removing the objectionable part. By way of anticipating a possible\nobjection to such an amendment. we may state that if. as a matter of substantive\nlaw, any High Court still takes the view that an action for seduction on the com-\nmon law pattern is still permissible in India, there will not be any casus nmissus\nin the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1445\">Limitation Act</a>. If <a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1446\">article 77</a> as sought to be amended is regarded as in-\napplicable to such an action, the residuary article would still apply.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_690\">37.33. In the light of the above discussion, we recommend that <a href=\"/doc/990669/\" id=\"a_1447\">article 77</a>\nshould be revised as under: --~\n\n&quot;77. For compensation for seduction. One\n\nyear.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_691\">When the sedzmio/z occurs.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_825\">37.34. This takes us to aritcle 78. <a href=\"/doc/220543/\" id=\"a_1448\">Article 78</a> prescribes a period of limita-\ntion of one year &quot;for compensation for inducing a person to break a contract\nwith the plaintiff&quot;. The period is computed from the date of the breach. The\n\narticle is identical with <a href=\"/doc/211413/\" id=\"a_1449\">article 27</a> oi the Acts of 1908 and 1877, and with <a href=\"/doc/1734560/\" id=\"a_1450\">article\n28</a> of the Act of 187-1.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_826\">In &quot;this article also, having regard totthe position in substantive law, there\nappears to be need for a small change. This need arises from the fact that the\nconcept of inducement of breach of contract as an actionable wrong is, as a\n\nmatter of substantive law, subject to certain limitations. We deal with this aspect\nin the succeeding paragraphs.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_827\">37.35. inducement of breach of contract is actionable. as a tort if certain\nconditions are satis\ufb01ed. But this is subject to two important limitations. In the\n\ufb01rst place, by legislation, certain inroads have been made into the common law\ndoctrine, legalising conduct that would otherwise be tortous under this head.\nSecondly, in some cases, even at common law, special circumstances supply a\njusti\ufb01cation for conduct that would be otherwise actionable under this head.\nThese circumstances constitute an exception to the general rule that the direct\ninducement of breach of contract is a tort, and that where &quot;the intervener, assum-\ning he knows of the contract and acts with the aim and object of procuring its\nbreach (he) will be liable if he directly intervenes by persuading A to break it?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_828\">To this general rule, however, there arise exceptions in speci\ufb01c circums-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_829\">tances.3&gt; It is enough to cite the classic test of what constitute the essential ingre-\ndients of this tort as formulated by Romer, L.J.* namely, &quot; .... ..regard might be\nhad to the nature of the contract broken: the position of the parties to the con-\ntract; the grounds for the breach; the means employed to procure the brcaclI;\n&#x27;Para 37.27 (supra)_\n9Thomson v. Deakin (1952) Ch..646. (I7!) (Lord Evershed).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_830\">3&#x27;Smi!hI&#x27;e.\\&#x27; V. It is the latter test that has been more current as the\naccepted one in recent years.&#x27;-&quot; The more serious difficulties have arisen in regard\nto bonafidc advice given to a person to withdraw from a contract, particularly\nin the performance of a duty to give the advice that is given to a person to with-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_843\">&#x27;draw from a contract. This situation arises particularly in the case of advice to\n\nterminate a contract of marriage which the person giving the advice regards as\nharmful to the interests of the parties concerned.&quot; Similarly, in modern times,\nmany cases concern the use of unlawful means such as violence. restrictions on\nliberty, intimidation. defamation and so on.&#x27; The substantive law on the subject\nhas thus developed extensively, and it is desirable that <a href=\"/doc/220543/\" id=\"a_1452\">article 78</a> should be re-\nvised in the light thereof. A\n\n37.37. Having regard to the above considerations, it appears to us that the\nlanguage of the article is capable of improvement, so as to make it conform to\nthe substantive law without, at the same time, making it too cumbersome. We,\ntherefore, recommend that <a href=\"/doc/220543/\" id=\"a_1453\">article 78</a>, first column. should be revised to read as\n\nunder : ---- \u00ab\n\n&quot;For compensation for wrong- (Rest of the columns as at present).\nfully inducing a person to break.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_844\">a contract with the plaintiff.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_845\">37.38. D. Heydon, &quot;Justification in intentional economic torts law&quot; (1970) 20 University\nof Toronto Law Journal 139, 161, 163, 168 and 182. &#x27;\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW&#x27; COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, l963\n\n(Chapter 37\u00bb -Articles 72 to 91~---Suits relating to tort.)\n\nV <a href=\"/doc/1119830/\" id=\"a_1460\">Articles 79</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1864214/\" id=\"a_1461\">80</a> are taken together, since they represent one group of\narticles dealing with the subject to distraint of property under legal process or\nseizure by &quot;extra-legal methods. The Law Commission&#x27; in its Report on the Act\nof 1908 had suggested their inclusion in one general article, dealing with torts,\n\nwith a period of three years.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_700\">&quot; .... ..there is no reason why they should not be brought under the general\ncategory of torts and the period of three years applied to them. The dates\nin column three coincide with the dates of accrual of the causes of action&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_859\">37.39. The expression &#x27;legal process&#x27; occurring in <a href=\"/doc/1864214/\" id=\"a_1462\">article 80</a> had given rise\nto certain con\ufb02ict. The Allahabad High Court&quot; ruled that it included all processes\nissued under any law. In regard to the seizure of crops under tenancy law by\nthe landlord. the High Court observed : ~--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_860\">g &quot;In my opinion. a distraint effected under the local tenancy Act (No. II of\n\\l90l) is a seizure of movable property under legal process. because it is\n\ndone under the special provisions of the local act and subject to the due&#x27;\n\nobservance of the procedure therein laid down.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_861\">A contrary view was taken by the Bombay High Court,&#x27; to the effect that\nthe &quot;legal process&quot; envisaged by this article necessarily implied some process\nthrough the Court and accordingly a direction from the Collector, acting under\nthe Bombay Land Revenue Code. was outside the scope of this article. Beamount\nCI. observed thus:---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_701\">&quot;The learned Government Pleader has argued that the words &#x27;under legal\n\nii process&#x27; in <a href=\"/doc/1888152/\" id=\"a_1463\">article 29</a> means &#x27;according to law is legal process&#x27;. In my opinion,\nthat is not the meaning of the words. I think &#x27;legal process&#x27; denotes proce-\ndure by some sort of Court, and the Collector acting under section 154.\nBombay Land Revenue Code, is not acting as a creditor. He, as the officer\nentitled to recover arrears of land revenue, is given power to seize the de-\nfaulter&#x27;s goods. He is in a position analogous to that of a creditor who is\ngiven power under the contract to seize his debtor&#x27;s goods, if his moneys\nare not paid. If legal process merely means process according to law, it\nseems to me that seizure under a valid contract would be just as much sei-\nzure according to law as seizure under aistatute. In my opinion, <a href=\"/doc/1888152/\" id=\"a_1464\">Article 29</a>\napplies to seizure under a process issued by a Court. Possibly a revenue\nCourt may issue legal process, but in my view the Collector was not acting\nin any sense as a Court when he seized these goods.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_862\">_ However, in a later judgment of the Bombay High Court,&#x27; the meaning of\nthe expression &#x27;&#x27;legal proceedings&quot; was considered and the following observations\n\nmade:~---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_702\">&quot;It must be remembered in this context that the expression &#x27;legal proceedings&#x27;\nis not synonymous with &#x27;judicial proceedings&#x27;. Proceedings may be legal\neven if they are not judicial proceedings, if they are not authorised by law;\n&#x27;and Mr. Palkhiwala, by his argument undoubtedly requires us to equate the\nexpression &#x27;legal proceedings&#x27; in S. 48 sub-section (2)(ii) with judicial pro-\nceedings. for which. in our opinion. there is no warrant in law.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_863\">&#x27;Law Commission of India. 3rd Report. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1465\">(Limitation Act</a>. 1908), pages 44-45, para 118.\n&#x27;Marrsmght. V. Ram Nath, A.I.R. 1924 All. 828.\n&#x27;SIu&#x27;vrao Shsgiri v. Secretary of Stare, A.l.R. 1942 Bombay 300. Also see Paharf v_\nSurat&#x27;. (1948) I.L.R. 27 Pat. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1177726\" id=\"span_159\">680. i\n<a href=\"/doc/1177726/\" id=\"a_1466\">&#x27;Abdul Aziz v. State of Bombay A.I.R.</a> 1958 Bombay 279, 282.</span>\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_160\">175</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_864\">Expression\n&quot;legal process&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_703\">in <a href=\"/doc/1864214/\" id=\"a_1467\">article 80.</a>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_704\">I76\n\nNo change\nas to legal\nprocess.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_705\">Starting\npoint-com-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_706\">ments (1908).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_707\">Suggestions\nconsidered.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_708\">Need for\nchange in\nthe law.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_709\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37---Arti\u00e9les 72 to 9I----Sm&#x27;ts relating to tort.)\n\n37.40. It is likely that the expression &quot;legal process&#x27; would also be held to\nencompass all processes issued under any law, as has been held by the Allahabad\nHigh Court.&#x27; As the matter has not been speci\ufb01cally raised after 1958 in report-\ned cases, we would not recommend any amendment of the article on that score.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_710\">37.41. The starting point under this article requires some comments. When\nthe draft Limitation Bill 1908 was circulated, Dr. H.S. Gaur made the_following\ncomments also on the starting point of limitation : -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_711\">&quot;At present, the starting point for Limitation is the date of attachment and\nnot the release (Rcmmarain v. Umrao Singh, l.L.R. 29 All. 615). I submit\nthat the starting point should be the release, not the date of attachment. In\nthe view of the law, it may be that enquiry into the propriety of attachment\nmay take a year or more and the party aggrieved may, in the meantime, lose\nits reniedy.&quot;-&#x27;~&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_865\">The opinion of Mr. Justice Banerji also dealt with the same di\ufb02iculty in\nthese words:--\u00bb\n\n&quot;Articles 29-30: In case of wrongful distress or seizure. limitation should be\ncomputed from the date of distress or seizure and where the distress or sei-\nzure continues. from the date of the termination of the distress or seizure.\nIf property remains under wrongful distress or attachment it is di\ufb02icult for\nthe plaintiff to assess his compensation until the property is released. The\nattachment may continue for a period exceeding one year. In such a case\nthe owner of the property cannot. under the present law. get the compensa-\ntion to which he might be justly entitled. It is doubtful whether the attach-\nment can be held to be a continuing wrong.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_866\">The Chief Justice of Bombay wanted to extend the scope of this article fur-\nther, suggesting, as under:---- ~\n\n&quot;This might be extended to a prohibitory order.&quot;&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_712\">37.42. It is not clear from the \ufb01le of the Act of 1908 as to why these sug-\ngestions were not accepted. We have however given some thought to the matter\nand \ufb01nd considerable force in these suggestions. It is true that generally\nthe person aggrieved would know about the wrongful attachment within a few\ndays thereof, and can move the court or other authority issuing the process of\nattachment for relief. If the attaching authority takes only a few months to pass\n\n&#x27;the \ufb01nal order upholding the contentions of the petitioner, the latter would still\n\nhave enough time left to file a civil suit for damages, as the prescribed period of\nlimitation is one year.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_713\">37.43. However. considering the long duration of pendency of cases in courts\nand the time taken in their disposal, it is likely that the attaching authority may\ntake a long time before objections regarding the attachment are decided. In\nsuch a case, a period of limitation starting from the attachment may cause serious\nhardship. Till the result of the objection \ufb01led before the attaching authority is\nknown, the civil court cannot decide the matter. It would rather be harsh to\ndrive an aggrieved person to a civil court even before a cause of action has acc-\nrued in his favour. or at least before his claim can be effectively decided.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_714\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"973018\" id=\"span_161\"><a href=\"/doc/973018/\" id=\"a_1468\">&#x27;Man Sing/I V. Ram Nath, A.l.R.</a> 1924 All. 828.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_715\">3Dr. H. S. Gaur. Bar-at-Law. Raipur. Letter No. 2063/V. 4-5 dt. 19-12-1907 National\nArchives File 1908, Paper No. 2. page 9. _\n\n3Cf. Eng. Gim Molt V. Clzinese Merited Com. Bariking A.L.R. 1940 276 (F.B.).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_716\">&#x27;Mr. Justice Banerii. High Court of Judicature NW. To obviate such hardships as well as to avoid a multiplicity of litiga- R\u00b0\u00b0\u00b0m&#x27;&quot;\u00b0&quot;d&quot;&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_867\">. -- . . . 3\ntion, we recommend that the third column of <a href=\"/doc/1119830/\" id=\"a_1469\">article 79</a> should be rev1sed_as \u00a7&#x27;,&#x27;f&#x27;c,2\u00a7 7&#x27;\u00a7_g0_\n\nfollows:&#x27;\n&quot;The date of release of the distress.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_868\">Similarly, the third column of <a href=\"/doc/1864214/\" id=\"a_1470\">article 80</a> should be revised as under:---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_718\">&quot;The date of release from the seizure.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_869\">37.44. <a href=\"/doc/1501881/\" id=\"a_1471\">Article 81</a> reads as under:---- <a href=\"/doc/1501881/\" id=\"a_1472\">Article 81\n\n&quot;81.</a> By executors, administrators or One&#x27; The date of the death of the person\nrepresentatives under the Legal year. wronged.&quot;\n<a href=\"/doc/1057059/\" id=\"a_1473\">Representatives&#x27; Suits Act, 1885</a>.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_870\">.It is identical with <a href=\"/doc/655638/\" id=\"a_1474\">article 20</a> of the Act of 1908.\n<a href=\"/doc/655638/\" id=\"a_1475\">Article 20</a> of the Act of 1877 was as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_719\">.&quot;20.
