Changa Mal A.I.R.</a> 1925 All. 1311.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_726\">24-14 M of I.J&amp;CA/ND/83\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_164\">178</span>\n\nLaw com-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_727\">mission\nReport-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_728\">point con-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_729\">cerning survival\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_730\">-of cause of\naction on\ndeath.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_731\">History.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_732\">Law Commis-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_733\">Iron Report-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_734\">point concern-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_735\">ing limitation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_736\">35.\n\n&quot;33. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1492\">Under Act</a> No. XII of l855 (to Two\n\n89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION AC1&#x27;, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37----~Articles 72 to 9I----SrIits relating to fort.)\n\nWhen the wrong complained of is\ndone.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_880\">Under the same Act against any Two\nother representative. years.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_881\"><a href=\"/doc/829916/\" id=\"a_1493\">Article 33</a> of the Act of 1877 was as under:----\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_882\">When the wrong complained of is\nenable executors, administrators, done.&quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_883\">or representatives to sue and to be\nsued for certain wrongs) against\nan executor, administrator. or\n\nother representative.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_884\"><a href=\"/doc/555882/\" id=\"a_1494\">Article 39</a> of the Act of 1871 was in identical terms.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_885\">years.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_886\">37.45. The,Law Commission&#x27; in its Report on the Act of 1908, while consi-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_887\">dering the scheme of the relevant Act, observed as under:~--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_888\">Act of 1908.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_889\">81 it is one year.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_890\">two years (suits under the <a href=\"/doc/594667/\" id=\"a_1495\">Fatal Accidents Act, 1855</a>).\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_737\">&quot;The maxim of English Law &quot;actio personalis moritur cum persona&quot; has\nbeen modi\ufb01ed in India by various Acts. <a href=\"/doc/594667/\" id=\"a_1496\">The Fatal Accidents Act</a> provides\nthat in the case of death of a person injured by a wrongful act, neglect or\ndefault, a right to suit accrues to recover damages for the bene\ufb01t of the wife,\nhusband, parent and child. if any. of the person who dies. &quot;But the suit\nhas to be instituted in the name of the executor, administrator or representa-\ntive of the deceased person. <a href=\"/doc/1057059/\" id=\"a_1497\">Under the Legal Representatives&#x27; Suits Act</a>,\nXII of 1855, the executor, administrator or representative of any deceased\nperson has been given a right to bring a suit for a wrong committed in the\nlife-time of such person which occasioned pecuniary loss to his estate, provi-\nded the suit was in respect of a wrong committed within one year before\n\nthe death. Death will not abate any cause of action relating to loss of\ndamage to property. The damages recovered form part of the estate of the\ndeceased.&quot; A suit may be maintained against the executor, administrator\n\nor representative ofthe deceased for any wrong committed by him in his\nlife-time for which he would have been subject to an action if the wrong\nwas committed within one year before his death. <a href=\"/doc/103447/\" id=\"a_1498\">Section 2</a> of that Act\nfurther provides that the death of either, party to a suit shall not abate the\nsuit. <a href=\"/doc/261549/\" id=\"a_1499\">Section 306</a> of the Indian Succession Act provides that the right to\nprosecute or defend any action or special proceeding existing in favour of or\nagainst a person at the time of his death survives to&#x27; and against his executor,\nadministrator or representative. But an exception is made in respect of a\ncause of action based on defamation, assault as &quot;de\ufb01ned in the <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_1500\">Indian Penal\nCode</a>, or other personal injury not causing the death of the party. In\nEngland, until recently, the maxim above referred to applied generally till it\nwas abrogated by the Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1934.\nBut even under this Act, a cause of action for defamationior seduction or\nfor inducing one spouse to leave or remain apart from the other and for\ndamages on the ground of adultery were excepted. The law, therefore,\nboth in England and in India at the present moment is more or less the\n\nsame. The difference lies only in the exceptions existing under the Indian\nLaw and the English law.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_891\">37.46. <a href=\"/doc/1463849/\" id=\"a_1501\">Article 83</a> of the Act of 1963 replaces <a href=\"/doc/829916/\" id=\"a_1502\">articles 33</a><a href=\"/doc/846153/\" id=\"a_1503\">, 34</a>, and <a href=\"/doc/448465/\" id=\"a_1504\">35</a> of the\nThe period under this article is two years, whereas under article\nIn article 82. the period of one year has been increased to\n\n37.47. The Law Commission&quot; in its Report on the Act of 1908, observed\n\nas under : -\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_738\">114-115.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_739\">&#x27;Law Commission of\n\n_ _ India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act 1908), page 41, para 112.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_740\">&quot;Law Commission of\n\nIndia, 3rd Report (Limitation Act 1908), pages 42-45, para 113.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_741\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA on TH}: LIMYIATILN ACT, 1963 179\n\n(Chapter 37----<a href=\"/doc/1008926/\" id=\"a_1505\">Articles 72</a> to 91--Suits relating to tort.)\n\n&quot;ll3. <a href=\"/doc/655638/\" id=\"a_1506\">Article 20</a> relates to a suit filed by a legal representative for torts\ncausing pecuniary loss to the estate, while <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1507\">article 21</a> relates to a suit \ufb01led\nby a legal representative for damages ior death, which has to be a\nrepresentative action. Theother group of <a href=\"/doc/829916/\" id=\"a_1508\">Articles 33</a><a href=\"/doc/846153/\" id=\"a_1509\">, 34</a> and <a href=\"/doc/448465/\" id=\"a_1510\">35</a> relate to\nsuits against the legal representatives. Under <a href=\"/doc/655638/\" id=\"a_1511\">articles 20</a> and Zl, the date\nof death of the person is taken as the starting point of limitation. <a href=\"/doc/594667/\" id=\"a_1512\">Under\nthe Fatal Accidents Act</a>, the suit is for damages for causing death by any\nwrongful act, neglect or default and the suit is for the bene\ufb01t of the depen-\ndants. The suit under the <a href=\"/doc/1057059/\" id=\"a_1513\">Legal Representatives&#x27; Suits Act</a> is restricted to\nwrongs which occasion pecuniary loss to the estate of the deceased and\ncause of action in respect of which, according to the law then prevailing, did\nnot survive. <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_1514\">Under the Succession Act</a>, all rights of action survive to the\nexecutors or administrators except actions for defamation, assault or per-\nsonal injuries not causing the death of a party. The substantive law preven-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_742\">, ting the abatement of the cause of action is <a href=\"/doc/64829/\" id=\"a_1515\">laid down by</a> the said Acts. The\ncause of action under the <a href=\"/doc/594667/\" id=\"a_1516\">Fatal Accidents Act</a> is the death and time under\n<a href=\"/doc/655638/\" id=\"a_1517\">Article 20</a> begins to run from the date of the accrual of the cause of action.\nHowever, under the <a href=\"/doc/1057059/\" id=\"a_1518\">Legal Representatives&#x27; Suits Act</a>, as the suit is in respect\nof a wrong committed in the life time of a person but time is made to run\nfrom the date of the death, the running of time does not synchronise with\nthe date of accrual of the cause of action. On the other hand under <a href=\"/doc/829916/\" id=\"a_1519\">Articles\n33</a> and <a href=\"/doc/448465/\" id=\"a_1520\">35</a>, a period of 2 years is provided which runs from the date when the\nwrong complained of is done. This synchronises with the date of the\naccrual of the cause of action. The suits contemplated under the two\nActs, i.e. Legal Representatives&#x27; Suits Act and the Fatal Accidents Act relate\nto torts. No special period of limitation is provided for actions contem-\nplated by <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_1521\">section 306</a> of the Succession Act, as it was assumed that the pro-\nvisions <a href=\"/doc/64829/\" id=\"a_1522\">laid down in</a> the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1523\">Limitation Act</a> will govern such actions.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_743\">&quot;114. A Provision for the survival of the right of action having been\nmade, the action may be treated as one founded on tort whether it is by or\nagainst an executor, administrator or representative and the time for limita-\ntion may be made to commence with the accrual of the cause of action.\nIf a period of three years from the accrual of the cause&#x27; of action is provided,\nno hardship will be caused to either party. In view of the proposed period\nof 3 years from the date of accrual of the cause of action the period of one\nyear before death provided in the <a href=\"/doc/1057059/\" id=\"a_1524\">Legal Representatives&#x27; Suits Act</a> will\nhave to be abrogated. lt may be observed &quot;here that instead of leaving\nthe question of survival of the cause of action to be dealt with by three\nseparate Acts, a consolidating amendment in an appropriate manner may be\nmade in <a href=\"/doc/261549/\" id=\"a_1525\">section 306</a> of the Indian Succession Act.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_744\">&quot;I15. There is also a con\ufb02ict of decisions under section 306 <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_1526\">5</a>&#x27;r&quot;&#x27;t&#x27;1&#x27;l&#x27;e\n<a href=\"/doc/1450343/\" id=\"a_1527\">Indian Succession Act</a> as to whether a right to an action for malicious prose-\ncution is one relating to personal injury not causing the death of the party\nand whether it survives. This con\ufb02ict may be set at rest by speci\ufb01cally\nbringing within the exception to section 306 of that Act, actions for malicious\nprosecution if it is intended that the cause of action in respect of such wrongs\nshould not survive the death of the person aggrieved.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_892\">37.48. There is a period of limitation of one year under all these articles, Joint com-\nexcept in the case of fatal accidents under <a href=\"/doc/1682669/\" id=\"a_1528\">article 82</a>, wh\u00e9re it has been increased mime Report\n\nto two years in the Act of 1963 on the recommendation of the Joint Committee.\nThe Joint Committee Report stated&#x27; thus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_745\">&#x27;Joint Committee Report, (1963 Act).\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_165\">180</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_746\">Comment.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_747\">Causes of _\naction surviv-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_748\">ing on death.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_749\">Reason for\nspecial\nprovision.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_750\"><a href=\"/doc/568164/\" id=\"a_1529\">Article 84.</a>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_751\">89mm REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMIFATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37~Artic1e.r 72 to 91--~Su1&#x27;ts relating to tort.)\n\n&quot;The Committee feels that the limitation of one year provided for suits under\nthe <a href=\"/doc/594667/\" id=\"a_1530\">Indian Fatal Accidents Act, 1855</a>, is short and should be increased to\ntwo years.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_752\">37.49. The period of one year in earlier articles (corresponding to present\n<a href=\"/doc/1501881/\" id=\"a_1531\">articles 81</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1463849/\" id=\"a_1532\"><a href=\"/doc/1463849/\" id=\"a_1533\">83</a>)</a> was commented upon by W.J. Money, O\ufb02iciating Judge\nMaimansingh, in his old opinion of 187l,&#x27;as to be too short for the purpose in\nhand: &#x27;\n\n&quot;That, under Act XII of 1855, there was a limitation of two years in the\ncase of actions of a certain kind against executors, &amp; c. points out that delay\nin instituting actions of this class may often be unavoidable, owing to the\ndil\ufb01culty of obtaining full information, and urges that it is not apparent.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_893\">therefore, why the period of limitation should be reduced to one year. He\nsubmits whether in such cases it would not be more for the public interest\nthat the limitation should be \ufb01xed at two years in the cases of actions both\nby and against executors, such limitation being in respect of wrong committed\nwithin one year of the death of the person, and to be calculated from the\ndate of decease, and not from thecommission of the wrong?&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_753\">37.50. So much as regards matters of primarily historical interest. A ques-\ntion that may need to be looked into is this. Under <a href=\"/doc/261549/\" id=\"a_1534\">section 306</a> of the Indian\nSuccession Act, the right to prosecute or defend an action or proceeding in\nfavour of or against a person at the time of his death survives to, and against,\nhis executors, administrators or representatives. It seems desirable to refer to\nthe impact of this provision on the law of limitation. In contrast with the gene-\nral position, the starting point mentioned in <a href=\"/doc/1501881/\" id=\"a_1535\">article 81</a> is the date of death of\nthe person wronged, and not the date of commission of the wrong. In other\nwords, though the suit is in respect of a wrong committed in the life-time of a\nperson, time is made to run from the date of the death, and the running of time\ndoes not synchronise with time with the accrual of the cause of action.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_754\">37.51. The reason why this special provision was made might be that the\nwronged person, when alive, might not have found time to take legal steps, and\nif the starting point is counted immediately from the date of commission of the\nwrong, then the prescribed period may, in many cases, terminate very soon while\nthe executors, administrators or representatives are still taking time to settle down\nand to takestock of the situation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_755\">In this context we have considered the question whether the enactment of\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1450343/\" id=\"a_1536\">Indian Succession Act</a> (which is not referred to in <a href=\"/doc/1501881/\" id=\"a_1537\">articles 81</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1463849/\" id=\"a_1538\">83</a>) had\nany impact on the articles under consideration. It appears that this question\ncan, if necessary, be more conveniently considered when the <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_1539\">Succession Act</a> is\nitself taken up for review by the Law Commission.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_894\">37.52. This takes us to <a href=\"/doc/568164/\" id=\"a_1540\">article 84</a>, which reads as under:----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_756\">&quot;84. Against one who, liaving a right Two . The Calcutta High Court,&#x27; dilating upon the word &quot;damages&quot; in the\nAct of 1871, observed as under:----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_788\">&quot;In my opinion <a href=\"/doc/1678224/\" id=\"a_1570\">article 11</a> of schedule (ii) embraces any suit or action brought\nunder <a href=\"/doc/371879/\" id=\"a_1571\">section 22</a> of the Act XV of 1859, and there was no intention of\ndrawing any distinction between a suit framed as an action for damages, and\none framed as a suit for an account. The taking of an account of pro\ufb01ts is\n\n&#x27;only a mode of compensating an inventor for the infringement of his pri-\nvilege other than by an assessment of damages, and it seems unreasonable\nthat if the period of limitation is one year in the one case, it should .be six\nyears in the others&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_921\">37.59. This view was also favoured by Dr. Stockes, in the following\nwords:----- &#x27; e\n\n&quot;The article should be extended expressly to suits for an account of the\npro\ufb01ts obtained by infringement?&quot; \u00bb\n\nAccordingly, in the Act of 1877, the word &#x27;damages&#x27; was replaced by the word\n&#x27;compensation&#x27;. &#x27;\n\n37.60. The present period of limitation under <a href=\"/doc/453721/\" id=\"a_1572\">article 88</a> is three years. Chief\nJustice Sir Richard Garth and the then Secretary, Legislative Department had\nmade certain comments on <a href=\"/doc/1678224/\" id=\"a_1573\">article 11</a> of the Act of 1871 (which had \ufb01xed a\nperiod of one year).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_922\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1989230\" id=\"span_168\"><a href=\"/doc/1989230/\" id=\"a_1574\">&#x27;Kinmond v. Jackson</a>, (1877) I.L.R. 3 Cal. 17, 19.</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_789\">&quot;Stokes, The Anglo-Indian Codes, Vol. 2 page 981.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_790\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 37~--ArticIes 72 to 91~Suits relating to tort.)\n\n&quot;By clause 11, one year only is \ufb01xed as the period of limitation in case\nof infringement of copyright. This, certainly, seems an unreasonably short\ntime. It must be remembered that infringements of copyright are from\ntheir very nature frequently, indeed generally, not discovered until long after\nthe infringement has occurred. It is often the work of months, if not years,\nto make a book or a piece of music (unless it is of the very striking charac-\nter) well known to the public; and in the greatest majority of cases, if such\nbook or piece of music is an infringement of some author&#x27;s copyright the\nauthor might probably not hear of the infringement until some time after it\nhad taken place. This is, therefore, one of those instances, as it seems to\nme, in which it would be right to allow a plaintiff a much longer period of\nlimitation.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_923\">And again observed 2*\n\n&quot;Clause 11--~--The limitation, I think should be three years instead of one?&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_791\">Mr. J. H. Nelson stated:----- &#x27;\n\n&quot;<a href=\"/doc/555882/\" id=\"a_1575\">Article 39----</a>Literary piracy is theft; I would make the starting point the\n\ndiscovery of infringement?&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_924\">37.61. So it is, but it was the period prescribed by\nAct XIV of 1859, <a href=\"/doc/206324/\" id=\"a_1576\">section 1</a>, clause 2, and in framing Act IX of 1871, the\nold periods were, for obvious reasons, kept when possible.&quot; &quot;I think we\nshould lengthen the period if we alter the law at al .&#x27;&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_793\">37.62. Thus, the period of one year in the Act of 1871 and Draft Limita- Ea;-11\u00a2;\ntion Bill, 1877 was changed in 1877 to three years. To make the discussion concrete, we recommend the following re-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_928\">draft of <a href=\"/doc/453721/\" id=\"a_1580\">article 88</a>, so as to cover the points made above&#x27;:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_803\">Three\nyears.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_804\">&quot;88. To restrain waste. When the waste begins.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_930\">It is identical with <a href=\"/doc/1975922/\" id=\"a_1582\">article 41</a> of the Acts of 1908 and 1877.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_931\">There was no comparable provision in the Act of 1877.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_932\">37.66. In the absence of practical difliculties, we see no reason to disturb\nthe present article.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_933\">37.67. <a href=\"/doc/1226972/\" id=\"a_1583\">Article 90</a> reads as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_810\">&quot;90. For compensation for injury cau- Three\nsed by an injunction wrongfully years.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_811\">obtained.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_812\">It is identical with <a href=\"/doc/111604/\" id=\"a_1584\">article 42</a> of the Acts of 1908 and 1877.\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1585\">In the Act</a> of 1871, <a href=\"/doc/736613/\" id=\"a_1586\">article 86</a> reads as under :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_813\">When the injunction ceases.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_814\">&quot;86. For compensation for damage Three\ncaused by an injunction wrong- years.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_815\">fully obtained. &#x27;\n\nWhen the injunction ceases.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_934\">When the draft of the Act of 1908 was circulated for comments, ajDistrict\nJudge suggested that attachment should be brought within the scope of those\nprovisions. His suggestion was as followsr--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_816\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/1975922/\" id=\"a_1587\">Article 41--</a>After the word &#x27;an&#x27;, I would insertz &#x27;attachment&#x27; &quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_935\">However, this suggestion does not seem to have been accepted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_936\">37.68. We have given some thought to the matter. &#x27;In our view, there is a\n\ncase for adding in <a href=\"/doc/1226972/\" id=\"a_1588\">article 90</a> a suit for compensation for wrongful attachment. -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_937\">No doubt, as a summary remedy, <a href=\"/doc/72934358/\" id=\"a_1589\">section 95</a> of the Code of Civil Procedure,\n1908 provides for the grant of compensation for arrest before judgment or attach-\nment before judgment or issue of a temporary injunction, but this does not, bar a\nregular suit for compensation for such wrongful act3 arrest, attachment or injunc-\ntion. At present, it would appear that wrongful attachment is not a continuing\nwrong&#x27;. The article relating to compensation for statutory acts may not apply\nto attachment. As to attachment before judgment, old <a href=\"/doc/1888152/\" id=\"a_1590\">article 29</a> of the Act of\n1908 (relating to wrongful seizure) seems to have been applied&#x27;, under the Act of\n1908. However, it is desirable to make <a href=\"/doc/1226972/\" id=\"a_1591\">article 90</a> comprehensive, and, for this\npurpose, to cover an attachment wrongfully obtained.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_938\">1Paragraph 37.63 supra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_939\">&#x27;Dewan Bahadur S. I577.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_945\"><a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1598\">In the Act</a> of 1871. six articles covered the subject matter. viz. <a href=\"/doc/1452355/\" id=\"a_1599\">articles 48</a><a href=\"/doc/1551554/\" id=\"a_1600\">, 47.</a>\n35, 34, 33, and 26, with varying periods. lt is unnecessary to quote them for\nthe present &#x27;purpose, since they do not throw particular light on many important\ningredients of the present article. ln,l877 and 1908, all these articles were\nbrought under two articles. and a uniform period of limitation of 3 years was\nprovided. The Law Commission in its Report&#x27; on the Act of 1908 recommen~\nded a different arrangement of the articles, but that recommendation has not\nbeen accepted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_946\">37.71. The expression speci\ufb01c movable property&quot; occurring in the article Case law as\nrenders relevant a discussion&#x27; of two cases of application of the article to money to money\n~, in specie (as against simple money claims). The first case is an Allahabad one.m Spam&#x27;\n\nin which a company had instructed its bahkcrs to make payment of excise duty\namounting to Rs. 50,000 in the sub--trcasury and got the challan duly stamped\n\nand signed by the accountant and the treasurer. The amount paid was, however.\ndefalcated by the treasury ollicials. and was not credited to the account of the\nCollector of Central Excise. As a result, the company had to make payment\n\nof the excise duty for the second time. The company then \ufb01led a-suit for re-\ncovery of the money from the State Government. The Allahabad High Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_947\">inter alia, heldi that thesuit was neither for certain &quot;speci\ufb01c movable property&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_821\">nor for the return of particular coins or currency notes (which the plaintiff had\ndeposited) and, therefore. <a href=\"/doc/1452355/\" id=\"a_1601\">articles 48</a> and <a href=\"/doc/9107/\" id=\"a_1602\">49</a> of the Act of l908 did not apply to\n\nthe case. Holding that the suit was based on the liability of the Government\n\nfor the misconduct of its servants. the High Court applied the residuary <a href=\"/doc/1226884/\" id=\"a_1603\">article\n\n120</a> of the Act of 1908, which gave a longer period of six years to the plaintiff.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_822\">37.72. In the second case,&quot; which is from Rajasthan, a person had deposited\nmoney with the municipal council for the construction of a septic tank and soak-\npit for the disposal of waste water. On the failure of the Municipality to carry\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act 1908) page 45. para 121 and\npage 83. articles 1.2.6. &#x27;\n\n3StrIIe of UP. V. In a Calcutta case&#x27; decided with reference to <a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_1612\">article 134</a> of the Act of Successor\n1908 as it stood before I929, a trustee executed on 8th February, 1910 a lease mm\u00b0\u00b0&#x27;\nof the trust property in derogation of the trust. and subsequently appointed a\nsuccessor trustee who \ufb01led a suit to set aside thelease on 21st January, 19-32.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_844\">The successor to the original trustee was appointed only on 30th September,\n1931. However, relying on section 2(8) of the Limitation Act, 1908, the court\nheld the successor trustee to be within the de\ufb01nition of the word &quot;plaintiff&quot; and\nheld that the suit was barred by time. With respect, this overlooks the context.\nThe time should now begin to run only from the date of the knowledge of the\n\nsucceeding trustee who brings the suit&#x27;. 1\nI\n\n38.3. No other points requiring discussion from the point of view of possible Nodclcllanize\nneed for amendment in the article have come to our notice. Inthe Act. of 1908. these articles were inserted in 1929, resolving the 1115;.&quot;-_\neon\ufb02iet between two Privy Council rulings.&#x27;-&#x27;-&#x27; They have been consolidated In\n<a href=\"/doc/1018551/\" id=\"a_1634\">article 96</a> in the Act of 1963. with the added advantage given to the plaintiff --\nas respects the computation of time. Sicilyamma</a>. A.I.R. 1963 Ker. 137 (F.B.).</span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1012\">0.21, R. 58.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1013\"><a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1653\">C.P.C</a>. as\namended in\n1976.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1014\">Scheme of\nthe aniemlecl\nCode.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1015\">Scope of\n<a href=\"/doc/817281/\" id=\"a_1654\">article 98</a>\nnow limited.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1016\">Change\nneeded in\n<a href=\"/doc/817281/\" id=\"a_1655\">article 98.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1017\">v\n\n89rH REPORT or LAVV co.~1\\/IN. or INDIA ON THE Ll1\\llT.\\Tl0N ACT. 1963\n\n(C/zaplcr 39 A/&#x27;I/I/c.\\&#x27; 97 to I12: Sir/&#x27;Is Re/(zting to /l/I/.S&#x27;(&#x27;(&#x27;//(I/&#x27;t&#x27;0ll.S&#x27; M11110/&#x27;s.)\n\n39.6. The Kcrala Judgment. being a case arising out of the old <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1656\">Limitation\nAct</a>. would not throw light upon the interpretation of the expression &#x27;\ufb01nal order&#x27;\nnow occurring in the third column of <a href=\"/doc/817281/\" id=\"a_1657\">article 98</a> of the 1963 Act. The use of the\nword &#x27;\ufb01nal&#x27; would now seem to shift the starting point of limitation from the date\nof the order of the executing court to the date of the order passed in revision, and\nan argument on this scope. as well as on the applicability of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_1658\">section 14</a> to such\ncases and the period available should not now arise. Moreover. as is elaborated\nbelow,&#x27; the scheme of the procedural provisions with which <a href=\"/doc/817281/\" id=\"a_1659\">article 98</a> is linked\nhas been radically revised in 1976. I\n\n39.7. The very important amendment made in 1976 in the Code of Civil Pro-\ncedure, 1908, concerning Order 21, Rule. 58, deserves to be noticed at -this place.\nThis amendment sustantially implements the recommendation of the Law Com-\nmission? in its Report on the Code.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1018\">39.8. Under the amended <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1660\">Code of Civil Procedure</a>. there has been a drastic\nchange in the procedure. the mode of approach and the method by which the court\nshould arrive at a decision. when an application under Order 21, Rule 58 of the\nCode is \ufb01led. Under the law before 1976. such an investigation into a claim or\nobjection was summary in its nature. and was. in a very large number of c-u.w.&lt;;. lia-\nble to be set aside by a regular suit. The present law, after 1976, on the other hand.\nmaking a signi\ufb01cant departure in the method ofidisposals and adjudication of such\nclaims and objections. contemplates a full enquiry into all questions. including\nquestions of right. title and interest in the property. Its mandate is that the court\ninquiring into such a claim or objection shall determine all such questions. Except\nin the very limited number of cases mentioned in Order 21, Rule 58(5) there is a\ntotal embargo on a separate suit. Order 21, Rule 58(4) now provides that the order\nmade in such adjudication shall have the same force and be subject to the same\nconditions as to appeal or otherwise. as a decree. Thus. the order passed in these\nproceedings is a substitute for a decision in an ordinary litigation resulting in a\ndecree. The adjudication contemplated by amended Order 21. Rule 58 as amended\nis not summary. the intention being that it should be like a decision rendered in a\nregular -suit. and should result in an appealable decree. so that. in these very pro-\nceedings. the court could ultimately decide and adjudicate all questions, including\nquestions relating to right. title or interest in the property attached. which might\narise directly or indirectly between the parties.&quot; 9\n\n39.9. In other words. the scope of <a href=\"/doc/817281/\" id=\"a_1661\">article 98</a> is now con\ufb01ned to those very\nlimited number of cases where in respect of claims or objections to an attachment,\na suit can still be instituted or permitted under <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1662\">the Code of Civil Procedure</a> (as\namended in 1976). Once the (&#x27;laim \ufb01led in execution proceedings is entertained,\nand adjudicated, the adjudication by the court has now been given the status of\na &quot;decree&quot;. Hence. the scope for \ufb01ling a suit to assert a claim in respect of the\nattachedjproperty is very limited after the amendment of 1976.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1019\">39.10. It is desirable to amend the zirticle in the light of the changed posi-\ntion as resulting from the amendment made in <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1663\">the Code of Civil Procedure</a>. 1908.\nIt is necessary that. in this article. the reference to the rules of --Order.2l <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1664\">C.P.C</a>.\nshould be made more precise. by framing it as a reference to Rule 58(5) of Order\n21 of the Code. that being the only provision under which a suit can be \ufb01led to\nchallenge any attachment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1020\">1See para 39.7 infra.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1021\">2Law Commission of India 54th Report (<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1665\">Code of Civil Procedure</a>. 1908), Chapter 21.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1022\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1964635\" id=\"span_178\">3Southern Stec/met &amp; <a href=\"/doc/1964635/\" id=\"a_1666\">Alloys Ltd. On the applicability of <a href=\"/doc/813152/\" id=\"a_1672\">article 99</a> to a suit \ufb01led by a Hindu son to Suits by\nhave a court sale in execution of a decree against his father set aside. there exists Hindu son.\na con\ufb02ict of views. The first view is represented by a judgment of the Kerala\nHigh Court.&#x27; It was a suit brought by Hindu son, alleging that his share in the\njoint family properties was not liable to be sold in execution of a decree obtain-\ned against his father and that the sale in execution was not binding on his share.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1036\">It was -held that the son was by the decree against the father precluded from\nquestioning the existence of the debt on which that decree was obtained. and\nthat it was open to him to challenge the decree and the execution proceedings on\nthe ground that the original debt itself was non-existent or \ufb01ctitious. The court\nrelied on a Madras judgment? for holding that if the son&#x27;s interest is found not to\nhave been sold or if the execution sale is void as against him. it is unnecessary to\n\n&#x27;l.(!/rx/Imaitdas V, I\\&#x27;m&#x27;m1akrI.&quot;nu, A.l.R. I957 Ker. I26.\n&#x27;3La/lshmadu V. Ramudu, A LR. 1939 Mad. 867.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1037\">26~--14 M of LJ &amp; CA/ND/83\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_179\">194</span>\n\nCon\ufb02ictj--\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1038\">the earlier\ncomments.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1039\">Com ments-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1040\">District\nJudge,\nCuddapah\n(1877).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1041\">Comments----\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1042\">Divisional\nJudge,\nNagpur.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1043\">(1907).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1044\">89TH REPORT or L WV comm. or mom ON his Ll.\\tlT.\\TlON ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 39\u00a5ArticIes 97 to 1/2: Suits Re./(Hing In .&#x27;1{r&#x27;.rce1&#x27;/rzricatzs 11/{(n&#x27;1&#x27;er&lt;&#x27;.1\n\nmake a prayer for setting aside the sale in a suit for aecovtrring possession of his\nshare and <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_1673\">article 12</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1674\">Limitation Act</a>.&#x27; 190&#x27;: (pt&#x27;escnt <a href=\"/doc/813152/\" id=\"a_1675\">article 99</a>). can have no\napplication to such a case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1045\">The Punjab High Court.&#x27; dissenting from the above view. has held that in\nsuch cases the sale is :1 voidable one and not :_t__ void sale. with the result that such\na suit by a Hindu son to have set aside a court sale in execution of :2 decree\nagainst his &#x27;father is governed by <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_1676\">article 12(a)</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1677\">Limitation Act</a>. 1908 (pre-\nsent <a href=\"/doc/813152/\" id=\"a_1678\">article 99</a>). A Travancore-Cochin case takes th=: szunc view.&quot; A Supreme Court\ndecision, while touching the point. does not settle this particular contlictf&#x27;\n\n39.14. The con\ufb02ict dates back to the days when the draft Bill of the 1877 Act\nwas circulated for comments. The problem was mentioned in the comments on\n\nthe 1908 Bill.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1046\">39.15. The District Judge. Cuddapah. commented&#x27; in 1877 as under. when\noffering his suggestions on the Bill then circulated :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_893\">&quot;I have known repeated instances in which a suit brought really for the\nestablishment of one&#x27;s right to own and possess an immovable that has been\nthe subject of a court sale has been wrongly dismissed as barred by the lapse\nof time as being a suit to set aside a sale and brought more than one year\nafter the date of the sale. If there are suits that can properly be brought to\n&#x27;set aside a sale ordered and effected by a court of Justice. 1 think it desirable\nto introduce (the section) into words (lcscriptivc of the nature of such suits.\nTn any case. something should he done to remove the impression that exists.\nperhaps Very generally. that if C is ousted from his possession of an immov-\nable in consequence of a sale to A of B&#x27;s supposed right over that immov-\nable. C cannot recover possession unless he sites within ore year from the\ndate of the sale being con\ufb01rmed.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_894\">&quot;The starting time {in this article/131 appears to me toh: ill-chosen. in-\nasmuch as ordinarily in this Presidency. at least the courts seem not to trou-\nble themselves to con\ufb01rm a sale unless and until rt dispute about the regula-\nrity thereof actually arises and not always then.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1047\">39.16. Thirty years thenceiwhen the Limitation Bill, which led to the Act of\n1908 was circulated. the Divisional Judge. Nagpur.&#x27; made the following com-\nment:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_895\">&quot;Defendants frequently argue with subtle plausibility that a decree or\norder must be &#x27;set aside&#x27; before a possessory relief can be obtained and try\nto put this and like short period of limitation in the way of the plaintiff. The\ncounter-argument often is that it is not necessary to &#x27;set aside&#x27; the order or\ndecree as it is mere nullity by which the plaintiff is not bound. One expect-\ned that the Legislature would give an expression of its opinion in embar-\nrassing cases of the kind unless it has taken some recent Privy Council cases\n(l.L.R. 25 Born. 337: l.L.R. 32 Cal. 296) as settling the controversy.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1048\">&#x27;Ajir Singh V. Hem Raj. A.1.R. 1956 Puniab 139.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1049\">&#x27;NeIt&#x27;akama Iyer Vrmcliirwnrn lye!&#x27; V, Narayana [Yer Venkim.mb.ha Iver, A.l.R_ 1956\nTrav. The Law Conimissioni. in its Report on the Act of 1908, observed that\n<a href=\"/doc/1704729/\" id=\"a_1702\">articles 117</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1283667/\" id=\"a_1703\">122</a> of the Act of 1908, relating to suits upon a foreign judgment\nand upon a judgment or recognisance. the periods prescribed being six years and\ntwelve years from the date of judgment or recognisance, that in England, such\nsuits were treated as suits on contracts. V\u20ac!lliIP,?i/Clln, A&#x27;.I.R. 1958 Ker. 178 (FB).\n5Ling\u00a2&#x27;mgouda V. _Sangangomia, A.l.R. 1933 Born. 386, 392.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1145\">/ &#x27; V\n\n/\n\n\n\n89TH REPORT or i,,+_\\v COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_186\">0</span>\n\n(Chapter 39-----Articles 97 to 1.72--- Suits Relating to Miscellaneous Matters.)\n\n39.48. In an Allahabad case&#x27; it was held that the word &quot;dispossess&quot; in this 33519 anlswinas\nArticle should be given a wide&#x27; meaning, and even if the defendant was inpossessjon of\npossession of the road much before the road come to be vested in tlie District d\u00b0ef&quot;&#x27;g&#x27;I1nae&quot;&#x27;o?&#x27;\nBoard, the District Board should be deemed to have been &quot;dispossessed&quot; of the vesting,\nroad immediately on the date of vesting thereof, and limitation for a suit under\narticle 146<a href=\"/doc/54424454/\" id=\"a_1749\">A of the Act</a> (1908 Act) should be deemed to have started from the\n\ndate of such vesting. _\nA contrary view was taken in u Calcutta case?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1146\">In our view, it would be straining the language of the &#x27;article too far to say\nthat a person was &quot;dispossessed&quot; of a property when, on or before the date of\nalleged dispossession, he was not in possession of&#x27;the same. &#x27;\n\n39.49. It is, we think, probable that the view taken in the Calcutta decision Egan?&#x27;\nwill be followed by other High Courts also. For that reason, we do not suggest ne\u00a2ded_\nany clari\ufb01catory amendment of the article on this particular point. The point, in\nany case, may not recur frequently.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1147\">39.50. We should mention another question that is somewhat related to Ema&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_972\"><a href=\"/doc/158646/\" id=\"a_1750\">article 111.</a> Various State Acts establishing Municipal Corporations, Zilla Pari- after\nshads and other local authorities contain provisions empowering the local autho- fmg\nrity to require a person to remove any projection, obstruction or encroachment &#x27;\nupon a public road3. Usually, a Municipal Corporation takes recourse to the\nexpeditious process of eviction prescribed by such special Acts, rather than go in\nfor the time consuming process of a civil suit- However. the question sometimes\narises whether the Corporation is legally entitled to take recourse to such sum-\nmary proceeding if, for the period of thirty y ars (prescribed by <a href=\"/doc/158646/\" id=\"a_1751\">article 111</a>), the\nCorporation has acquiesced in the encroachm nt. There has been a conflict of\nopinion on this subject. According to the High Courts of Bombay&#x27; and Punjabi&#x27;,\nthe right of the Corporation to remove the encroachment by the summary pro-\ncedure is not lost, even after such accguiescene. But a contrary view has been\nexpressed by the Madras&#x27; and &#x27;Lahore&#x27; High Courts. The question primarily\ncouoems interpretation of the state laws, which is the reason why it cannot be\ndealt with by\u00bb amending the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1752\">Limitation Act</a>.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_973\">A 39.51. This takes us to <a href=\"/doc/280240/\" id=\"a_1753\">article 112</a>, which reads as under :-- Amide 112&#x27;\n\n_&quot;l 12. LlMlTATION ACT, 1963\n\n.(Chapter 39--<a href=\"/doc/786895/\" id=\"a_1760\">Articles 97</a> to ][2--\u00abSuI&#x27;ts Relating to Miscellaneous Matters.)\n(Chapter 40---Article I13----SuI&#x27;ts for Which there is no Prescribed Period.)\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1761\">In the Act</a> of 1877, <a href=\"/doc/23104/\" id=\"a_1762\">article 149</a> reads as under: _\n\n&quot;l49. Any suit by or on behalf of the Sixty When the period of 1imita&quot;\u00b0.11\n\n1 Secretary of State for India in years. would begin to run undenthls\n\nCouncil. Act against a Eike suit by a private\nperson.&quot;-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1159\"><a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1763\">In the Act</a> of 1871, <a href=\"/doc/1244239/\" id=\"a_1764\">article 150</a> read as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_979\">&quot;150. Any suit in the name of the sec- Sixty When the right to sue accrues.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1160\">retary of State for Indian in years.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1161\">Council. i &#x27;\nAs the period of 60 years available to Governmeni under the earlier Acts\nfor \ufb01ling any suit was rather on the high side, it was reduced to 30 years by the\n\npresent Act, on the recommendation of the Law Commission&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1162\">39.52. In an article published in 1980, a plea has been made&quot; to extend the\nbene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/280240/\" id=\"a_1765\">article 112</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1766\">Limitation Act</a>, to banks and other \ufb01nancial institu-\ntions, on the ground that in view of the extensive lending by the banks, several\ndebts are getting barred, and this has happened even though the banks try to\nkeep all-the security documents relating to the advances alive and enforceable. It\n\n&#x27; has been stated that the shorter loans granted by the banks, like over-drafts and\n\ncash credits, have to be renewed or rc-loaned at the expense of vast manpower,\n\njust to savejthe loans from getting barred under the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1767\">Limitation Act</a> and this .\n_ process involves considerable delay and expense.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1163\">We are afraid that the case of banking institutions can hardly be treated like\nGovernment. Unlike the Government is often faced with multiple problems\nthat require decision making at various points (somehow right upto the Cabinet\nlevel), and the process is time-consuming. This is not the position in the case of\nbanks which are run on commercial line. Secondly, if a period of 30 years is\nmade applicable to banks, similar demands for a special treatment would also be\nput forth by other large business houses, who havea network of o\ufb01ices through-\nout the country. For example, a large tea industry may claim&#x27;that it has\noffices throughout India in every village, and should be given an enlarged period\nof limitation when it comes to the question of \ufb01ling suits arising out of supply\nof tea to its retailers. It would obviosuly be impossible to extend the conces-\nsion to all other cases. -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1164\">We do not, therefore, favour acceptance of the suggestion.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1165\">;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1166\">. The Law Commissionl, in its Report\non that Act, took the view that in the light of improvement in the means of\nquick transport enabling the litigant to undertake travel to the seat of the High\nCourt, the period of ninety days was unrealistic and should be reduced to thirty\ndays. The Limitation Bill, 1962, as introduced, sought to implement this recom-\nmendation. However, when the matter came up before the Joint Committee, the\nCommittee accepted an amendment moved by an Honourable Member (Shri\nS. K. Basu) which sought to retain the period of ninety days. That is how\n<a href=\"/doc/436447/\" id=\"a_1794\">article 116 (combining the earlier <a href=\"/doc/227057/\" id=\"a_1795\">articles 156</a></a> and <a href=\"/doc/1471143/\" id=\"a_1796\"><a href=\"/doc/1471143/\" id=\"a_1797\">152</a>)</a> came to be enacted in the\npresent form.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1201\">41.6. We should reiterate the earlier recommendation of the Law Commis- Rccommendw\nsion for reduction of the period for appeal to the High Court to thirty days. tion as to\nReduction of the period is justified for the reasons given in the Report on the am\u00b0i\u00b0 H6&#x27;\nearlier Act. It may, to some extent. also help to reduce the number of appeals\nto the High Court, since a shorter period might dissuade a wavering appellant\nfrom taking a chance.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1202\">41._7. Artie&#x27;e 1 18 reads as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1001\">&quot;For leave to appear and dell,-rid it suit Ten When the summons is served.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1217\">undersummarygproecdure. &#x27; days.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1218\">This corresponds, in substance, to <a href=\"/doc/586056/\" id=\"a_1802\">article 159</a> of the Acts of 1908 and 1877.\nThey read as under:-- &#x27;\n\nTen When the summons Is served.&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1002\">&quot;For leave to appear and defend a suit\ndays.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1003\">under summary procedure referred to\nin section 128(2) (f) or under Order\nXXXVII of the same Code (<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1803\">Code of\nCivil Procedure</a>, 1908).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1004\">42.2. The article has to be read with <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1804\">Order 37, Code of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908,\n\n&#x27;which lays down the procedural law applicable to suits triable under the summary\n\nprocedure. The Courts and suits governed by the Order are enumerated in\nOrder 37, rule 1. It is enough to note that their range and ambit have been\nconsiderably widened by the amendments made in 1976 in the Code. The pro-\ncedure to be followed step-by-step is provided for iI1 Order 37, rules 2 and 3. The\nremaining rules of the Order &#x27;deal with matters of detail. &#x27; ,\n\nThe scheme laid down in Order 37, rules 2-3, for the trial of a suit under the\n\nsummary procedure (so far as is :II..terial for the present purpose) ca11 be said &#x27;\n\nto comprise several stages, of which two are important--\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1005\">(1) appearance by the defendant, and\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1006\">(ii) seeking leave to defend.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1219\">There are two distinct categori-;s&#x27;of summons envisaged by Order 37, rule 3,\ncorresponding to the two stages mentioned above:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1007\">42.3. The summons mu\napplication verifying certain facts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1223\">(2) The next--and the second most importanr------stage is the seeking by the\ndefendant of leave to defend. Under Order 37. rule 3(5), within ten days from\n\n&#x27;the service of the summons for judgment. the defendant may, by affidavit or\n\notherwise disclosing sufficient facts. apply for leave to d-cfend the suit. If leave\nis not sought or (though sought) is not granted, a decree follows. We have referred above to the provisions in the Code of Civil Proce- Proposal of\nGovernment of\n\ndure in view of the fact that we find that the Government of Maharashtra has. Maha,,shm._\n\nfor sometime, been actively considering a proposal for amending <a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1806\">article 118</a> of\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1807\">Limitation Act</a> and also the relevant provisions of <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1808\">Order 37 of the Code of\nCivil Procedure</a>, with a view to mitigating certain di\ufb01iculties that have been ex-\nperienced under the present provisions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1226\">The Government of Maharashtra has stated&#x27; that the period of limitation\nof ten days under <a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1809\">article 118</a> is found to be too short, by persons residing outside\nthe jurisdiction of the court and by the big concerns (inciuding Government\ndepartments and local authorities) who require more time to obtain, orders of\ntheir appropriate authorities. Similarly. the present procedure (in order 37.\n<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1810\">C.P.C</a>.) of \ufb01rs_t entering appearance and then obtaining leave to defend. results in\n\navoidable delay and cost to both the parties. To save the litigant from such\n\nhardship, the State Government has proposed that in a summary suit when the\nsummons is served, the defendant shouid apply for leave to defend and, for this\npurpose, the period of limitation should be extended to thirty days from the\ndate on which the summons is served on him. The proposal is to amend <a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1811\">article\n118</a> in its application to the State of l aharashtra for this purpose. after which\namendment, it is expected that the High Court will&#x27; take necessary action to\namend <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1812\">Order 37 C.P.C</a>. suitably. The High Court, it is stated, has been con-\nsulted an_d is in favour of the arnendrrent. The proposal was forwardedvby\nthe State Government to the Government of India for .&#x27;*-dministrative approval\naccording to the instructions of the Government of India regarding legislative\nproposals concerned with matters in the Concurrent List. Copies of the rele-\nvant papers were forwarded to this Commission by the Legislative Department\nof the Government of India, with a request for information as to whether any\nproposal for amendment of article MS has been considered by the Commission\nfor amendment of article l&#x27;l8 and. if so, with what result. Though there is.\nbefore us, no formal suggestion for amendment of the article. we have found the\nproposal of the Government of Maharashwa as deserving of favourable considera-\ntron.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1227\">&#x27;Papers in Government of India Lcaislative Dear: r at F14 as L -\nMaharashtra Stat &#x27; LA. Since the proposal of the Government of Maharashtra is a composite\none, involving an amendment both <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1814\">of the Code of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908 and of\n<a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1815\">article 118</a> ofthe <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1816\">Limitation Act</a>. we are not. in this Report, recommending any\namendment of <a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1817\">article 118</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1818\">Limitation Act</a> on an all-India basis. t the\n\nsame time, it&#x27;appears to us that acceptance of the proposal of State Government .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1233\">of Maharashtra for amendment of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1819\">Limitation Act</a> in its application to that\nState is ultimately likely to improve the expeditious disposal of Summary Suits,\nand our recommendation for the present is that any such proposal by a State\nGovernment for replacing the Various stages of Order 37. rule 1&#x27;. by a simpler\nmode of trial should. in principle. beviewed favourably. if the experience of\nworking of the proposed procedure in the State or States where such an amend-\nment is effected shows good results, it will be worthwhile to consider the in-\ncorporation of similar amendments on an all-India basis in the (&#x27;ode of Civil\nProcedure, I908. Order 37 and in ar\u00e9icle 118 of th: 3 imitation Act, 1963.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1234\">42.6. A verbal point concerning article llfi&#x27; any &#x27;be mentioned at this stage.\nThe \ufb01rst column of the article describes the application as one &quot;for leave to\nappear and defend a suit under summary procedure&quot;. I-Yowever, it should be\nmentioned that Order 37 rule 3, as it now stands, does not require leave for\nappearance [of the defendant. though leave is still required for defending the\nsuit. In order to ensure that this position is reflected in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1820\">Limitation Act</a>, we\nwould have considered recommending a verbal change in the first column.\nHowever, it should, at the same time, be pointed out that a number_ of time\nlimits, for taking various steps contemplated by the rules of procedure are speci-\n\ufb01ed in the rules also--Order 37, rule 3(1) to 3(5).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1235\">Somewhat elaborate amendments may be required in Order 37, Rule 3.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1236\">42.7. The question of the arbitrator \ufb01lling an award is of interest. In a mbitmto,\n\nNagpur case&#x27;, after summarising the case law, the court observed: \ufb01ling 3??-|&#x27;d\n111 00111&#x27; .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1245\">.&quot;It has been held in a series of cases that <a href=\"/doc/1762713/\" id=\"a_1835\">article 178</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1836\">Limitation Act</a>\n(of 1908) does not apply to (the) arbitrator,&quot; i.e. to the arbitrator who himself\n\ufb01les the award.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1246\">42.11. This matter was considered by the Law Commission2 in its Report Law,Commi,_\non the Act of 1908. It recommended that there should be a time limit for the si0n&#x27;s Report\narbitrator to \ufb01le the award, and that the period should be thirty days from the 3? 1tE\u00a7g_Mt\nlast date of service of notice of the making of the award on any one of the\nparties. This recommendation of the Law Commission was not, however, ac-\ncepted at the drafting stage. One of the comments3 received was that the arbi-\ntrator should have no right at all under the <a href=\"/doc/1052228/\" id=\"a_1837\">Arbitration Act</a> to \ufb01le an award in\ncourt by himself without the parties moving within 90 days or the court ordering\n&quot;him to \ufb01le the award in court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1247\">_, -Another District Judge&#x27; opposed the recommendation for curtailing the period\nof 90Adays under <a href=\"/doc/1762713/\" id=\"a_1838\">article 178.</a> Ultimately, the article emerged as quoted above.\nWe have no further suggestion to make on this point. It would seem that the\n\ufb01ling of an award by an arbitrator need not be accompained by an application&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1248\">_&#x27; 42,12. A\n\nThis article corresponds to <a href=\"/doc/1093471/\" id=\"a_1845\">articles 176</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1790137/\" id=\"a_1846\">177</a> of the 1908 Act, which read\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1257\">- as under:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1016\">Ninety The date of the death of the dc-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1017\">days. ceased plaintiff or appellant.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1018\">&quot;l76. Under the same Code to have\nthe legal representative of a de-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1019\">ceased plainti\ufb01&#x27; or of a deceased\nappellant made a party.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1020\">177. The Delhi High Court&#x27; has held that under <a href=\"/doc/721282/\" id=\"a_1847\">section 53</a> of the Land\nAcquisition&#x27; Act, 1895, the provisions of Order 9 as well as <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1848\">Order 22 of the\nCode of Civil Procedure</a>, 1908 apply to proceedings before the District Court\nunder that Act and, the District Court must bring the legal representatives on\nrecord on the death of a party to such proceedings; at the same time, the court\n\n&#x27; held that the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1849\">Limitation Act</a> would not be attracted, and the\napplication for substitution should be made within reasonable time.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1260\">42.15. On principle, a person whose land has been acquired and who claims\nmore compensation than that given by the Collector should be vigilant, and, if\nthe original owner dies during the pendency of the proceedings, it should be\nobligatory for the legal representative of the deceased to apply for being brought\non record in the same manner~--as he would do if the deceased was a plainti\ufb02 in a\n\n&#x27; regular civil suit.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1261\">To permit the legal representative to apply within a &quot;reasonable time&quot; might\nencourage undue pendency of the proceedings. However, we are not recon\ufb01-\nmending an amendment of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1850\">Limitation Act</a>, as this question has to be dealt\nwith in the special laws, rather than in the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1851\">Limitation Act</a>.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1262\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"125423980\" id=\"span_195\">We may incidentally mention here that in discussing certain other sections,\nwe have dealt with the position of Tribunals.&#x27;\n\n<a href=\"/doc/125423980/\" id=\"a_1852\">&#x27;Union of India v. Sanwalia, I.L.R.</a> (1975) Delhi 837.\n&#x27;See discussion as to <a href=\"/doc/372189/\" id=\"a_1853\">section 29</a> and <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_1854\">article 137.</a></span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1263\">8911; REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 42----<a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1855\">Articles 118</a> to 136: Applications in Specified Cases.)\n\n42-16. The third column of the article under discussion speaks about the Sta\ufb01in\ufb02\ndate of death of the plaintiff (or the appellant, defendant or the respondent), as the p\u00b0_m\nstarting point of limitation. The Supreme Court (in another context), while&#x27; inter-\npreting <a href=\"/doc/1198859/\" id=\"a_1856\">article 171</a> of the Act of 1908 (corresponding to present <a href=\"/doc/598239/\" id=\"a_1857\">article 121</a>), held&#x27;\nthat the date of the appellant&#x27;s knowledge of the death of deceased respondent\nwas irrelevant for the purposes of computation of time under that article.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1264\">However, the Judicial Commissioner, Goa has held&#x27; that the period of\nlimitation under <a href=\"/doc/1226884/\" id=\"a_1858\">article 120&#x27;</a> starts on the day on which the parties opposing the\ndeceased party acquire knowledge of the death of the deceased. When it was\nargued that the text of column 3 leaves no margin to accommodate such an\ninterpretation, the Court observed :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1025\">&quot;However, it seems to me that &#x27;if such an interpretation had been accepted,\nit would lead, to starting results in cases in which deliberately or otherwise\nthe death of a party was kept in secret by the party interested in not having\nthe heirs brought on record in time so that they might raise the defence of\nlimitation. The party who did not have the knowledge of the death would\nin such circumstances be put in serious jeopardy. This leads me to believe\nthat the interpretation placed g by Shri Usgaonkar on the passage quoted\nabove is not correct. The words &quot;the date of death of the plaintiff, appel-\nlant, defendant or respondent, as the case may be&#x27; apply to the heirs or\nlegal representatives of the plaintiff, appellant, defendant or respondent as\nthe case may be when such heirs or legal representatives have the duty of\nbeing brought on record or the duty of informing the other side about the\nfact of the death. The period of limitation under Item 120 therefore starts\non the day on which the parties opposing the deceased party acquire know-\nledge of the death of the deceased.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1265\">42.17. No other. case has come to our notice to support the interpreta-\ntion of the Judicial Commissioner, Goa, and in a sense, such an interpretation,\nwith respect, runs counter to the one placed by the Supreme Court on <a href=\"/doc/1198859/\" id=\"a_1859\">article 171</a>\n\nof the Act of 1908.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1266\">42.18. Nor does the present position really cause serious hardship. An abate- No change\nment that follows on non-substitution can be set aside under <a href=\"/doc/598239/\" id=\"a_1860\">article 121</a>, and, in re- &quot;mica-\ngard to an application under that article, <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_1861\">section 5</a> of the Act is also applicable.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1267\">Bonaiide ignorance of the death of a party is su\ufb02icient cause in this context?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1268\">42.19. <a href=\"/doc/598239/\" id=\"a_1862\">Article 121</a> reads as under ;--&quot; _ . <a href=\"/doc/598239/\" id=\"a_1863\">Article 121.</a>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1026\">&quot;Under the same Code for an order to _ Sixty The date of abatement.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1269\">set asipc an abatement. days.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1270\"><a href=\"/doc/1198859/\" id=\"a_1864\">Article 171</a> of the Act of 1908 reads as .under:---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1027\">&quot;Under <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1865\">the Code of Civil Procedure</a>, Sixty The date of abatement.&quot; I\n\n1908, for an order to set aside an aba- days.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1028\">temont.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1029\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"77627764\" id=\"span_196\">This article has not given rise to any controversy, and needs no change. A question arose in the past as to the applicability of this article to Insolvency\nan application to set aside a sale in insolvency proceedings. The Judicial Com-gggiffflfnfgj.\nmissioner, Nagpur} held that the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1916\">Order 21, Code of Civil Procedure</a>,\n\n1908 (Execution), were applicable to insolvency proceedings and the period of &#x27;\nlimitation applicable to a petition for setting aside a sale in insolvency proceedings\nmust be thirty days under <a href=\"/doc/358020/\" id=\"a_1917\">article 166</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1918\">Limitation Act</a> of 1908 (present article\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1309\">127). But the Chief Court of Punjab took a contrary view,&#x27; and held that <a href=\"/doc/358020/\" id=\"a_1919\">article\n166 (Act of 1908)</a> was not applicable to such an application, as it was not an\napplication to set aside a sale in &quot;execution of a decree&quot; and that an application\nto set aside a sale that had been conducted by the court in realising the assets&#x27;\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India. 3rd Report (Limitation Act, 1908), page 88, <a href=\"/doc/9107/\" id=\"a_1920\">article 49.</a>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1079\">&quot;<a href=\"/doc/327628/\" id=\"a_1921\">Section 98</a>, Code of Civil Procedure (Amendment) Act, 1976 (104 of 1976&#x27;).\n3Balaii v; Gopal Mali, A.I.R. 1927 Ng. 262. ~\n\n&#x27;Afzal Ali v. Aman Ali. A.I.R. 1914 Lab. 209-\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_200\">218</span>\n\nParties to the\napplication-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1080\">joInder_ of.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1081\">Fraud-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1082\">antecedent or\nsubsequent.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1083\">Effect of\nfraud of\nalone on\ndecree holder\nauction-pun\nchaser alone.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1084\">89TH REPORT 01-&#x27; LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 42\u00bb <a href=\"/doc/1670439/\" id=\"a_1922\">Articles 118</a> to 136: Applications in speci\ufb01ed cases)\n\nof the insolvent was governed by <a href=\"/doc/1865420/\" id=\"a_1923\">article 181</a> of the Limitation Act, 1908. These\ntwo are comparatively old cases, and since then, the controversy does not appear\n\nto have come on the surface during the last \ufb01fty years. Hence no explanatory\namendment on this _count appears to be needed.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1085\">42.32. Another controversy is concerned with the joinder of parties in an\napplication under 0.21, Rule 92, <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1924\">C.P.C</a>. The Patna High Court has held1 that the\nauction-purchaser is a necessary party to an application under Order 21, Rule 92,\nto set aside an execution sale and where he is not made a party within the time\nallowed, the application cannot be entertained. A contrary view has been taken\nby Rajasthan High Court, holding&#x27;-&#x27; that it is not_necessary to mention the name\nof the party in the heading of the application and if any party is left out, it is\nthe duty of the Court to give notice under the proviso to 0.21, R.92(2) <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1925\">C.P.C</a>.\nand the application would not be barred by limitation. Since the passing. of the\nLimitation Act in 1963, this article has not given rise to any controversy and the\nabove solitary con\ufb02ict need not detain us.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1086\">I\n\n42.33. Then, there is the question of fraud, be antecedent and subsequent. In\none case,&#x27; the Kerala High Court has observed that on principle, if the fraud\nantecedent to the sale was of such a nature as to sul\ufb01ce for the requirements of\n<a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_1926\">section 18</a> (Lim&#x27;itation_Act, 1908), such fraud could not be dismissed from consi-\nderation as having had its origin earlier. However. the Patna High Court&#x27; has\nheld that the petitioner must satisfy the court that he had been kept from the\nknowledge of his right to \ufb01le an application and that his right to set aside the\nsale occurred after the sale. The Court further observed that fraud prepetrated\nby the opposite party must be a fraud committed after the sale, and not a\nfraud committed in bringing about the sale. In this context, <a href=\"/doc/1968689/\" id=\"a_1927\">section 17</a> of the\nLimitation Act, 1963 doesinot differentiate between antecedent fraud and subse-\nquent fraud. The limitation runs from the time the applicant has discovered\nthe fraud or could, with reasonable diligence, have discovered it. In the absence\nof further case law on the point, we would leave the matter at that.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1087\">\\\n\n42.34. A question has arisen whether the fraud of the decree-holder alone or\nof the auction-purchaser alone would be su\ufb02icient to extend the period of limita-\ntion. The Mysore High Court holds5 that <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_1928\">section 18</a> (of the Act of 1908), could\nbe attracted where the fraud in question was practised either by the decree-holder\nor by the auctio.n~purchaser. In a Calcutta case,&#x27; it was held that a judgment\ndebtor was not entitled to the bene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_1929\">section 18</a> for the purpose of making an\napplication under 0.2]. R.90, <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1930\">C.P.C</a>. to set aside an execution sale, if the decree-\nholder was not a party to the fraud alleged. In an Allahabad Full Bench case&#x27;,\nthe view taken is that fraud of the decree-holder su\ufb02ices and that it is immaterial\nwhether the decree-holder alone is guilty of the fraud. or whether the auction-\npurchaser was also a party to the fraud.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1088\">K.S. Hegde J. (in the Mysore case&quot; referred to above) has taken stock of the\njudicial opinion on the subject and concluded as under:--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1089\">1Sumitra Kaur v. Much may\ndepend on the manner in which the parties are arrayed and other relevant facts.\nIn view of this, We do not recommend any amendment on this point.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1311\">p\\/42.35. However, we should refer to a connected provision in the Code of F_.ccommenda-\nCivil Procedure, 1908, which seems to need amendment. Article 127 of the :{_&#x27;&quot;9\u00a3;)t\u00b0 0&#x27;21&#x27;\nLimitation Act is concerned with applications, in regard to which 0.21, R.92(2) of C.P.C.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1312\">the Code is relevant. The period of limitation for an application under <a href=\"/doc/1688766/\" id=\"a_1937\">article\n127</a> is sixty days, after its amendment1 in 1976. The application is to be accom-\npanied by a deposit. The period for making the required deposit under 0.21,\nR.92. <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1938\">C.P.C</a>. is, however, still thirty days. This disharmony between the two\nstatutory provisions should be removed. We may point out that the disharmony\nhas been adverted to in a recent judgment of the Kerala High Court&quot; also. To\nremove this discrepancy, .we recommend that 0.21, R.92(2) <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1939\">of the Code of Civil\nProcedure</a>, 1908, should be suitably amended by increasing the period from 30\ndays to 60 daysi.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1313\">42.36. There is some debate in the case law about the starting point in regard\n9\u00b0&quot;! \u00b0f to fresh obstructions. The Madras High Court has held that an application&#x27;\n\nlimitation.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1335\">&#x27; for the removal of a second obstruction, mademore than thirty days after aoquiele\ncence in a previous obstruction, was not barred by <a href=\"/doc/1091985/\" id=\"a_1955\">article 167 (of the Act of 1908)</a>\nand such&#x27; acquiescence did not deprive the person entitled to possession of any\nfurther right to obtain execution. The Calcutta High Court held&quot; that the auction-\n; purchaser was not limited to one application under 0.12, Rule 95, <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1956\">C.P.C</a>. and the\nperiod of 30 days prescribed by <a href=\"/doc/1091985/\" id=\"a_1957\">article 167 (Act of 1908)</a> was to be counted from\nthe date of the resistance or obstruction in respect of which the complaint was\nmade. However, the Bombay High Court has held? that the time limit for a\nsubsequent application in respect of an obstruction by the same person would\ncount from the earlier obstruction. -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1336\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"195113509\" id=\"span_206\">42.40. The Bombay view has been expressly dissented from in a <a href=\"/doc/195113509/\" id=\"a_1958\">Gujarat</a>\ncase,&#x27; on the ground that though the Bombay ruling was a Full Bench decision the\nobservations in that ruling were obiter. The Gujarat HighACourt held that since\nthe law allows the decree-holder to make a second application for execution and\nto complain about the obstruction within 30 days from the date of resistance or\nobstruction, it was immaterial whether the decree-holder came, or failed to come,\nto court within 30 days of the date of the first obstruction.</span> In view of the fact\nthat _the observations in the Bombay case were obiter, we make no recommenda-\ntion regarding any change therein.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1337\">42.41. This takes us to <a href=\"/doc/1822128/\" id=\"a_1959\">article 130</a> which reads as under :~\n\narticle 130-\n\n\ufb01:f,f&#x27;mm&quot;da- &#x27; &quot;130.,For leave to appeal as a pauper----\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1338\">(a) to the High Court Sixty The date of decree appealed from.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1339\">days.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1340\">(b) to any other court. &quot;,I&#x27;l&amp;irty The date of decree appealed from.&quot; -.5\nays. ,\n\n&#x27;Mayappan Cherry V.
