For leave to appeal asa pauper. Thirty The date of the decree appealed\ndays. against. V &#x27;\n\n2162. For leave to appealas a pauper. Ninety The date of the decree appealed\ndays. against.&quot; -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1345\">42.42. Prior to 1963, a uniform period of limitation of 30 days was prescribed P(&#x27;)&#x27;:i&#x27;t1i%?]3\nfor an application for &#x27;leave to appeal as a pauper. The present article provides &#x27; &#x27;\nfor two different periods, namely, 60 days and 30 days. The change was made\n\nat the committee stage.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1346\">42.43. We are of the view thatjthe period prescribed for such leave should R\u00a2\u00b0\u00b0mm\u00a2nd3-\n\nbe the same as that prescribed for the appeal, and that this principle should be \u00a7{2\u00a7cf\u00a7 :30\n\nvappliedto all courts. Such a procedure should, we believe. work smoothly. If\n\nultimately the leave to appeal as an indigent person is granted. the application\n\ncan be converted into an appeal. If such leave is not granted. the court would\ngenerally grant time for the payment of court fees and (where the limitation\n\nperiod for appeal has already expired), allow condonation of delay under section\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1347\">5. In either case, a period identical with the period prescribed for the appeal\n\nitself would cause no inconvenience.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1348\">Accordingly, we recommend that <a href=\"/doc/1822128/\" id=\"a_1964\">article 130</a> should be revised as under:---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1102\">&quot;l30. For leave to appeal as an indi- The same period as is prescribed The date\n\ngent person to any court. &#x27; for the appeal in respect ofwhich of decree\n&#x27; &#x27; the leave is sought. appealed\nfrom.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1349\">42.44. This takes us to <a href=\"/doc/786824/\" id=\"a_1965\">article 131.</a> It reads as under :-- &#x27; <a href=\"/doc/786824/\" id=\"a_1966\">Article 131&#x27;.</a>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1103\">&quot;To any court for me exercise of its Ninety The date of the (.&#x27;lLCI\u20ac\u20ac or order or\n\nwers of revision under the Code of days. sentence sought to be revised.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1350\">Civil Procedure, 1908, or the <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_1967\">Code of\nCriminal Procedure</a>, 1898.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1351\">There was no corresponding provision in the earlier enactments. though the\npractice of the High Courts generally laid down a time limit of 90 days.&#x27; The\nLaw Commission, in its Report&#x27; on the Act of 1908. recommended the incorpora-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1352\">tion of a new provision for making an application to any court for the exercise\n\nof its powers, of revision under <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_1968\">the C.P.C</a>. or <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_1969\">Cr.P.C</a>. and recommended a period\nof limitation of 30 days. The Joint Committee, in its Report, however. observed\nthat the period of thirty days was too short and recommended that it should be\nninety days. That is how the article came to be _enacted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1353\">42.45. A question concerning e starting point under <a href=\"/doc/786824/\" id=\"a_1970\">article 131</a> needs atten- case, 13w 55\ntion. In framing this article, it was presumed that the aggrieved person would tiiitf\ufb02i\ufb02\ufb01\nbe so connected with the proceedings affecting him that the date of the decree &#x27; A\nor order or sentence passed in the proceeding will be known to him immediately,\nbecause _he would (in all probability) be present in court at the relevant time, or\n\nwill be noti\ufb01ed by his counsel about the order affecting him. But this is not\n\n&#x27;Law, Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act, 1908), page 89. suggested\n<a href=\"/doc/950729/\" id=\"a_1971\">article 57.</a>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1354\">222 89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION Aer, 1963\n\n(Chapter 42;--Articles 1/8 to 136: Applications in speci\ufb01ed cases.)\n\nalways the case in reality. A Gujarat caseillustrates the matter.&#x27; An auto rick-\nshaw was seized as a conveyance used for theft. The rickshaw was the subject\nmatter of hire purchase. Neither the eal owner of the auto rickshaw, nor the\none to whom it wasgiven on hire purc%se, was directly connected with the case\n\n&#x27; relating to theft. A revision was preferred by one Balamal against the order of\nseizure of the auto rickshaw. It was discovered that if <a href=\"/doc/786824/\" id=\"a_1972\">article 131</a> was to be ,\ntaken literally, the revision would be barred by time, as being beyond ninety\ndays of the date on which the order of seizure was passed. &#x27;\n\n.Shelat J. (as he then was), realising the hardship that would arise if column\n\n3 of the article were given a literal meaning, relied upon the observations of the\n\nSupreme Court in an earlier case&quot; where the expression &quot;the date of the award&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1104\">in <a href=\"/doc/1920288/\" id=\"a_1973\">section 18(2)</a> of the Land Acquisition Act, 1894, was construed as requiring\n\neither actual or constructive knowledge of the order. Such knowledge was an\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1105\">-- essential requirement for fair play and natural justice. The Supreme Court had.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1106\">in that case, refused to put a literal or mechanical constru&#x27;ction on the expression\n&quot;from the date of the Collector&#x27;s award&quot;, need in the proviso to <a href=\"/doc/85586/\" id=\"a_1974\">section 18</a>. On -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1107\">the basis of this reasoning, Shelat, J. held that it would be reasonable and fair\n\nthat the period of ninety days (in <a href=\"/doc/786824/\" id=\"a_1975\">article 131</a>) should also run from the date of\n\n&#x27; the knowledge of the order, and not from the date of the order.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1108\">Igecommerrdw 42.46. With a view to preventing recurrence of the injustice which may re-\n&quot;\u00b0&quot;- &#x27; suit from a mechanical construction being placed on the language occurring in\n\ncolumn 3 of <a href=\"/doc/786824/\" id=\"a_1976\">article 131</a>, we recommend that column 3 of the article should be\nrevised as follows:---- 1 &#x27;\n\nI &quot;When thelapplicant had knowledge of the decree or order or sentence sought &#x27;-\nto be revised.&quot; A \u00bb\n\nIn the first column of the article, the reference to the Code of Crimin\nProcedure, 1898, should be replaced by a reference to the present Code of 1973.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1109\"><a href=\"/doc/783690/\" id=\"a_1977\">Article 132.</a> 42.47. <a href=\"/doc/783690/\" id=\"a_1978\">Article 132</a> reads as under :-- , &#x27;\n\n&quot;l32. The Law Commission, in its Report on the Act of 1908 observed,&#x27; l_-51W Eomfrliir\nthat new provisions prescribing periods of limitation were necessary for making &quot;on em &#x27;\napplications to the High Court for a certi\ufb01cate of \ufb01tness to appeal to the Supreme\nCourt. <a href=\"/doc/139447/\" id=\"a_1988\">Article 179</a> of the Limitation Act, 1908, as it then stood, provided a\nperiod of 90 days for an application under the Code of Civil&quot; Procedure and did\nnot prescribe a period of limitation for other applications. _The Law Commission\nrecommended that a comprehensive provisions should be made as to the limita-\ntion for applications to the High Court for a certi\ufb01cate of \ufb01tness &#x27;for appeal to\nthe Supreme Court, and that, for all such applications a period of 30 days might\nbe prescribed. This newly added provision has not given rise to any controversy. Recommenda-\nHowever, in view of the amendments that have been made in the CoI1stitution&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot;&#x27;\nit would be proper to revise the wording of <a href=\"/doc/783690/\" id=\"a_1989\">article 132</a> of the &#x27;<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1990\">Limitation Act</a> so\nas to make it conform to the changed constitutional phraseology.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1362\">Accordingly, we recommend a redrait of the first column of <a href=\"/doc/1471143/\" id=\"a_1991\">article 152</a> as\n\nI\nArticie 132, First Column (Revised)\n\n_ &quot;To the High Court for a certi\ufb01cate of the nature referred to in clause\n(1) of <a href=\"/doc/783690/\" id=\"a_1992\">article 132</a>, clause (1) of <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_1993\">article 133</a> or sub-clause (c) of clause (1) of\n<a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_1994\">article 134</a> of the Constitution, read with <a href=\"/doc/952725/\" id=\"a_1995\">article 134A</a> thereof, or under any\nother law for the time being in force.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1363\">42.50. This takes us to <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_1996\">article 133</a>, which reads as under :-- 1 <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_1997\">Article 133-</a>\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1117\">&quot;To the Supreme Court for special leave to appeal&#x27;----\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1118\">(a) ina case involving death sen- Sixty The date of the judgment, \ufb01nal or-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1364\">tenee. days. der or sentence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1365\">(b) in a case where leave to appeal Sixty The date of the order of refusal.\nwas refused by the High days. &#x27; ~\nCourt;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1366\">((2) in any other case. The Law Commission had, in its Report&quot; on the Act of 1908, recom- Report of\nmended a period of limitation of 30 days in respect of applications to the Supreme t(1\u00a7\u00a7mL,:}:si\u00b0n_\nCourt for special leave in a case involving death sentence. The Joint Committee, &quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1119\">however, felt that the period was too short, and that it should be sixty days.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1120\">42.52. With reference to this article, a suggestion of the Supreme Court has Suggestion of\n\nbeen forwarded to the Law Commission by the Legislative Department, being a g&#x27;\u00a7u\u00a7t&quot;&quot;&quot;&#x27;&quot;&#x27;\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Liinitation Act, 1908), page 64, para 169.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1121\">&#x27;Articles 132, 133, 134 and 134A of the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1122\">&#x27;Law Commission on India, 3rd Report {<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_1998\">Limitation Act</a>. 1908), pages 90, 168-169, article\nS9(a). _ \u00bb\n\n\n\n224 89TH REPORT or LAW COMNlN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter &#x27;42--~Articles 118 to 136: Applications in speci\ufb01ed eases.)\n\nsuggestion made by the Registrar of the Supreme Court&#x27; for amendn&#x27;1ent&#x27;of&#x27;the\narticle in regard to cases where a party against whom a judgment or order has\nbeen pronounced by the High Court applies orally for a certi\ufb01cate of \ufb01tness to\nappeal. The di\ufb02iculty felt in regard to this article may be thus stated in brief.\nIn some cases, in the High Court a request for a certi\ufb01cate of \ufb01tness for appeal\nto the Supreme Court is made by counsel orally. When this oral request is refus-\ned, the question as to whether a petition for special leave to appeal to the Sup-\nreme Court can be \ufb01led in the Supreme Court within 60 days---clause (b) of <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_1999\">article\n133</a> or within 90 days-clause (c) of <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2000\">article 133</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2001\">Limitation Act</a>--has arisen.\nUnder <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2002\">article 133(b)</a>, which deals with the cases of refusal of leave to appeal by\nthe High Court, the period of limitation is 60 days. Under article l33(c), which\napplies &quot;in any other case&quot;, the period of limitation is 90 days. The period of\n60 days mentioned in <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2003\">article 133</a>, clause (b) is to be counted from the date of\nthe order of refusal (by the High Court), while the period of 90 days mentioned\nin <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2004\">article 133</a>, clause (c), is to be counted from the date of the judgment or order\nof the High Court. Once the High Court refuses leave (the correct expression is\n&quot;certi\ufb01cate&quot;), clause (b) would presumably apply and the proceeding canbe &quot;\ufb01led.\nin the Supreme Court only within 60 days from the date of refusal of the certi\ufb01-\n_cate. But, by adopting this course, the litigant is deprived of the bene\ufb01t of the\nlonger period of 90 days, which would, under article l33(c), have been available\nto him, if he had come directly to the Supreme Court for special leave. No doubt,\nthe Supreme Court can condone the delay under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_2005\">section 5</a> of the Limitation Act\nin such cases, but in order to avoid unnecessary applications under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_2006\">section 5</a>, it\nhas been suggested that it is better to provide that in such contingencies the peti- V\ntioner shall have the bene\ufb01t of limitation whichever is more bene\ufb01cial to \nThat means that where the date of order refusing a certi\ufb01cate and the date of\nthe judgment appealed from are the same, the petition for special leave to appeal\nto the Supreme Court may be \ufb01led within 90 days. The Registrar of the Supreme\nCourt has requested the Government to take appropriate steps for suitably amend-\ning the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2007\">Limitation Act</a> for bringing out above position.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1123\">- Cons_tituiio_I-Ia1- 42.53. We have carefully considered this suggestion, and are in broad agree\u00bb\n\u00a3;&#x27;:\u00e9\u00a3&quot;ma5 &#x27;\u00b0 ment with it. Before we make our concrete recommendations in this behalf, it\n\nSupreme Court. may be convenient to set out brie\ufb02y the constitutional position. Under <a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_2008\">article\n136</a> of the Constitution, the Supreme Court may, in its discretion, grant special\nleave to appeal from any, judgment, decree, determination, sentence or order! in\nany cause or matter, passed or made by any court or tribunal in the territory of\nIndia--with the exception of a court or tribunal constituted by or under any law\n\n_ relating to the armed forces. In regard to judgments of High Courts, an appeal\n\n&#x27; to the Supreme Court lies under <a href=\"/doc/34776269/\" id=\"a_2009\">article 132(1)</a><a href=\"/doc/1011888/\" id=\"a_2010\">, 133(1)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1050900/\" id=\"a_2011\">134(1)</a> of the Constitu-\ntion in the speci\ufb01ed cases on a speci\ufb01ed&#x27; ground, where the High Court gives the\nrequisite certi\ufb01cate under <a href=\"/doc/952725/\" id=\"a_2012\">article 134A</a> of the Constitution. In certain criminal\n\ncases involving a sentence of death, an appeal lies to the Supreme Court under. -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1124\"><a href=\"/doc/63842383/\" id=\"a_2013\">article 134(1) (a)</a> and (b). even without a certi\ufb01cate. Further, <a href=\"/doc/775776/\" id=\"a_2014\">article 134(2)</a> and\narticle 135_ deal with appellate jurisdiction conferred on the Supreme Court by\nParliament by legislation and appellate jurisdiction in any matter exercisable\nby the Supreme Court inherited from the Federal Court, respectively. But we\nare concerned only with appeals where a certi\ufb01cate can be applied for.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1125\">Position 42.54.: Now, while appeals from courts other than the High Court to the\nL.n&quot;&#x27;i&#x27;mi\u00b0n_ Supreme Court under <a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_2015\">article 136</a> would, as regards the position for special leave\n\n\ufb01led in the Supreme Court, be governed by <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2016\">article 133(0)</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2017\">Limitation Act</a>,-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1126\">&#x27;Letter from the Registrar (Judicial). Supreme Court of India to the Secretary.&#x27;\nGovernment, Ministry of Law. Justice &amp; Company Affairs dated 28th January, -T9u.,\\\ncopy forwarded to the Law Commission by the Legislative Department under its O.M\u00a7&quot;\\\nNo. 11 (14)/82/Leg.\\II dated 24th August, 1982.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1127\">&#x27;  REPORT OF LAW COMMN. OF INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 42~Articles 118 to 136: Applications in speci\ufb01ed cases.)\n\nor in the rare case of death sentence imposed by some special court under statute\nbyarticle l33(a) of <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2018\">Limitation Act</a>--applications for leave to appeal from judg-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1128\">-ments and orders of the High Court by special leave can fall either under article\nl33(b) of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2019\">Limitation Act</a> or under article l33(c) of that Act.&quot; If the certi\ufb01cate\nis applied for in the High Court and refused, clause (b) becomes applicable and\nonly 60 days, it seems, would be available from the date of the order of refusal,\nwhile, if a direct application had been made to the Supreme Court, clause (c)\nwould apply, giving a period of 90 days. This 90 days period is counted from\nthe date of the judgment or order. Where the date of the order of refusal is\nmuch later than the date of the judgmentior order of the High Court, the litigant\nmay stand to gain by relying on clause (b), but where the two are simultaneous,\nhe would stand to lose if clause (b) is applied. This is the anomaly which is\nsought to be removed by the suggestion made by the Registrar of the Supreme\nCourt<a href=\"/doc/214713/\" id=\"a_2020\">, <a href=\"/doc/214713/\" id=\"a_2021\">referred to above</a></a>. As far as we could understand, it is not the intention\nJllat clause (c) of <a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2022\">article 133</a> should be disturbed, the only change desired is to\nensure that the litigant whose application in the High Court for certi\ufb01cate is re-\nfused should get 90 days from the date of the judgment. if the judgment.of the\nHigh Court and the order of refusal are simultaneous. We do not see any objec-\ntion to. the. acceptance of this suggestion and are recommending a re-draft of\n<a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2023\">article 133</a> to achieve the object. &#x27; ~\n\n42.55. It should be mentioned at this stage, that under <a href=\"/doc/952725/\" id=\"a_2024\">article 134A</a> of the 3&#x27;-_&quot;5\u00b01\u00b0 1345-\nConstitution, every High Court passing or making a judgment etc. (appealable on Constitution,\ncerti\ufb01cate)----\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1129\">&quot;(a) may, if it deems \ufb01t so to do, on its own motion; and\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1130\">(b) shall, if an oral application is made, by or on behalf of the party agg~\nrieved. immediately after passing or making of such judgment, decree,\n\ufb01nal order or sentence,&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1367\">determine, &quot;as soon as may be after such passing or making&quot;, the question whe-\nther the certi\ufb01cate in question should be given in respect of that case. Thus, the\nConstitution contemplates that the grant or refusal of certi\ufb01cate shall be &quot;as/soon\nas may be&quot; after-the passing of the judgment etc.. It can be presumed that in\nmost cases the grant or refusal would be simultaneous or at least within a very\nshort time. Hence. the amendment which we areicontemplating would be of\nsome practical utility. &#x27;\n\nI 42.56. Accordingly, we recommend that article l33.of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2025\">Limitation Act</a> Igecommenda-\nshould be revised as under: &#x27; &quot;\u00b0&quot;-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1131\">&quot;I33. To the Supreme Court for special leave to appeal.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1132\">(a) in a case involving death Sixty The date of the judgment, \ufb01nal\nsentence; days. order or sentence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1133\">(b) in a case where a certificate Sixty The date of the order of refusal.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1368\">of the nature referred to in days.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1369\"><a href=\"/doc/952725/\" id=\"a_2026\">article 134A</a> of the Consti-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1370\">tution was refused by the High\nCourt and the Order of refusal\nwas passed on a date later\n\n.than&#x27; the judgment or order\nin respect of which such\nleave was applied for.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_211\">226</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1371\"><a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_2027\">Article 134.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1372\">Reduction\nof period\nin 1963.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1373\">No change\nneeded.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1374\"><a href=\"/doc/137639/\" id=\"a_2028\">Article 135.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1375\">39TH REPORT or LAW. The period of Limitation (provided in <a href=\"/doc/1900887/\" id=\"a_2031\">article 180</a> of the Act of 1908)\nhas been reduced in 1963 to one year, upon the recommendation of the Law\nCommission.&#x27; The Statement of Objects and Reasons annexed to the Bill states&#x27;\nthe reasons as under: &#x27;\n\n&quot;As existing <a href=\"/doc/354660/\" id=\"a_2032\">article 182</a> is being omitted, <a href=\"/doc/1900887/\" id=\"a_2033\">article 180</a> A (which the proposed\n<a href=\"/doc/1325025/\" id=\"a_2034\">article 133 (now <a href=\"/doc/1446285/\" id=\"a_2035\">article 134</a>)</a> seeks to replace, will apply to all purchasers in\nexecution whether decree-holder or not. No change is, therefore, needed in the article.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1388\">42.59. <a href=\"/doc/137639/\" id=\"a_2037\">Article 135</a> reads as under :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1135\">&#x27;.&#x27;For the enforcement of a decree Three The date of the decree or where a\ngranting a mandatory injunction. years. date is \ufb01xed for performance,\n, -- such date.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1136\">There was no corresponding provision in the earlier enactments. The Law&#x27;\n\nCommission in its Report on the earlier.Act, recommended a period of three\n\nyears in the case of mandatory injunctions.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1137\">Accordingly, this provi\ufb01on came to be enacted in 1963.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1138\">42.60. The provision has not given rise to any controversy, and hence no\nchange is necessary. It may be mentioned that applications for such relief are\nexcluded from general article as to execution--article 13&#x27;. &#x27;\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act of 1908) page 67, para 180.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1389\">2Statement of Objects and Reasons, Limitation Bill, 1962. For the execution of any decree Twelve Where the decree or order becomes\n(other than a decree granting a years. enforceable or where the decree or\nmandatory injunction) or order any subsequent order directs any\nof any civil court. payment of money or the deli-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1391\">very of any property to be made\nat a certain date or at recurring\npeI&#x27;ic-ds, when default in making\n._the payment or delivery in respect\nor which execution is sought,\ntakes place, provided that an\napplication for the enforcement. or \\\nexecution of a decree granting\na pcrpettral injtinction shall not be\nsubject to any period of limita-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1392\">tion.&quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1393\">Corresponding provisions in the Act of 1908 were contained in <a href=\"/doc/354660/\" id=\"a_2039\">articles 182</a>\nand <a href=\"/doc/481936/\" id=\"a_2040\">183</a>, which were much more elaborate. The Law Commission. in its Report&#x27; . ,\nsion s Report.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1397\">under, with reference to <a href=\"/doc/354660/\" id=\"a_2046\">article 182 (applications for executions):</a>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1139\">&quot;l70. <a href=\"/doc/354660/\" id=\"a_2047\">Article 182</a> has been a very fruitful source of litigation and is a wea-\npon in the hands of both the dishonest decree-holder and the dishonest judg-\nment debtor. It has given rise to innumerable decisions. The commentary_\nin Rustomji&#x27;s Limitation Act (5th Edn.) on this article itself covers nearly\n200 pages. In our opinion the maximum period of limitation for the execu-\ntion of a decree or order of any civil court should be 12 years from the date\nwhen the decree. or order became enforceable (which is usually the date of\nthe decree) or where the decree or subsequent order directs any\npayment of :money or the delivery of any property to be made at a\ncertain date &quot;or at recurring periods, the date&#x27; of the default in making\nthe payment or delivery in respect of which the applicant seeks to execute\nthe decree. Tire is, therefore, no need for a provision compelling the\ndecree-holder to keep the decree alive by making an application every three\nyears. There exists a provision already in section 48 of the Civil Procedure\nCode that a decree ceases to be enforceable after a period of 12 years. In\nEngland also, the time \ufb01xed for enforcing a judgment is 12 years. Either\n&#x27; the decree-holder succeeds in realising his decree within this period or he\nfails and there should be no provision enabling-the execution of a decree\n&#x27; after that period. To this provision an exception will have to be made to\nthe e\ufb02&quot;ect&#x27;-- that the court may order the execution of a decree upon an appli-\ncation presented after the expiration of the period of 12 years. where the\njudgment-debtor has, by fraud or force,/ prevented the execution of the decree\nat some time within the twelve years immediately preceding the date of the\napplication. Section 48 of the Civil Procedure Code may be&#x27; deleted and its\nprovisions may be incorporated in this Act. <a href=\"/doc/481936/\" id=\"a_2048\">Article 183</a> should be deleted...&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1398\">In pursuance of the aforesaid recommendation, the present article has\nenacted in place of <a href=\"/doc/354660/\" id=\"a_2049\">articles 182</a> and <a href=\"/doc/481936/\" id=\"a_2050\">183</a> of the 1908 Act. <a href=\"/doc/102957838/\" id=\"a_2051\">Section 48</a>, Code of\nCivil Procedure 1908 has been repealed;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1399\">&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report, i(Limitation Act, 1908), pages 64-65, para 170. /\n31--14 M of LJ&amp;.CA[ND/83\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_212\">227</span>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_213\">228</span>\n\nNo change\nneeded.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1400\"><a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2052\">Article 137.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1401\">Scope of the\narticle.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1402\">Scope of\n<a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2053\">article 137-</a>\nspecial laws.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1403\">. &quot;60. Other applications for which no &#x27; Three\n\n89rH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 42~/lrticies /18 to 136: Applications in speci\ufb01ed cases.) Chapter \nOther Applications) &#x27;\n\n(Chapter. 43--<a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2054\">Article 137 :</a> Other Applications)\n\n42.63. There is extensive case law on the corresponding articles of the earlier\nAct; and we have examined a large number of those cases. We \ufb01nd that most\nof the controversies that once arose now do not survive, the matter having been\neither settled by subsequent Supreme Court decisions or clari\ufb01ed or rendered\nobsolete by the changes made by the Act of 1963. Hence, no change is needed\nin the article under consideration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1404\">CHAPTER 43\n<a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2055\">ARTICLE 137 :</a> OTHER APPLrCATId\u00a71s\n\n43.1. <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2056\">Article 137</a> reads as under-.--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1140\">&quot;I37. Any other application for which Three\nno period of limitation is provided year s.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1141\">elsewhere in this Division.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1142\">When the right to apply accrues.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1405\">This article corresponds to <a href=\"/doc/1762713/\" id=\"a_2057\">article 178</a> of Act 15 of 1877 and <a href=\"/doc/1865420/\" id=\"a_2058\">article 181</a> of\nthe Act of 1908. The latter article was as follows:--~\n&quot;l8l. Applicationsfor which no period Three When the right to apply accrues.&quot;\nof limitation is provided else- years.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1406\">where in this schedule or by sec-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1407\">tion 48 of the Code of Civil Pr&#x27;o-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1408\">cedure, 1908 (5 of 1908).&#x27;\n\n43.2. The recommendation of the Law Commission of India, in its Report\non the Act of 1908,&#x27; was to have a residuary article for applications (including\nprohibitions) as in thecase of suits. The period was to be the same as in the\nAct of 1908, namely three years from the date when the right to apply accrues.\nBut the description of the applications appearing in the \ufb01rst column, as recom-\nmended by the Law Commission was in a wider language than the Act of 1908.\nThe matter will be clear from the following extract from the Annexure to the\nReport of the Law Commission, which showed in a concrete form its recom-\nmendation on the subject :-- &#x27;\n\nWhen the right to apply accrues.&quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1409\">period of limitation is provided years. .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1410\">by any &#x27;zaw for the time being in\n\nforce.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1411\">Earlier, _by that Report, while dealing with the ambit of the word &quot;applica-\ntion&quot; (which was not de\ufb01ned in the Act of 1908), the Law Commission had made&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1143\">the following recommendations: A &#x27;\n\n&quot;We recommend that a new de\ufb01nition of the word &#x27;application&#x27; as to include\nany petition original or otherwise, should be added. The object is to provide\na period of limitation for original petitions and applications under special\nlaws, as there is no such provision now. Consequential alterations in the\nde\ufb01nition of the word &#x27;applicant&#x27; should also be made.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1412\">43.3. <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2059\">The Act</a> of 1963, as enacted, however, .does not carry out fully the\nthe recommendation made by the Law Commission in the above mentioned Re-\nport, as regards the residuary articles. A controversy therefore arose whether\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act, 1908), page _67, para 181 and\npage 90, article 60. &#x27;\n\n&#x27;Law Commission of India, 3rd Report (Limitation Act 1908) page 5, para 9 and page\n90, <a href=\"/doc/1102405/\" id=\"a_2060\">article 60.</a>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1413\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. or INDIA ON THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 43--ArticIe 137 : Other Applications)\n\nthe new article was intended for applications under special laws&#x27;. It was in 1977\nthat the controversy was resolvedz, by a decision of the Supreme Court. &#x27;1 his\ndecision of 1977 has practically overruled as much of an earlier decision of the\nSupreme Court as had held that the article did not apply to applications under\nspecial laws?-&#x27; However, that part of the earlier decision of the Supreme Court\nwhich had held that <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2061\">article 137</a> does not apply to bodies other than courts seems\nstill to hold the \ufb01eld?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1414\">Courts and other bodies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1415\">43.4. It is now necessary to refer to another aspect of the article under dis-\ncussion. It is connected with the distinction between courts and tribunals. By\nway of analogy, it is of interest to refer to the text of <a href=\"/doc/1331149/\" id=\"a_2062\">article 227</a> of the Constitution,\n<a href=\"/doc/1331149/\" id=\"a_2063\">Article 227</a>, as amended by the Forty-second Amendment, reads:--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1144\">&quot;Every High Court shall have superintendence over all courts subject to its appellate\njurisdiction.&quot; \\\n\nEarlier, <a href=\"/doc/1331149/\" id=\"a_2064\">article 227</a> was in these terms : ---\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1145\">&quot;Every High Court shall have superintendence over all courts and tribunals throughout the\nterritories in relation to which it exercises )LlLi5diCti0I!.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1416\">43.5. The effect of the deletion of the word &quot;tribunals&quot; came up in <a href=\"/doc/875627/\" id=\"a_2065\">article\n229</a> for consideration before a Full Bench of Bombay High Court&#x27;. The Court\nobserved:--~ &#x27;\n\n&quot;In the first place, neither the expression &#x27;court&#x27; nor the expression &#x27;tribunal&#x27;\nhas been de\ufb01ned in the Constitution and therefore, the dictionary meaning\nor the normal connotation of these expressions will have to be considered.\nSecondly, the proposition is,well-settled that all &quot;courts are tribunals. but all\ntribunals are not courts. If necessary, reference may be made to Justice\nHidayatullah&#x27;s judgment in Harinagar Sugar Millsiv. Shyam Sunder&#x27; where\nthe above proposition has been clearly stated; the proposition in other words\nmeans that some tribunals would be basically courts, i.e, courts in their\nnormal connotation, while some others would not be courts and therefore it\ncannot be said that Parliament wanted to exclude all tribunals from the pur-\nview of the High Court&#x27;s superintendence under the amended Article 227.\nbut it can be said that Parliament intended to exclude only such tribunals\nas are not basically courts from the High Court&#x27;s superintendence.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1417\">43.6. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"766774\" id=\"span_214\">In <a href=\"/doc/766774/\" id=\"a_2066\">M. Joshi v. Life Insurance Corporation of India3 the Suprme</a> Court\nheld that <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2067\">article 137</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2068\">Limitation Act, 1963</a>, contemplates only applications\nto courts and the scheme of the Act is, that it deals only with applications to\ncourts, and the Labour court is not a &#x27;court&#x27; within the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2069\">Limitation Act, 1963</a>.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1418\">43.7. Before the pronouncement of the Supreme Court in the <a href=\"/doc/1309014/\" id=\"a_2084\">Kcrala State\nElectricity Board</a> case&#x27;, the High Courts, by an ingenious application of the prin-\nciples of interpretation of statutes, tried to mitigate the hardships suffered by\nlitigants seeking redress before quasi-judicial bodies and tribunals which could\nnot fall within the meaning of the expression &#x27;court&#x27;. But there again, it is the&#x27;\nsame story of reversed judgments. For example, in one of the cases&#x27;&#x27;&#x27;, the Alla-\nhabad High Court observed that though <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2085\">section 14</a> of the Limitation Act does\nnot, in terms, apply to proceedings under the <a href=\"/doc/1645178/\" id=\"a_2086\">Sales Tax Act</a>, its principle may\napply to them and an assessee may be able, in a subsequent proceeding, to ex-\nclude the time spent in an earlier proceedings. This view was based on an ear-\nlier Full Bench decision of the same High Court.&#x27; But <a href=\"/doc/1309014/\" id=\"a_2087\">in that case</a> also the dis-\nsenting Judge had struck a strong discordant note:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1156\">&#x27;&#x27;In the absence of any statutory provision for enlarging the time, the time\n\ncannot be enlarged on the basis of some principle of equity, justice or good\n&#x27;conscience. The principle of<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2088\"> s. 14</a> by analogy can be applied by courts on\nthe basis of justice, equity and good conscience only if there is no maximum\nlimit \ufb01xed by the statute for extending the period of limitation. If the tri-\nbunal applies the principles of<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2089\"> s. 14</a> of the Limitation Act for computing\nthe period of limitation for the revision, it will, in the guise of computing\nthe period of limitation, extend and enlarge the time for \ufb01ling the revision\nbeyond the limit permitted by<a href=\"/doc/541605/\" id=\"a_2090\"> s. 10</a> (3-B) of the U.P. Sales Tax Act. When\nlimitation is prescribed by a special law and the provisions of the nature of<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2091\">\ns. 14</a> have not been incorporated there, it would be outside the scope of the\ntribunal created by the statute to import the provisions of<a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2092\"> s. 14</a> of the Limi-\ntation Act by analogy and thereby virtually amend the provisions limiting\n. the power to enlarge the period of limitation.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1157\">The Pars0n Tools case&#x27; went up in appeal by special leave to the Supreme\nCourt, which reversed the majority judgment and held that the appellate authority\nand the Judge (Revisions) (Sales Tax), exercising jurisdiction under the U.P.\nSales Tax Act, were not Courts, but were merely admimstrative tribunals. In\nview thereof, it was held that <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2093\">section 14</a> of the Limitation Act did not, in terms\napply to proceedings before such tribunals. The Supreme Court also negatived\nthe theory (propounded by the Allahabad High Court) that even though <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2094\">section\n14</a>, in terms, did not apply to proceedings before sales tax authorities, its principle\n\n&#x27;should apply. After scanning the scheme and language of the U.P. State Sales\n\nTax Act, the Supreme Court observed as under :----\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1158\">&quot;These provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2095\">Limitation Act</a> which the legislature did not, after\ndue application of mind, incorporate in the <a href=\"/doc/1645178/\" id=\"a_2096\">Sales Tax Act</a>, cannot be import-\ned into it by analogy. An enactment being the will of the Legislature, the\n\n&#x27;A.l.R. 1977 S.C. 282. -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1159\">&#x27;&#x27;&#x27;Kerala State Electricity Board, A.l.R._ 1977 SC. 282.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1160\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"820732\" id=\"span_223\">35, <a href=\"/doc/820732/\" id=\"a_2097\">C. It is possible that the Supreme Court may revise its thinking and take\na liberal view as regards the applicability of other provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2100\">Limitation\nAct</a> to Tribunals.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1424\">43.11. The Allahabad High Court had held that the provisions of section 12 High Court\nof the Limitation Act did not apply to the proceedings before a Registrar, eases asuto\nbecause a Registrar acting under the <a href=\"/doc/1489134/\" id=\"a_2101\">Registration Act</a> is not a &quot;Court&quot;, as no Courts&#x27;\njudicial functions are exercised by him. The controversy arose because the\nsub-Registrar refused to register a sale deed, against which refusal the aggrieved\nparty moved the District Registrar under <a href=\"/doc/1526177/\" id=\"a_2102\">section 73(1)</a> of the Registration Act,\n\n1908. The District Registrar gave the bene\ufb01t of <a href=\"/doc/1267250/\" id=\"a_2103\">section 12</a>, limitation Act, to\nthe vendee. The High Court reversed-&#x27; the judgement.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1425\">43.12. The Kerala High Court in U. Chacko v&quot;. P. Marakkar3 held that the\nappellate authority under the <a href=\"/doc/111893178/\" id=\"a_2104\">Kerala Buildings (Lease and Rent Control) Act</a> is\nnot a &quot;court&quot;, and where an appeal under the <a href=\"/doc/18783076/\" id=\"a_2105\">Rent Control Act</a> is barred by the\nLimitation, that authority has no power to condone delay under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_2106\">section 5</a> of\nthe Limitation Act. The High Court also held that the principle of <a href=\"/doc/409538/\" id=\"a_2107\">section 14</a>\nof the Act cannot be extended by analogy where, owing to uncertainty in the\nidentity of the forum where the appeal had to be preferred, the appellant \ufb02itted\n\n7 between one court and to another.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1426\">&#x27;.1\n\n43.13. Similar view was expressed by the Madras High Court,&#x27; holding that\nthe Rent Controller and Appellate Authority under the <a href=\"/doc/89640929/\" id=\"a_2108\">Tamil Nadu Buildings\n(Lease and Rent Control) Act</a> of 1960 are not courts and therefore <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_2109\">sections\n5</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1648955/\" id=\"a_2110\">29(2)</a>, Limitation Act are not applicable to proceedings before them.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1427\">43.14. The Mysore High Court5 has held that a Munsiifbefore whom an\nelection petition is \ufb01led, challenging the elections of the Chairman and Vice-\nChairman of Village Panchayats, is not a &quot;court&quot;,\n\n43.15. .On the other hand, in some cases it has been held&quot; that the question\nwhether the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2111\">Limitation Act</a> are con\ufb01ned in their operation to\nproceedings before courts is no longer res intcrgra, in view of the judgment of\nthe Supreme Court in Athanfs case&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1428\">43.16. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"103578558\" id=\"span_224\">In a <a href=\"/doc/103578558/\" id=\"a_2112\">Delhi</a> case, the argument that the Lt. Governor of Delhi before\nwhom an application was \ufb01led under <a href=\"/doc/48387387/\" id=\"a_2113\">section 91</a> of the Punjab Land Revenue\nAct (17 of 1877) as applied to Delhi is not a court, but functions as a persona\ndesignata was pressed before a Division Bench?</span> In that case certain property\n\n&#x27;C.S.T.U.P. V. Madam La]. A.l.R- 1977 SC. 523.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1429\">For comment, see S.N. N. Kumaraswami ALR</a>. 1975 Madras 383.</span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"786517\" id=\"span_226\"><a href=\"/doc/786517/\" id=\"a_2115\">5Nagreddy v. Khandappa, A.l.R.</a> 1970 Mysore J66.</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1161\">&quot;Bando Banaii v. Bhaskar Baiaji, A.l.R. 1972 Mysore &#x27;31l.\n7--Afham&quot;s case, supra.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1162\">3Raj Chopra V. SmI- Shanna Devi, A_I.R. 1891 Delhi 18.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_227\">232</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1163\">8911; REPORT or LAW COMMN oa INDIA on THE LIMITATION ACT, 1963\n\n(Chapter 43- -Article 137 .&#x27; Other applications.)\n\nin Nizamuddin West, New Delhi, had been put to auction by the Collector, Delhi\nin 1971, in order to realise a certain sum of money due to the Department of\nRehabilitation of the Delhi Administration and had been purchased by the\nappellant in the said auction. The sale was con\ufb01rmed by the Lt. Governor of\nDelhi on 23-12-1971 and the possession of the property was given to the appel-\nlant / auction purchaser. &#x27;,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/48387387/\" id=\"a_2116\">Section 91</a> of the Punjab Land Revenue Act (17 of 1877) as applied to\nDelhi, provides for making an application to set aside a sale within 30 days\nfrom the date of the sale. However, respondent no. 1 \ufb01led an application under\nthat section on 19-2-1972 and pleaded condonation of delay under <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_2117\">section 5</a> of\nthe Limitation Act, 1963, on the ground that he {respondent no. 1) came to know\nof the sale only on 9-2-1972.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1164\">It was argued before the Delhi High Court that <a href=\"/doc/100581/\" id=\"a_2118\">section 5</a> of the Limitation\nAct was not applicable because the Lt. Governor before whom the application\nwas \ufb01led was not a court but a persona designata. In support of this conten-\ntion, reliance was placed on a Full Bench judgement of the Kerala High Court.&#x27;\n\nDissenting from the Kerala view, the Delhi High Court held that the Kerala\nView had been over-ruled by the Supreme Court?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1165\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1309014\" id=\"span_228\">43.17 Surnmarising the law on the subject after the <a href=\"/doc/1309014/\" id=\"a_2119\">Kerala State Electricity\nBoard</a>&#x27;s case, the High Court said:~--</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1166\">&quot;We feel that the argument based on the contention that the application\nunder a special law must necessarily be to a court, before the provisions of\n<a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2120\">Limitation Act</a> are attracted is not borne out by any precedent or principles\nof law. This argument does not appreciate the dilference between case\nwhere there is no period prescribed by a special law and where it is so\nprovided. In the former instance, a party will have to invoke <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2121\">Article 137</a>\nof the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2122\">Schedule to Limitation Act, 1963</a>, and this can be resorted to for\n\ufb01ling an application under any Act, vide A.I.R. l970&#x27;S.C. 209, A.I.R. 1977\nS.C. 282. Such an application alone is required to be \ufb01led before a civil\n\ncourt. But in case of latter kind--as in the present case~where the period.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1167\">is prescribed under a special law like the Act, <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2123\">Article 137</a> of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2124\">Schedule\nto the Limitation Act</a> is not being resorted to at all and the requirement of\napplication being to a court does not necessarily arise.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1432\">43.18. Even prior to the Kerala State Electricity Boara&quot;s case, the Supreme\nCourt had applied the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2125\">Limitation Act</a> in computing the period\nof limitation under a special law like the U.P. Sales Tax Act.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1433\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"103578558\" id=\"span_229\">43.19. Considerable difficulties have been experienced in deciding whether a\nfunctionary acting under a statute is a &#x27;court&#x27; or not, for the purposes of enact&#x27;-\nments using that expression. The Supreme Court has held&#x27; that the Registrar\nof Cooperative Societies under the <a href=\"/doc/160137147/\" id=\"a_2126\">Bihar and Orissa Cooperative Societies Act,\n1935</a> was, to all intents and purposes. a court discharging the same functions and\nduties in the same manner as a court of law is expected to do. In a <a href=\"/doc/103578558/\" id=\"a_2127\">Punjab</a>\ncase&#x27;, it was held that a Commissioner under the <a href=\"/doc/6559712/\" id=\"a_2128\">Workmen&#x27;s Compensation Act</a>\n\n&#x27;Jokkim Fermmdes V. Amina Kuplii Umma, A.I.R. 1974 Ker. 162.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1434\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"34999496\" id=\"span_230\"><a href=\"/doc/34999496/\" id=\"a_2129\">&#x27;Kerala State Electricity Board v. T. P. Kunhalinumma A.I.R.</a> 1977 S.C. 282 para 21.</span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"124469\" id=\"span_231\"><a href=\"/doc/124469/\" id=\"a_2130\">3Commissioner of Sales Tax, U.P. v. Madan Lal, A.l.R.</a> 1977 S.C. 523.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1435\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"181153428\" id=\"span_232\"><a href=\"/doc/181153428/\" id=\"a_2131\">&#x27;Jugal Kishore v. Siramarhi Ccnmzl Cooperative Bank Ltd.----A.l.R.</a> 1967 S.C. 1494.</span>\n&#x27;Ram Sa-rup v. Gm-deb Singh (1969) Labour and Industries Cases 371 (Punjab).\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1436\">89TH REPORT or LAW COMMN. 0:: lNDIA ON THE LIMITATXON ACT, l963 233\n\n&#x27; (Chapter 43---Article 137 .&#x27; Other applications.)\n\nis not a &#x27;civil court&#x27;, within the meaning of section IIOF of the Motor Vehicles\nAct, 1939. &#x27;\n\n43.20. There are n_umerous decisions under <a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_2132\">article 136</a> of the Constitution <a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_2133\">Article 136</a>\non the construction of the expression &quot;tribunal&quot;. The earliest case was Bharat Eg\ufb01lggtu\ufb01on\nBank Ltd. V. Irs Employees.&#x27; in which it was observed that tribunals adorned&#x27;~--Case Law.\nwith the same trappings as courts which derive their power from the State and\nare exercising the judicial power of the State come within the scope of Article\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1437\">136. This principle has been followed in later cases, 3-3 but the tests were with\n\nreference to statutes other than the <a href=\"/doc/1317393/\" id=\"a_2134\">Limitation Act</a>. +\n\n43.21. The result is, that while the Bombay High Court&#x27;, concluded that the\nCommissioner of Workmen&#x27;s Compensation is a &quot;court&quot;, the Madhya Pradesh\n\nV High Court5 took a contrary view.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1438\">43.22. A single Judge of the Calcutta High Court&#x27; has listed no less than 11\nconditions or criteria on whose touchstone an enactment creating a statutory\nauthority will have to be scrutinised, in order to decide whether that authority\nis a &quot;Court&quot;. According to him. on the basis of the judicial decisions, it would\nappear that a tribunal or authority shall be a court if the following conditions, in\neffect, are present: (i) the source of power of the tribunal or authority is the&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1168\">State as the fountain of justice and it is charged with and exercises the inherent\n\njudicial powers of the State; (ii) the jurisdiction to adjudicate the lis between\ncontending parties involving their rights is conferred on it by law and does not\ndepend on any voluntary act or submission of parties; (iii) the right to move the\ntribunal or authority is conferred on the aggrieved party by law: (iv) the proceed-\ning on the lis commences by presentation of the case by the aggrieved party with\na corresponding right on the other party to meet the case: (V) in adjudicating the\ndispute the tribunal or authority follows an established or prescribed procedure:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1169\">(vi) if the dispute is on a question of fact. an opportunity to the parties is given\nto adduce evidence and the facts are to be ascertained through evidence, supple-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1439\">mented by argument; (vii) if the dispute is on questions of law, there will be a\nsubmission of arguments on such questions of law by the parties before such\ntribunal or authority; (viii) the tribunal or authority. in arriving at its decision,\nacts judicially and according to law, following the principles of natural justice and\nfair play and not on any other consideration of policy: (ix) there is a decision\nwholly disposing. of the matter by a \ufb01nding upon the facts and disputed questions\n\nof law (if required); (x) \ufb01nality (subject to appeal, if provided) and authoritati- .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1440\">veness of the decisions. as being binding on parties; (xi) enforceability of the\ndecisions by the tribunal or authority through the process of law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1441\">43.23. The above exposition has been quoted as of some interest for under- No change\nstanding the expression &quot;Court&quot;. We do not. of course, propose to introduce &quot;ceded-\nany de\ufb01nition of &#x27;court&#x27; in the Act of Limitation.&#x27;\n\n\\\n\n43.24. Nor  we recommend any change in <a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2135\">article 137</a> on the above point.\u00bbN0 change\n\nneeded in\n<a href=\"/doc/249731/\" id=\"a_2136\">article 137.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1442\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"653417\" id=\"span_233\"><a href=\"/doc/653417/\" id=\"a_2137\">&#x27;Bharat Bank Ltd. v. Its Employees</a>. A,I.R. 1950 S.C. 188.</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1170\">&quot;Engineering Mazdoo? Sahha v. Hind Cycles Ltd. Slzarma, A.i.R.</a> 1963 SC. 1595.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1171\">&#x27;Rajvabi v. Mackinnon Mackenzie &amp; Co. P. Ltd.
